chapters.title: Complement System
chapters.subtitle: A protein cascade that opsonises, lyses, and recruits — bridging innate sensors to adaptive memory.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: C5a chemotaxis
stations.subtitle: How complement recruits the first wave of cells.

flow.text: Inflammation needs reinforcements.
flow.subtext: Resident tissue cells detect the threat. But the fight requires help from cells circulating in the blood.
flow.insight: Blood cells must be called into tissue

flow.text: Complement is the messenger system that does it.
flow.subtext: Thirty-plus plasma proteins, numbered C1 through C9, activated in sequence. A cascade.
flow.insight: Complement = a cascade of plasma proteins

flow.text: Near the end of the cascade, C5 gets cleaved.
flow.subtext: A convertase enzyme, built earlier in the cascade, chops C5 in half.
flow.insight: C5 is cleaved late in the cascade

flow.text: The cut produces two pieces: C5a and C5b.
flow.subtext: C5a is the small fragment. C5b is the large one.

flow.text: C5b stays. It anchors to a nearby membrane and begins building the MAC.
flow.subtext: The membrane attack complex — the pore that lyses pathogens. C5b is its foundation stone.

flow.text: C5a drifts away. Free in the fluid.
flow.subtext: No membrane anchor. It simply diffuses outward through the tissue — a mobile molecule.

flow.text: C5a has two jobs.
flow.subtext: Once it's out there diffusing, it does two things at once.

flow.text: Job 1 · Anaphylatoxin. C5a finds a mast cell, fires it, histamine pours out.
flow.subtext: It acts like an allergen would — without an allergen being there. The vessels dilate and leak.

flow.text: Job 2 · Chemoattractant. C5a forms a gradient — strong at the source, fading outward.
flow.subtext: Molecules of C5a are densest where they were made, thin out with distance. A molecular scent trail.

flow.text: Neutrophils follow first.
flow.subtext: Highest density of C5a receptors.
flow.insight: Neutrophils are most sensitive to C5a

flow.text: The most sensitive readers.
flow.subtext: They leave the bloodstream and follow the trail.

flow.text: Monocytes follow next.
flow.subtext: They also carry C5a receptors. Slower to arrive than neutrophils, but they come.

flow.text: Eosinophils and basophils come too.
flow.subtext: C5a pulls in every granulocyte lineage. One fragment, four cell types.
flow.insight: C5a recruits neut + mono + eos + baso

flow.text: C5a is complement's most potent chemoattractant.
flow.subtext: C3a can do it weakly.

flow.text: C5a does it much more strongly.
flow.subtext: If both are in the answer choices, the chemotactic one is C5a.
flow.insight: C5a does it much more strongly

flow.text: Now the vignette: serum sickness.
flow.subtext: Drug-protein immune complexes deposit in skin, joints, kidneys.

flow.text: They activate complement.
flow.subtext: C5a pours out.

flow.text: Neutrophils, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils flood the tissues.
flow.subtext: Urticaria, arthralgia, proteinuria, fever.

stations.bridge: A young woman has developed serum sickness ten days after an injectable antibiotic. Immune complexes are depositing in her skin, joints, and kidneys. Her plasma is loaded with a factor that is actively pulling neutrophils, monocytes, eosinophils, and basophils out of her blood vessels and into her tissues. Which molecule does that?
stations.vignette: A 28-year-old woman develops fever, urticaria, arthralgias, and proteinuria 10 days after receiving an injectable antibiotic. Workup suggests a type III hypersensitivity reaction with immune complex deposition. Her plasma is found to contain a factor that strongly attracts neutrophils, monocytes, eosinophils, and basophils to sites of inflammation.
stations.stem: Which mediator is most likely responsible for this chemotactic activity?
keypoints: The injectable antibiotic formed drug-protein immune complexes that deposited in vessels, joints, and glomeruli (the hallmark of serum sickness, a type III hypersensitivity).
The immune complexes bound C1q and activated the classical pathway.
Downstream of the cascade, C5 was cleaved into C5a (diffusible) and C5b (membrane-anchored).
C5a formed a concentration gradient emanating from the deposition sites.

Circulating leukocytes with C5a receptors read that gradient and crossed the endothelium, accumulating at the sites of inflammation — the tissue findings (urticaria, arthralgia, proteinuria) reflect the damage done when they arrived.

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: C3a anaphylatoxin
stations.subtitle: Complement's other anaphylatoxin.

flow.text: Last station we met C5a. Complement makes one more anaphylatoxin — released earlier in the cascade.
flow.subtext: Same job.

flow.text: Less potent.
flow.subtext: Always present whenever complement activates.

flow.text: That molecule is C3a.
flow.subtext: Smaller than C5a. Released the moment C3 is cleaved — the central event of the cascade.

flow.text: C3 sits in the middle of the cascade. It's the most abundant complement protein in plasma.
flow.subtext: Every activation pathway converges on it. The classical, lectin, and alternative pathways all cleave C3.

flow.text: When C3 is cleaved, it splits into C3a and C3b.
flow.subtext: C3a is the small fragment.

flow.text: C3b is the large one.
flow.subtext: Same pattern as C5 → C5a + C5b.

flow.text: C3b stays. It sticks to the pathogen surface and tags it for phagocytes — opsonization.
flow.subtext: We'll meet opsonization properly in the B-cell chapter. For now: C3b marks the target.

flow.text: C3a drifts away. Just like C5a — free, mobile, diffusing through tissue.
flow.subtext: No membrane anchor.

flow.text: Small enough to travel.
flow.subtext: It becomes a signaling molecule.

flow.text: C3a has one main job: anaphylatoxin.
flow.subtext: Unlike C5a, C3a is a weak chemoattractant. Its main role is firing mast cells.

flow.text: Anaphylatoxin means: a molecule that mimics anaphylaxis.
flow.subtext: It fires mast cells the same way an allergen would — but without an allergen being there. Complement did it directly.

flow.text: C3a binds mast cells (in tissues) and basophils (in blood).
flow.subtext: Basophils are essentially mast cells in the bloodstream.

flow.text: Same granules.
flow.subtext: Same trigger.

flow.text: Binding triggers degranulation.
flow.subtext: Preformed granules fuse with the membrane and spill their contents into the tissue. Chief among them: histamine.

flow.text: Histamine dilates vessels.
flow.subtext: Arteriolar smooth muscle relaxes. Blood flow to the area increases.

flow.text: Histamine also makes vessels leaky.
flow.subtext: Endothelial cells pull apart at their junctions. Plasma proteins and fluid escape into the tissue.

flow.text: Result: the local signs of inflammation.
flow.subtext: Warmth (more blood), redness (more blood), swelling (fluid leak), and sometimes wheal-and-flare on the skin.

flow.text: Complement makes three anaphylatoxins: C3a, C4a, C5a.
flow.subtext: All three fire mast cells. The 'a' in each name marks the diffusible, signaling fragment.

flow.text: Potency ranking: C5a >> C3a > C4a.
flow.subtext: C5a is by far the strongest.

flow.text: C3a is solid middle-ground.
flow.subtext: C4a is weak — rarely tested but exists for completeness.

flow.text: All three converge on the same target: the mast cell.
flow.subtext: Any of the three can fire it. The downstream histamine release and vessel response are identical.
flow.insight: C3a/C4a/C5a → same mast cell → same histamine

flow.text: Back to the case: serum sickness ten days after ceftriaxone.
flow.subtext: Drug-IgG complexes activate the classical pathway.
flow.insight: Classical pathway cleaves both C3 and C5 → both anaphylatoxins released

flow.text: Both C3 and C5 get cleaved.
flow.subtext: C3a and C5a both flood the tissue.
flow.insight: Both C3 and C5 get cleaved

flow.text: The test-taking rule: C5a if offered, C3a if not.
flow.subtext: The question tests whether you know the anaphylatoxin role.
flow.insight: Rule: C5a first choice, C3a backup

flow.text: The best answer here would be C5a — but C5a isn't a choice.
flow.insight: The best answer here would be C5a — but C5a isn't a choice

flow.text: C3a is.
flow.subtext: So C3a is the answer.

stations.bridge: Another case of serum sickness — this time from an IV antibiotic. Her plasma again contains a complement peptide that degranulates mast cells and releases histamine. But this time, the answer list doesn't include C5a. Which fragment fills the anaphylatoxin role?
stations.vignette: A 27-year-old woman develops fever, urticaria, and arthralgias 10 days after receiving intravenous ceftriaxone for a skin infection. Serum analysis reveals the presence of a small complement-derived peptide known to trigger mast cell degranulation and release of histamine.
stations.stem: Which of the following peptides is the most likely mediator of this effect?
keypoints: This vignette and the last one (Station 01) look almost identical on purpose — both are serum sickness cases, both describe a complement-derived peptide causing inflammation. But the answer choices are different. Station 01 offered C5a; this one doesn't. The change forces you to notice what's being asked: mast cell degranulation and histamine release. That's the anaphylatoxin job.

Three complement fragments share that job: C3a, C4a, and C5a. Of them, C5a is the most potent, C3a is intermediate, and C4a is the weakest — and C4a is rarely tested. So the practical rule is: C5a if available, C3a if C5a isn't. Here, C3a is the only anaphylatoxin offered.

The full mechanism of this case: ceftriaxone binds serum proteins → acts as a haptenized antigen → IgG forms against it → circulating immune complexes deposit in skin, joints, and kidneys → the classical pathway fires → both C3 and C5 get cleaved → C3a and C5a are released simultaneously → both bind mast cells and basophils → histamine drives the urticaria, fever, and joint pain. The question isolates the C3a contribution because that's the anaphylatoxin it offers you.

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: MAC pore
stations.subtitle: When the membrane attack complex can't form.

flow.text: Start with the pattern: this patient has had Neisseria twice.
flow.subtext: Meningococcemia doesn't usually strike the same person more than once. Something in her defenses is selectively missing.

flow.text: Neisseria is a gram-negative diplococcus.
flow.subtext: Two species matter clinically.

flow.text: N. meningitidis causes meningitis and meningococcemia.
N. gonorrhoeae causes disseminated gonococcal disease.
Gram-negatives have an outer membrane.
flow.subtext: A second fatty layer outside the cell wall — absent in gram-positives. It's their defining architectural feature.
flow.insight: G(–) = extra outer membrane outside cell wall

flow.text: That outer membrane is hard to penetrate.
flow.subtext: Phagocytes struggle with it.
flow.insight: Outer membrane resists antibody + phagocytes alone

flow.text: Antibodies alone often can't drive killing.
flow.subtext: Something has to make an actual hole.
flow.insight: Antibodies alone often can't drive killing

flow.text: The immune system has a special tool for this.
flow.subtext: Complement's final product. A drill.

flow.text: Its name: the MAC — Membrane Attack Complex.
flow.subtext: A multi-protein assembly that embeds in the target membrane and punches a hole through it.

flow.text: The MAC is the very end of the complement cascade.
flow.subtext: Upstream events (C1–C5) build up to this one structural outcome: the pore.

flow.text: The MAC is built from five proteins: C5b, C6, C7, C8, C9.
flow.subtext: Together they're called the terminal complement components. Miss any one and the pore can't form.
flow.insight: Terminal complement = C5b, C6, C7, C8, C9

flow.text: They assemble in sequence, right on the target membrane.
flow.subtext: Not in plasma.
flow.insight: MAC assembles ON the bacterial membrane

flow.text: On the bug itself.
flow.subtext: Each protein recruits the next.

flow.text: C5b lands first. Then C6, C7, and C8 join — one after another.
flow.subtext: Each addition locks the growing complex more tightly into the membrane.

flow.text: Then many copies of C9 polymerize around the complex.
flow.subtext: Ten to eighteen C9 molecules stack into a ring — the barrel of the pore.

flow.text: The finished MAC is a pore.
flow.subtext: A hole punched clean through the bacterial outer membrane.

flow.text: Water and ions rush in. The bacterium bursts.
flow.subtext: Osmotic pressure does the rest. The cell can't maintain its own interior, and it lyses.

flow.text: For most bacteria, this is a backup.
flow.subtext: Phagocytes, antibodies, and opsonization can kill them without ever needing MAC.
flow.insight: MAC = backup tool for most bacteria

flow.text: Neisseria is different.
flow.subtext: It resists phagocytosis.
flow.insight: Neisseria → only reliably killed by MAC

flow.text: It evades antibody-mediated killing.
flow.subtext: The MAC is essentially its only reliable weakness.

flow.text: So if any of C5b, C6, C7, C8, or C9 is missing — the MAC can't form. Neisseria survives.
flow.subtext: That's the molecular signature of 'recurrent Neisseria in an otherwise healthy person.'
flow.insight: Any missing terminal component → recurrent Neisseria

flow.text: This patient's history fits: two Neisseria episodes. Terminal complement deficiency.
flow.subtext: Workup: measure CH50 (total hemolytic complement).
flow.insight: Recurrent Neisseria → check CH50 → find missing terminal component

flow.text: It will be low.
flow.subtext: Then identify the specific missing component.

stations.bridge: A young woman has survived two serious Neisseria infections. Normal immune systems rarely get one, much less two. Something specific has failed in her defenses. Of the choices listed, which missing protein best fits her pattern?
stations.vignette: A 23-year-old woman is admitted with fever, headache, nuchal rigidity, altered mental status, and a petechial rash. CSF analysis reveals many neutrophils and gram-negative diplococci. Her medical history is notable for a similar episode of severe bacterial infection with the same organism several years earlier. She has been otherwise healthy, with no recurrent sinopulmonary infections or autoimmune disease.
stations.stem: Deficiency of which of the following is most likely responsible for her pattern of infection?
keypoints: This patient's history has a specific fingerprint: two serious Neisseria infections, and nothing else. No recurrent sinopulmonary infection, no lupus, no fungal disease. That narrow pattern points to a single defect — terminal complement deficiency, which disables the membrane attack complex (MAC).

The MAC is a pore built from C5b, C6, C7, C8, and C9. It drills through the target cell's membrane, letting water and ions flood in until osmotic pressure bursts the cell. For most bacteria this is a backup mechanism — phagocytes, antibodies, and opsonization can kill them without it. But Neisseria (both N. meningitidis and N. gonorrhoeae) is unusual: it resists intracellular killing and evades antibody-mediated destruction, so the MAC is essentially its only reliable executioner. Lose any terminal component and Neisseria runs free. Among the answer choices, C8 is a terminal component — it fits this pattern exactly.

The clinical picture in this vignette — fever, nuchal rigidity, petechial rash, gram-negative diplococci in CSF — is meningococcemia with meningitis. A single episode could happen to anyone. A second episode of Neisseria in the same patient is the clue that pushes you toward a complement screen, specifically testing total hemolytic complement (CH50), which will be low or absent if any classical/terminal component is missing.

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: C6 deficiency
stations.subtitle: What C6 deficiency breaks. And what it leaves perfectly intact.

flow.text: Three stations in, you've met both halves of complement.
flow.subtext: Stations 01–02: the anaphylatoxins (C3a, C5a).
flow.insight: Complement has two halves: anaphylatoxins + MAC

flow.text: Station 03: the MAC.
flow.subtext: They come from different parts of the cascade.

flow.text: The whole cascade, laid out: C1 → C4 → C2 → C3 → C5 → C6 → C7 → C8 → C9.
flow.subtext: Notice where C6 sits. Right between C5 and the MAC-building team.
flow.insight: C6 sits between C5 and the MAC assembly

flow.text: Halfway through, C3 is cleaved: C3a (anaphylatoxin) + C3b (opsonin).
flow.subtext: Both products are made before anything reaches C6.
flow.insight: C3 cleavage happens upstream of C6

flow.text: Then C5 is cleaved: C5a (anaphylatoxin + chemoattractant) + C5b (MAC foundation).
flow.subtext: Also upstream of C6.
flow.insight: C5 cleavage also happens upstream of C6

flow.text: C5a drifts off.
flow.subtext: C5b stays on the membrane and waits for what comes next.

flow.text: So by the time the cascade reaches C6, four products already exist: C3a, C3b, C5a, C5b.
flow.subtext: Three are immediately functional. One (C5b) is waiting for its next partner.
flow.insight: Upstream of C6: C3a, C3b, C5a all functional; C5b on standby

flow.text: The downstream half of the cascade is MAC assembly.
flow.subtext: C5b recruits C6, then C7, C8, and finally polymerized C9. That's the whole downstream job.
flow.insight: Downstream = C6 + C7 + C8 + C9 building MAC

flow.text: The MAC's job is one thing only: drill a pore, lyse the pathogen.
flow.subtext: No MAC, no pore, no osmotic killing by complement.

flow.text: Now the thought experiment: what if C6 is missing?
flow.subtext: The cascade doesn't know in advance.
flow.insight: If C6 is missing — trace what runs and what stalls

flow.text: It just runs.
flow.subtext: What actually happens?

flow.text: Everything upstream of C6 runs fine.
flow.subtext: C1, C4, C2 assemble.

flow.text: C3 convertase forms.
flow.subtext: C3 gets cleaved.

flow.text: C5 convertase forms.
flow.subtext: C5 gets cleaved.

flow.text: C3a is made. Anaphylatoxin function: preserved.
flow.subtext: Mast cells still fire when C3a reaches them.

flow.text: Histamine still released.
flow.subtext: Vessels still respond.

flow.text: C5a is made. Anaphylatoxin + chemotaxis: preserved.
flow.subtext: Mast cells fire.

flow.text: Neutrophils, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils still get recruited.
flow.subtext: C5a gradient still forms.

flow.text: C3b is made. Opsonization: preserved.
flow.subtext: C3b coats pathogens, phagocytes grab opsonized bugs with their C3b receptors. Most bacteria can still be phagocytosed.

flow.text: C5b is made — but it's made with nowhere to go.
flow.subtext: C5b lands on the bacterial membrane and reaches for C6. C6 is what anchors the nascent complex so C7 and C8 can join.
flow.insight: C5b produced normally — but needs C6 to continue

flow.text: No C6 available. Assembly stalls.
flow.subtext: The complex can't recruit C7 without C6 first. The whole downstream chain pauses — permanently.
flow.insight: Missing C6 → assembly halts at C5b

flow.text: No MAC. No pore. No osmotic lysis.
flow.subtext: The complement-driven killing arm fails. Bacterial membrane stays intact.
flow.insight: No MAC → no pore → complement killing ↓

flow.text: Neisseria survives. This is where the Station 03 clinical story comes from.
flow.subtext: Most bacteria have backup weaknesses — phagocytosis via C3b, antibodies, direct killing.
flow.insight: Neisseria survives because its only reliable killer (MAC) is gone

flow.text: Neisseria doesn't.
flow.subtext: It needs MAC.

flow.text: Put it together as a table.
flow.subtext: Anaphylatoxins: normal (upstream still runs).

flow.text: Bacterial killing: decreased (no MAC).
flow.subtext: Two columns — one broken, one intact.

stations.bridge: A family has multiple siblings with recurrent Neisseria infections. Lab confirms C6 deficiency. Researchers test one patient's plasma against antibody-coated Neisseria meningitidis. What happens?
stations.vignette:
Several siblings from the same family have a history of recurrent infections with Neisseria species. Laboratory evaluation reveals deficiency of complement component C6. When antibody-coated Neisseria meningitidis is incubated with plasma obtained from one of these patients, which of the following outcomes is most likely?

Bacterial killing  |  Generation of anaphylatoxins
keypoints: C3a is made → anaphylatoxin function preserved.
C5a is made → anaphylatoxin + chemotaxis preserved.
C3b is made → opsonization preserved.
C5b is made — it's produced, but it has nowhere to go. It needs C6 to continue.

chapters.title: Antigen Presentation & MHC
chapters.subtitle: How cells advertise self vs. not-self on MHC platters so T cells know what to attack.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: MHC Class I
stations.subtitle: How your immune system sees inside every cell in your body.

flow.text: A virus has invaded a respiratory epithelial cell.
flow.subtext: It's now replicating inside — making more virus, making viral proteins. But all of that is happening behind the cell membrane.

flow.text: From the outside, the cell looks normal. Antibodies can't see inside.
flow.subtext: Antibodies only work on what's in the extracellular fluid — free virions, bacteria outside cells, toxins. They can't cross a cell membrane.

flow.text: The immune system needs a way to see inside every cell.
flow.subtext: If some cells become infected and some don't, the immune system has to be able to tell the difference from the outside.
flow.insight: Immune system needs an inside-out inspection system

flow.text: Every nucleated cell displays MHC Class I on its surface.
flow.subtext: MHC = Major Histocompatibility Complex. Class I is the one on virtually every cell in the body (except red blood cells).
flow.insight: MHC Class I is on every nucleated cell

flow.text: Class I is a display window. It holds a peptide and shows it to the outside.
flow.subtext: The molecule has a groove at the top. A small peptide sits in that groove, facing outward, visible to passing T cells.
flow.insight: Class I displays a peptide in a surface groove

flow.text: The peptides come from inside the cell.
flow.subtext: Cytoplasmic proteins get chopped into ~9-amino-acid fragments, which are then loaded onto Class I. We'll meet the machinery that does the chopping in Station 02.

flow.text: In a healthy cell: only self-peptides on Class I.
flow.subtext: Self-peptides are ignored by T cells (thanks to thymic selection — we'll cover that later). Healthy cells are left alone.
flow.insight: Healthy cells display self peptides · ignored

flow.text: In an infected cell: viral peptides appear on Class I too.
flow.subtext: Viral proteins in the cytoplasm get chopped just like self proteins. The fragments get loaded onto Class I right alongside self peptides.
flow.insight: Infected cell displays viral peptides on Class I

flow.text: CD8+ T cells patrol the tissues, scanning every cell's Class I.
flow.subtext: Each CD8 T cell has a unique T-cell receptor (TCR) that recognizes one specific peptide–Class I combination.
flow.insight: CD8 T cells scan Class I for foreign peptides

flow.text: When the TCR recognizes a viral peptide on Class I, it locks on.
flow.subtext: Recognition is precise — the TCR contacts both the peptide and parts of the Class I molecule itself.
flow.insight: TCR + Class I + viral peptide = engagement

flow.text: Activation triggers release of perforin and granzymes.
flow.subtext: Perforin makes holes in the target cell's membrane. Granzymes flow through and trigger apoptosis from within.

flow.text: The infected cell undergoes apoptosis. The virus dies with it.
flow.subtext: Apoptosis is orderly — the cell breaks into membrane-bound fragments that get phagocytosed. No viral release into the environment.

flow.text: This is how virus-infected respiratory epithelium gets cleared.
flow.subtext: One infected cell at a time, CD8 cells read Class I, find viral peptides, and trigger death. The URI clears.
flow.insight: Class I + CD8 = viral URI clearance

flow.text: So: the stem asks for the mechanism. The answer: Class I MHC–viral peptide presentation.
flow.subtext: That's choice B. It's the only choice that describes the specific molecular event — a viral peptide on Class I engaged by a CD8 TCR.
flow.insight: Answer: B · Class I MHC–viral peptide

stations.bridge: A child with an ordinary viral URI. Her body has to eliminate the infected respiratory epithelial cells somehow — but the virus is inside them. Antibodies can't reach it. What mechanism does the job?
stations.vignette: A 6-year-old girl has had rhinorrhea, low-grade fever, and cough for 4 days. Her symptoms are consistent with a viral upper respiratory infection. Elimination of virus-infected respiratory epithelial cells primarily depends on which of the following immune mechanisms?

choices.text: Class I MHC–viral peptide complex presentation
Class II MHC–viral peptide complex presentation
Phagocytosis of viral particles by CD8+ T lymphocytes

keypoints: Viruses replicate inside host cells. Their proteins end up in the cytoplasm of the infected cell.
Cytoplasmic proteins are chopped into small peptides by the proteasome.
Those peptides get loaded onto MHC class I molecules in the endoplasmic reticulum.
MHC class I ferries the peptide to the cell surface and displays it.
Every nucleated cell does this continuously — normally they just display self-peptides, which are ignored.
In an infected cell, viral peptides appear on MHC class I alongside the self peptides.
Patrolling CD8+ T cells scan MHC class I. When the TCR recognizes a foreign (viral) peptide bound to MHC I, the CD8+ cell activates.
It releases perforin and granzymes, inducing apoptosis in the infected cell. The virus dies along with its host.

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: Proteasome
stations.subtitle: Where the peptides come from.

flow.text: Meet the proteasome.
flow.subtext: A barrel-shaped protease complex, sitting in the cytoplasm of every cell. The shredder.

flow.text: It cuts cytoplasmic proteins into peptides ~9 amino acids long.
flow.subtext: Exactly the size MHC class I displays.
flow.insight: Proteasome output = Class I-sized peptides (self + viral)

flow.text: Self proteins, viral proteins — same machinery handles both.
flow.subtext: The shredder doesn't know self from foreign.

flow.text: Now the drug. Block the proteasome.
flow.subtext: Bortezomib and similar drugs bind the proteasome's active site. The shredding stops.
flow.insight: Proteasome inhibitor shuts off peptide generation

flow.text: No peptides means Class I stays empty in the ER.
flow.subtext: Class I without a peptide is unstable.
flow.insight: No peptide → Class I never reaches surface

flow.text: It's retained in the ER and eventually degraded.
flow.subtext: It never reaches the surface.
flow.insight: It's retained in the ER and eventually degraded

flow.text: CD8 patrols, but finds nothing to recognize.
flow.subtext: The T cells are healthy.
flow.insight: CD8 cells intact but lack recognition targets

flow.text: The targets they'd normally kill are silent.
flow.subtext: Cytotoxic T-cell activity drops.
flow.insight: The targets they'd normally kill are silent

flow.text: Why is this drug given? Multiple myeloma.
flow.subtext: Plasma cells secrete huge amounts of antibody.

flow.text: That means huge amounts of protein being made, folded, and processed.
flow.subtext: They depend on the proteasome more than most cells.
flow.insight: That means huge amounts of protein being made

flow.text: The drug kills myeloma cells — but also silences Class I across the whole body.
flow.subtext: That's the side effect.
flow.insight: Side effect: Class I silenced everywhere

flow.text: Infected cells get spared.
CD8 has no targets.
flow.subtext: Viral infections are less well controlled.

flow.text: Answer: C · Presentation of antigens to CD8+ T lymphocytes.
flow.subtext: The step directly impaired is antigen presentation.
flow.insight: Answer: C · Class I antigen presentation

flow.text: Everything else in the pathway — perforin, cytokines, T-cell viability — is untouched.
flow.subtext: Just no signal to read.
flow.insight: Everything else in the pathway — perforin

stations.bridge: A patient on a proteasome inhibitor starts losing CD8 cytotoxic T-cell responses. Why? The drug doesn't touch T cells directly. It works on the cell being watched, not the one doing the watching. What step is it breaking?
stations.vignette: A researcher administers a proteasome inhibitor to patients undergoing treatment for a plasma cell malignancy. Shortly after therapy, a decrease in cytotoxic T-cell responses to viral infections is observed. Which of the following immunologic processes is most directly impaired by this medication?

choices.text: Presentation of antigens to CD8+ T lymphocytes
Secretion of histamine by mast cells

keypoints: Cytoplasmic proteins (including viral proteins in infected cells) are fed into the proteasome.
The proteasome chops them into peptides ~9 amino acids long — precisely the size that fits the MHC class I groove.
Those peptides are loaded onto MHC class I in the endoplasmic reticulum.
MHC class I needs a peptide in its groove to be stable. With a peptide, it travels to the cell surface. Without one, it is retained and degraded.
On the surface, CD8+ T cells read the peptide-MHC complex. Viral peptides get recognized, the cell is killed.

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: TAP transporter
stations.subtitle: Why a missing transporter empties every Class I window in the body.

flow.text: The problem: peptides are in the cytoplasm. Class I is in the ER.
flow.subtext: Two separate compartments, separated by the ER membrane. They don't communicate freely.
flow.insight: Peptides cytoplasmic · Class I ER · separated

flow.text: A transporter has to bridge them. Its name is TAP.
flow.subtext: TAP = Transporter associated with Antigen Processing. An ABC (ATP-driven) transporter embedded in the ER membrane.
flow.insight: TAP = peptide transporter in ER membrane

flow.text: TAP pumps peptides from cytoplasm into the ER lumen.
flow.subtext: One-way traffic. Peptides enter the ER, where Class I is waiting.

flow.text: In the ER lumen, the peptide loads onto Class I.
flow.subtext: Chaperones (tapasin, calreticulin) hand the peptide to Class I's groove.
flow.insight: ER lumen: peptide binds Class I

flow.text: Loaded Class I is stable and traffics to the cell surface.
flow.subtext: Empty Class I is unstable — it gets retained in the ER and eventually degraded. Peptide loading is the pass key.

flow.text: Now the defect: TAP is mutated.
flow.subtext: In this patient, TAP is non-functional. Everything upstream and downstream of it still exists — only the ferry is broken.
flow.insight: Isolated TAP defect · everything else intact

flow.text: Peptides stay stuck in the cytoplasm.
flow.subtext: The proteasome keeps making them.

flow.text: They keep accumulating.
flow.subtext: They can't cross the ER membrane without TAP.

flow.text: In the ER, Class I molecules never get loaded.
flow.subtext: They keep getting synthesized. They keep waiting for peptides that never come.
flow.insight: Class I in ER · empty · no cargo

flow.text: Empty Class I is unstable. It gets retained and degraded.
flow.subtext: The cell's quality-control system recognizes unstable Class I and recycles it rather than sending it to the surface.
flow.insight: Quality control removes empty Class I

flow.text: Surface expression of Class I plummets across every nucleated cell.
flow.subtext: This is exactly what the flow cytometry in the stem showed: markedly decreased MHC Class I on nucleated cells.
flow.insight: ↓ surface Class I = the patient's flow finding

flow.text: With Class I gone, CD8 surveillance breaks.
flow.subtext: Infected cells can't display viral peptides. CD8 T cells patrol normally, but there's nothing to recognize.
flow.insight: No Class I display = no CD8 recognition

flow.text: Clinically: recurrent sinopulmonary infections.
flow.subtext: Bacterial infections in sinuses, middle ear, and lungs — because clearance of intracellular pathogens depends on CD8-mediated killing.

flow.text: Here's the twist: B cells, T cell counts, and antibodies are all normal.
flow.subtext: Only the Class I display is broken.
flow.insight: Normal T, B, Ig · only ↓ Class I · unique pattern

flow.text: Everything else on the immune panel looks fine.
flow.subtext: This is the diagnostic fingerprint.
flow.insight: Everything else on the immune panel looks fine

flow.text: That specific panel = Bare Lymphocyte Syndrome type I.
flow.subtext: Named because the cells look "bare" of Class I on the surface. Cause: loss-of-function mutations in TAP1 or TAP2.

flow.text: Answer: D · Peptide transporter (TAP).
flow.subtext: The other choices (ADA, Fas ligand, IL-2Rα) cause completely different immunodeficiency syndromes.

stations.bridge: A child with recurrent sinus and lung infections. Lymphocyte counts normal. Antibodies normal. But flow cytometry shows almost no MHC Class I on her cell surfaces. What single protein, when missing, produces this exact pattern?
stations.vignette: A 3-year-old girl is evaluated for recurrent sinus infections and multiple episodes of pneumonia since infancy. She is currently well. Physical examination is unremarkable. Laboratory studies reveal normal numbers of CD4⁺ and CD8⁺ T lymphocytes, normal B-lymphocyte counts, and normal serum immunoglobulin levels. Flow cytometric analysis of peripheral blood shows markedly decreased surface expression of MHC class I molecules on nucleated cells. Genetic testing identifies a mutation responsible for this defect. This mutation most likely affects which of the following?
keypoints: Proteasome-generated peptides sit in the cytoplasm.
MHC class I molecules fold in the ER lumen.
The two compartments are separated by the ER membrane.
TAP (Transporter associated with Antigen Processing) is an ATP-driven pump in the ER membrane. It moves cytoplasmic peptides into the ER lumen, where they can be loaded onto Class I.
Peptide-loaded Class I is stable and traffics to the cell surface. Empty Class I is unstable, retained, and degraded.
Without TAP, peptides never reach the ER. Class I stays empty. Surface expression drops across all nucleated cells.

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: MHC Class II
stations.subtitle: Where Class II meets its peptides — and why it isn't the ER.

flow.text: Class II is a different MHC class, handling a different kind of antigen.
flow.subtext: Class I shows what's inside the cell. Class II shows what the cell ate from outside.
flow.insight: Class I = inside · Class II = outside

flow.text: HLA-DR, DP, DQ are the human Class II molecules.
flow.subtext: Expressed mainly on professional antigen-presenting cells: dendritic cells, macrophages, and B cells.

flow.text: APCs swallow extracellular material — phagocytosis or endocytosis.
flow.subtext: A piece of bacterium, a viral glycoprotein, a soluble protein — anything from outside can be taken in.

flow.text: The swallowed cargo ends up in an endosome.
flow.subtext: A membrane-bound vesicle that matures: it gets more acidic, gains more proteases, merges with lysosomal compartments.
flow.insight: Ingested cargo = in endosome · acidic · proteolytic

flow.text: Class II is made in the ER — same place as Class I.
flow.subtext: Both classes start in the ER. But they end up in completely different loading compartments.
flow.insight: Class II starts in ER · does not stay

flow.text: In the ER, Class II's groove is blocked by an invariant chain (Ii).
flow.subtext: Ii physically occupies the groove so Class II can't accidentally bind any peptide floating around the ER (where Class I-bound peptides live).
flow.insight: Ii keeps Class II empty while in ER

flow.text: Class II + Ii leaves the ER and enters the endocytic pathway.
flow.subtext: Vesicular trafficking routes Class II to the endosome instead of the cell surface. Ii is actually the signal that directs this routing.
flow.insight: Class II is routed to the endocytic system

flow.text: In the endosome, proteases degrade Ii down to a small fragment called CLIP.
flow.subtext: CLIP = Class-II-associated Invariant-chain Peptide. It's the leftover piece of Ii that still sits in the groove.
flow.insight: Ii → chewed → CLIP remains in groove

flow.text: Meanwhile in the same endosome, the antigen is being shredded.
flow.subtext: The viral glycoprotein taken up earlier has been broken into peptide fragments by the same proteases.
flow.insight: Antigen chewed into peptides · same compartment

flow.text: HLA-DM swaps CLIP out and swaps the antigenic peptide in.
flow.subtext: HLA-DM is a chaperone that only works in the endosome. It catalyzes the exchange.

flow.text: Now Class II is loaded. It traffics to the cell surface.
flow.subtext: The peptide-bound complex is stable. Exocytic trafficking delivers it to the plasma membrane.

flow.text: CD4+ T cells read Class II-peptide complexes.
flow.subtext: CD4 is the co-receptor that binds Class II. This is how CD4 helper T cells get activated to drive antibody production, macrophage activation, and other responses.
flow.insight: CD4 reads Class II · triggers helper responses

flow.text: So: the antigen and Class II met in the endosome.
flow.subtext: Not the ER (that's where Class II started, but the groove was blocked).

flow.text: Not the cytosol (that's Class I's peptide source).
flow.subtext: The endosome is where the two streams merge.
flow.insight: Not the cytosol (that's Class I's peptide source)

flow.text: The answer: C · Endosomes.
flow.subtext: The other choices describe compartments that don't load Class II: the ER (Class II made but not loaded), the cytosol (Class I source), mitochondria and nucleus (unrelated).

stations.bridge: A researcher has a CD4+ T cell clone that responds to a viral glycoprotein presented on HLA-DR. The virus wasn't replicating in the APC — the protein was phagocytosed from outside. Somewhere inside the cell, that viral protein got combined with HLA-DR. Which compartment?
stations.vignette: A researcher isolates a CD4⁺ T-cell clone that specifically responds to an influenza surface glycoprotein presented by HLA-DR molecules. The viral protein was first internalized by an antigen-presenting cell and processed before being displayed on the cell surface. Within which of the following intracellular compartments was this antigen most likely combined with HLA-DR?
keypoints: The antigen stream: Extracellular proteins are taken up by antigen-presenting cells (dendritic cells, macrophages, B cells) via phagocytosis or receptor-mediated endocytosis. The cargo is routed through the endocytic pathway into increasingly acidic, protease-rich endosomes, where the protein is degraded into peptides.

The MHC stream: Class II molecules are synthesized in the ER, but their peptide-binding groove is blocked by the invariant chain (Ii). This prevents Class II from accidentally binding ER peptides (which are meant for Class I). Class II + Ii then exits the ER and is routed into the endocytic pathway.

The rendezvous: The two streams meet in a specialized late endosomal compartment (sometimes called MIIC). Here, proteases degrade Ii down to a small fragment called CLIP, which still occupies the groove. The chaperone HLA-DM then swaps CLIP out for an antigenic peptide. Loaded Class II traffics to the surface, where CD4+ T cells read it.

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: HLA restriction
stations.subtitle: Why some HLA alleles can't present a particular polypeptide — and why that breaks antibody production.

flow.text: A subunit vaccine: one recombinant polypeptide. That's the entire antigen.
flow.subtext: No multiple proteins.

flow.text: No diversity.
flow.subtext: Just one protein, repeated dose after dose.

flow.text: Making antibody against a protein is T-cell dependent.
flow.subtext: B cells alone can make weak, short-lived IgM responses to proteins. For class switching and durable high-affinity antibody, they need help from CD4+ T cells.
flow.insight: Protein antigen → needs CD4 help for durable antibody

flow.text: CD4 help starts with CD4 recognizing Class II + peptide on an APC.
flow.subtext: Station 04 covered this. CD4's TCR engages the HLA Class II molecule displaying a peptide derived from the antigen.
flow.insight: CD4 activation = reading Class II display

flow.text: For this vaccine, the peptide must be a fragment of the polypeptide.
flow.subtext: The APC internalizes the vaccine, chops it in the endosome, and tries to load a fragment onto Class II — same pathway as Station 04.
flow.insight: Class II peptide = fragment of the vaccine protein

flow.text: Here's the twist: every person's Class II alleles are different.
flow.subtext: HLA-DR, DP, and DQ alleles are among the most polymorphic genes in the human genome. Different alleles = different groove shapes = different binding specificities.
flow.insight: HLA Class II is highly polymorphic across people

flow.text: Any given Class II groove can only bind a subset of possible peptides.
flow.subtext: The groove's shape accepts some amino-acid side chains and rejects others. Most peptides will fit at least one of a person's Class II alleles — but not always all of them.
flow.insight: Each allele binds only some peptides · not all

flow.text: For the non-responders, no fragment of this polypeptide fits any of their Class II alleles.
flow.subtext: A single polypeptide can only be chopped so many ways. If none of those ~9–15 aa fragments fit this person's Class II grooves, they're out of options.
flow.insight: All possible fragments fail to bind their Class II

flow.text: APCs can't display this antigen. CD4 never sees it.
flow.subtext: CD4 T cells with TCRs against this polypeptide exist, but they never encounter their antigen because it's never displayed.
flow.insight: No Class II display = no CD4 activation

flow.text: No CD4 help reaches the B cells.
flow.subtext: B cells still recognize the polypeptide via their BCRs. But without CD40-CD40L co-stimulation and cytokines from CD4, they can't class-switch to IgG.
flow.insight: B cells unhelped · no class switching

flow.text: Without class switching, there's no durable antibody response.
flow.subtext: Maybe a tiny transient IgM.
flow.insight: No class switch → no measurable antibody

flow.text: But no sustained IgG titer.
flow.subtext: Which is exactly what "no measurable antibody titer" means.

flow.text: Answer: D · Lack of Class II MHC presentation of the polypeptide.
flow.subtext: The non-responders have HLA-DR (or DP or DQ) alleles that can't bind any fragment of this one polypeptide. The entire downstream response collapses.
flow.insight: Answer: D · Class II presentation fails

stations.bridge: A subunit vaccine made from a single recombinant polypeptide. 10% of adults make no antibody at all after the full series. Genetic testing: they share an HLA allele. Trace it back — what immunologic step is missing?
stations.vignette: After a full series of immunizations with a new subunit vaccine made from a recombinant polypeptide, 10% of healthy adults do not develop measurable antibody titers against the polypeptide. Genetic testing shows that the nonresponders have a higher frequency of a particular HLA allele compared with responders. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this group's failure to respond to immunization?

choices.text: Failure of B lymphocytes to recognize the polypeptide
Failure of T lymphocytes to recognize the polypeptide
Lack of class I MHC presentation of the polypeptide
Lack of class II MHC presentation of the polypeptide

keypoints: Antibody production against a protein antigen is a T-dependent response. It requires CD4+ helper T cells to recognize the antigen via HLA Class II + peptide, then deliver co-stimulation to B cells so they can class-switch and produce durable high-affinity antibody. Break that chain anywhere, and antibody production fails.

The key insight is that HLA Class II alleles differ between people. Each HLA-DR (and DP, and DQ) allele has a groove with its own binding preferences. A particular allele might bind thousands of peptide sequences beautifully — but there are always some peptides it simply can't hold. If a vaccine consists of one recombinant polypeptide, and your HLA Class II alleles happen to have no compatible binding groove for any fragment of that polypeptide, your APCs have nothing to display. CD4 never sees the antigen. B cells never get the help they need. Antibody titers stay at zero.
That's D · Lack of class II MHC presentation of the polypeptide.

Clinical parallel: this mechanism is one reason why multivalent vaccines (combining multiple antigenic proteins) and conjugate vaccines (linking a polysaccharide to a carrier protein with diverse T-cell epitopes) work better than single-polypeptide subunit vaccines. The more potential Class II-binding peptides the vaccine carries, the less likely it is that any given person's HLA alleles will miss all of them.

stations.label: Station 06
stations.title: HLA typing
stations.subtitle: Why HLA typing predicts autoimmune disease risk — and what HLA actually is.

flow.text: A young woman. Her mother has SLE. Her sister has RA. She wants to know her own risk.
flow.subtext: Autoimmune disease clusters in families. The genetic contribution is real, and it's partly (though not entirely) carried in HLA.

flow.text: HLA = Human Leukocyte Antigen. It's the human name for MHC.
flow.subtext: Same molecules we've been studying for five stations. 'HLA' is just the clinical/genetic name; 'MHC' is the functional/immunologic name.
flow.insight: HLA = MHC · same thing · two names

flow.text: HLA genes encode Class I (HLA-A, B, C) and Class II (HLA-DR, DP, DQ).
flow.subtext: All clustered tightly on the short arm of chromosome 6. One of the most polymorphic regions in the human genome.
flow.insight: HLA-A/B/C = Class I · HLA-DR/DP/DQ = Class II

flow.text: The name histocompatibility comes from tissue transplantation.
flow.subtext: Histo = tissue. These molecules were first identified as the main antigens that determine whether a transplanted tissue is accepted or rejected.

flow.text: HLA alleles are inherited as haplotypes — linked blocks, one from each parent.
flow.subtext: Because all HLA genes sit close together on chromosome 6, they usually travel together during meiosis. You get one complete block from mom, one from dad.
flow.insight: Haplotype = linked HLA block inherited from one parent

flow.text: That means siblings share HLA alleles more than unrelated people do.
flow.subtext: Each sibling randomly inherits one of 2 haplotypes from each parent — 4 possible combinations.
flow.insight: Siblings share HLA haplotypes · more than strangers

flow.text: Roughly 1/4 are fully HLA-identical, 1/2 share one haplotype, 1/4 share none.
flow.subtext: We'll come back to this in Station 07.

flow.text: Different HLA alleles display different self-peptides.
flow.subtext: Every person walks around with Class I and Class II molecules showing a slightly different sample of self-peptides — determined by which HLA alleles they happened to inherit.
flow.insight: Each allele presents a distinct self-peptide repertoire

flow.text: Some HLA alleles happen to present self-peptides in an autoimmune-activating way.
flow.subtext: A particular self-peptide in a particular groove can look foreign enough to trigger a T cell that slipped past thymic deletion.
flow.insight: HLA allele + self-peptide → autoreactive T-cell trigger

flow.text: That's the molecular basis of HLA-linked autoimmunity.
flow.insight: That's the molecular basis of HLA-linked autoimmunity

flow.text: That's why certain HLA alleles strongly associate with specific autoimmune diseases.
flow.subtext: These pairings are tested repeatedly on board exams.

flow.text: HLA typing tells this patient whether she inherited the same at-risk alleles her mother and sister carry.
flow.subtext: Not a crystal ball — HLA isn't the only factor. But it's the biggest single-gene contributor to most autoimmune diseases.
flow.insight: HLA typing quantifies inherited autoimmune risk

flow.text: Answer: E · Histocompatibility antigens.
flow.subtext: HLA = MHC = histocompatibility antigens.

flow.text: Inherited as haplotypes.
flow.subtext: Vary between people.

flow.text: Linked to autoimmune disease risk.
flow.subtext: The test the physician ordered answers the family-history question because those are the only molecules on this list that are both inherited and disease-linked.

stations.bridge: Autoimmune disease runs in families. HLA typing is the test that formalizes that observation. But to know what the test is telling you, you have to know what HLA actually is — and which of these five options describes it correctly.
stations.vignette: A 23-year-old woman is concerned about her risk of developing an autoimmune disease. Her mother has systemic lupus erythematosus and her older sister has recently been diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis. Her physician orders HLA typing to evaluate her genetic predisposition to autoimmune disease. Which of the following best describes what HLA alleles represent?
keypoints: HLA genes encode MHC Class I (HLA-A, -B, -C) and Class II (HLA-DR, -DP, -DQ) molecules.
The genes are clustered on the short arm of chromosome 6 and are extremely polymorphic — each locus has dozens to hundreds of alleles in the population.
Each person inherits one haplotype (a linked block of HLA alleles) from each parent, so HLA is codominantly expressed (both parental haplotypes show up on the cell surface).

Because haplotypes are inherited as blocks, relatives share HLA alleles — which is why siblings often have similar autoimmune disease risk, and why HLA matching matters for bone marrow and solid organ transplantation.

stations.label: Station 07
stations.title: Sibling matching
stations.subtitle: Why any random sibling has a 25% chance of being a perfect HLA match.

flow.text: Patient needs a kidney. Brother volunteers. Both parents in common.
flow.subtext: Full siblings — same mom, same dad. The HLA genetics is the cleanest possible case.

flow.text: HLA matching matters because MHC drives transplant rejection.
flow.subtext: Donor HLA that doesn't match recipient HLA looks foreign to recipient T cells → allograft rejection. Matching = less immune attack.

flow.text: Each parent carries two HLA haplotypes.
flow.subtext: One on each copy of chromosome 6.

flow.text: Call mom's haplotypes A and B.
flow.subtext: Call dad's haplotypes C and D.
flow.insight: Call mom's haplotypes A and B

flow.text: Each parent passes one haplotype per child — chosen randomly.
flow.subtext: Meiosis gives each gamete one of the two parental chromosomes 6.

flow.text: Mom passes A or B (50/50).
flow.subtext: Dad passes C or D (50/50).

flow.text: So each child has one of four possible combinations: A·C, A·D, B·C, B·D.
flow.subtext: Each combination has equal probability — 1/4. This is the Punnett square for HLA haplotype inheritance.

flow.text: The patient has one specific combination — say A·C.
flow.subtext: Whatever she drew, her combination is fixed. The brother's inheritance is a fresh independent draw from the same four options.

flow.text: For a full match, the brother needs to match both haplotypes.
flow.subtext: Same maternal haplotype (A, not B).
flow.insight: Full match requires both parental haplotypes to match

flow.text: AND same paternal haplotype (C, not D).
flow.subtext: Both conditions must hold.

flow.text: Probability brother gets A from mom: 1/2. Probability brother gets C from dad: 1/2.
flow.subtext: Each parent's meiosis is an independent 50/50 event. No bias, no linkage between parents.
flow.insight: P(match mom) = ½ · P(match dad) = ½

flow.text: Independent events multiply: ½ × ½ = ¼.
flow.subtext: That's the classic Punnett-square outcome for a dihybrid setup where both conditions must co-occur.

flow.text: Full sibling match distribution: ¼ full match · ½ haploidentical · ¼ no share.
flow.subtext: Those are the three possible sibling outcomes, and their probabilities follow directly from the Punnett square.
flow.insight: ¼ / ½ / ¼ = match / haplo / none

flow.text: Compare: identical twins = 100%. Parent-child = always haploidentical.
flow.subtext: Twins share the same zygote — perfect match. Parent-child always shares exactly one haplotype (the one the parent donated) — never a full match, never zero.

flow.text: Back to the stem: brother, same parents. Full sibling. 1-in-4 chance.
flow.subtext: Nothing else about the vignette changes the math. 32-year-old brother, 49-year-old sister, both from the same parents = full sibling = 25%.

flow.text: Rule out the other options quickly: 1:1, 1:2, 1:6, 1:8 all fail the Punnett math.
1:1 would be identical twins.
1:2 would be parent-child (always haploidentical).
flow.subtext: 'Perfect match' is 0 for parent-child pairing anyway.

flow.text: 1:6 and 1:8 don't correspond to any standard Mendelian setup.
Answer: C · 1:4 (25%).
flow.subtext: The rule to remember: any full sibling has a 1-in-4 chance of being an HLA match. Applies to kidney, stem-cell, and every other HLA-matched transplant scenario.

stations.bridge: End-stage renal disease. A transplant is needed. The patient's full sibling volunteers. Without any lab work, just from Mendelian inheritance of HLA haplotypes — what's the probability he's a perfect match?
stations.vignette: A 49-year-old woman with end-stage renal disease from polycystic kidney disease is evaluated for a kidney transplant. Her 32-year-old brother, with whom she shares both biological parents, volunteers as a potential donor. What is the probability that he will be a perfect HLA match for her?
keypoints: Each parent carries two HLA haplotypes (one on each copy of chromosome 6). Call mom's haplotypes A and B, dad's C and D.
Each parent passes one haplotype to each child, chosen randomly. Mom passes A or B with equal probability. Dad passes C or D with equal probability.
Every child therefore ends up with one of four possible combinations: A·C, A·D, B·C, or B·D — each with probability 1/4.

The patient has one specific combination. For her brother to be a perfect HLA match, he needs to have inherited the same haplotype from mom (probability 1/2) and the same haplotype from dad (probability 1/2).
These are independent events: 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/4.

stations.label: Station 08
stations.title: Mixed lymphocyte reaction
stations.subtitle: How the mixed lymphocyte reaction reads everything we've learned.

flow.text: Pre-transplant workup. Question: will recipient T cells attack donor tissue?
flow.subtext: If they recognize donor HLA as foreign, they'll trigger rejection. The MLR tests exactly this reaction in a dish.

flow.text: HLA-DR typing: recipient = DR3/DR4. Sister = DR3/DR6.
flow.subtext: They share DR3 — one haplotype match, haploidentical.
flow.insight: Shared DR3 · recipient-only DR4 · donor-only DR6

flow.text: DR4 is recipient-only.
flow.subtext: DR6 is donor-only.

flow.text: Three categories: shared (DR3), recipient-only (DR4), donor-only (DR6).
flow.subtext: This three-way sort is the entire key. Every answer choice is about which of these categories drives proliferation.

flow.text: The MLR: mix recipient T cells with irradiated donor cells.
flow.subtext: 'One-way' means only one side responds. Irradiation stops donor cells from dividing — so any measured proliferation is recipient-driven.

flow.text: Donor cells display their own Class II: DR3 and DR6 on the cell surface.
flow.subtext: Irradiated cells can't divide, but surface MHC molecules are still present. Those are what recipient T cells see.
flow.insight: Donor cells present DR3 and DR6

flow.text: Recipient T cells were thymically selected to ignore self.
flow.subtext: During development, any T cell with a TCR that strongly recognized self-MHC was deleted. Remaining T cells are tolerant to recipient's own DR3 and DR4.
flow.insight: Thymic tolerance to self DR3 and DR4

flow.text: DR3 is on donor cells — but recipient is tolerant → no response.
flow.subtext: Shared alleles look like self. No proliferation despite DR3 being physically present.

flow.text: DR4 isn't on donor cells — recipient-only. Absent from the culture as a stimulus.
flow.subtext: You can only respond to what's there. DR4 is on recipient cells (self), not on donor cells.

flow.text: DR6 is on donor cells — and foreign to the recipient.
flow.subtext: Recipient never encountered DR6 during thymic education. T cells capable of reacting to DR6 are still in her repertoire.
flow.insight: DR6 foreign · reactive T cells remain

flow.text: Recipient T cells proliferate in response to DR6.
flow.subtext: This is the measurable readout: T-cell division, quantified by thymidine incorporation or CFSE flow cytometry.

flow.text: So the answer: T cells respond to DR6 only.
flow.subtext: Not DR3 (self-tolerated).

flow.text: Not DR4 (not in the culture).
Not any combination including DR3 or DR4.
flow.subtext: Single allele: DR6.
flow.insight: Not any combination including DR3 or DR4

flow.text: This is the molecular basis of transplant rejection.
flow.subtext: In vivo, recipient T cells encounter foreign HLA on the graft.

flow.text: Same recognition event, same proliferation, same cytotoxic attack — only now on transplanted tissue instead of cells in a dish.
Answer: F · HLA-DR6 only.
flow.subtext: Recognition is driven by what's foreign AND what's present. DR6 is both — nothing else qualifies.

flow.text: Chapter 6 complete. You've covered every MHC concept the USMLE will ask.
flow.subtext: Class I pathway · Class II pathway · HLA = histocompatibility antigens · sibling match ¼ · linkage disequilibrium · allorecognition. Next chapter: the T cells themselves.

stations.bridge: A pre-transplant workup. Recipient carries DR3/DR4. Donor sister carries DR3/DR6. In a one-way MLR, recipient T cells are mixed with irradiated donor cells. Which HLA allele(s) will recipient T cells respond to?
stations.vignette: A transplant candidate's HLA typing shows HLA-DR3 and HLA-DR4. Her sister, a potential donor, is typed as HLA-DR3 and HLA-DR6. A one-way mixed lymphocyte reaction (MLR) is performed: the recipient's peripheral blood T lymphocytes are cultured with irradiated peripheral blood lymphocytes from the donor sister, and recipient T-cell proliferation is measured. Proliferation of the recipient's T lymphocytes will occur in response to which of the following HLA allele(s)?
keypoints: Recipient T cells carry recipient's HLA-DR3 and HLA-DR4 on their own cell surfaces. Self — not the stimulus.
Irradiated donor cells present HLA-DR3 and HLA-DR6 (the donor's alleles). Irradiation prevents donor cells from proliferating, so any measurable proliferation comes from the recipient.

chapters.title: T-Cell Biology
chapters.subtitle: Thymic education, activation, and the effector and helper differentiations T cells make in the periphery.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: Thymic development
stations.subtitle: How a flow cytometry panel pins a leukemic cell to a single stage of T-cell development.

flow.text: The question: match a flow panel to a normal cell population.
flow.subtext: Every normal developmental stage has a distinctive combination of surface and intracellular markers. Leukemic cells are frozen at the stage where transformation occurred.
flow.insight: Match the leukemic phenotype to a developmental stage

flow.text: Key markers: CD4+, CD8+, TdT+, TCR β D-J rearranged.
flow.subtext: These four are the diagnostic constellation. Everything else on the panel is context or distraction.

flow.text: Critical clue: CD4 AND CD8 together. That's "double-positive."
flow.subtext: Mature T cells in the periphery are either CD4+ OR CD8+, never both. Double-positive means still inside the thymus.

flow.text: Specifically: thymic cortex.
flow.subtext: The thymus has two regions — outer cortex and inner medulla. DP cells live in the cortex, where positive selection happens.
flow.insight: DP cells live in the thymic cortex

flow.text: Quick T-cell development map: bone marrow → thymus → periphery.
flow.subtext: Pro-T cells originate in the bone marrow, migrate to the thymus, mature there, and then circulate as mature single-positive cells.

flow.text: Inside the thymus, cells pass through three stages: DN → DP → SP.
flow.subtext: Double-negative (outer cortex, no CD4/CD8) → double-positive (cortex, CD4+CD8+) → single-positive (medulla, CD4 or CD8 alone).

flow.text: Our patient's cell is DP. That places it in the thymic cortex.
flow.subtext: Locked in — no other location in the body shows double-positive T-lineage cells.

flow.text: Next marker: TdT+. That means immature lymphocyte.
flow.subtext: TdT (terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase) adds random nucleotides during V(D)J rearrangement. Only expressed in cells actively building receptors — never in mature lymphocytes.
flow.insight: TdT+ = cell still building its receptor

flow.text: Next: TCR β-chain with D-J rearrangement — partial, not complete.
flow.subtext: Full TCR assembly is V-D-J joined together. D-J only means the cell is mid-rearrangement — consistent with an early-to-mid thymocyte.
flow.insight: D-J only = mid-thymic stage · partial TCR

flow.text: All four clues converge: thymic cortex DP thymocyte.
flow.subtext: CD4+ CD8+ = DP.

flow.text: TdT+ = immature.
D-J rearrangement = mid-thymic.
flow.subtext: The constellation points to one stage only.

flow.text: What about the surface IgM and cytoplasmic μ? Cross-lineage expression.
flow.subtext: Aggressive leukemias sometimes express markers of both lineages.
flow.insight: Cross-lineage markers don't override rearrangement evidence

flow.text: The diagnostic rule: when cross-lineage markers appear, go with the receptor gene that's rearranged.
flow.subtext: TCR β → T-cell.
flow.insight: The diagnostic rule: when cross-lineage markers appear

flow.text: Answer: E · T-lymphocyte thymocytes localized to the thymic cortex.
flow.subtext: This phenotype maps to T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia/lymphoma (T-ALL), which transforms from cortical thymocytes. Often presents with a mediastinal mass in adolescents.
flow.insight: Answer: E · thymic cortex DP thymocyte

stations.bridge: The question hands you a flow cytometry profile of a leukemic cell and asks which normal population it resembles. Four markers matter. Line them up and only one developmental stage fits.
stations.vignette:
A 14-year-old boy is evaluated after being diagnosed with an aggressive lymphoid malignancy. Flow cytometry of the malignant cells reveals expression of CD4, CD8, surface IgM, cytoplasmic IgM μ heavy chain, and TdT. Additional studies show that the cells express MHC molecules and demonstrate rearrangement of the T-lymphocyte receptor β-chain gene involving only the D and J segments.

Which of the following normal cell populations most closely corresponds to the stage of differentiation represented by these malignant cells?

choices.text: Activated cytotoxic effector T lymphocytes in the circulation
Mature IgM-secreting B lymphocytes in the lymph node
Mature immunoglobulin-secreting plasma cells in the lymph node
Pre-B lymphocyte progenitor of mature B lymphocytes in the bone marrow
T-lymphocyte thymocytes localized to the thymic cortex

keypoints: CD4+ AND CD8+ simultaneously — this "double-positive" (DP) phenotype exists in only one place in the body: the thymic cortex. Mature T cells in the blood and tissues are single-positive (CD4+CD8− or CD4−CD8+).
TdT (terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase) — adds random N-nucleotides at V(D)J junctions during receptor rearrangement. Found in immature lymphocytes of either lineage but absent in mature cells.
TCR β-chain with only D-J rearrangement — partial rearrangement. Full TCR assembly is V-D-J; this cell is mid-rearrangement, consistent with an early-to-intermediate thymocyte.
MHC expressed — nucleated cells all express MHC Class I; non-diagnostic.

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: CD8 activation
stations.subtitle: Why a naive CD8 T cell needs more than just antigen to become a cytotoxic T lymphocyte.

flow.text: Acute viral infection. Naive CD8 T cells need to become cytotoxic effector cells.
flow.subtext: The goal: clonal expansion into many CTLs that can find and kill infected cells across the body.
flow.insight: Goal: naive CD8 → army of CTLs

flow.text: CD8 is Class I-restricted — recap from Chapter 6.
flow.subtext: Every CD8 TCR reads peptides on Class I MHC.

flow.text: It never recognizes Class II.
flow.subtext: That fact alone already rules out three of the six answer choices.

flow.text: Signal 1: Class I MHC + viral peptide engages the CD8 TCR.
flow.subtext: That's the specificity signal — it tells this particular CD8 clone it has found its antigen.
flow.insight: Signal 1 = TCR recognition of Class I + peptide

flow.text: Signal 1 alone: not sufficient. Naive T cells need a second signal.
flow.subtext: Recognition without a growth signal actually produces anergy — a kind of functional inactivation. The cell needs something more to commit to proliferation.

flow.text: The second signal is a cytokine: interleukin-2 (IL-2).
flow.subtext: IL-2 is the T-cell growth factor. Binding IL-2R on the CD8 surface drives transition into the cell cycle and triggers massive proliferation.

flow.text: Where does the IL-2 come from? CD4+ helper T cells.
flow.subtext: CD4 cells activated on Class II by the same pathogen (often on the same APC) secrete IL-2. That IL-2 diffuses to nearby CD8 cells and licenses them to expand.
flow.insight: IL-2 from CD4 helper · delivered to CD8

flow.text: IL-2 binds the IL-2 receptor on the activated CD8 surface.
flow.subtext: Upon recognition (signal 1), CD8 upregulates the high-affinity IL-2R (including CD25, the α chain). Now it can respond to circulating IL-2.

flow.text: Effect: clonal expansion — one naive cell becomes thousands of effectors.
flow.subtext: IL-2 signaling drives rapid division every ~6 hours for several days. The CD8 population specific for this virus grows by orders of magnitude.

flow.text: Those effectors are cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs), ready to kill infected cells.
flow.subtext: Loaded with perforin and granzymes, now circulating, scanning every cell for Class I + viral peptide. When they find a match, the target dies (Station 03).
flow.insight: Output: circulating CTLs hunting infected cells

flow.text: Answer: B · Class I MHC + IL-2.
flow.subtext: The one combination that matches CD8's restriction AND delivers the growth signal naive CD8 needs. Two signals, one correct pairing.

stations.bridge: A naive CD8 T cell meets a virus-infected cell displaying viral peptide on Class I. That's signal one. Alone, it isn't enough. What second signal is required to transform the naive cell into an effector CTL?
stations.vignette: A patient has an acute viral infection. Antigen-presenting cells activate naive CD8⁺ T cells so they can differentiate into cytotoxic T lymphocytes that kill infected cells. This activation requires recognition of antigen in the appropriate MHC context plus a key cytokine signal. Which combination is required?
keypoints: Signal 1 — antigen recognition: The naive CD8 TCR engages a viral peptide presented on Class I MHC of an infected cell or cross-presenting APC. This is the CD8-Class I restriction we built in Chapter 6.

Signal 2 — IL-2: Naive CD8 cells need interleukin-2 to proliferate. IL-2 typically comes from CD4+ helper T cells that were activated by the same pathogen on Class II MHC (or, in some cases, from the CD8 cell itself via autocrine signaling after robust activation).

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: Tumor surveillance
stations.subtitle: Why pieces of a melanoma can disappear on their own — and what that tells us about how CTLs kill.

flow.text: A 17-year-old with an enlarging pigmented lesion. Biopsy shows invasive melanoma with patches of regression.
flow.subtext: Regression = areas where tumor cells have disappeared. Something is actively eliminating them.

flow.text: The immune system is killing tumor cells. But how?
flow.subtext: The answer choices lay out five distinct immune mechanisms. The pattern — scattered, cell-by-cell elimination within host tissue — points to just one.
flow.insight: Need a mechanism that fits cell-by-cell attack

flow.text: Tumor cells carry neoantigens — peptides from mutated proteins.
flow.subtext: Melanoma accumulates mutations. The altered proteins produce peptide sequences the immune system has never seen before.

flow.text: Neoantigens are displayed on Class I MHC — the proteasome/TAP pathway.
flow.subtext: Every nucleated cell, tumor cells included, continually samples its own cytosolic proteins onto Class I. Mutated proteins get sampled too.
flow.insight: Class I surface display includes neoantigens

flow.text: A CD8 CTL with a matching TCR sees the neoantigen as non-self.
flow.subtext: Among millions of circulating CD8 clones, some have TCRs that fit the neoantigen-Class I combination. They recognize the tumor cell as a target.

flow.text: CTL and tumor cell form an immune synapse.
flow.subtext: TCR-MHC engagement, plus adhesion molecules (LFA-1/ICAM-1), creates a tight contact zone where the CTL can deliver its payload directly.

flow.text: CTL releases perforin — pores in the tumor membrane.
flow.subtext: Perforin monomers polymerize in the target membrane to form transmembrane pores, allowing granzymes to enter the tumor cell's cytosol.

flow.text: Through the pores come granzymes — proteases that trigger apoptosis.
flow.subtext: Granzyme B cleaves pro-caspase 3 and BID inside the target.

flow.text: Both hits converge on the intrinsic apoptotic cascade.
flow.subtext: The cell starts dying from within.
flow.insight: Both hits converge on the intrinsic apoptotic cascade

flow.text: Parallel route: Fas–FasL engagement triggers death receptor apoptosis.
flow.subtext: CTLs also carry FasL on their surface.

flow.text: When FasL binds Fas (CD95) on the target, it engages the extrinsic pathway via caspase 8 → caspase 3.
flow.subtext: Same apoptotic endpoint.
flow.insight: When FasL binds Fas (CD95) on the target

flow.text: Result: tumor cell apoptoses quietly. No inflammation. No collateral damage.
flow.subtext: Apoptotic cells bleb and fragment cleanly into membrane-bound bodies, which neighboring cells clear. That's why regression looks like missing cells rather than necrotic tissue.
flow.insight: Apoptosis = silent death · no bystander damage

flow.text: On biopsy: scattered tumor cells missing. That's the regression pattern.
flow.subtext: Pathologists see patchy loss of tumor with lymphocytic infiltrate — a fingerprint of ongoing T-cell-mediated killing.
flow.insight: Regression = histologic fingerprint of T-cell kill

flow.text: Answer: E · T-lymphocyte-mediated cytotoxicity.
flow.subtext: CD8 CTLs recognize neoantigens on Class I and kill tumor cells individually via perforin/granzymes and Fas-FasL — producing patchy regression on biopsy.

flow.text: The blueprint for modern cancer immunotherapy.
flow.insight: The blueprint for modern cancer immunotherapy

stations.bridge: A pigmented lesion biopsy shows invasive melanoma with zones of regression — areas where tumor cells have disappeared. Five hypersensitivity-style mechanisms are listed. Only one fits a scattered, cell-by-cell elimination of tumor cells embedded in host tissue.
stations.vignette:
A 17-year-old boy presents with a pigmented lesion on his upper back that has enlarged over several weeks. Biopsy demonstrates a superficially invasive melanoma with areas showing partial regression. The regression is believed to result from an immune response against tumor cells.

Which of the following best explains the mechanism responsible for this regression?
keypoints: Perforin — polymerizes in the target membrane to form pores, allowing granzyme entry.
Granzymes (A, B) — serine proteases that cleave intracellular substrates including caspases, triggering apoptosis from within.
Fas-FasL engagement — a parallel pathway where CTL surface FasL ligates Fas on the target, activating the extrinsic apoptotic cascade.

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: Atypical lymphocytes
stations.subtitle: How an unusual cell on a routine smear tells you CD8 killing is happening — and which virus started it.

flow.text: A 17-year-old with sore throat, fever, fatigue, posterior cervical LAD, and splenomegaly.
flow.subtext: That's five features — the classic mono pentad. Each one supports the others.

flow.text: CBC: leukocytosis. Smear: atypical lymphocytes — large, basophilic cytoplasm 'hugging' RBCs.
flow.subtext: These aren't blasts and aren't ordinary lymphs. They're reactive — activated, swollen, looking like they're doing something.
flow.insight: Atypical lymph = big, activated, cytoplasm molds around RBCs

flow.text: Critical distinction: atypical lymphocytes are NOT the infected cells.
flow.subtext: They're the IMMUNE RESPONSE to infected cells. That reframe is the whole key to the question.

flow.text: The virus: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV).
flow.subtext: EBV is the textbook cause of classic infectious mononucleosis. CMV can cause a similar mono-like syndrome but typically without pronounced pharyngitis.

flow.text: EBV target: B lymphocytes via the CD21 receptor (CR2).
flow.subtext: CD21 is a complement receptor on B cells. EBV exploits it as its entry portal — first in pharyngeal B cells, then circulating B cells across the body.
flow.insight: EBV enters B cells via CD21

flow.text: Infected B cells proliferate and present viral antigens on Class I.
flow.subtext: The infected B cells aren't silent. They display EBV peptides on their surface MHC Class I — marking themselves as targets for CD8 recognition.
flow.insight: EBV peptides on B-cell Class I attract CD8

flow.text: CD8 CTLs attack infected B cells — same machinery as Station 03.
flow.subtext: Perforin, granzymes, Fas-FasL. The CD8 response massively expands — into thousands of reactive T cells flooding the blood.

flow.text: Those expanded CD8 cells are what you see as atypical lymphocytes (Downey cells).
flow.subtext: Activated T cells are larger than resting T cells. Their abundant basophilic cytoplasm molds around neighboring RBCs on the glass slide — the signature 'hugging' look.

flow.text: Answer: C · Infectious mononucleosis.
flow.subtext: Confirm with heterophile (Monospot) antibody or EBV-specific serology. Counsel on avoiding contact sports (splenic rupture risk) and beware of amoxicillin rash.

stations.bridge: A teenager with fatigue, sore throat, posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly, and a CBC showing large atypical lymphocytes. Five leukemia/lymphoma entities and one viral syndrome on the differential. The morphology of one specific cell type closes the case.
stations.vignette:
A 17-year-old girl comes with 5 days of sore throat, fever, and marked fatigue. She has posterior cervical lymphadenopathy and mild splenomegaly. CBC shows leukocytosis. Peripheral smear shows large atypical lymphocytes with abundant basophilic cytoplasm that "hugs" nearby RBCs.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
keypoints: Infected cell: EBV binds CD21 (CR2) on B lymphocytes, first in pharyngeal tissue and then in circulating B cells. Infected B cells proliferate and drive lymphadenopathy and splenomegaly.

Reactive cell: the immune system mounts a massive CD8 T-cell response against the infected B cells. Those activated CD8 cells are what you see on the smear as atypical lymphocytes (Downey cells). They're enlarged, with abundant basophilic cytoplasm that molds around adjacent RBCs — the "hugging" morphology.

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: Th1 macrophage
stations.subtitle: Why an intracellular bacterium needs a T cell to clear it — not an antibody, not complement.

flow.text: Clinical: Legionella pneumonia. Industrial worker, aerosol exposure.
flow.subtext: Cooling towers, AC systems, and showers at large facilities are classic sources of aerosolized Legionella. The patient inhales it into the alveoli.

flow.text: Legionella's trick: lives inside macrophages, preventing phagolysosome fusion.
flow.subtext: The macrophage eats Legionella, but Legionella manipulates the phagosome so it never fuses with lysosomes. The bug survives and replicates inside.

flow.text: Why A · antibodies fail: antibodies are extracellular. They can't cross the host cell membrane.
flow.subtext: B cells and antibodies neutralize extracellular pathogens and toxins. A bug hiding inside a cell is out of reach.

flow.text: Why B · complement fails: MAC assembly happens on EXTRACELLULAR bacteria.
flow.subtext: The membrane attack complex lyses cells from outside. It can't form on an organism already inside a host cell.

flow.text: Why C · type II pneumocytes fail: they make surfactant, not phagocytose.
flow.subtext: Type II pneumocytes are surfactant-producing and progenitor cells for the alveolar epithelium. They're not dedicated phagocytes.

flow.text: Why D · mucociliary clearance fails: it handles the airways, not the alveoli.
flow.subtext: The mucociliary escalator sweeps particles out of the conducting airways. Once pathogens reach alveoli and enter cells, the escalator is out of play.

flow.text: Only way through: cell-mediated immunity — CD4 Th1 cells.
flow.subtext: To reach a pathogen inside a host cell, you have to work THROUGH that host cell. That's T-cell-mediated immunity, specifically the CD4 Th1 subset.
flow.insight: Th1 = the CD4 helper for intracellular bugs

flow.text: Infected macrophage displays Legionella peptides on Class II MHC.
flow.subtext: Endosomal processing (Ch 6 Station 04) — bacterial peptides from the phagosome end up on Class II at the macrophage surface.

flow.text: A CD4 Th1 cell with a matching TCR recognizes the Class II–peptide complex.
flow.subtext: CD4 is Class II-restricted (Chapter 6 opener). Th1 subset is what you get when naive CD4 is activated in the presence of IL-12 and IFN-γ.
flow.insight: Th1 TCR reads Class II + bacterial peptide

flow.text: Th1 releases interferon-γ (IFN-γ) back toward the macrophage.
flow.subtext: IFN-γ is the signature Th1 cytokine. It acts on the macrophage's IFN-γ receptor and triggers the activation program.

flow.text: IFN-γ activates the macrophage: phagolysosome fusion, ROS, NO.
flow.subtext: The activated macrophage now actually kills what it swallowed — phagolysosome fusion restored, reactive oxygen species and nitric oxide production ramped up.

flow.text: Legionella is killed inside its own hideout.
flow.subtext: The organism that survived by blocking phagolysosome fusion now can't block it anymore. The macrophage digests the bacteria.
flow.insight: Intracellular kill achieved via Th1 help

flow.text: This mechanism clears every intracellular bacterium: TB, Listeria, Salmonella typhi, Brucella.
flow.subtext: One pathway, many pathogens. Patients with impaired CD4, IL-12, or IFN-γ signaling get mycobacterial and Legionella infections — exactly the pathogens this pathway is built for.

flow.text: Answer: E · T-lymphocyte-mediated immunity.
flow.subtext: CD4 Th1 cells, operating through IFN-γ on macrophages, are the only effective response to an intracellular bacterium. The remaining choices address the wrong compartment.

stations.bridge: A patient gets pneumonia from an organism that lives INSIDE macrophages — the same cells that are supposed to eat it. Five immune mechanisms on the menu. Only one actually reaches something hiding inside a host cell.
