stations.vignette: A 40-year-old man presents with high fever, nonproductive cough, and shortness of breath for 4 days. He works at a large industrial facility. On examination, crackles are heard bilaterally, and chest imaging is consistent with pneumonia. Sputum cultures later grow Legionella pneumophila. After this organism gains access to the alveoli, which of the following immune mechanisms is most important for eliminating it?
keypoints: Antibodies (B cells) can't cross the host cell membrane to reach the organism inside.
Complement operates in the extracellular space; membrane attack complexes can't assemble on intracellular bacteria.
Mucociliary clearance handles the conducting airways. Once bacteria reach the alveoli and enter cells, the escalator is irrelevant.
Type II pneumocytes produce surfactant and repair alveoli. They're not a principal antimicrobial cell.

stations.label: Station 06
stations.title: Granuloma formation
stations.subtitle: What chronic macrophage activation looks like on tissue — and the one cytokine driving the transformation.

flow.text: Clinical: chronic cough, weight loss, night sweats — classic B-symptoms.
flow.subtext: B-symptom pattern + hilar lymphadenopathy + granulomas on biopsy: top differential is TB, atypical mycobacteria, fungal disease, sarcoidosis.

flow.text: Biopsy: epithelioid histiocytes + multinucleated giant cells.
flow.subtext: Granuloma hallmarks.

flow.text: Epithelioid = enlarged macrophages with pink cytoplasm looking like squamous cells.
flow.subtext: Giant cells = fused multinucleate macrophages.
flow.insight: Epithelioid = enlarged macrophages with pink cytoplasm...

flow.text: The stem tells us the mechanism: "Th1 activation of macrophages."
flow.subtext: Players identified. The question is just which cytokine-cell pair actually drives the morphologic change.
flow.insight: Mechanism given · pick the specific pair

flow.text: Recall Station 05: Th1 → IFN-γ → macrophage.
flow.subtext: Same axis we built for Legionella clearance. IFN-γ is the signature Th1 cytokine; macrophages are its target.

flow.text: Persistent infection = weeks to months of sustained IFN-γ.
flow.subtext: TB, fungi, atypicals don't clear quickly. Th1 keeps releasing IFN-γ, and macrophages keep responding.

flow.text: Macrophages transform into epithelioid histiocytes.
flow.subtext: Enlarged, abundant pink (eosinophilic) cytoplasm, elongated vesicular nuclei. They look like squamous epithelial cells — hence 'epithelioid.'

flow.text: Epithelioid cells fuse into Langhans giant cells.
flow.subtext: Cytokine-driven cell-cell fusion yields multinucleated giant cells.

flow.text: In TB, nuclei arrange in a peripheral horseshoe.
flow.subtext: Foreign-body giant cells have scattered nuclei.

flow.text: Together = granuloma architecture.
flow.subtext: Core of epithelioid histiocytes + giant cells, lymphocyte cuff, sometimes central caseation (TB). The whole structure is what's on the biopsy.

flow.text: Answer: B · IFN-γ on macrophages.
flow.subtext: Same axis you already know. Sustained IFN-γ reshapes macrophages into epithelioid histiocytes and Langhans giant cells — the histologic signature of granulomas.

stations.bridge: Chronic cough, weight loss, night sweats. Hilar node biopsy: granulomas with epithelioid histiocytes and multinucleated giant cells. The stem confirms Th1 activation of macrophages is the mechanism. Which cytokine-cell pairing actually produces this histology?
stations.vignette:
A 49-year-old man has several months of cough, weight loss, and night sweats. A biopsy of an enlarged hilar lymph node shows granulomatous inflammation with many epithelioid histiocytes and multinucleated giant cells. These findings reflect activation of macrophages by Th1 cells.

The abnormal appearance of these cells is most likely the result of the action of which of the following?
keypoints: Epithelioid histiocytes — enlarged macrophages with abundant pink cytoplasm and elongated, vesicular nuclei that look like squamous epithelial cells (hence "epithelioid").

Multinucleated giant cells — several epithelioid macrophages fuse into one cell with many nuclei. In TB, nuclei arrange peripherally in a horseshoe pattern (Langhans giant cells). Foreign-body giant cells (nuclei scattered) are the non-TB variant.

stations.label: Station 07
stations.title: Superantigen
stations.subtitle: How a bacterial toxin short-circuits normal T-cell activation by clamping two molecules together.

flow.text: Clinical: teen, prolonged tampon use, fever, hypotension, rash, vomiting. Staph aureus.
flow.subtext: Classic menstrual toxic shock syndrome. The toxin is TSST-1 (toxic shock syndrome toxin 1), a Staph superantigen.

flow.text: The toxin causes massive, non-specific T-cell activation.
flow.subtext: This is the key phrase. 'Non-specific' means it doesn't care what peptide the T cell recognizes — it just turns on a huge fraction of T cells at once.

flow.text: Normal T-cell activation is the opposite: specific. TCR sees peptide in the MHC groove.
flow.subtext: For any given antigen, only ~1 in 100,000 T cells has a matching TCR. Most of the T-cell pool stays quiet.

flow.text: The toxin binds two molecules simultaneously — the clue in the stem.
flow.subtext: One on the APC, one on the T cell. That physical bridge delivers the activation signal regardless of peptide specificity.
flow.insight: Toxin = molecular bridge APC ↔ T cell

flow.text: On the APC side: Class II MHC (not Class I).
flow.subtext: Superantigens bind the α chain of Class II. Class II is on APCs (macrophages, dendritic cells, B cells) and engages CD4 T cells.

flow.text: On the T-cell side: TCR — specifically the Vβ region, outside the peptide groove.
flow.subtext: Superantigens bind conserved surfaces of particular TCR Vβ families. Any T cell with the matching Vβ gets clamped — not just antigen-specific ones.
flow.insight: T-cell side = TCR Vβ (outside the groove)

flow.text: Superantigen bridges Class II and TCR Vβ, peptide-independent.
flow.subtext: The toxin sits between the two molecules, glued to the outside of each. It doesn't matter what peptide is in the groove — the signal fires anyway.
flow.insight: Bridge outside the groove · peptide doesn't matter

flow.text: Result: up to ~25% of all T cells activate simultaneously — polyclonal.
flow.subtext: All T cells sharing the targeted Vβ family respond.

flow.text: That's tens of thousands of clones, not one.
flow.subtext: Massive cytokine release follows.
flow.insight: That's tens of thousands of clones

flow.text: Cytokine storm: IL-2, IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-1 flood the circulation.
flow.subtext: TNF-α and IL-1 on the vasculature produce distributive shock.

flow.text: IFN-γ and IL-2 drive systemic inflammation.
flow.subtext: That's the TSS clinical picture.
flow.insight: IFN-γ and IL-2 drive systemic inflammation

flow.text: Answer: C · Class II MHC + T-cell receptor.
flow.subtext: Superantigen = molecular clamp between Class II on the APC and TCR Vβ on the T cell, bypassing peptide specificity. Polyclonal activation → cytokine storm → toxic shock.

stations.bridge: Staph TSST-1 activates T cells by binding two molecules simultaneously — one on the APC, one on the T cell. Normal antigen recognition binds both, but through the peptide groove. Superantigens bind OUTSIDE the groove. Which two molecules are bridged?
stations.vignette:
A 17-year-old girl is hospitalized with high fever, hypotension, diffuse erythematous rash, and vomiting after prolonged tampon use. Blood cultures grow a toxin-producing strain of Staphylococcus aureus. The responsible exotoxin causes massive, nonspecific activation of T lymphocytes by simultaneously binding two immune system molecules.

Which of the following pairs of molecules must interact to induce immune cell activation by this toxin?
keypoints: One end binds the α chain of Class II MHC on an APC — peptide-independent. It doesn't care which peptide is in the groove.
The other end binds the Vβ region of the TCR on a T cell — outside the antigen-binding site. It binds any TCR that uses a particular Vβ family (typically 2–4 Vβ families).

stations.label: Station 08
stations.title: Conjugate vaccine
stations.subtitle: Why a polysaccharide-only vaccine fails in infants, and one modification fixes it.

flow.text: Clinical: Hib polysaccharide vaccine. Poor response in children under 2.
flow.subtext: The original Hib vaccine used just the capsular polysaccharide.
flow.insight: Old Hib vaccine · weak in infants

flow.text: It worked in older children and adults but failed in the age group that needed it most — infants with bacterial meningitis risk.
flow.insight: It worked in older children and adults but failed in the...

flow.text: Polysaccharide alone is a T-independent antigen.
flow.subtext: Repeating polysaccharide epitopes crosslink BCRs directly.

flow.text: B cells make IgM.
That's all.
flow.subtext: No class switch, no memory.

flow.text: Young children (<2y) have especially weak T-independent responses.
flow.subtext: Marginal-zone B cells and T-independent pathways mature slowly. Infants simply can't mount a T-independent response strong enough for protection.

flow.text: Fix: link polysaccharide to a protein carrier.
flow.subtext: Carrier proteins are diphtheria toxoid CRM-197, tetanus toxoid, or meningococcal OMP — proteins infants already have T-cell repertoires against, or can build them for.

flow.text: B cell's BCR still binds the polysaccharide epitope.
flow.subtext: Specificity stays with the polysaccharide (the actual protective antigen). BCR uptake of the entire conjugate follows.
flow.insight: BCR reads polysaccharide · takes in whole conjugate

flow.text: B cell processes the protein, displays peptides on Class II MHC.
flow.subtext: Internalized protein goes through endosomal processing (Chapter 6 Station 04). Protein-derived peptides end up on Class II on the B cell surface.
flow.insight: Class II presents carrier protein peptides

flow.text: CD4 T cell specific for the protein recognizes the peptide on B-cell Class II.
flow.subtext: CD4 TCR matches carrier-protein peptide.
flow.insight: CD4 recognizes protein peptide on B-cell Class II

flow.text: Connection made.
flow.subtext: This is where the T-cell help begins.

flow.text: CD4 delivers help: CD40L↔CD40 engagement + cytokines (IL-4, IL-21).
flow.subtext: CD40L on the CD4 T cell binds CD40 on the B cell.

flow.text: Cytokines add the specific class-switching directions (IL-4 → IgG1/IgE
flow.insight: Cytokines add the specific class-switching directions (IL-4...

flow.text: TGF-β → IgA
IFN-γ → IgG2/3).
B cell now undergoes class switching, affinity maturation, memory formation.
flow.subtext: IgM → IgG (the protective isotype for encapsulated bacteria).
flow.insight: Class switch · affinity mat · memory = real immunity

flow.text: Somatic hypermutation produces higher-affinity BCRs.
flow.subtext: Memory B cells persist for decades.

flow.text: Result in infants: robust IgG response with durable memory.
flow.subtext: Infants now mount the same kind of antibody response adults do — Hib meningitis and pneumococcal disease have been nearly eliminated in immunized populations.

flow.text: Answer: A · CD4+ T lymphocytes are activated.
flow.subtext: Protein conjugation converts polysaccharide from T-independent to T-dependent. CD4 help unlocks class switch, affinity maturation, and memory — making the vaccine work in infants.
flow.insight: Answer: A · CD4 activation = the key change

stations.bridge: Polysaccharide vaccines alone produce weak, short-lived IgM in children under 2. Linking the polysaccharide to a carrier protein converts the response into a robust IgG + memory response. Two questions: which modification is this (Q3)? And why does it work (Q12)?
stations.vignette:
A pediatric clinic previously used a vaccine composed only of capsular polysaccharides from Haemophilus influenzae type b. It produced weak protection in children younger than 2 years. A newer formulation links the polysaccharide to a carrier protein and produces much stronger immunity in infants.

Why does this conjugation improve immunogenicity?

choices.text: Cytotoxic T lymphocyte responses are increased
Direct activation of B lymphocytes by the vaccine is increased
Nonimmune clearance of the vaccine is decreased
Phagocytosis of the vaccine is increased

keypoints: No class switching to IgG/IgA — only short-lived IgM.
No affinity maturation — antibodies stay low-affinity.
No memory B cells — next exposure starts over from scratch.
Response is especially weak in children under 2 years, whose marginal-zone B cells and T-independent responses are still immature.

stations.label: Station 09
stations.title: Mixed chimerism
stations.subtitle: How giving donor bone marrow with a kidney transplant teaches the recipient's T cells not to attack it.

flow.text: Kidney transplant + donor hematopoietic stem cells. Minimal immunosuppression. No rejection.
flow.subtext: Every other station in this chapter would predict rejection by now.
flow.insight: Unusual setup · kidney + donor HSCs · no rejection

flow.text: Recipient T cells should attack donor HLA.
flow.insight: Recipient T cells should attack donor HLA

flow.text: They're not.
flow.subtext: Why?

flow.text: Normal rejection biology (Ch 6 Station 09, MLR): recipient T cells attack donor HLA.
flow.subtext: Foreign HLA on a graft engages recipient T cell TCRs and triggers cytotoxic/helper responses. That's why every routine transplant needs lifelong immunosuppression.
flow.insight: Usually: recipient T cells see donor HLA → rejection

flow.text: The trick here: donor HSCs go into the recipient's bone marrow.
flow.subtext: Those donor HSCs start producing donor-origin hematopoietic cells inside the recipient's own marrow — alongside recipient hematopoiesis.

flow.text: That's mixed chimerism: two sets of hematopoietic cells in one body.
flow.subtext: Both recipient and donor hematopoietic lineages produce their own lymphocytes and dendritic cells in parallel.

flow.text: Donor APCs traffic to the recipient's thymus along with recipient APCs.
flow.subtext: Dendritic cells and macrophages from donor lineage migrate into the thymus — the educational headquarters we built in Station 01.

flow.text: Recall Station 01: developing T cells learn self vs non-self in the thymus.
flow.subtext: Thymic selection — double-positive cortical thymocytes encounter self-MHC on cortical and medullary APCs.

flow.text: T cells with TCRs binding self-MHC too strongly are deleted (negative selection).
flow.insight: T cells with TCRs binding self-MHC too strongly are deleted...

flow.text: In this recipient, 'self' now includes donor HLA.
flow.subtext: Because donor APCs are present in the thymus during selection, donor HLA behaves exactly like recipient HLA to the developing T cells.
flow.insight: Self set expands to include donor HLA

flow.text: T cells with TCRs reactive to donor HLA are deleted in the thymus.
flow.subtext: Anti-donor clones never make it to the periphery. The mature T-cell repertoire of the recipient is born tolerant to donor tissue.
flow.insight: Anti-donor T-cell clones deleted during development

flow.text: Result: central tolerance extends to donor tissue.
flow.subtext: The graft kidney, expressing donor HLA, looks like 'self' to the recipient's T cells.
flow.insight: Graft = self · tolerated, not just suppressed

flow.text: No attack.
flow.subtext: This is true biological tolerance, not pharmacologic suppression.

flow.text: Peripheral tolerance reinforces central: Tregs, anergy, exhaustion.
flow.subtext: For any anti-donor clones that slip past thymic selection, regulatory T cells and peripheral anergy mechanisms suppress them in the periphery.

flow.text: Minimal immunosuppression suffices because the tolerance is genuine.
flow.subtext: A conventional transplant needs heavy immunosuppression because anti-donor clones are circulating. Here, those clones don't exist.
flow.insight: No anti-donor clones = no need to suppress

flow.text: Answer: E · Tolerance.
flow.subtext: Donor HSCs rewire thymic education so donor HLA is learned as self.

flow.text: Anti-donor T-cell clones never develop.
flow.subtext: The graft is biologically ignored.

flow.text: Chapter 7 complete. T-cell biology fully mapped.
flow.subtext: Development → CD8 effector → Th1 help → superantigen edge case → CD4→B help → tolerance capstone.

flow.text: Next chapter: B-cell biology, where Station 08's help gets translated into antibodies.

stations.bridge: Kidney transplant plus donor hematopoietic stem cells. Minimal immunosuppression. No rejection. Everything we've learned about CD4/CD8 biology predicts rejection — so what immune phenomenon is overriding that prediction?
stations.vignette:
A 46-year-old woman with end-stage kidney disease undergoes renal transplantation along with infusion of donor-derived hematopoietic stem cells. Despite receiving minimal immunosuppressive therapy, the transplanted kidney continues to function normally with no evidence of rejection.

The donor bone marrow is most likely inducing which of the following immune phenomena in the recipient?
keypoints: Remember what we built in Chapter 6 Station 09 (the MLR capstone): recipient T cells attack donor HLA because they see it as non-self. That's the biology of acute cellular rejection, and it's why transplant patients need lifelong immunosuppression.
This case is different. Donor hematopoietic stem cells were infused along with the kidney. Here's what that accomplishes:
The kidney graft, expressing donor HLA, now looks like self to the recipient's immune system. No rejection. Minimal immunosuppression needed because the tolerance is genuine, not pharmacologic.

Clinical translation: mixed chimerism protocols are one of the most promising strategies in modern transplantation. Combining partial bone marrow transplantation with solid organ transplantation from the same donor can produce operational tolerance — patients weaned off immunosuppression who continue to accept their graft indefinitely. This has been achieved in selected HLA-matched kidney transplants and is being extended to other organs. The biology is exactly what this station describes.

chapters.title: B-Cell Biology & Antibodies
chapters.subtitle: From naïve B cell to plasma cell — class-switching, affinity maturation, and the antibodies they produce.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: V(D)J recombination
stations.subtitle: How a finite genome generates ~10¹¹ unique antibodies — by cutting and pasting DNA in early B-cell development.

flow.text: A healthy 7-year-old responds to vaccines with antibodies against any antigen.
flow.subtext: Including antigens his immune system has never seen — completely novel synthetic peptides included. His repertoire is essentially universal.

flow.text: Math problem: only ~20,000 genes in the human genome.
flow.subtext: If the body needed one gene per antibody, that's 100 billion genes — five orders of magnitude more than we have. The math doesn't work.

flow.text: Solution: rearrange DNA itself during B-cell development.
flow.subtext: Instead of one gene per antibody, the body keeps a library of gene segments and combines them randomly to build each B cell's unique receptor.

flow.text: The germline Ig heavy-chain locus has V, D, J segments + constant regions.
flow.subtext: ~40 V (Variable), ~25 D (Diversity), ~6 J (Joining) for heavy chain. Light chains have V and J only (no D), with κ or λ loci.
flow.insight: V/D/J segment library in germline DNA

flow.text: RAG1/RAG2 enzymes recognize signal sequences flanking each segment.
flow.subtext: Recombination signal sequences (RSSs) sit on either side of every V, D, and J. RAG complex binds to these and cuts the DNA precisely.

flow.text: The intervening DNA loops out and is excised. Chosen segments are rejoined.
flow.subtext: Each developing B cell randomly picks one V, one D, one J for heavy chain — and one V, one J for light chain. Everything between is deleted.
flow.insight: Random V-D-J selection · everything else deleted

flow.text: TdT adds random N-nucleotides at each junction.
flow.subtext: Terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase — the same enzyme from Ch 7 Station 01.

flow.text: TdT adds 1–10 extra bases at each cut site.
flow.subtext: These are random, untemplated additions.

flow.text: This generates junctional diversity on top of combinatorial diversity.
Combinatorial × junctional diversity = ~10¹¹ possible BCRs.
flow.subtext: ~6,000 heavy-chain combinations × ~200 light-chain combinations × junctional diversity from TdT = orders of magnitude more receptors than antigens we'll ever encounter.
flow.insight: Multiplied diversity covers any antigen we'll meet

flow.text: Answer: C · Recombination.
flow.subtext: V(D)J recombination via RAG1/RAG2 + TdT junctional diversity is the only mechanism that can build ~10¹¹ unique antibody specificities from a finite genome.

flow.text: Same mechanism builds TCR diversity in T cells.
flow.insight: Same mechanism builds TCR diversity in T cells

stations.bridge: A booster vaccine can elicit antibodies against any peptide the body has never seen. The germline genome doesn't have one gene per antibody — it can't. So which DNA mechanism actually generates this diversity?
stations.vignette:
A 7-year-old boy has never had a serious infection and is up to date on all vaccines. After receiving a booster shot, his immune system can produce antibodies that recognize a huge variety of previously unseen antigen shapes.

The ability to generate this enormous diversity of antigen-binding sites occurs during early B-cell development and relies primarily on which of the following DNA mechanisms?
keypoints: ~40 V (Variable) gene segments
~25 D (Diversity) gene segments
~6 J (Joining) gene segments
One set of C (Constant) regions (μ, δ, γ, ε, α)

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: B vs T cells
stations.subtitle: Why a J-region probe finds a small fragment in B cells but a large one in T cells.

flow.text: Southern blot · probe binds the Ig heavy-chain J region.
flow.subtext: Probe will hybridize to whatever DNA fragment contains the J segments — independent of cell type. Then we measure fragment size on the gel.
flow.insight: J-region probe finds J wherever it sits

flow.text: B-cell DNA: 1.5-kb fragment. T-cell DNA: 6-kb fragment.
flow.subtext: Two different sizes from the same probe. Something about the underlying DNA must be different between these cells.
flow.insight: B small band, T big band

flow.text: Recall Station 01: V(D)J recombination physically deletes intervening DNA.
flow.subtext: When V, D, and J are joined together, all the segments between them — and most of the DNA between V/D and D/J — are looped out and excised.

flow.text: B cell's Ig locus is shorter than germline → smaller band.
flow.subtext: Less DNA between restriction enzyme cut sites → smaller fragment → faster gel migration → smaller apparent band size.
flow.insight: Shorter locus = smaller band on gel

flow.text: T cells don't rearrange Ig. They rearrange TCR instead.
flow.subtext: Same RAG1/RAG2 enzymes, different loci. T cells leave their Ig locus alone in the germline configuration — including all the V, D, J segments and intervening DNA.
flow.insight: T cells: TCR rearranged, Ig untouched

flow.text: T cell's Ig locus is in germline configuration → larger band.
flow.subtext: All segments still separated by intact intervening DNA. Restriction enzymes cut at the same flanking sites, but now the fragment between them is the full germline length.

flow.text: Answer: E · Unrearranged immunoglobulin gene.
flow.subtext: T cells leave Ig in germline.

flow.text: The 6-kb band is the full unrearranged distance between restriction sites flanking the J region.
flow.subtext: Same logic underlies clonality testing in lymphoma diagnosis.
flow.insight: The 6-kb band is the full unrearranged distance between...

stations.bridge: A J-region probe is hybridized to digested DNA from B cells and T cells. B cells show a smaller band (rearranged); T cells show a larger band. What does the larger band represent?
stations.vignette:
A 68-year-old man is enrolled in a research study in which peripheral blood B cells and T cells are separated for analysis. A Southern blot is performed using a probe that binds the immunoglobulin heavy-chain J (joining) region. In the B-cell DNA sample, the probe detects a 1.5-kb fragment. In the T-cell DNA sample, the same probe detects a 6-kb fragment.

The 6-kb band most likely represents which of the following?
keypoints: B cell (1.5 kb band): V(D)J recombination has DELETED the intervening DNA between the chosen V, D, and J segments. The locus is now much shorter — small band on the gel.
T cell (6 kb band): Ig locus is in germline configuration — V, D, J segments still separated by their full intervening DNA. Larger band on the gel.

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: T-independent antigens
stations.subtitle: How an antibody response that lacks class switch and memory tells you the antigen never recruited a T cell.

flow.text: Vaccine response: IgM early, modest IgG later, no exaggerated secondary response.
flow.subtext: The key feature is the second-dose curve.

flow.text: If a vaccine produces real memory, the second dose triggers a fast, large IgG burst (anamnestic).
flow.subtext: This vaccine doesn't.
flow.insight: If a vaccine produces real memory

flow.text: No memory means no T-cell help. Memory requires germinal centers. Germinal centers require T-cell help.
flow.subtext: T-cell help drives class switching, affinity maturation, and memory B-cell formation. Without it, you're stuck with IgM-dominant primary-style responses every time.
flow.insight: No memory ↔ no T-cell help ↔ T-independent antigen

flow.text: T-independent antigens have one defining feature: repetitive structure.
flow.subtext: Bacterial polysaccharides have many identical sugar epitopes in a row.

flow.text: They crosslink dozens of BCRs on one B cell at once — enough to trigger IgM secretion without T-cell co-stimulation.
flow.insight: They crosslink dozens of BCRs on one B cell at once —...

flow.text: Result: B cell makes IgM, with limited class switch and no memory.
flow.subtext: Some isotype switching can happen with T-independent antigens in mature individuals (especially via marginal-zone B cells), but the response is shallow, low-affinity, and amnesic.

flow.text: Bacterial polysaccharide matches the description — answer A.
flow.subtext: Capsular polysaccharide vaccines like the original Hib (HbPS) and PPSV23 generate exactly this profile. That's why these vaccines fail in infants and why conjugate versions exist.

flow.text: Answer: A · Bacterial polysaccharide.
flow.subtext: T-independent antigen → IgM, weak class switch, no memory → no anamnestic response on second exposure. Conjugating to a carrier protein (Ch 7 Station 08) fixes this.

stations.bridge: A 2-year-old gets a vaccine. The serology curve shows IgM rising early, modest IgG later, and nothing dramatic on rechallenge. Which antigen produces this exact pattern?
stations.vignette:
A 2-year-old child receives a routine vaccination. Over the next several weeks, laboratory testing shows an early rise in IgM levels followed by a later increase in IgG, with no exaggerated secondary response.

Which of the following types of antigen is most likely responsible for this antibody pattern?
keypoints: Don't engage CD4 T cells (no peptide-MHC presentation involved).
Produce limited or no isotype switching (some IgG can occur in mature individuals but is weak).
Generate no affinity maturation (no germinal center).
Generate no memory B cells.
Are poorly immunogenic in children < 2 years (whose marginal-zone B cells are still maturing).

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: IgG placental transfer
stations.subtitle: Why one antibody isotype protects newborns for 6 months — and creates diagnostic puzzles in the meantime.

flow.text: Pregnancy + booster vaccine. Cord blood has maternal antibodies.
flow.subtext: Pre-delivery vaccination is widely used (Tdap, flu, RSV) precisely because of this transfer mechanism.

flow.text: Five isotypes to choose from: IgG, IgA, IgM, IgD, IgE.
flow.subtext: Each isotype has distinct structure, distribution, and effector functions. The placental-crossing question depends on size, structure, and which receptors transport it.
flow.insight: Five isotypes · only one crosses placenta

flow.text: IgG is a monomer — single 4-chain Y-shaped unit, ~150 kDa.
flow.subtext: Small enough to be transported. Two Fab arms bind antigen; one Fc tail engages effector receptors and complement.

flow.text: Placenta has FcRn (neonatal Fc receptor) on syncytiotrophoblasts.
flow.subtext: FcRn binds the IgG Fc tail specifically. It's an active transcytosis mechanism — not passive diffusion.

flow.text: Maternal IgG → bound by FcRn → transcytosed across → released into fetal blood.
flow.subtext: By term, fetal IgG levels can equal or exceed maternal levels. This protective IgG persists for ~6 months postpartum, decaying as the infant builds their own immunity.

flow.text: Answer: D · IgG.
flow.subtext: Same answer pattern covers many high-yield scenarios.

flow.text: Maternal vaccine transfer to fetus = IgG (Q5, Q62).
flow.subtext: Crosses placenta via FcRn.

flow.text: Neonatal myasthenia gravis = anti-AChR IgG transfer (Q66).
Therapeutic monoclonal antibody half-life = IgG (Q103).
Newborn HIV testing source = maternal IgG crossing placenta (Q97).

stations.bridge: A pregnant mother's vaccine antibodies reach her fetus. Of the five antibody isotypes, only one regularly crosses the placenta. Which?
stations.vignette:
A 28-year-old pregnant woman at 34 weeks gestation received a booster vaccine earlier in pregnancy. At delivery, her newborn's umbilical cord blood is tested and shows protective maternal antibodies that reached the fetal circulation before birth.

Which immunoglobulin class is able to cross the placenta to provide this passive immunity?
keypoints: IgG is the workhorse antibody. It's the most abundant serum immunoglobulin (~75% of total Ig), the longest-lived (~21-day half-life), and the only isotype that crosses the placenta in clinically meaningful amounts. Five questions in the bank test variations of this same biology — they all converge on IgG.

Structure. IgG is a monomer — a single Y-shaped 4-chain unit (2 heavy + 2 light, ~150 kDa). The two Fab arms bind antigen; the Fc tail engages effector functions (FcγR on phagocytes, FcRn on placenta, complement C1q).

Placental transfer mechanism. The neonatal Fc receptor (FcRn) on placental syncytiotrophoblasts binds the IgG Fc tail at the maternal surface, transcytoses the IgG across the cell, and releases it into fetal circulation. This is an active, FcRn-mediated transport — not simple diffusion. Maternal IgG levels in cord blood at term often equal or exceed maternal serum levels.

IgG subclasses: IgG1, IgG2, IgG3, IgG4 — different half-lives, FcγR affinities, and complement-fixing abilities. IgG1 dominates anti-protein responses; IgG2 dominates anti-polysaccharide responses; IgG4 is associated with chronic exposure (and IgG4-related disease).

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: IgM cold agglutinin
stations.subtitle: Why one antibody isotype can clump red blood cells in a test tube on the counter.

flow.text: Clinical: dry cough + patchy infiltrates = Mycoplasma pneumonia.
flow.subtext: Walking pneumonia in young adults.

flow.text: Often disproportionate imaging findings vs exam.
flow.subtext: Mycoplasma is associated with cold agglutinin disease.
flow.insight: Often disproportionate imaging findings vs exam

flow.text: Cold agglutinins = anti-I antibodies that bind RBCs at low temperature.
flow.subtext: Bind at cooler temps (extremities, lab bench), crosslink RBCs, cause clumping and mild hemolytic anemia.

flow.text: Cold agglutinins are IgM antibodies. Why?
flow.subtext: Agglutination depends on having many binding sites that can crosslink multiple cells. IgM has more binding sites than any other isotype.
flow.insight: IgM = many binding sites = best agglutinator

flow.text: IgM is a pentamer — 5 monomers joined by J chain · 10 Fab arms · ~970 kDa.
flow.subtext: Each monomer has 2 Fab arms.

flow.text: Five monomers around a central J chain.
flow.subtext: Total: 10 antigen-binding sites for agglutinating cells.
flow.insight: Five monomers around a central J chain

flow.text: 10 binding sites can crosslink multiple RBCs simultaneously.
flow.subtext: Each Fab grabs an I antigen on a different RBC.

flow.text: The pentamer becomes a molecular bridge between cells.
flow.subtext: Multiple bridges = visible clumping.
flow.insight: The pentamer becomes a molecular bridge between cells

flow.text: Answer: E · IgM.
flow.subtext: Pentameric IgM with 10 binding sites is the dominant agglutinator. Mycoplasma → anti-I cold IgM → tube clumping + cold-induced acrocyanosis.

stations.bridge: A young adult with walking pneumonia, cold-induced finger pain, and a blood specimen that visibly agglutinates at room temperature. Five isotypes — only one has the structure for this.
stations.vignette:
A 22-year-old woman comes to the clinic with 4 days of low-grade fever, dry cough, and malaise. She also reports feeling unusually tired and notices her fingers become painful and bluish when she walks outside in cold weather. Physical examination shows mild conjunctival pallor. Chest auscultation is unremarkable. Chest x-ray demonstrates diffuse, patchy interstitial infiltrates. While her blood sample is sitting on the counter before being sent to the laboratory, the tube develops visible clumping.

Which of the following antibody isotypes is most likely responsible for this agglutination?
keypoints: Pentameric structure — 5 four-chain units joined by a J chain (~970 kDa).
10 antigen-binding sites per molecule (vs IgG's 2). Multiple Fabs simultaneously bind antigens on different cells, physically crosslinking them.
Excellent complement activation — IgM binds C1q efficiently (a single IgM molecule can activate complement; IgG needs adjacent pairs).

stations.label: Station 06
stations.title: IgA mucosal
stations.subtitle: How a vaccine given in the nose protects the vagina — and how IgA gets across epithelium in the first place.

flow.text: Intranasal vaccine. Antibodies appear in vaginal mucosa.
flow.subtext: Two completely different mucosal sites. Yet the same antigen-specific antibody shows up at the second site weeks later.
flow.insight: Distant mucosal protection from local vaccination

flow.text: Mucosal surfaces are part of one shared immune compartment — the common mucosal immune system.
flow.subtext: Gut, respiratory, urogenital, mammary, salivary, conjunctival mucosae are all linked by a common pool of recirculating IgA-producing lymphocytes.

flow.text: Antigen primes B cells in nasal MALT → those B cells class-switch to IgA.
flow.subtext: MALT (mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue) at the priming site activates B cells that commit to producing IgA — the dominant mucosal antibody.

flow.text: IgA-committed B cells express α4β7 + CCR9/CCR10 — mucosal homing receptors.
flow.subtext: These molecules are the address labels. They direct migrating lymphocytes to mucosal venules, not lymph node ones.

flow.text: B cells enter the circulation and exit via venules at any mucosal site.
flow.subtext: MAdCAM-1 is expressed on gut venules; CCL28 attracts cells to respiratory and urogenital mucosae. The same B cells can land at any of them.

flow.text: Once seeded, plasma cells secrete dimeric IgA locally.
flow.subtext: IgA is two monomers joined by a J chain, secreted into the lamina propria of the new mucosal site. Awaiting transport across the epithelium.
flow.insight: Local plasma cells make dimeric IgA

flow.text: Epithelium transports IgA across via pIgR (transcytosis), releases as secretory IgA.
flow.subtext: Polymeric Ig receptor on basolateral surface binds IgA dimer, transcytoses to apical (luminal) side, then is cleaved — leaving secretory component attached for protease protection.
flow.insight: pIgR transcytosis · secretory component protects IgA

flow.text: Answer: C · IgA-producing lymphocytes seed all mucosal sites.
flow.subtext: The common mucosal immune system.
flow.insight: Answer: C · Common mucosal immune system

flow.text: Same biology underlies multiple mucosal IgA phenomena.
flow.subtext: Mother's gut → milk → infant gut.

flow.text: Breast milk transfers IgA against pathogens the mother encounters.
flow.subtext: Maternal gut plasma cells home to lactating breast.
flow.insight: Breast milk IgA = mom's gut history

flow.text: Oral polio vaccine generates protective gut IgA.
flow.subtext: Live attenuated vaccine via the natural infection route.
flow.insight: OPV = gut IgA via oral route

flow.text: Intranasal vaccines can induce vaginal IgA.
flow.subtext: Common mucosal immune system links distant mucosae.

stations.bridge: Intranasal vaccine produces antibodies at a distant mucosal site. Choose the mechanism that explains how the immune response gets there.
stations.vignette:
A researcher is testing a live-attenuated vaccine targeting Neisseria gonorrhoeae. The vaccine is administered intranasally to female laboratory animals. Weeks later, analysis of vaginal secretions demonstrates markedly increased levels of antibodies specific to gonococcal surface antigens.

Which of the following mechanisms best explains the presence of these antibodies in the genital mucosa?

choices.text: Synthesis of IgG by circulating lymphocytes exposed to antigens in the lung
Trafficking of IgA-producing lymphocytes to seed all mucosal sites
Transport of attenuated Neisseria gonorrhoeae from the lung to the vaginal mucosa
Transport of circulating IgM across the vaginal epithelium

keypoints: Mucosal immunity is organized differently from systemic immunity. The body treats all mucosal surfaces — gut, respiratory tract, urogenital tract, mammary gland, salivary glands, conjunctiva — as one shared compartment. This is the common mucosal immune system.
When antigen reaches one mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT) site (Peyer's patches in the gut, NALT in the nose, BALT in the bronchi):

Local secretion of IgA involves a second clever step. Plasma cells in the lamina propria secrete IgA dimer (two monomers joined by a J chain). The basolateral surface of overlying epithelial cells expresses the polymeric Ig receptor (pIgR), which binds the dimer, internalizes it, and transcytoses it to the apical (luminal) surface. There the receptor is cleaved, releasing the IgA into the lumen with a piece of pIgR still attached — the secretory component (SC). The SC protects IgA from proteases in the harsh mucosal environment.

Companion biology — Q1 from Module 2: the same pIgR-mediated transcytosis that secretes IgA into the gut lumen also secretes maternal IgA into breast milk via mammary epithelium. That's why breastfed infants get protective IgA covering their gut — same mechanism as adult mucosal IgA, just in the mammary gland.

stations.label: Station 07
stations.title: Class switching
stations.subtitle: The handshake that lets a B cell stop making IgM and start making something useful.

flow.text: Infant gets a protein vaccine. Response: IgM first → high-affinity IgG later.
flow.subtext: Two distinct events: class switch (IgM→IgG) and affinity maturation (low affinity → high affinity). Both happen in the germinal center.

flow.text: Both require T-cell help via a B-T molecular handshake.
flow.subtext: B cell presents protein peptide on Class II to a CD4 T cell.

flow.text: The T cell delivers signals back.
flow.subtext: One pair of molecules is the central license.
flow.insight: The T cell delivers signals back

flow.text: CD40 on B cells. CD40L (CD154) on activated CD4 T cells.
flow.subtext: CD40 is constitutively expressed on B cells. CD40L appears on T cells only after activation — ensuring help only fires when antigen is present.

flow.text: CD40-CD40L engagement triggers class switch recombination (CSR).
flow.subtext: Plus cytokine direction from T cell
flow.insight: CD40 = switch on · cytokines = which isotype

flow.text: IL-4 pushes IgG1/IgE; TGF-β pushes IgA; IFN-γ pushes IgG2/IgG3.
CD40 is the on-switch; cytokines pick the destination.
Same CD40 signaling drives germinal center entry → somatic hypermutation.
flow.subtext: AID introduces mutations in V regions.

flow.text: Cells with higher-affinity BCRs survive selection.
flow.subtext: That's affinity maturation.
flow.insight: Cells with higher-affinity BCRs survive selection

flow.text: Answer: A · CD40 – CD40 ligand.
flow.subtext: The required B-T handshake for both class switching and affinity maturation. Disruption (CD40L mutation, X-linked) causes hyper-IgM syndrome.

flow.text: Hyper-IgM syndrome: ↑↑ IgM, ↓ IgG, ↓ IgA, ↓ IgE.
flow.subtext: X-linked form (CD40L mutation, Q194). Recurrent sinopulmonary infections (no IgG opsonization) + opportunistic infections (Pneumocystis, Cryptosporidium).
flow.insight: Hyper-IgM = no class switch · only IgM made

flow.text: Q99 follow-up: predominant Ig is IgM → main biological property is complement activation.
flow.subtext: IgM doesn't cross placenta, doesn't bind eosinophil/mast Fc, isn't easily mucosally secreted. Its dominant function: efficient C1q binding → complement.

stations.bridge: An infant receives a protein vaccine and produces IgM, then high-affinity IgG. Which B-T interaction enables that switch?
stations.vignette:
A 4-month-old infant receives a protein-based vaccine and develops an early IgM antibody response, followed several weeks later by production of high-affinity IgG antibodies against the same antigen.

Which of the following B- to T-lymphocyte interactions is required for this class-switching and affinity maturation process?
keypoints: CD40 is constitutively expressed on B cells.
CD40L (CD154) is upregulated on CD4 T cells after they've been activated by antigen on Class II MHC.
When a CD4 T cell engages a B cell presenting the same antigen on Class II, CD40L binds CD40 — sending B-cell-side signals through TRAFs and NFκB.

Combined with cytokines from the T cell (IL-4 → IgG1/IgE; IFN-γ → IgG2/IgG3; TGF-β → IgA), this triggers class switch recombination (CSR) — the B cell deletes the μ constant region and joins V(D)J to a downstream constant region.

The same CD40-driven signaling licenses entry into the germinal center, where B cells undergo somatic hypermutation at the V regions (mediated by activation-induced cytidine deaminase, AID) followed by affinity-based selection — the molecular basis of affinity maturation.

stations.label: Station 08
stations.title: Affinity maturation
stations.subtitle: Why an antibody that binds the right antigen but binds it badly tells you which germinal-center step is broken — and which Ig domains change with isotype.

flow.text: Clinical: 6yo with recurrent infections. Normal B/T counts. Antibodies present but weak.
flow.subtext: The patient's immune machinery is mostly intact — except for one specific quality-control step.

flow.text: Three diagnostic phrases: weak binding · poor opsonization · poor precipitation.
flow.subtext: All three signal LOW AFFINITY.

flow.text: Antigen binds, but loosely.
flow.subtext: The key insight is that low-affinity antibodies CAN still do some things — but not the things requiring tight grip.

flow.text: What CAN they do? Hemagglutination + complement activation. Both work even at low affinity.
flow.subtext: Pentameric IgM can crosslink RBCs (10 binding sites) and activate complement (one IgM does it) regardless of how tightly each individual Fab grips.
flow.insight: Hemagglutination + complement = avidity tricks, not affinity

flow.text: That's why those tests are positive.
flow.insight: That's why those tests are positive

flow.text: Affinity maturation is the germinal-center process that makes antibodies tight-binding.
flow.subtext: Two coupled steps: (1) AID introduces point mutations in V regions — somatic hypermutation. (2) B cells with higher-affinity BCRs win competition for antigen and survive selection.

flow.text: If affinity maturation fails: antibodies stay at germline affinity — too weak to opsonize.
flow.subtext: Germline antibodies bind ~1000× weaker than mature antibodies.

flow.text: Hemagglutination still works (multivalent IgM); complement still works (IgM C1q binding); but FcR engagement on phagocytes needs higher affinity.
Answer: A · Affinity maturation.
flow.subtext: Germline-affinity antibodies + intact V(D)J + intact class switch but failed selection = the affinity maturation diagnosis.

flow.text: Q165 companion: when class switch happens, only heavy-chain constant region changes.
flow.subtext: Variable regions and light chains are preserved → antigen specificity stays the same. The 'switch' is just swapping the Fc tail (μ → γ, α, ε) while keeping the Fab arms intact.
flow.insight: Class switch changes Fc only · variable regions preserved

stations.bridge: Antibodies exist, can detect antigen by hemagglutination, and activate complement. But they bind weakly, fail to opsonize, and don't precipitate antigen. Which process is defective?
stations.vignette:
A 6-year-old boy with a history of frequent upper respiratory infections receives a booster vaccination containing tetanus toxoid. Laboratory evaluation shows normal numbers and function of peripheral B lymphocytes and T lymphocytes. He produces measurable antibodies that are detectable by passive hemagglutination and can activate complement-mediated lysis of antigen-coated erythrocytes. However, these antibodies bind antigen weakly, fail to opsonize effectively, and do not efficiently precipitate antigen.

This child most likely has a defect in which of the following immune processes?

choices.text: Recombination of heavy chain variable region genes
Recombination of light chain variable region genes

keypoints: Can agglutinate erythrocytes (hemagglutination just needs many binding sites — IgM pentamer works fine even at low affinity).
Can activate complement (IgM is excellent at this regardless of affinity).
Can't opsonize well (needs tight binding for FcR engagement).
Can't precipitate antigen efficiently (needs high-affinity crosslinking).

stations.label: Station 09
stations.title: Antibody functions
stations.subtitle: What antibodies actually DO once they're built — and how five questions converge on a hierarchy of effector functions.

flow.text: Vaccinated patient. Has serum antibodies binding viral surface proteins. Re-exposed to virus.
flow.subtext: Classic protective immunity setup. The question is mechanism: HOW do those antibodies prevent infection?
flow.insight: Antibodies vs free virus = mechanism question

flow.text: Antibodies have a hierarchy of effector functions: neutralization > opsonization > complement > ADCC.
flow.subtext: For free virus, neutralization is the most efficient — block entry before infection starts. Other functions clean up if neutralization fails.
flow.insight: Effector hierarchy · neutralization first for virus

flow.text: Neutralization = Fab arms physically block the virus's attachment site.
flow.subtext: Virus needs to bind a host receptor to enter. Antibody covers that binding site → virus can't dock → no infection.
flow.insight: Block attachment = block entry = block infection

flow.text: Answer: B · Blocking viral attachment to host cell receptors.
flow.subtext: Neutralization is the primary protective mechanism for vaccine-induced immunity against viruses.

flow.text: It's the basis of measles, polio, HPV, hepatitis B, and most other viral vaccine protection.
Q86: For influenza, the neutralizing target is hemagglutinin (HA).
flow.subtext: HA binds sialic acid on respiratory epithelium.

flow.text: Anti-HA antibodies block HA-sialic acid binding → no entry.
flow.subtext: Anti-NA antibodies are useful for limiting spread, but not for primary neutralization.
flow.insight: Anti-HA antibodies block HA-sialic acid binding → no entry

flow.text: Q175/Q74: HPV vaccine generates high neutralizing titers → cervical cancer prevention.
flow.subtext: L1 capsid virus-like particles are highly immunogenic.

flow.text: The resulting antibodies neutralize HPV attachment to cervical epithelium → no infection → no oncogenic E6/E7 activity → no cancer.
flow.insight: The resulting antibodies neutralize HPV attachment to...

flow.text: Q122: No competition between two antibodies = no shared epitope.
flow.subtext: Competition assays test epitope overlap.

flow.text: If antibody A doesn't displace antibody B, they bind different sites.
flow.subtext: No competition = distinct epitopes.

flow.text: This principle is useful for epitope mapping.
Also useful for designing combination therapy that hits multiple sites.
Q49: Polyclonal antibodies have greater epitope diversity than monoclonal.
flow.subtext: Polyclonal pool = many B-cell clones, many epitopes recognized.

flow.text: Monoclonal = one clone, one epitope.
Polyclonal: high sensitivity (more chances to bind), lower specificity.
flow.subtext: Monoclonal: high specificity, narrow.
flow.insight: Polyclonal: high sensitivity (more chances to bind)

flow.text: Chapter 8 closes. 9 stations · 17+ source questions · diversity → effector functions.
flow.subtext: From V(D)J recombination at the bottom of the stack to vaccine-induced neutralization at the top — the complete arc of B-cell biology and antibody function.
flow.insight: Chapter 8 complete · 9 stations · ready for Ch 9

flow.text: Chapter 9 next: vaccines and immune memory.
flow.insight: Chapter 9 next: vaccines and immune memory

stations.bridge: Antibodies bind viral surface proteins. The patient is re-exposed to the virus. What's the FIRST and most important thing those antibodies do?
stations.vignette: A 25-year-old man received a vaccine 6 months ago and is now being evaluated for protection against a viral infection. Laboratory studies show that he has antibodies in his serum that bind to surface viral proteins. If exposed to the same virus today, which mechanism is the most likely way these antibodies prevent infection?

choices.text: Blocking viral attachment to host cell receptors
Direct destruction of infected host cells

keypoints: The single most important antibody function for preventing viral infection is neutralization: physically blocking the pathogen from binding its host-cell receptor. If the virus can't attach, it can't enter, and infection never starts. This is the basis of essentially all protective vaccine immunity against viruses.
Antibodies have a hierarchy of effector functions:
For free virus in the bloodstream, neutralization is the first line. Answer: B · Blocking viral attachment to host cell receptors.

Q86 connection — influenza: the protective neutralizing antibody target is hemagglutinin (HA). HA is the viral surface glycoprotein that binds sialic acid on respiratory epithelium. Anti-HA antibodies block the HA–sialic acid interaction → no viral attachment → no infection. (Antibodies to neuraminidase are also helpful but not neutralizing — they limit viral spread, not initial entry.)

Q175/Q74 connection — HPV: The HPV vaccine generates very high antibody titers because the L1 capsid protein assembled into virus-like particles is highly immunogenic AND the lifetime exposure burden is significant. The result: high titer neutralizing antibodies that prevent HPV attachment to cervical epithelium → cervical cancer prevention.

Q49 connection — polyclonal vs monoclonal: polyclonal antibodies (a mixed pool from many B-cell clones) recognize MANY epitopes on the same antigen. Monoclonal antibodies (one clone) recognize ONE epitope. Polyclonal pools have greater epitope diversity → higher sensitivity in detection assays (more chances to bind), but lower specificity (more cross-reactivity).

chapters.title: Vaccines & Immune Memory
chapters.subtitle: How memory is built, why it lasts, and how each vaccine type exploits a different arm of immunity.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: Live vs killed
stations.subtitle: Why two polio vaccines targeting the same virus produce protection through completely different antibody compartments.

flow.text: IPV (injected) prevents paralysis but allows gut replication and shedding.
flow.subtext: Two endpoints, one success and one failure. The split is the diagnostic clue.

flow.text: Paralysis happens when virus reaches CNS via bloodstream. Shedding happens from gut epithelium.
flow.subtext: Two anatomical compartments.

flow.text: CNS protection needs serum antibody.
flow.subtext: Gut protection needs mucosal antibody.

flow.text: Injected antigen drains to systemic lymph nodes — primes serum IgG only.
flow.subtext: IM injection of inactivated virus engages the systemic compartment. B cells in lymph nodes class-switch to IgG that circulates in blood.

flow.text: Serum IgG can't reach mucosal lumen efficiently. No IgA at the gut surface.
flow.subtext: There's no transcytosis system that pumps serum IgG across mucosal epithelium into lumen (FcRn handles placental IgG, not mucosal).

flow.text: Mucosal IgA must be made LOCALLY by primed B cells.
flow.insight: Mucosal IgA must be made LOCALLY by primed B cells

flow.text: Without mucosal IgA, virus still attaches and replicates in gut epithelium.
flow.subtext: Wild poliovirus can colonize the gut of an IPV-vaccinated person. They won't get paralyzed (serum IgG handles that), but they shed virus and can transmit it.

flow.text: OPV (oral) replicates in gut → primes mucosal B cells → secretory IgA.
flow.subtext: Live attenuated virus engages Peyer's patches and GALT directly. B cells class-switch to IgA, are transported across epithelium via pIgR, become secretory IgA in gut lumen.

flow.text: Answer: B · IPV induces serum IgG but limited mucosal IgA.
flow.subtext: Route → compartment → isotype. The route principle is the most important vaccinology lesson in this module.
flow.insight: Answer: B · serum IgG · weak gut IgA

stations.bridge: Why does the inactivated polio vaccine prevent paralysis but fail to stop the virus from being shed in stool?
stations.vignette:
A medical student is reviewing differences between the inactivated poliovirus vaccine (IPV, given by injection) and the oral attenuated poliovirus vaccine (OPV). She notes that IPV reliably prevents paralytic polio but, in vaccinated individuals exposed to wild-type virus, the virus can still replicate in the gut and be shed in stool.

Which of the following best explains why IPV does NOT prevent gut replication and shedding?

choices.text: IPV cannot generate any neutralizing antibody response
IPV induces serum IgG but limited mucosal IgA
IPV generates only IgM that fails to mature
IPV antigens differ from circulating poliovirus strains

keypoints: Killed virus particles introduced to systemic circulation.
Drains to systemic lymph nodes.
Primes B cells to make serum IgG.
IgG circulates in blood — neutralizes virus when it enters bloodstream from gut → prevents CNS invasion → prevents paralysis.
BUT no mucosal IgA generated → wild virus can still replicate in gut → still shed in stool → can still spread to others.

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: Conjugate vaccine
stations.subtitle: Why an unmodified Hib polysaccharide vaccine fails in infants — and why attaching it to a protein converts it into one of the most successful vaccines ever made.

flow.text: 5-month-old, bulging fontanelle, gram-negative coccobacilli in CSF — Hib meningitis.
flow.subtext: Haemophilus influenzae type b was the leading cause of bacterial meningitis in young children pre-vaccine. The PRP capsule is its key virulence factor.

flow.text: Anti-PRP antibodies opsonize the capsule → splenic clearance → protection.
flow.subtext: PRP-specific IgG coats the bacterium.

flow.text: Spleen macrophages recognize IgG-Fc and complement deposition.
flow.subtext: Bacterium cleared.
flow.insight: Spleen macrophages recognize IgG-Fc and complement...

flow.text: Problem: PRP is a polysaccharide. Polysaccharides are T-independent.
flow.subtext: Polysaccharides can't be processed by APCs into peptides for MHC class II presentation. So no CD4 help can be recruited via the polysaccharide itself.

flow.text: T-independent antigens fail in children < 2 years.
flow.subtext: Marginal-zone B cells (which mediate T-independent responses in adults) don't fully mature until age ~2. So the population most at risk for invasive Hib can't respond to plain PRP.

flow.text: Solution: conjugate PRP to a carrier protein (tetanus toxoid, CRM197, etc.).
flow.subtext: Chemical linkage.
flow.insight: Conjugation adds peptide for MHC II

flow.text: Now the antigen has BOTH polysaccharide epitopes AND carrier protein.
flow.subtext: The B cell recognizes PRP; the protein gets processed for T-cell help.
flow.insight: Now the antigen has BOTH polysaccharide epitopes AND...

flow.text: B cell binds PRP via BCR → internalizes whole conjugate → presents carrier peptides on MHC II.
flow.subtext: The B cell's BCR specificity is for PRP. But once the conjugate is internalized and processed, the carrier protein peptides are what get displayed on MHC class II.
flow.insight: B cell shows carrier peptides on MHC II

flow.text: Carrier-specific CD4 T cell sees peptide on MHC II → provides CD40L + cytokines.
flow.subtext: The T cell doesn't care that the B cell is PRP-specific; it just sees its peptide on MHC II. Help flows back to the B cell anyway.
flow.insight: Carrier T cell helps PRP B cell

flow.text: Result: class-switched anti-PRP IgG + memory. Works even in infants.
flow.subtext: Same B cell that started PRP-specific now produces high-affinity class-switched anti-PRP IgG, with memory.
flow.insight: Anti-PRP IgG + memory · works in infants

flow.text: Hib conjugate has reduced invasive Hib disease by >99% in vaccinated populations.
flow.insight: Hib conjugate has reduced invasive Hib disease by &gt

flow.text: Answer: C · PRP conjugated to a carrier protein — recruits T-cell help.
flow.subtext: The single most important vaccine innovation for protecting young children against encapsulated bacteria. Same trick used for pneumococcus (PCV) and meningococcus (MenACWY).
flow.insight: Answer: C · conjugation enables T help

stations.bridge: An unimmunized 5-month-old presents with bacterial meningitis. Which vaccine, given earlier, would have prevented this — and what's the molecular reason a plain polysaccharide version wouldn't have worked at this age?
stations.vignette: A 5-month-old infant presents to the emergency department with fever, lethargy, and a bulging fontanelle. Lumbar puncture reveals gram-negative coccobacilli. The infant has not received any vaccines. Which feature of the Hib vaccine that is now part of the routine infant immunization schedule allows it to elicit a protective antibody response in infants younger than 2 years?

choices.text: It is a live attenuated organism
PRP polysaccharide is presented alone, without carrier
PRP is conjugated to a carrier protein, recruiting T-cell help
It is administered by the oral route

keypoints: Hib's pathogenicity is due to its polyribosylribitol phosphate (PRP) capsular polysaccharide.
Anti-PRP antibodies are protective (opsonize the capsule for splenic clearance).
But PRP is a polysaccharide, not a protein → can't be processed by APCs into peptides → can't be presented on MHC class II → can't recruit CD4 T-cell help.
That makes plain PRP a T-independent antigen: directly crosslinks BCRs, generates IgM, no class switching, no memory.
In infants < 2 years, the marginal-zone B cells that respond to T-independent antigens haven't fully matured → essentially no response at all.

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: Asplenia vaccines
stations.subtitle: Why a splenectomized patient needs three vaccines they didn't need before — and what the spleen actually does for encapsulated bacteria.

flow.text: Splenectomized patient. The spleen is the main organ for clearing opsonized encapsulated bacteria.
flow.subtext: Splenic macrophages in slow-flow sinusoids catch antibody/complement-coated bacteria. Take out the spleen — bacteria persist in circulation.

flow.text: Encapsulated organisms: S. pneumoniae · N. meningitidis · H. influenzae b (also S. typhi, GBS).
flow.subtext: Their polysaccharide capsules resist complement and phagocytosis UNLESS first coated with specific antibody.

flow.text: Then spleen clears them.
flow.subtext: Without spleen — they persist.

flow.text: Without spleen → can't clear them → OPSI (overwhelming post-splenectomy infection), 50% mortality.
flow.subtext: Rapid bacteremia, sepsis, DIC, death within hours of symptom onset. Most commonly pneumococcus.

flow.text: Pneumococcus is the leading OPSI pathogen (50–90% of cases).
flow.subtext: More common than meningococcus or Hib in OPSI. That's why pneumococcal vaccination is the highest priority.

flow.text: Answer: D · Streptococcus pneumoniae.
flow.subtext: Plus meningococcus and Hib (the SHiN triad). Asplenic patients need: PCV20, PPSV23, MenACWY, MenB, Hib + annual flu.

stations.bridge: A splenectomized patient is at high risk for which specific class of pathogen — and which vaccine should they receive that healthy adults don't routinely need?
stations.vignette: A 32-year-old woman underwent splenectomy 2 years ago following trauma. Her primary care physician wants to ensure she is protected against the infections she is at highest risk for. Beyond routine immunizations, which of the following organisms should she be specifically vaccinated against given her splenectomy?
keypoints: Splenic macrophages (especially marginal zone macrophages) recognize antibody-coated and complement-coated bacteria via FcγR and CR1/CR3.
The unique architecture (slow-flow sinusoids, dense macrophage population) ensures that even small numbers of opsonized organisms get caught and phagocytosed.
This is especially important for encapsulated organisms — their polysaccharide capsules resist complement deposition unless coated with specific antibody first.

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: Toxoid vaccine
stations.subtitle: Why an inactivated toxin is the perfect antigen for diseases caused by the toxin itself.

flow.text: Diphtheria disease is caused by a secreted exotoxin, not by bacterial invasion.
flow.subtext: C. diphtheriae can colonize the throat without invading.

flow.text: The pseudomembrane, myocarditis, and neuropathy all come from the toxin's effects on cells.
Diphtheria toxin = A-B toxin. B binds receptor; A ADP-ribosylates EF-2 → halts protein synthesis → cell death.
flow.subtext: Same A-B architecture as cholera, anthrax LF, Pseudomonas exotoxin A. Targeted enzymatic inactivation of a critical host factor.

flow.text: Vaccine strategy: chemically inactivate the toxin with formaldehyde. The toxoid keeps its shape but loses its enzymatic activity.
flow.subtext: Surface epitopes are preserved → B cells still recognize them → antibodies still made. But the inactivated form can't kill cells.

flow.text: Vaccinated person makes antitoxin IgG antibodies.
flow.subtext: Toxoid is a protein → T-dependent antigen → class-switched IgG → memory. Boosters every 10 years maintain antitoxin titers.

flow.text: On real exposure: antibodies bind the active toxin → neutralize → prevent cell entry.
flow.subtext: Even if C. diphtheriae colonizes the throat and produces toxin, antitoxin IgG neutralizes it before it can damage host cells.
flow.insight: Neutralization stops toxin even if bacterium present

flow.text: Answer: B · Antibodies neutralize diphtheria toxin.
flow.subtext: Same toxoid logic for tetanus.

flow.text: Antibody-mediated neutralization of an extracellular toxin.
flow.subtext: Toxoids also serve as carrier proteins in conjugate vaccines.
flow.insight: Antibody-mediated neutralization of an extracellular toxin

stations.bridge: Diphtheria toxoid in the DTaP vaccine — what type of antigen is it, and what does it do?
stations.vignette: The diphtheria component of the DTaP vaccine is a chemically inactivated form of diphtheria toxin. Which of the following best describes how this vaccine component induces protection?

choices.text: It contains live attenuated Corynebacterium diphtheriae
It generates antibodies that neutralize diphtheria toxin
It generates cytotoxic T cells specific for infected cells
It primes mucosal IgA in the upper respiratory tract
It uses Corynebacterium polysaccharide capsule conjugated to a carrier

keypoints: An A-B toxin: B subunit binds heparin-binding EGF-like growth factor receptor on host cells.
A subunit enters the cell and ADP-ribosylates elongation factor 2 (EF-2).
EF-2 inactivation halts protein synthesis → cell death.
Local effect: pseudomembranous pharyngitis. Systemic effects: myocarditis, demyelinating peripheral neuropathy, renal failure.

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: Passive immunity
stations.subtitle: How giving someone else's antibodies can prevent a disease that hasn't happened yet — and why RhoGAM works in a way that's the OPPOSITE of vaccination.

flow.text: Rh-neg mom + Rh-pos baby. At delivery, some fetal Rh+ RBCs leak into maternal circulation.
flow.subtext: Feto-maternal hemorrhage is essentially universal at delivery. Without intervention, mom's immune system encounters Rh-D antigen for the first time.
flow.insight: Delivery → fetal RBCs cross to mom

flow.text: Without intervention: mom's B cells make anti-D antibody → memory.
flow.subtext: First exposure: IgM (limited harm).

flow.text: Class switch follows over weeks to months → anti-D IgG.
flow.subtext: And memory B cells.
flow.insight: Class switch follows over weeks to months → anti-D IgG

flow.text: Next Rh+ pregnancy: maternal anti-D IgG crosses placenta → attacks fetal RBCs → hemolytic disease.
flow.subtext: From Ch 8 Station 04: IgG is the only isotype that crosses the placenta via FcRn. Same biology that delivers protective vaccine antibodies delivers harmful anti-D antibodies.
flow.insight: Anti-D IgG crosses placenta · attacks next baby

flow.text: RhoGAM solution: inject pre-formed anti-D IgG within 72h of delivery.
flow.subtext: Pre-formed antibody binds any circulating fetal Rh+ RBCs in maternal blood. Splenic macrophages clear them via Fc-mediated phagocytosis.

flow.text: Mom's B cells never get sustained exposure → no sensitization → no memory → next pregnancy safe.
flow.subtext: The antigen is removed before the B cell response can develop.
flow.insight: No exposure → no memory → next pregnancy safe

flow.text: No anti-D IgG made by mom.
flow.insight: No anti-D IgG made by mom

flow.text: No memory.
flow.subtext: The next Rh+ pregnancy is treated as a 'first encounter' again.

flow.text: Answer: C · Preformed anti-D IgG clears Rh+ fetal cells before maternal sensitization.
flow.subtext: Passive immunization — pre-formed antibody.
flow.insight: Answer: C · passive anti-D Ig prevents sensitization

flow.text: Same logic for TIG, RIG, HBIG, palivizumab, IVIG.
flow.subtext: Immediate, short-lived (~3 weeks IgG half-life), no memory generated.

stations.bridge: An Rh-negative woman delivers an Rh-positive baby. We give her an injection of anti-D immune globulin. What is the immunologic mechanism?
stations.vignette: A 26-year-old Rh-negative woman has just delivered her first child, who is determined to be Rh-positive. The obstetrician administers Rh immune globulin (RhoGAM) within 72 hours of delivery. Which of the following best describes the immunologic mechanism by which this prevents future Rh hemolytic disease of the newborn?

choices.text: It actively immunizes the mother against Rh-D
It generates maternal cytotoxic T cells against fetal RBCs
It supplies preformed anti-D IgG that clears Rh+ fetal cells
It activates maternal complement against fetal antigen
It non-specifically suppresses maternal IgM production

keypoints: Rh-negative mother + Rh-positive fetus.
At delivery (or with any feto-maternal hemorrhage), some fetal Rh+ RBCs cross into maternal circulation.
If mom's immune system encounters Rh-D antigen, she'll mount an antibody response — initially IgM (limited consequence), but with time and class switching, IgG.
Anti-D IgG CROSSES THE PLACENTA in subsequent pregnancies (Ch 8 Station 04 — IgG via FcRn).
Result in a future Rh+ pregnancy: maternal anti-D IgG attacks fetal RBCs → hemolytic disease of the newborn (HDFN), erythroblastosis fetalis.

stations.label: Station 06
stations.title: Subunit vaccine
stations.subtitle: How a single viral surface protein, made in yeast, prevents one of the most common chronic viral infections in human history — and how to read the serology that follows.

flow.text: HBV vaccine = recombinant HBsAg made in yeast. No live virus, no other viral proteins.
flow.subtext: The HBsAg gene is inserted into yeast.

flow.text: Yeast makes the surface protein.
Protein self-assembles into virus-like particles.
flow.subtext: Pure HBsAg subunit — that's the vaccine.

flow.text: Vaccinated person makes anti-HBs antibody. Period.
flow.subtext: The only antigen they were exposed to was HBsAg.
flow.insight: Only anti-HBs made by vaccinated person

flow.text: Their immune system makes anti-HBs IgG.
flow.subtext: That's the protective antibody.
flow.insight: Their immune system makes anti-HBs IgG

flow.text: Vaccine has no core antigen (HBcAg) → no anti-HBc made.
flow.subtext: Core antigen is internal to the virus.
flow.insight: No HBcAg in vaccine → no anti-HBc in serum

flow.text: The vaccine, made of just the surface protein, never exposes the immune system to core.
flow.subtext: No anti-HBc.

flow.text: Vaccine has no live virus → no HBsAg circulating in blood.
flow.subtext: There's no replicating virus in the patient.

flow.text: Any HBsAg from the vaccine is rapidly cleared by the immune response.
flow.subtext: So HBsAg in serum = negative.
flow.insight: Any HBsAg from the vaccine is rapidly cleared by the immune...

flow.text: Vaccine fingerprint: HBsAg neg · anti-HBs POS · anti-HBc neg.
flow.subtext: This pattern is diagnostic of successful vaccination — never infected. Any other pattern means something else.

flow.text: Compare: recovered natural infection = anti-HBs POS + anti-HBc POS.
flow.subtext: Natural infection exposes the immune system to ALL viral proteins, including core.
flow.insight: anti-HBc = exposure to real virus (not vaccine)

flow.text: So recovered patients have BOTH antibodies.
flow.subtext: Anti-HBc is the marker of past or present REAL infection.
flow.insight: So recovered patients have BOTH antibodies

flow.text: Answer: B · HBsAg neg, anti-HBs POS, anti-HBc neg.
flow.subtext: The single positive marker (anti-HBs) is the vaccine fingerprint. Recombinant subunit vaccine platform now used for HPV, shingles, and others.

stations.bridge: A young clinician completes the 3-dose hepatitis B vaccine series. What's the only positive marker on her serology, and what does that pattern mean?
stations.vignette: A 24-year-old medical student completes her 3-dose hepatitis B vaccine series. Six weeks after the last dose, serology is performed. Which of the following best describes her expected hepatitis B serologic profile?

choices.text: HBsAg positive, anti-HBs negative, anti-HBc positive
HBsAg negative, anti-HBs positive, anti-HBc negative
HBsAg negative, anti-HBs positive, anti-HBc IgM positive
HBsAg positive, anti-HBs negative, anti-HBc negative
HBsAg negative, anti-HBs negative, anti-HBc IgG positive

keypoints: HBsAg is on the viral envelope. Anti-HBs antibodies neutralize the virus by blocking attachment to hepatocyte sodium taurocholate cotransporting polypeptide (NTCP).
HBsAg is also present in vast excess as non-infectious sub-viral particles in chronically infected patients — meaning anti-HBs is enough to indicate protection.

stations.label: Station 07
stations.title: HepB vaccine
stations.subtitle: Why some viruses are deliberately NEVER attenuated for vaccine use — and what features of HBV make it a permanent subunit-only vaccine target.

flow.text: Live attenuated vaccines work great for many viruses (MMR, VZV, OPV, YF). Why not HBV?
flow.subtext: The question is asking what specific feature of HBV biology makes it dangerous as a live vaccine.

flow.text: HBV uses reverse transcription. DNA intermediates can integrate into hepatocyte genome.
flow.subtext: HBV is a DNA virus, but its replication includes a reverse transcription step. The resulting DNA can incorporate into the host hepatocyte chromosome.

flow.text: Integrated HBV DNA → hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) risk over decades.
flow.subtext: Chronic HBV infection is one of the leading causes of HCC worldwide. Integration disrupts host gene regulation and provides ongoing antigen.

flow.text: HBV also establishes chronic carriage as cccDNA in hepatocyte nuclei.
flow.subtext: Even if surface infection is controlled, cccDNA persists in nucleus indefinitely.

flow.text: No current therapy reliably eliminates it.
flow.subtext: A live vaccine could establish exactly this kind of persistent reservoir.
flow.insight: No current therapy reliably eliminates it

flow.text: Even an attenuated HBV would still integrate and persist. Attenuation can't fix biology.
flow.subtext: Reduced replication doesn't eliminate the integration step or the cccDNA reservoir formation. Those features are intrinsic to HBV's lifecycle.

flow.text: Subunit vaccine solves it: HBsAg only, no DNA, no integration possible.
flow.subtext: Recombinant HBsAg has no viral genome at all.

flow.text: Can't replicate, can't integrate, can't establish chronic infection.
flow.subtext: Safe even in immunocompromised, including newborns of HBsAg+ mothers.

flow.text: Answer: C · Live HBV could integrate and establish chronic infection.
flow.subtext: Same logic for HIV (integrates into CD4 DNA), HCV (chronic infection), HPV (oncogenic E6/E7). When the natural disease can be lifelong cancer, you don't make a live vaccine.
flow.insight: Answer: C · integration risk · live unsafe

stations.bridge: Why is the HBV vaccine recombinant subunit and not live attenuated like MMR or VZV?
stations.vignette: A pharmacology student asks her preceptor why the hepatitis B vaccine uses a recombinant subunit approach (HBsAg expressed in yeast) rather than a live attenuated approach like the MMR or varicella vaccines. Which of the following best explains why a live attenuated hepatitis B vaccine is not used?

choices.text: HBV cannot be attenuated by serial passage
HBV cannot be cultured in any cell line
Live HBV could integrate and risk chronic infection
Live HBV vaccine would require oral administration
Recombinant HBsAg outperforms any live virus

keypoints: Reverse transcription: HBV is a partially double-stranded DNA virus that uses reverse transcriptase during replication. Its replication has DNA intermediates that are prone to integration.
Hepatocyte DNA integration: HBV DNA can integrate into the host hepatocyte genome. Integrated viral DNA is a major mechanism of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) in chronic HBV infection.

Chronic carriage: Once HBV establishes infection (especially perinatally), it can persist for life as cccDNA (covalently closed circular DNA) in hepatocyte nuclei. There's no current therapy that reliably eliminates this reservoir.
Reversion potential: Like all live attenuated vaccines, an attenuated HBV could theoretically revert to virulence. The consequences of reversion (chronic hepatitis, cirrhosis, HCC) are unacceptable.

stations.label: Station 08
stations.title: Cross-reactive immunity
stations.subtitle: How vaccinating with one virus protects against a different virus — and why this cross-reactivity is the foundation of bioterrorism preparedness for smallpox.

flow.text: Smallpox vaccine = vaccinia virus, NOT variola.
flow.subtext: Vaccinia is a separate orthopoxvirus species — derived historically from cowpox or related, then propagated as the vaccine strain.

flow.text: Both are orthopoxviruses — closely related genus with many shared surface antigens.
flow.subtext: Variola, vaccinia, cowpox, monkeypox, camelpox all share genus-level surface protein homology. Antibodies and T cells against one recognize the others.

flow.text: Anti-vaccinia antibodies bind shared epitopes on variola → neutralization.
flow.subtext: Many surface proteins are highly conserved across the genus. Antibodies against vaccinia surface proteins recognize the homologous variola proteins.

flow.text: Vaccinia-specific T cells also recognize variola peptides on MHC → broader cross-protection.
flow.subtext: Internal proteins (polymerase, structural proteins) are also highly conserved. T cells primed against vaccinia peptides cross-react with variola peptides on infected cell MHC.

flow.text: Answer: B · Antigenic similarity → cross-protective immunity.
flow.subtext: Same principle protects vaccinia recipients against monkeypox today (~85%).

flow.text: The smallpox vaccine stockpile is critical bioterrorism preparedness — routine vaccination ended in 1972, so anyone under ~52 has no immunity.
flow.insight: The smallpox vaccine stockpile is critical bioterrorism...

stations.bridge: In a smallpox bioterrorism scenario, public health authorities would deploy vaccinia. Why does vaccinating with one virus protect against a different virus?
stations.vignette: In a hypothetical smallpox bioterrorism scenario, public health authorities plan to administer vaccinia virus to exposed and at-risk populations. Which of the following best explains how vaccination with vaccinia virus provides protection against variola (smallpox) virus?

choices.text: Vaccinia is an attenuated form of variola itself
Vaccinia shares enough antigens for cross-protective immunity
Vaccinia integrates anti-variola DNA into the host genome
Vaccinia produces an antitoxin against variola exotoxin

keypoints: Variola virus: Causes smallpox. Now exists only in two high-containment laboratories (CDC and Russian VECTOR).
Vaccinia virus: The vaccine strain. Origin unclear — derived historically from cowpox or a now-extinct horsepox-like virus, then propagated and refined.
Cowpox virus: Edward Jenner's original observation (1796) was that milkmaids who'd had cowpox didn't get smallpox.
Monkeypox (mpox) virus: Causes ongoing zoonotic and human outbreaks. Smallpox vaccine provides cross-protection (~85% effectiveness against mpox).

stations.label: Station 09
stations.title: Live vaccine risk
stations.subtitle: Why a vaccine that works beautifully in healthy people becomes life-threatening in immunocompromised people — and how live attenuated principles tie this whole chapter together.

flow.text: 4-month-old, low TRECs → suspected SCID (severe combined immunodeficiency).
flow.subtext: Low T-cell receptor excision circles on newborn screen indicates failure of T-cell development. Multiple genetic causes (X-linked γc, JAK3, RAG1/2, ADA, etc.).

flow.text: SCID patients can't control replicating viruses. Live vaccines = potentially lethal infection.
flow.subtext: T-cell control of viral replication is essential.
flow.insight: Live virus dangerous in T-cell deficient host

flow.text: Without it, even attenuated vaccine virus can replicate uncontrolled.
flow.subtext: Documented fatalities from rotavirus vaccine in SCID infants.

flow.text: Of the choices, rotavirus vaccine is LIVE attenuated. The others are not.
flow.subtext: Rotavirus vaccine (RotaTeq, Rotarix) contains live weakened rotavirus given orally. The others use inactivated, recombinant, conjugate, or toxoid platforms.
flow.insight: Rotavirus = the only live one in the list

flow.text: Answer: D · Rotavirus vaccine.
