flow.subtext: Same logic for any live vaccine in SCID: MMR, varicella, BCG, OPV, yellow fever — all contraindicated. Defer until SCID is ruled out or treated.

flow.text: Pregnancy is the other major contraindication: no live vaccines in pregnancy.
flow.subtext: MMR, varicella, yellow fever all theoretically risk fetal infection.

flow.text: Inactivated vaccines (Tdap, flu, COVID, RSV) are encouraged.
flow.subtext: Live vaccines should be given before pregnancy or postpartum.

flow.text: Chapter 9 closes. 9 stations · 13+ source questions · vaccine principles complete.
flow.subtext: Route, antigen type, conjugation, asplenia, toxoid, passive, recombinant subunit, why no live HBV, vaccinia cross-protection, live attenuated contraindications.

flow.text: Chapter 10 next: primary immunodeficiencies.

stations.bridge: A 4-month-old with suspected severe combined immunodeficiency. Which routine infant vaccine should be DEFERRED?
stations.vignette: A 4-month-old infant is being evaluated for suspected severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) after the routine newborn screen showed low T-cell receptor excision circles (TRECs). The patient has not yet received the rotavirus vaccine. Which of the following live attenuated vaccines is contraindicated in this patient if SCID is confirmed?

choices.text: Diphtheria, tetanus, and acellular pertussis (DTaP) vaccine
Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) conjugate vaccine

keypoints: MMR (measles, mumps, rubella)
Varicella (chickenpox)
Zoster (live): Zostavax (largely replaced by recombinant Shingrix, which is not live)
Yellow fever
Rotavirus (oral)
OPV (oral polio — not used in US, used internationally)
Smallpox (vaccinia) — ACAM2000 is fully replicating; JYNNEOS (MVA) is non-replicating
BCG (Mycobacterium bovis — used internationally for TB)
Live attenuated influenza vaccine (FluMist): nasal
Adenovirus (military)
Typhoid (oral Ty21a)

chapters.title: Primary Immunodeficiencies
chapters.subtitle: When a single gene defect breaks adaptive immunity — the inherited patterns boards love to test.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: IgA deficiency
stations.subtitle: Why the most common primary immunodeficiency is silent in most patients — but causes anaphylaxis the first time they receive a blood transfusion containing IgA.

flow.text: Anaphylaxis on first blood transfusion in an otherwise healthy young patient.
flow.subtext: Healthy until exposure. The trigger is the transfusion itself — something in donor blood crosslinked her mast cell IgE.

flow.text: Workup: normal IgG, normal IgM, UNDETECTABLE IgA.
flow.subtext: Selective absence of one isotype only. This is the diagnostic fingerprint of selective IgA deficiency.
flow.insight: Isolated IgA absence · other Ig normal

flow.text: Patient also has anti-IgA IgE antibodies.
flow.subtext: Some IgA-deficient patients view IgA as 'foreign' (their immune system never learned to tolerate it) and produce antibodies against it.
flow.insight: Anti-IgA IgE armed on mast cells

flow.text: When these antibodies are IgE class, they bind mast cells.
flow.insight: When these antibodies are IgE class

flow.text: Donor blood contains IgA → crosslinks anti-IgA IgE on mast cells → degranulation → anaphylaxis.
flow.subtext: Multivalent IgA from the donor crosslinks adjacent IgE molecules on the mast cell surface, triggering massive histamine and mediator release.
flow.insight: Donor IgA crosslinks anti-IgA IgE → anaphylaxis

flow.text: Same mechanism as classic IgE-mediated allergy.
flow.insight: Same mechanism as classic IgE-mediated allergy

flow.text: Answer: C · Selective IgA deficiency.
flow.subtext: Most common PID (~1:300–1:700).

flow.text: Most patients asymptomatic.
flow.subtext: Treatment: avoid IgA-containing blood products, use washed RBCs, IgA-deficient IVIG.

stations.bridge: An otherwise healthy patient develops anaphylaxis during her first blood transfusion. What's the most likely underlying immunodeficiency, and what's the mechanism?
stations.vignette: A 24-year-old woman with no significant medical history is hospitalized after a motor vehicle collision. She requires a blood transfusion. Within minutes of starting the transfusion, she develops severe shortness of breath, hypotension, urticaria, and wheezing. The transfusion is stopped immediately and she is treated with epinephrine and IV fluids. Subsequent immunologic workup demonstrates IgG and IgM levels within normal limits but undetectable serum IgA and the presence of anti-IgA IgE antibodies. Which of the following primary immunodeficiencies does she most likely have?
keypoints: Some IgA-deficient patients produce anti-IgA antibodies (IgG or IgE class). These can develop after exposure to "foreign" IgA in pregnancy, prior transfusion, or environmental sources.
If those anti-IgA antibodies are IgE class, they bind to mast cell FcεRI receptors.
When the patient receives blood products containing donor IgA, the donor IgA crosslinks the anti-IgA IgE on mast cells.
Result: massive mast cell degranulation → anaphylaxis (urticaria, bronchospasm, hypotension).

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: Bruton XLA
stations.subtitle: Why a 9-month-old boy with recurrent infections shows essentially no gamma globulin band on serum protein electrophoresis — and how the BTK gene controls B-cell maturation.

flow.text: 9-month-old boy with recurrent bacterial infections starting after 6 months.
flow.subtext: Onset timing matches when transplacental maternal IgG (Ch 8 Station 04) is exhausted. Male sex + maternal uncle history → think X-linked.
flow.insight: Onset at 6 mo + male + family pattern = X-linked humoral

flow.text: SPEP shows flat gamma band. Quantitative IgG, IgA, IgM all very low.
flow.subtext: Pan-hypogammaglobulinemia — all antibody isotypes are absent.

flow.text: The gamma region of the SPEP is where immunoglobulins migrate.
flow.subtext: Flat band = no antibodies.
flow.insight: The gamma region of the SPEP is where immunoglobulins...

flow.text: Flow cytometry: absent CD19+/CD20+ B cells. T cells normal.
flow.subtext: Pure B-cell deficiency. The lineage that makes antibodies is missing entirely from peripheral blood.
flow.insight: No B cells in periphery · T cells normal

flow.text: Cause: mutation in Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK). X-linked.
flow.subtext: BTK is essential for pre-B cell receptor signaling. Without BTK, B cells arrest at the pre-B stage in bone marrow and never mature into peripheral B cells.

flow.text: Result: vulnerability to encapsulated bacteria after maternal IgG wanes.
flow.subtext: Pneumococcus, H. influenzae, meningococcus — the same SHiN triad we saw in Ch 9 Station 03 for asplenic patients.

flow.text: No antibodies = no opsonization = no clearance.
flow.insight: No antibodies = no opsonization = no clearance

flow.text: Answer: E · X-linked agammaglobulinemia.
flow.subtext: Treatment: lifelong IVIG replacement, aggressive antibiotics, NO live vaccines. Same X-linked inheritance pattern matters for genetic counseling.
flow.insight: Answer: E · XLA · BTK · IVIG for life

stations.bridge: 9-month-old boy, recurrent bacterial infections starting after 6 months. SPEP shows essentially absent gamma band. What's the diagnosis and the molecular defect?
stations.vignette: A 9-month-old boy is brought in with his fourth episode of pneumonia. He has had recurrent otitis media since age 7 months and one prior episode of bacterial sepsis. Family history is notable for a maternal uncle who died of overwhelming bacterial infection in childhood. Serum protein electrophoresis shows a markedly diminished gamma globulin band. Quantitative immunoglobulin testing reveals very low IgG, IgA, and IgM. Flow cytometry shows absent peripheral B cells (CD19/CD20). T-cell numbers and function are normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
keypoints: X-linked recessive — affects boys; mothers are silent carriers (history may include affected maternal uncles).
Asymptomatic for first ~6 months — protected by transplacental maternal IgG (Ch 8 Station 04). As maternal IgG wanes, infections begin.
Recurrent encapsulated bacterial infections — pneumococcus, H. influenzae, meningococcus. Sinopulmonary infections, otitis, bacteremia, meningitis.
Enteroviral susceptibility — particularly enterovirus meningoencephalitis. Antibody-mediated viral clearance is critical for some viruses.
Small or absent tonsils, adenoids, lymph nodes on exam — no B cells means no germinal centers means no follicular tissue.

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: DiGeorge
stations.subtitle: Why an infant with hypocalcemic seizures, a heart murmur, and Pneumocystis pneumonia has the same underlying defect — failed development of the third and fourth pharyngeal pouches.

flow.text: Three findings in one infant: tetralogy of Fallot + hypocalcemic seizures + Pneumocystis pneumonia.
flow.subtext: Cardiac defect + parathyroid failure + immunodeficiency. The challenge is finding the diagnosis that unifies all three.

flow.text: Common embryologic origin: 3rd and 4th pharyngeal pouches.
flow.subtext: These pouches give rise to thymus (3rd), inferior parathyroids (3rd), and superior parathyroids (4th).

flow.text: Neural crest cells migrating from the same region build the conotruncal cardiac region.
flow.insight: Neural crest cells migrating from the same region build the...

flow.text: 22q11.2 deletion is the cause. Disrupts pharyngeal arch development.
flow.subtext: Microdeletion of the 22q11.2 region (TBX1 gene critical) impairs the developmental signals for pharyngeal arch derivatives. The result is the multi-system phenotype.

flow.text: No thymus → no T-cell maturation. Severely low CD3+ T cells.
flow.subtext: T-cell precursors leave the bone marrow as expected, but there's no thymus to receive them.
flow.insight: No thymus = no T cells = T-cell deficiency

flow.text: They die without maturing.
flow.subtext: Result: lymphopenia with absent T cells.

flow.text: T-cell deficiency → susceptibility to Pneumocystis, CMV, fungi, intracellular bacteria.
flow.subtext: Pneumocystis pneumonia in an infant is a hallmark of T-cell deficiency. CTL and Th1 immunity are essential for these pathogens.

flow.text: No parathyroids → no PTH → hypocalcemia → tetany/seizures.
flow.subtext: PTH normally maintains calcium by mobilizing it from bone, increasing renal reabsorption, and activating vitamin D. Without parathyroids, calcium levels drop within days of birth.

flow.text: Conotruncal cardiac defects from neural crest cell failure: TOF, IAA, truncus arteriosus.
flow.subtext: Same neural crest cells that should populate the developing heart's outflow tract also depend on the 22q11.2 region. Their failure produces conotruncal cardiac anomalies.

flow.text: Answer: B · 3rd/4th pharyngeal pouch failure — thymic and parathyroid aplasia.
flow.subtext: CATCH-22 mnemonic. Treatment: cardiac surgery, calcium/vit D, PCP prophylaxis, avoid live vaccines, possible thymic transplant for complete DiGeorge.
flow.insight: Answer: B · DiGeorge = pharyngeal pouch failure

stations.bridge: An infant with tetralogy of Fallot, hypocalcemic seizures, and Pneumocystis pneumonia. What's the unifying diagnosis and the embryologic explanation?
stations.vignette: A 6-week-old infant born with tetralogy of Fallot is admitted with new respiratory distress. He has had episodes of hypocalcemic tetany since the first week of life. Chest CT shows bilateral interstitial infiltrates; bronchoalveolar lavage demonstrates Pneumocystis jirovecii on silver stain. Physical exam shows low-set ears and a small cleft palate. CBC reveals lymphopenia. Flow cytometry demonstrates very low CD3+ T cells with normal B cells. Which of the following best explains the underlying immunologic defect?

choices.text: CD40 ligand mutation impairing class switching

keypoints: Pouch 1: Middle ear cavity, auditory tube.
Pouch 2: Palatine tonsils.
Pouch 3: Inferior parathyroid glands AND thymus.
Pouch 4: Superior parathyroid glands AND ultimobranchial body (parafollicular C cells of thyroid).

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: Wiskott-Aldrich
stations.subtitle: Why a boy with petechiae, eczema, and recurrent infections has a defect in a single cytoskeletal protein — and why his lab values include a paradoxical mix of low and high immunoglobulins.

flow.text: Triad: thrombocytopenia + recurrent infections + eczema. TIE.
flow.subtext: Three findings in one young boy. The challenge is to find the single gene defect that ties them together.
flow.insight: TIE triad = Wiskott-Aldrich until proven otherwise

flow.text: Critical detail: SMALL platelets. Microthrombocytes are pathognomonic.
flow.subtext: Most thrombocytopenias show normal-size or large platelets.

flow.text: Wiskott-Aldrich is unique: small platelets (low MPV).
flow.subtext: This finding is essentially diagnostic.
flow.insight: Wiskott-Aldrich is unique: small platelets (low MPV)

flow.text: Ig profile: ↓ IgM, normal IgG, ↑ IgA, ↑ IgE. Distinctive paradox.
flow.subtext: Low IgM + high IgA/IgE is unusual.

flow.text: The high IgE correlates with the eczema phenotype.
flow.subtext: This pattern is essentially the WAS fingerprint.
flow.insight: The high IgE correlates with the eczema phenotype

flow.text: Cause: WAS gene mutation → WASp protein loss. X-linked recessive.
flow.subtext: WASp is a cytoskeletal regulator.

flow.text: It links cell-surface signaling to actin polymerization.
flow.insight: It links cell-surface signaling to actin polymerization

flow.text: Hematopoietic cells need WASp for normal function.
flow.subtext: Loss affects multiple lineages.
flow.insight: Hematopoietic cells need WASp for normal function

flow.text: Answer: E · Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome.
flow.subtext: X-linked WAS gene mutation → WASp deficiency → TIE triad with characteristic Ig pattern.

flow.text: Curative treatment: HSCT.
flow.subtext: Gene therapy approaches emerging.

stations.bridge: A boy with petechiae, atopic dermatitis, and recurrent ear infections. What's the WAS protein, and why does losing it produce this exact triad?
stations.vignette: A 14-month-old boy is brought in for evaluation of recurrent otitis media, persistent atopic dermatitis since infancy, and easy bruising. His mother reports that he has had multiple episodes of bleeding from minor injuries. CBC shows thrombocytopenia (platelet count 35,000/µL) with small platelets on smear (mean platelet volume markedly decreased). Immunoglobulin testing shows decreased IgM, normal IgG, elevated IgA, and elevated IgE. He is the second male in the maternal line affected by similar features. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
keypoints: Thrombocytopenia with small platelets — distinctive feature. Most thrombocytopenic conditions have normal or large platelets; WAS uniquely shows microthrombocytes (low MPV).
Infections — recurrent encapsulated bacteria, viral infections, opportunistic infections.
Eczema — atopic dermatitis, often severe.

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: ADA SCID
stations.subtitle: Why a single missing enzyme in purine salvage poisons exactly the cells that need to divide rapidly — lymphocytes — and creates one specific form of SCID.

flow.text: 5-month-old with classic SCID picture: thrush, failure to thrive, diarrhea, low TRECs.
flow.subtext: Combined T- and B-cell defect.

flow.text: Severe lymphopenia.
flow.subtext: The same SCID syndrome can be caused by many different genetic defects — this question asks the mechanism of one specific cause.

flow.text: Specific labs: low ADA enzyme + high deoxyadenosine + high dATP.
flow.subtext: ADA normally converts adenosine → inosine and deoxyadenosine → deoxyinosine.

flow.text: Without ADA, both substrates accumulate.
flow.subtext: The clinically important one is deoxyadenosine.

flow.text: Deoxyadenosine is phosphorylated to dATP by deoxyadenosine kinase.
flow.subtext: Cells convert deoxyadenosine to dATP via salvage kinases.

flow.text: Normally dATP is made and used in balance.
flow.subtext: With ADA loss, the input flood overwhelms regulation.
flow.insight: Normally dATP is made and used in balance

flow.text: dATP allosterically inhibits ribonucleotide reductase (RNR).
flow.subtext: RNR converts ribonucleotides to deoxyribonucleotides — the precursors for DNA synthesis. dATP is a normal allosteric regulator of RNR

flow.text: pathologically high dATP shuts RNR down completely.
flow.insight: pathologically high dATP shuts RNR down completely

flow.text: No RNR → no dNTPs → no DNA synthesis → no cell division.
flow.subtext: Cells can't divide without making new DNA.
flow.insight: Block DNA synthesis · proliferative cells die first

flow.text: Cells that don't need to divide much (most somatic tissues) tolerate this.
flow.subtext: Cells that NEED to divide rapidly (lymphocytes) die first.
flow.insight: Cells that don't need to divide much (most somatic tissues)...

flow.text: Lymphocytes are the most proliferative immune cells → first and most affected.
flow.subtext: Clonal expansion needs massive DNA synthesis.

flow.text: T cells expand 10,000-fold after antigen exposure.
Block RNR and you kill the lymphoid lineage selectively.
flow.subtext: ADA-SCID is T-B-NK- (all three lineages fail).
flow.insight: Block RNR and you kill the lymphoid lineage selectively

flow.text: Answer: A · dATP inhibits ribonucleotide reductase → blocks DNA synthesis.
flow.subtext: Treatment options: HSCT (curative), PEG-ADA enzyme replacement, gene therapy (Strimvelis, first approved gene therapy for genetic disease).
flow.insight: Answer: A · dATP blocks RNR · lymphocytes die

flow.text: Gene therapy is the topic of the next station.
flow.insight: Gene therapy is the topic of the next station

stations.bridge: An infant with SCID has elevated lymphocyte dATP and very low ADA enzyme activity. What's the molecular mechanism that selectively kills lymphocytes?
stations.vignette: A 5-month-old girl is being evaluated for SCID after presenting with persistent oral thrush, failure to thrive, and severe diarrhea. Newborn screen showed extremely low TRECs. Laboratory studies reveal markedly decreased adenosine deaminase (ADA) enzyme activity in red blood cells, and elevated levels of deoxyadenosine and dATP in lymphocytes. Which of the following best explains why ADA deficiency selectively impairs lymphocyte development and function?

choices.text: Accumulated dATP inhibits ribonucleotide reductase, blocking DNA synthesis especially in rapidly dividing lymphocytes
ADA deficiency activates apoptosis through Fas signaling specifically in lymphocytes
ADA deficiency directly damages lymphocyte cell membranes via lipid peroxidation
ADA deficiency impairs purine de novo synthesis, depleting cellular ATP in all cells equally
Loss of ADA blocks T-cell receptor signaling at the immune synapse

keypoints: Adenosine deaminase (ADA) deficiency is one of the most molecularly satisfying primary immunodeficiencies — every step of the pathophysiology is well understood. It accounts for ~15% of all SCID cases and is autosomal recessive (unlike most other SCID forms, which are X-linked).
The biochemistry:

Why lymphocytes specifically? All cells need DNA synthesis, but lymphocytes need it fast and often. They undergo massive clonal expansion after antigen encounter (a single naive T cell can produce 10⁴+ daughters in a week). They're among the most proliferative cells in the body. Block RNR and they're the first cell type to fail.

stations.label: Station 06
stations.title: Gene therapy
stations.subtitle: How a genetically engineered virus restores a missing gene to a patient's own bone marrow cells — and why that approach has a notorious complication.

flow.text: Procedure: harvest CD34+ HSCs → transduce with retroviral ADA vector → reinfuse.
flow.subtext: Three-step ex vivo gene therapy. Patient's own cells, modified outside the body, returned to make functional immune cells.

flow.text: Retroviruses stably integrate into the host genome — that's why the cure is durable.
flow.subtext: Once integrated, the therapeutic gene is replicated with the host DNA every cell division.
flow.insight: Integration = heritable cure across daughter cells

flow.text: Every progeny cell carries the corrected gene.
flow.subtext: This is the big advantage of retroviral vectors.
flow.insight: Every progeny cell carries the corrected gene

flow.text: Problem: integration is semi-random. Retroviruses prefer active genes.
flow.subtext: The vector integrates somewhere in the genome — but not at a fixed location.
flow.insight: Random integration · prefers active gene regions

flow.text: It tends to insert near actively transcribed regions (open chromatin), which is where many proto-oncogenes also live.
flow.insight: It tends to insert near actively transcribed regions (open...

flow.text: Vector LTRs contain strong viral enhancers/promoters. If inserted near a proto-oncogene, can activate it.
flow.subtext: The retroviral long terminal repeats (LTRs) drive expression of the therapeutic gene.

flow.text: They're strong promoters — and if positioned near LMO2 or a similar proto-oncogene, they can drive its expression too.
flow.insight: They're strong promoters — and if positioned near LMO2 or a...

flow.text: Activated LMO2 → uncontrolled T-cell proliferation → T-cell leukemia.
flow.subtext: LMO2 is a transcription factor important in early T-cell development.

flow.text: Aberrant overexpression causes T-cell ALL.
flow.subtext: Several patients in early X-SCID gene therapy trials developed leukemia from this exact mechanism.

flow.text: Answer: C · Insertional mutagenesis → proto-oncogene activation → leukemia.
flow.subtext: Modern lentiviral vectors with self-inactivating LTRs and insulator elements have largely solved this. Strimvelis (2016) was the first approved ex vivo gene therapy for ADA-SCID.

stations.bridge: An ADA-SCID infant is treated with gene therapy. Walk through the procedure and explain why insertional mutagenesis was the historical safety problem.
stations.vignette: A 4-month-old girl with ADA-deficient SCID has no HLA-matched donor available for HSCT. Her medical team plans gene therapy. Autologous CD34+ hematopoietic stem cells are harvested, transduced ex vivo with a viral vector carrying the normal ADA gene, and reinfused. Which of the following best describes a major risk historically associated with retroviral vector-based gene therapy for SCID?

choices.text: Anaphylactic reaction to the vector capsid proteins
Graft-versus-host disease from the introduced gene
Insertional mutagenesis with activation of proto-oncogenes leading to leukemia
Lifelong viremia from the engineered retrovirus
Severe transfusion reactions from non-matched HSC donor cells

keypoints: SCID gene therapy is one of the great triumphs and cautionary tales of modern medicine. The basic idea is straightforward: deliver a working copy of the missing gene to the patient's own bone marrow stem cells, then transplant those corrected cells back. Done correctly, it cures the disease without needing an HLA-matched donor.
The procedure for ADA-SCID gene therapy:

Why the retroviral vector? Retroviruses (and their derivative lentiviruses) integrate stably into the host genome. Once integrated, the therapeutic gene is heritable to all daughter cells. This is what makes the cure durable — every progeny lymphocyte carries the corrected gene.

Why autologous cells? No HLA-matching needed. No GVHD risk. No need for lifelong immunosuppression. The only "rejection" risk is from the small immune response to the vector itself, which is usually manageable.

stations.label: Station 07
stations.title: X-linked carriers
stations.subtitle: Why a mother's Southern blot showing two bands at an X-linked locus identifies her as a carrier — and how the same logic identifies which sons are affected.

flow.text: X-linked gene. Wild-type allele = 4-kb fragment. Mutant allele = 6-kb fragment.
flow.subtext: The mutation alters the restriction enzyme cut sites, producing a different fragment size. Each X chromosome carries either the 4-kb or 6-kb version.

flow.text: Mom has TWO bands (4 + 6 kb). She has both alleles → heterozygous carrier.
flow.subtext: Females have two X chromosomes.

flow.text: Two different bands = one wild-type X + one mutant X.
flow.subtext: Carrier phenotype usually clinically normal due to random X-inactivation in most lymphocytes.
flow.insight: Two different bands = one wild-type X + one mutant X

flow.text: Affected proband: one band at 6 kb. He inherited the mutant X from his carrier mother.
flow.subtext: Males have only ONE X (plus Y).

flow.text: One band only — whatever single X allele they got.
flow.subtext: The 6-kb band tells you it's the mutant allele.
flow.insight: One band only — whatever single X allele they got

flow.text: Healthy brother: one band at 4 kb. He inherited the wild-type X from mom.
flow.subtext: Each son of a carrier mother has 50% chance of either X.
flow.insight: Healthy brother = single WT band, NOT carrier

flow.text: This brother got the wild-type one.
flow.subtext: He's hemizygous wild-type, unaffected, and importantly NOT a carrier (males can't be heterozygous for X-linked genes).
flow.insight: This brother got the wild-type one

flow.text: Answer: B · Mother heterozygous carrier; brother inherited wild-type X.
flow.subtext: Same logic for prenatal/sibling testing in XLA, X-SCID, WAS, hyper-IgM (CD40L), CGD. Modern testing uses PCR/sequencing but the inheritance logic is identical.
flow.insight: Answer: B · Carrier mom + WT brother

stations.bridge: A pedigree shows X-linked inheritance. Mother's Southern blot shows two bands at the locus; her affected son shows one band. What does this mean?
stations.vignette: A family is being evaluated for an X-linked primary immunodeficiency. The proband (a 2-year-old boy) had a confirmed deleterious mutation at an X-linked gene locus identified by a 6-kb restriction fragment on Southern blot, while the wild-type allele produces a 4-kb fragment. The mother's Southern blot is performed at the same locus and shows TWO bands: one at 4 kb and one at 6 kb. The proband's healthy 5-year-old brother shows only the 4-kb band. Which of the following best interprets these findings?

choices.text: The mother is homozygous wild-type; the proband is a de novo mutation
The mother is a heterozygous carrier; the brother inherited only the wild-type X
The mother is affected; both sons are affected
The mother is a heterozygous carrier; the brother is also a heterozygous carrier
The Southern blot results indicate autosomal recessive inheritance

keypoints:
Females have two X chromosomes (X^A X^A or X^A X^a or X^a X^a). They can be:
  • Homozygous wild-type (X^A X^A): shows ONE band on Southern blot — the wild-type fragment.
Heterozygous carrier (X^A X^a): shows TWO bands — one wild-type, one mutant.
Homozygous affected (X^a X^a): very rare; shows ONE band — the mutant fragment.

stations.label: Station 08
stations.title: Transfusion GVHD
stations.subtitle: Why a routine blood transfusion can kill a SCID infant — unless the blood is first irradiated to inactivate donor lymphocytes.

flow.text: Blood products contain viable donor T lymphocytes, even after standard processing.
flow.subtext: Even after standard leukoreduction, some donor T cells remain in the blood unit.
flow.insight: Donor T cells survive standard processing

flow.text: In a normal recipient, these are quickly eliminated.
flow.subtext: In a SCID recipient, they're not.

flow.text: Normal recipient: own T cells eliminate donor T cells via host-vs-graft response.
flow.subtext: HLA mismatch is recognized within hours.
flow.insight: Normal HVG response clears donor T cells

flow.text: Donor lymphocytes are killed before they can do anything.
flow.subtext: Standard transfusion is safe.
flow.insight: Donor lymphocytes are killed before they can do anything

flow.text: SCID recipient: no T cells to reject donor lymphocytes. Donor T cells engraft.
flow.subtext: The host can't recognize or reject the foreign T cells. They take up residence in the recipient and start expanding.
flow.insight: No host T cells = donor T cells engraft

flow.text: Donor T cells recognize host HLA as foreign → attack: skin, gut, liver, marrow.
flow.subtext: GVHD targets — same tissues as in HSCT GVHD.
flow.insight: TA-GVHD targets skin, gut, liver, marrow

flow.text: Skin rash, severe diarrhea, hepatitis, pancytopenia.
flow.subtext: Mortality ~90%.

flow.text: Solution: gamma irradiate blood products (25 Gy) before transfusion.
flow.subtext: Radiation damages T-cell DNA.
flow.insight: 25 Gy gamma · damages T-cell DNA · prevents proliferation

flow.text: Donor T cells can't proliferate when they encounter recipient HLA.
flow.subtext: RBCs and platelets tolerate the dose without functional loss.
flow.insight: Donor T cells can't proliferate when they encounter...

flow.text: Answer: C · Gamma irradiation prevents transfusion-associated GVHD.
flow.subtext: Required for SCID, Hodgkin lymphoma, HSCT recipients, fludarabine, intrauterine, premature infants, granulocyte transfusions, and directed donations from relatives.

stations.bridge: An infant with SCID needs a blood transfusion. Why must the blood be irradiated first, and what would happen with regular non-irradiated blood?
stations.vignette: A 2-month-old infant has been diagnosed with severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) and requires a blood transfusion for severe anemia. The blood bank is preparing the unit. Which of the following preparations is essential before the transfusion to prevent a potentially fatal complication, and what is that complication?

choices.text: Gamma irradiation to prevent transfusion-associated GVHD
Washed RBCs to prevent IgA anaphylaxis

keypoints: SCID patient has no functional T cells → can't reject donor T cells.
Donor T cells engraft in the recipient.
Donor T cells encounter recipient HLA antigens → see them as foreign → mount full GVHD response.
Targets: skin (rash), gut (diarrhea), liver (hepatitis), bone marrow (pancytopenia).
Mortality of TA-GVHD: ~90%. Often presents within 1-6 weeks of transfusion.

stations.label: Station 09
stations.title: PID overview
stations.subtitle: How to organize the primary immunodeficiency catalog by which lineage is broken — and how to choose the next test based on the infection pattern.

flow.text: PID has 5 broad categories: humoral, cellular, combined, phagocyte, complement.
flow.subtext: Each category corresponds to a different immune compartment. Each has signature infection patterns that should drive the workup.

flow.text: Case 1: encapsulated bacteria after 6 mo + low Ig + normal T → HUMORAL (XLA).
flow.subtext: Encapsulated bacteria need IgG opsonization.

flow.text: After 6 months, maternal IgG wanes.
If patient can't make their own → recurrent encapsulated infections.
flow.subtext: Pure B-cell defect.
flow.insight: If patient can't make their own → recurrent encapsulated...

flow.text: Case 2: PCP + thrush + diarrhea from BIRTH → COMBINED (SCID).
flow.subtext: Pneumocystis is T-cell territory.

flow.text: Thrush is T-cell.
Onset from birth = no maternal IgG protection helped.
flow.subtext: Combined T+B failure.
flow.insight: Onset from birth = no maternal IgG protection helped

flow.text: Case 3: S. aureus + Aspergillus + granulomas → PHAGOCYTE (CGD).
flow.subtext: Catalase-positive organisms (S. aureus, Aspergillus, Burkholderia, Serratia, Nocardia) cause infection in CGD.

flow.text: NADPH oxidase defect prevents oxidative burst.
flow.subtext: Granulomas form because phagocytes can't kill the ingested organisms.
flow.insight: NADPH oxidase defect prevents oxidative burst

flow.text: Case 4: recurrent Neisseria meningitis → COMPLEMENT (terminal C5-C9).
flow.subtext: Neisseria is uniquely vulnerable to MAC (membrane attack complex).

flow.text: Terminal complement deficiency (C5-C9) prevents MAC formation → recurrent Neisseria infections.
flow.subtext: Almost pathognomonic.

flow.text: Other answer choices match WRONG cases to categories. Only A is correct.
flow.subtext: B/C/D/E all swap categories around. The pattern recognition tells you the right pairing.

flow.text: Answer: A · humoral, combined, phagocyte, complement.
flow.subtext: Pattern recognition is the start of every PID workup. Confirm with quantitative tests: Ig levels, lymphocyte subsets, DHR/NBT, CH50.

flow.text: Chapter 10 closes. 9 stations · 10+ source questions · PID catalog complete.
flow.subtext: IgA deficiency, XLA, DiGeorge, Wiskott-Aldrich, ADA-SCID, gene therapy, X-linked Southern blot, irradiated transfusion, comprehensive PID map.

flow.text: Chapter 11 next: hypersensitivity reactions.

stations.bridge: Match the infection pattern to the most likely PID category. This is how clinicians actually start the workup.
stations.vignette:
You are evaluating four pediatric patients with suspected primary immunodeficiency. Match each clinical scenario to the most likely category of immune defect:

(1) 8-month-old boy with recurrent pneumococcal pneumonia and otitis since 6 months of age, normal T cells, all immunoglobulins low.
(2) 4-month-old with Pneumocystis pneumonia, persistent thrush, and chronic diarrhea since birth.
(3) 5-year-old with recurrent skin abscesses (Staphylococcus, Aspergillus), liver granulomas.
(4) 22-year-old with three episodes of meningococcal meningitis.

Which of the following correctly matches all four cases?
keypoints: Examples: XLA (Station 02), CVID, selective IgA deficiency (Station 01), hyper-IgM (Ch 8 Station 07).
Hallmark infections: Encapsulated bacteria (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, N. meningitidis), starting AFTER 6 months when maternal IgG wanes.
Key tests: Quantitative IgG/IgM/IgA, B-cell numbers (CD19/CD20), specific antibody responses, SPEP.
Treatment: IVIG replacement, prophylactic antibiotics.

chapters.title: Hypersensitivity Reactions
chapters.subtitle: Type I through IV — when the same machinery that protects starts attacking the wrong target.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: Hypersensitivity types
stations.subtitle: How four hypersensitivity types divide along two axes — antibody vs T-cell, and which immune effector does the damage.

flow.text: Coombs-Gell classification: 4 types based on mechanism (antibody type, target, timing).
flow.subtext: Same B cells, T cells, antibodies, complement we built across Ch 5-10 — now in pathological mode.

flow.text: Type I = IgE on mast cell crosslinked by allergen → immediate degranulation.
flow.subtext: Pre-existing IgE bound to mast cell FcεRI.

flow.text: Allergen crosslinks two adjacent IgE → mast cell releases histamine within seconds.
flow.subtext: Anaphylaxis develops within minutes.
flow.insight: Allergen crosslinks two adjacent IgE → mast cell releases...

flow.text: Peanut + urticaria + wheezing + hypotension in MINUTES = classic Type I.
flow.subtext: All three features result from histamine + tryptase + leukotrienes: urticaria (cutaneous mast cells), bronchospasm (airway), hypotension (vasodilation, capillary leak).
flow.insight: Multi-system reaction in minutes = Type I

flow.text: Answer: A · Type I (IgE-mediated immediate hypersensitivity).
flow.subtext: Stations 02-03 detail Type I scenarios. Then we'll walk through Type II (Station 04), Type III (Stations 05-06), Type IV (Stations 07-09).
flow.insight: Answer: A · Type I · sets up entire chapter

stations.bridge: Five clinical scenarios. Each one matches a different hypersensitivity type. Pick the right type for the patient with sudden urticaria after a peanut.
stations.vignette:
Five patients present to clinic over the course of one day:

(1) 22-year-old develops sudden urticaria, wheezing, and hypotension within minutes of eating peanuts.
(2) 28-week pregnant Rh-negative mother delivers her second Rh-positive child; the newborn has severe hemolytic anemia.
(3) 32-year-old woman with SLE develops glomerulonephritis with sub-endothelial immune deposits on biopsy.
(4) 35-year-old gardener develops itchy, weeping vesicles on his arms 48 hours after pulling weeds.
(5) 5-year-old boy develops palpable purpura on his legs and abdominal pain after a recent URI; biopsy shows IgA-dominant immune complexes in dermal vessels.

The patient with peanut allergy (case 1) represents which type of hypersensitivity?
keypoints: Mechanism: allergen-specific IgE bound to mast cell/basophil FcεRI receptors. Re-exposure crosslinks IgE → degranulation.
Mediators: histamine (immediate), then leukotrienes/prostaglandins/cytokines (late phase).
Timing: minutes for immediate phase, 2-12 hours for late phase.
Clinical: urticaria, anaphylaxis, allergic rhinitis, asthma, food allergies, atopic dermatitis.

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: Type I anaphylaxis
stations.subtitle: Why eating one bite of shrimp causes a 30-year-old to break out in hives within 15 minutes — and which mediator dominates the picture.

flow.text: 30yo woman + shrimp + hives within 15 min + prior shellfish reactions = Type I, IgE-mediated.
flow.subtext: Pre-existing sensitization (prior reactions) tells us IgE is already armed on mast cells. New exposure crosslinks it.

flow.text: Allergen (shrimp tropomyosin) crosslinks IgE on mast cell FcεRI.
flow.subtext: FcεRI is the high-affinity IgE receptor on mast cells and basophils.
flow.insight: Allergen crosslinks adjacent IgE on FcεRI

flow.text: IgE binds with very high affinity and stays bound for weeks.
flow.subtext: Crosslinking by multivalent allergen triggers degranulation.
flow.insight: IgE binds with very high affinity and stays bound for weeks

flow.text: Mast cell releases pre-formed histamine within seconds.
flow.subtext: Granules pre-loaded with histamine, tryptase, heparin. Crosslinking → calcium influx → granule fusion with membrane → mediators dumped into surrounding tissue.

flow.text: Histamine → vasodilation + ↑ capillary permeability + itch = wheal.
flow.subtext: H1 receptors on endothelium: vasodilation (erythema) and increased permeability (raised wheal from leaked plasma). H1 on sensory nerves: itch.
flow.insight: H1 receptor effects = wheal + flare + itch

flow.text: Answer: C · Histamine from mast cell degranulation.
flow.subtext: Treatment: H1 antihistamine for urticaria; epinephrine + H1+H2 + steroids for anaphylaxis. Same mechanism: mast cell histamine, just different magnitude.

stations.bridge: Itchy raised wheals appearing within 15 minutes of shrimp. Which mediator is the dominant cause?
stations.vignette: A 30-year-old woman develops itchy, raised wheals on her trunk and arms within 15 minutes of eating shrimp at a restaurant. She has had similar reactions to shellfish in the past. Vital signs are stable; she has no respiratory distress or hypotension. Which of the following mediators is most directly responsible for the urticarial wheals?

choices.text: Histamine released by mast cell degranulation

keypoints: Acute urticaria after a known food allergen is the prototypical Type I hypersensitivity reaction. The patient is already sensitized — meaning her B cells have previously seen shrimp antigen, made shrimp-specific IgE, and the IgE is now sitting on her mast cells via FcεRI receptors.
The molecular sequence:

For acute urticaria specifically, histamine is the dominant mediator. It binds H1 receptors on endothelial cells (vasodilation, permeability) and sensory nerves (itch). That's why H1 antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine, diphenhydramine) are first-line therapy for urticaria.

Late phase reaction: 2-12 hours after the immediate IgE response, eosinophils, basophils, and Th2 cells infiltrate. This is mediated by mast-cell-derived cytokines (IL-4, IL-5, TNF) and lipid mediators. Important in chronic asthma and atopic dermatitis but not the primary mediator of acute urticaria.

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: Late-phase asthma
stations.subtitle: Why an asthma attack has both an immediate IgE/histamine phase and a late eosinophil/cytokine phase — and what each phase means for treatment.

flow.text: Patient bronchodilates with albuterol = early phase mast cell mediators were primary.
flow.subtext: Albuterol (β2 agonist) reverses bronchospasm.
flow.insight: Albuterol responsive = early phase mast cell

flow.text: The fact that it WORKED tells you the immediate problem was smooth muscle contraction from histamine/leukotrienes — early phase Type I.
flow.insight: The fact that it WORKED tells you the immediate problem was...

flow.text: 6 hours later, symptoms recur with MUCUS = late phase.
flow.subtext: Late phase reaction occurs 4-12 hours after initial trigger.

flow.text: The mast cell that triggered the early phase ALSO released cytokines (IL-4, IL-5, TNF) that act over hours to recruit and activate other cells.
flow.insight: The mast cell that triggered the early phase ALSO released...

flow.text: Late phase: eosinophils recruited by IL-5 dominate the airways.
flow.subtext: IL-5 from mast cells (and Th2 cells) is the master eosinophil-recruiting cytokine. Eosinophils infiltrate the bronchial mucosa within hours.

flow.text: Eosinophils + macrophages secrete IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α → sustained inflammation.
flow.subtext: These pro-inflammatory cytokines maintain the inflammatory state. Eosinophils also release MBP and ECP (epithelial toxins) that damage airway lining over time.
flow.insight: IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α + MBP/ECP epithelial damage

flow.text: Answer: D · Macrophages + eosinophils · IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α.
flow.subtext: Same biology in allergic rhinitis (Q139/Q198).

flow.text: Treatment: ICS for asthma late phase, intranasal steroids for rhinitis.
flow.subtext: Anti-IL-5 (mepolizumab) for severe eosinophilic asthma.
flow.insight: Treatment: ICS for asthma late phase

stations.bridge: Asthmatic patient bronchodilates after albuterol but symptoms recur 6 hours later. Which cells dominate the late phase, and which cytokines do they secrete?
stations.vignette: A 28-year-old man with allergic asthma develops wheezing, chest tightness, and dyspnea within minutes of being exposed to cat dander. He uses an albuterol inhaler with rapid relief. Six hours later, however, his wheezing returns and is now associated with increased mucus production. Bronchoalveolar lavage at this time would most likely show a predominance of which cell type, secreting which cytokines?

choices.text: CD8 cytotoxic T cells secreting perforin
Macrophages and eosinophils secreting IL-1, IL-6, and TNF-α

keypoints: Allergen (cat dander, dust mite, pollen) crosslinks IgE on mast cells in airway mucosa.
Mast cell degranulation releases pre-formed histamine, tryptase, then synthesizes leukotrienes (LTC4/D4/E4) and prostaglandin D2.
Effects: bronchoconstriction, mucus hypersecretion, vascular leak.
Clinical: wheezing, dyspnea, chest tightness — classic acute asthma attack.
Treatment: short-acting β2 agonist (albuterol) reverses bronchospasm.

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: Type II HDFN
stations.subtitle: Why an Rh-negative mother who didn't get RhoGAM after her first pregnancy delivers a second baby with severe hemolytic anemia — and which antibody isotype does the damage.

flow.text: Pregnancy 1: fetal Rh+ RBCs leak into Rh-neg mom at delivery.
flow.subtext: Feto-maternal hemorrhage at delivery is essentially universal. Without RhoGAM (Ch 9 Station 05), mom's immune system encounters Rh-D antigen for the first time.

flow.text: Mom's B cells make anti-D antibody → class switch to IgG → memory.
flow.subtext: Initial IgM, then T-helper-driven class switch to IgG over weeks.

flow.text: Memory B cells persist for life.
flow.subtext: Mom is now sensitized.
flow.insight: Memory B cells persist for life

flow.text: Pregnancy 2 (Rh+): maternal anti-D IgG crosses placenta via FcRn.
flow.subtext: From Ch 8 Station 04: IgG is the only isotype actively transported across placenta via the FcRn (neonatal Fc receptor). Anti-D IgG accumulates in fetal circulation.

flow.text: Anti-D IgG binds Rh-D antigen on every fetal Rh+ RBC.
flow.subtext: Each fetal RBC gets coated with maternal IgG. The Fc tails point outward, ready to engage Fc receptors on macrophages.
flow.insight: Every fetal Rh+ RBC opsonized by IgG

flow.text: Fetal splenic macrophages recognize IgG Fc → phagocytose opsonized RBCs.
flow.subtext: Macrophages express FcγRI, FcγRIIA, FcγRIII.

flow.text: They bind the IgG Fc tails coating the RBCs and engulf the cells.
flow.subtext: Extravascular hemolysis in the spleen.
flow.insight: They bind the IgG Fc tails coating the RBCs and engulf the...

flow.text: Clinical: fetal anemia → hydrops fetalis + postnatal hyperbilirubinemia → kernicterus risk.
flow.subtext: Severe anemia → high-output heart failure → hydrops. Massive RBC breakdown → unconjugated bilirubin → can cross blood-brain barrier in newborn → kernicterus.

flow.text: Answer: C · Maternal anti-D IgG lysing Rh+ fetal RBCs (Type II).
flow.subtext: Same Type II mechanism: AIHA, ITP, Goodpasture, MG, pemphigus, hyperacute rejection. Prevention of HDFN: RhoGAM (Ch 9 Station 05).
flow.insight: Answer: C · maternal anti-D IgG · Type II

stations.bridge: Rh-negative mom didn't receive RhoGAM after first delivery. Second baby has severe hemolytic anemia. Which mechanism and which antibody class?
stations.vignette: An Rh-negative woman delivered her first Rh-positive child without complications and did not receive RhoGAM postpartum. She is now pregnant again with a second Rh-positive fetus. Late in the pregnancy, fetal ultrasound shows hydrops fetalis with severe anemia. After delivery, the newborn has marked hyperbilirubinemia and a positive direct Coombs (DAT) test. Which of the following best describes the immunologic mechanism of the fetal hemolysis?

choices.text: Fetal IgM antibodies attacking maternal RBCs
IgE crosslinking on fetal mast cells
Maternal anti-D IgG crossing placenta to lyse Rh+ fetal RBCs
Maternal immune complexes in fetal kidney
Maternal T cells crossing placenta directly

keypoints: Hemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN) from Rh incompatibility is the prototypical Type II hypersensitivity reaction. The mechanism is direct: IgG antibodies against a cell-surface antigen (Rh-D) bind to that antigen on the surface of target cells (fetal RBCs), and the cells are then destroyed via Fc-receptor-mediated phagocytosis (and to a lesser extent complement).
The two-pregnancy sequence:

Why intravenous vs extravascular hemolysis matters: Anti-D IgG primarily causes EXTRAVASCULAR hemolysis (splenic Fc-mediated). Anti-ABO IgM (acute transfusion reaction) causes INTRAVASCULAR hemolysis (complement MAC). Different antibody class, different effector, different clinical syndrome.

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: Type III SLE
stations.subtitle: Why anti-DNA antibodies in SLE don't directly attack the kidney — and yet kidney disease is a leading cause of morbidity in lupus.

flow.text: SLE patient makes anti-dsDNA antibodies. Apoptotic cells release DNA into circulation.
flow.subtext: Nuclear autoantibodies are the SLE hallmark.

flow.text: Anti-dsDNA is highly specific.
flow.subtext: Self-DNA from dying cells is the antigen they bind.

flow.text: Antibody + antigen form circulating immune complexes.
flow.subtext: Anti-dsDNA + DNA bind in circulation.
flow.insight: ag-ab complexes circulate, get trapped in vessels

flow.text: Multivalent binding makes lattice-like complexes.
flow.subtext: They circulate until they get filtered through small vessels.

flow.text: Complexes deposit in glomerular sub-endothelium ("wire loops").
flow.subtext: Glomerular capillaries are a high-pressure filtration site — favored deposition location. Sub-endothelial position (under endothelium, on BM side) gives the wire-loop appearance.

flow.text: Deposits activate complement classical pathway → C3/C4 consumed.
flow.subtext: C1q binds the IgG Fc.

flow.text: Cascade activates.
flow.subtext: C3 and C4 cleaved and consumed at deposition sites.

flow.text: Result: low serum C3 and C4.
flow.subtext: This consumption is one of the most useful disease activity markers.
flow.insight: Result: low serum C3 and C4

flow.text: C3a, C5a recruit neutrophils → enzymes/ROS → glomerular damage.
flow.subtext: Anaphylatoxins drive neutrophil chemotaxis.

flow.text: Neutrophils release lytic enzymes, ROS.
flow.subtext: Damage glomerular cells → proliferative GN, proteinuria, hematuria.

flow.text: IF shows GRANULAR IgG + C3 deposition. (Compare: LINEAR in Goodpasture.)
flow.subtext: Granular pattern reflects deposited immune complexes — discrete clumps along the GBM.

flow.text: Linear pattern in Goodpasture syndrome reflects antibody bound directly along a uniformly distributed antigen (collagen IV).
flow.insight: Linear pattern in Goodpasture syndrome reflects antibody...

flow.text: Answer: B · Anti-dsDNA immune complex deposition (Type III hypersensitivity).
flow.subtext: Treatment: steroids + mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide.
flow.insight: Answer: B · SLE nephritis = Type III IC

flow.text: Rituximab/belimumab for refractory.
flow.subtext: Monitor C3/C4 + anti-dsDNA for activity.

stations.bridge: SLE patient with active glomerulonephritis. Sub-endothelial immune deposits on kidney biopsy. What's the mechanism, and what serum finding supports it?
stations.vignette: A 32-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus develops worsening proteinuria and active urinary sediment. Renal biopsy shows endocapillary proliferative glomerulonephritis with prominent sub-endothelial immune deposits ("wire loops"). Immunofluorescence shows granular IgG and C3 deposition along the glomerular basement membrane. Serum studies show low C3 and C4 with elevated anti-dsDNA antibody titers. Which of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiologic mechanism?

choices.text: Anti-GBM antibodies binding GBM directly (Type II)

keypoints: Lupus nephritis is the prototypical Type III hypersensitivity disease. The damage isn't from antibodies attacking the kidney directly. It's from antibody-antigen COMPLEXES forming in circulation, depositing in the glomerulus, and triggering local complement activation and neutrophil recruitment.
The full mechanism:

Why low complement is so useful: Type III disease consumes complement (C3, C4) at the deposition sites. Low serum C3/C4 in a lupus patient with active disease is one of the most useful real-time markers of immunologic activity. Treatment that controls disease (steroids, mycophenolate, rituximab) restores complement levels.

stations.label: Station 06
stations.title: IgA vasculitis
stations.subtitle: Why a child with palpable purpura on the legs, abdominal pain, joint pain, and microscopic hematuria has IgA depositing in his small vessels — and how this differs from SLE.

flow.text: Pediatric patient + palpable purpura + abdominal pain + arthritis + post-URI = HSP tetrad.
flow.subtext: PAAN: Purpura, Arthralgia, Abdominal pain, Nephritis.

flow.text: Most common vasculitis of childhood.
flow.subtext: Often follows URI by 1-2 weeks.

flow.text: Skin biopsy: IgA-dominant immune complex deposition + leukocytoclastic vasculitis.
flow.subtext: Direct IF shows IgA primarily (with C3, sometimes IgG).

flow.text: Leukocytoclasis = neutrophil debris from vasculitis.
flow.subtext: The IgA dominance is what defines this disease.

flow.text: Mechanism: same Type III as SLE — but with IgA.
flow.subtext: Circulating IgA-antigen complexes deposit in small vessels.
flow.insight: Type III · IgA instead of IgG · alternative complement

flow.text: Activate complement (alternative pathway, since IgA doesn't activate classical).
Recruit neutrophils.
flow.subtext: Damage vessel wall.

flow.text: Answer: C · Henoch-Schönlein purpura (IgA vasculitis).
flow.subtext: Treatment: usually supportive (self-limited).

flow.text: Steroids for severe abdominal pain or significant renal involvement.
flow.subtext: Monitor renal function long-term.
flow.insight: Steroids for severe abdominal pain or significant renal...

stations.bridge: 5-year-old with palpable purpura, abdominal pain, and hematuria after URI. Skin biopsy shows IgA-dominant immune complexes. What's the diagnosis?
stations.vignette: A 5-year-old boy presents with a 3-day history of palpable purpura on his lower extremities and buttocks, accompanied by abdominal pain and ankle arthralgias. He had an upper respiratory infection 2 weeks earlier. Urinalysis shows microscopic hematuria with red cell casts. Skin biopsy demonstrates leukocytoclastic vasculitis of small dermal vessels with IgA-dominant immune complex deposition on direct immunofluorescence. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
keypoints: Palpable purpura on lower extremities and buttocks (small vessel skin vasculitis with IgA deposits).
Arthralgia/arthritis — often ankles and knees.
Abdominal pain — from gut wall vasculitis. Intussusception is a feared complication.
Nephritis — IgA nephropathy with hematuria, occasionally progressive renal disease.

stations.label: Station 07
stations.title: Type IV contact
stations.subtitle: Why poison ivy doesn't itch the FIRST time you touch it — and why the rash takes 48 hours to appear after every subsequent exposure.

flow.text: First exposure to urushiol = silent sensitization. No symptoms.
flow.subtext: Initial poison ivy contact in childhood or earlier.

flow.text: Urushiol penetrates skin, haptenizes self-protein, primes Th1 CD4 cells.
flow.subtext: Patient has no symptoms but is now sensitized.

flow.text: Re-exposure: urushiol haptenizes skin proteins → MHC II presentation.
flow.subtext: Same hapten-carrier mechanism. Skin APCs (Langerhans cells) now process and present to MEMORY T cells, which respond rapidly compared to first encounter.
flow.insight: Hapten + self protein → MHC II presentation

flow.text: Memory Th1 CD4 cells recognize the urushiol-modified peptide and respond.
flow.subtext: Memory T cells are already in or near the skin.

flow.text: They engage their cognate antigen quickly.
flow.subtext: No need for the slow naive-cell priming step.
flow.insight: They engage their cognate antigen quickly

flow.text: Th1 cells release IFN-γ → activates macrophages.
flow.subtext: IFN-γ is the signature Th1 cytokine. It binds macrophage IFN-γR → upregulates antigen processing, MHC II, oxidative burst, antimicrobial activity.

flow.text: Activated macrophages release IL-1, TNF, chemokines → recruit more cells, damage skin.
flow.subtext: Activated macrophages amplify the response.

flow.text: They recruit more T cells and macrophages.
flow.insight: They recruit more T cells and macrophages

flow.text: They release ROS and lytic enzymes.
flow.subtext: Visible inflammation develops over hours.
flow.insight: They release ROS and lytic enzymes

flow.text: Result at 48h: vesicular, weeping dermatitis at contact site.
flow.subtext: Spongiotic dermatitis on histology.

flow.text: Linear streaks because of how the leaf brushed the skin.
flow.subtext: Resolves in 1-3 weeks; treatment is topical/systemic steroids.
flow.insight: Linear streaks because of how the leaf brushed the skin

flow.text: Answer: D · CD4 T cells + macrophages + IFN-γ.
flow.subtext: Same mechanism: nickel allergy, latex (some forms), topical antibiotics, fragrances. The 48-hour delay is the diagnostic feature.

stations.bridge: Gardener with vesicular dermatitis 48 hours after pulling weeds. He's encountered poison ivy before. What's the immunologic mechanism, and what cells dominate the skin biopsy?
stations.vignette: A 35-year-old gardener develops itchy, weeping vesicles on his arms 48 hours after pulling weeds in his yard. The pattern of the rash is linear in some areas, suggesting plant contact. He has had similar reactions in the past after exposure to poison ivy. Skin biopsy at the affected area would most likely show which of the following infiltrates and dominant cytokines?

choices.text: Perivascular CD4+ T cells and macrophages with IFN-γ
Plasma cell infiltrate with IgG immune complexes

keypoints: Allergic contact dermatitis (poison ivy is the prototype) is the most accessible Type IV hypersensitivity teaching example. Two phases must occur, separated by enough time for memory T cells to develop:
Phase 1 — Sensitization (first exposure, no symptoms):

The 48-hour delay is what defines "delayed" hypersensitivity. T cells need time to traffic to the affected skin, recognize antigen, and recruit/activate enough macrophages to produce visible inflammation.

Why the rash often shows linear streaks: the leaf or stem of the plant brushes the skin in a line as the patient moves through brush. Urushiol is deposited along that line. The resulting dermatitis follows the contact pattern.

stations.label: Station 08
stations.title: PPD reaction
stations.subtitle: Why a patient with prior TB exposure develops 18 mm of induration 48 hours after a tiny intradermal injection — and what cells produce that bump.

flow.text: Intradermal PPD = cocktail of mycobacterial antigens injected into dermis.
flow.subtext: PPD is a precipitated extract of culture-grown M. tuberculosis.

flow.text: Contains many bacterial proteins.
flow.subtext: Provides antigens for memory Th1 cells to recognize.

flow.text: If patient has been previously sensitized (TB infection or BCG), memory Th1 cells exist.
flow.subtext: Prior infection with M. tuberculosis — even latent — primed Th1 CD4 cells specific for mycobacterial antigens.

flow.text: Same for BCG vaccine.
flow.subtext: Memory T cells persist for years.

flow.text: Memory Th1 cells recognize PPD antigens on local APC MHC II → release IFN-γ.
flow.subtext: Same mechanism as poison ivy elicitation phase.

flow.text: Memory Th1 cells engage cognate antigen rapidly.
flow.subtext: IFN-γ is the signature Th1 cytokine.
flow.insight: Memory Th1 cells engage cognate antigen rapidly

flow.text: IFN-γ activates and recruits macrophages to the injection site.
flow.subtext: IFN-γ is the master macrophage activator.

flow.text: Macrophages upregulate MHC II, antigen processing, ROS production, lysosomal enzymes.
flow.subtext: They also recruit more T cells and macrophages.

flow.text: Accumulated T cells + macrophages = palpable INDURATION at 48-72h.
flow.subtext: Induration (firm bump) reflects cellular infiltration, not edema.

flow.text: Erythema is variable; only induration is measured.
flow.subtext: Diameter is measured perpendicular to the long axis of the forearm.

flow.text: 18 mm in healthcare worker = positive (≥10 mm cutoff).
flow.subtext: Cutoffs depend on patient risk.

flow.text: Healthcare workers use ≥10 mm.
flow.subtext: Positive = prior exposure (or BCG); requires further evaluation for active vs latent TB.

flow.text: Answer: C · CD4 Th1 cells + IFN-γ + activated macrophages (DTH).
flow.subtext: PPD = same biology as poison ivy, used diagnostically. IGRA blood test uses the same Th1/IFN-γ axis but in vitro with TB-specific antigens (no BCG cross-reactivity).
flow.insight: Answer: C · DTH · Th1 + IFN-γ + macrophage

stations.bridge: Patient gets PPD placed. Returns at 48 hours with 18 mm of induration. Which cells dominate the lesion, and what's the immunologic interpretation?
stations.vignette: A 45-year-old healthcare worker undergoes routine annual tuberculin skin testing. She received the standard intradermal injection of purified protein derivative (PPD) on her forearm. She returns 48 hours later, and the area shows 18 mm of induration. Skin biopsy at the site of induration would most likely show which of the following predominant cellular infiltrates and dominant cytokine?

choices.text: B cells producing anti-mycobacterial IgG locally
Eosinophils with IL-5 driving allergic inflammation
Macrophages activated by IFN-γ from CD4 Th1 cells (delayed-type hypersensitivity)
Mast cell degranulation with histamine release
Neutrophilic abscess formation around mycobacterial fragments

keypoints: The PPD (purified protein derivative, tuberculin) skin test is the textbook example of a Type IV delayed-type hypersensitivity (DTH) reaction used as a DIAGNOSTIC tool. It works because the same Th1/IFN-γ/macrophage axis that builds protective immunity against M. tuberculosis also produces a measurable skin reaction when the patient is re-challenged with mycobacterial antigens.
The procedure:
This patient (healthcare worker, 18 mm) is positive (≥10 mm cutoff applies), indicating prior TB exposure or BCG vaccination.

Why immunocompromised patients can have false-negative PPD (anergy): The PPD requires functional T-cell-mediated immunity to read out. Severely immunocompromised patients (advanced HIV, malnutrition, steroid use, chemotherapy) may not mount a measurable Th1 response even if they have active TB. This is why HIV-positive patients use a lower cutoff (5 mm) and may need additional testing (sputum culture, IGRA).

stations.label: Station 09
stations.title: Sarcoidosis
stations.subtitle: Why a young woman's hilar lymph nodes contain non-caseating granulomas full of CD4 T cells and macrophages — and how this is just Type IV in slow motion.

flow.text: Bilateral hilar adenopathy + erythema nodosum + non-caseating granulomas = sarcoidosis.
flow.subtext: Classic Löfgren syndrome variant. Multi-system granulomatous disease with female predominance, more common in African-Americans and Northern Europeans.

flow.text: Granuloma = activated macrophages + CD4 T cells in organized architecture.
flow.subtext: Center: epithelioid macrophages and giant cells.

flow.text: Rim: CD4 Th1 cells.
flow.subtext: The same Th1/macrophage axis from poison ivy and PPD, but chronic and 3-dimensional.

flow.text: CD4 Th1 cells release IFN-γ + TNF-α. Same cytokines as poison ivy, sustained over weeks.
flow.subtext: Persistent antigen presentation drives chronic Th1 activation. IFN-γ and TNF-α maintain macrophage activation indefinitely.

flow.text: Macrophages transform into epithelioid cells + multinucleated giant cells.
flow.subtext: Continuously activated macrophages adopt epithelial-like morphology (elongated, eosinophilic). TNF-α drives fusion of multiple macrophages into giant cells with multiple nuclei.
flow.insight: Mφ → epithelioid + giant cells under chronic Th1

flow.text: Sarcoid granulomas don't caseate (compared to TB granulomas which do).
flow.subtext: Caseation = central necrosis.

flow.text: TB granulomas have central caseation because the intracellular bacteria persist and damage cells.
flow.subtext: Sarcoid granulomas don't caseate because there's no persistent intracellular pathogen.
flow.insight: TB granulomas have central caseation because the...

flow.text: Sarcoid hypercalcemia: granuloma macrophages have 1α-hydroxylase → ↑ active vit D.
flow.subtext: Activated macrophages express 1α-hydroxylase, converting 25-OH vit D to 1,25-(OH)2 vit D (active form).

flow.text: Increased calcium absorption → hypercalcemia.
flow.subtext: Distinct from PTH-driven hypercalcemia.

flow.text: Answer: D · Type IV chronic CD4 Th1 with granuloma formation.
flow.subtext: Treatment: corticosteroids first-line for symptomatic disease. Methotrexate, anti-TNF (infliximab) for refractory cases — anti-TNF works because TNF maintains the granuloma.

flow.text: Chapter 11 closes. 9 stations · 13+ source questions · hypersensitivity catalog complete.
flow.subtext: Type I urticaria, Type I asthma, Type II Rh hemolysis, Type III SLE, Type III HSP, Type IV poison ivy, Type IV PPD, Type IV sarcoid. Chapter 12 next: autoimmune disease.

stations.bridge: Young woman with hilar adenopathy and erythema nodosum. Lymph node biopsy shows non-caseating granulomas. What's the immunologic mechanism, and which cells dominate the granuloma?
stations.vignette: A 32-year-old African-American woman presents with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on chest X-ray, mild dyspnea, and tender erythematous nodules on her shins (erythema nodosum). Mediastinal lymph node biopsy shows multiple well-formed non-caseating granulomas composed of epithelioid macrophages, multinucleated giant cells, and surrounding CD4-positive T lymphocytes. AFB stain and fungal stains are negative. Which of the following best describes the underlying immunologic mechanism?

choices.text: Type I IgE-mediated mast cell activation in the lung interstitium
Type II antibody-mediated cytotoxicity against pulmonary epithelium
Type III immune complex deposition in alveolar capillaries
Type IV CD4 Th1-mediated chronic delayed-type hypersensitivity with granuloma formation
Type V stimulatory antibody activating macrophages directly

keypoints: Center: Activated macrophages transformed into epithelioid cells (elongated, eosinophilic cytoplasm, resembling epithelial cells) and multinucleated giant cells (formed by macrophage fusion).
Rim: CD4 Th1 lymphocytes (sometimes with smaller numbers of CD8 cells) surround the macrophage core.
Sarcoid specifically: No central caseation (necrosis). This is the "non-caseating" descriptor that distinguishes sarcoid from TB granulomas (which are typically caseating).

chapters.title: Autoimmune Disease
chapters.subtitle: When self-tolerance breaks down — the autoantibodies, the affected organs, the diagnostic patterns.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: Autoantibody panel
stations.subtitle: How most autoimmune diseases can be sorted by which autoantibody they make — and how that single piece of information predicts the syndrome.

flow.text: Autoantibodies are diagnostic fingerprints for autoimmune diseases.
flow.subtext: Most autoimmune diseases produce one or more characteristic autoantibodies. Knowing the catalog lets you move from vague presentation to concrete diagnosis.

flow.text: Sensitivity vs specificity — different antibodies serve different roles.
flow.subtext: ANA: 95% sensitive but not specific (also in healthy people).

flow.text: Anti-Sm: only 30% sensitive but 99% specific.
flow.subtext: Use sensitive tests to screen, specific tests to confirm.

flow.text: For SLE: ANA screen → anti-dsDNA + anti-Sm confirm.
flow.subtext: Standard workup.

flow.text: ANA negative essentially rules out SLE.
flow.subtext: Positive ANA → check anti-dsDNA (95% specific, tracks activity) and anti-Sm (99% specific).
flow.insight: ANA negative essentially rules out SLE

flow.text: Question asks MOST SPECIFIC for SLE → anti-Sm.
flow.subtext: Anti-Sm has the highest specificity for SLE (~99%). Only positive in ~30% of SLE patients but when positive, very strongly supports the diagnosis.

flow.text: Answer: C · Anti-Sm (most specific for SLE).
flow.subtext: Stations 02-09 walk through the major autoimmune diseases by autoantibody/syndrome combination. We start with the SLE flagship next.
flow.insight: Answer: C · anti-Sm · sets up entire chapter

stations.bridge: Five patients with five different autoantibody patterns. Match the SLE patient — what's the most specific antibody for SLE diagnosis?
stations.vignette:
Five patients are evaluated for autoimmune disease. The serologic findings are:

(1) ANA positive, anti-dsDNA positive, anti-Smith positive, low C3/C4.
(2) Anti-CCP positive, RF positive.
(3) c-ANCA (anti-PR3) positive.
(4) Anti-acetylcholine receptor antibodies positive.
(5) Anti-TSH receptor antibodies positive.

Which of the following autoantibodies is the most specific (though not the most sensitive) for systemic lupus erythematosus?
keypoints: ANA: ~95% sensitive for SLE, but ~5% of healthy people are also positive. Good SCREEN — if negative, SLE is unlikely.
Anti-Sm: Only ~30% sensitive for SLE, but ~99% specific. Good CONFIRM — if positive, SLE is very likely.
Anti-dsDNA: ~70% sensitive, ~95% specific. Also tracks with disease activity (especially renal).

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: SLE
stations.subtitle: Why a 28-year-old woman with butterfly rash, joint pain, oral ulcers, and proteinuria has SLE — and which test follows her disease activity over time.

flow.text: SLE patient with worsening proteinuria + new malar rash + low C3/C4 = active flare.
flow.subtext: Multi-system flare with renal involvement. The clinical worsening is mirrored by serologic activity markers.

flow.text: Anti-dsDNA titer rises in flares, falls with treatment.
flow.subtext: Anti-dsDNA is the autoantibody that produces the immune complexes depositing in glomeruli. More active disease = more complexes = higher titer + more complement consumption.

flow.text: Especially correlates with RENAL disease activity.
flow.subtext: Lupus nephritis is the most consistent target for anti-dsDNA tracking. Other manifestations (arthritis, rash) less reliably tracked by anti-dsDNA alone.

flow.text: C3/C4 fall during flares (consumed at immune complex deposits).
flow.subtext: Complement is consumed when classical pathway is activated by immune complexes. Low C3/C4 = active complement consumption = active disease.

flow.text: Answer: B · Anti-dsDNA — correlates with SLE flares and renal disease.
flow.subtext: Combine with C3/C4 for the standard activity panel. Track anti-dsDNA + C3/C4 + creatinine + urine protein to monitor lupus nephritis treatment response.
flow.insight: Answer: B · anti-dsDNA tracks disease activity

stations.bridge: Lupus patient develops worsening renal disease. Which serologic finding tracks her renal activity?
stations.vignette: A 28-year-old woman with previously well-controlled SLE develops worsening proteinuria, hypertension, and a new malar rash. Her serologic studies show ANA positive, anti-dsDNA antibody titer markedly elevated compared to baseline, and decreased C3 and C4 complement levels. Renal biopsy confirms active lupus nephritis. Which of the following is the most useful laboratory test for monitoring her renal disease activity over time?

choices.text: Anti-Sm — specific but not activity tracker

keypoints: Serositis (pleuritis, pericarditis)
Oral ulcers (often painless)
Arthritis (non-erosive, distinguishes from RA)
Photosensitivity
Blood disorders (hemolytic anemia, leukopenia, lymphopenia, thrombocytopenia)
Renal disease (lupus nephritis — six WHO classes)
ANA positive
Immunologic disorders (anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm, anti-phospholipid)
Neurologic (seizures, psychosis)
Malar rash (butterfly)
Discoid rash

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: Scleroderma
stations.subtitle: Why a 45-year-old woman's hands have become tight, shiny, and clawed — and which cytokine her fibroblasts are over-responding to.

flow.text: Skin thickening on hands/face + Raynaud + esophageal dysmotility = scleroderma.
flow.subtext: Triad of skin + Raynaud + esophageal involvement defines the disease. Two subtypes share these features but differ in distribution and severity.

flow.text: Anti-centromere antibody + distal skin only + slow progression = LIMITED (CREST).
flow.subtext: Anti-centromere is highly specific for limited cutaneous SSc / CREST.

flow.text: Skin involvement stays distal to elbows/knees.
flow.subtext: Slow progression over years.
flow.insight: Skin involvement stays distal to elbows/knees

flow.text: CREST mnemonic: Calcinosis, Raynaud, Esophagus, Sclerodactyly, Telangiectasias.
flow.subtext: Five classic features.

flow.text: Patient has at least Raynaud + esophagus from the vignette.
flow.subtext: Other CREST features may be present on exam.
flow.insight: Patient has at least Raynaud + esophagus from the vignette

flow.text: Late complication of CREST: pulmonary arterial hypertension.
flow.subtext: Chronic vasculopathy of pulmonary arterioles develops over years.

flow.text: Most feared complication.
Annual echo screening recommended.
flow.subtext: Treatment: endothelin antagonists, PDE5 inhibitors, prostanoids.

flow.text: Answer: C · Limited cutaneous sclerosis (CREST) — anti-centromere antibody.
flow.subtext: Mechanism: TGF-β-driven fibroblast activation → excess collagen → skin and organ fibrosis. Same biology as diffuse SSc but slower and limited distribution.

stations.bridge: Patient with progressive skin tightening + Raynaud + dyspnea. Pulmonary HTN on echo. Anti-centromere antibody positive. Diagnosis?
stations.vignette: A 45-year-old woman presents with 2 years of skin thickening on her hands, face, and forearms. She also describes severe Raynaud phenomenon, esophageal dysmotility with difficulty swallowing solids, and progressive exertional dyspnea. Echocardiogram shows pulmonary hypertension. Anti-centromere antibody is positive; anti-Scl-70 is negative. Skin biopsy shows excessive dermal collagen deposition with relative sparing of inflammation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
keypoints: Calcinosis (subcutaneous calcium deposits)
Raynaud phenomenon (often years before other symptoms)
Esophageal dysmotility (LES dysfunction, dysphagia)
Sclerodactyly (skin thickening of fingers)
Telangiectasias
Skin involvement: Distal to elbows/knees + face. Slow progression.
Autoantibody: Anti-centromere (~70-80% of limited).
Late complications: Pulmonary arterial hypertension (most feared, ~10-15%), primary biliary cholangitis association.

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: Dermatomyositis
stations.subtitle: Why a 50-year-old woman who can't lift her arms above her head also has a heliotrope rash and Gottron papules — and which biopsy finding makes the diagnosis.

flow.text: Symmetric proximal weakness + can't climb stairs/comb hair = inflammatory myopathy.
flow.subtext: Distal sparing early.

flow.text: CK markedly elevated.
EMG myopathic.
flow.subtext: The differential is DM, PM, IBM, PMR, drug-induced.

flow.text: Heliotrope rash + Gottron papules = SKIN findings → DERMATOMYOSITIS.
flow.subtext: Heliotrope = violaceous eyelids.
flow.insight: Skin findings distinguish DM from PM

flow.text: Gottron = papules over MCP/PIP.
These two skin findings are essentially diagnostic of DM.
flow.subtext: PM doesn't have skin findings.
flow.insight: These two skin findings are essentially diagnostic of DM

flow.text: Biopsy: perifascicular atrophy = DM-specific finding.
flow.subtext: Atrophy of muscle fibers at the edges of fascicles.

flow.text: Reflects ischemic damage from microvasculopathy (DM is a microangiopathy + perifascicular ischemia).
flow.subtext: PM shows endomysial inflammation instead.
flow.insight: Reflects ischemic damage from microvasculopathy (DM is a...

flow.text: Adult-onset DM: screen for occult malignancy.
flow.subtext: 15-30% paraneoplastic.
flow.insight: Adult DM = paraneoplastic risk = cancer screen

flow.text: Common: ovarian, lung, breast, GI, lymphoma.
Risk highest at DM diagnosis.
flow.subtext: Age-appropriate cancer screening mandatory.

flow.text: Answer: A · Dermatomyositis.
flow.subtext: Treatment: high-dose steroids first-line, steroid-sparing agents (MTX, AZA, MMF).
flow.insight: Answer: A · DM · skin + muscle + cancer screen

flow.text: IVIG, rituximab for refractory.
flow.subtext: Cancer screen at diagnosis in adults.

stations.bridge: Older woman with progressive proximal weakness, heliotrope rash, and Gottron papules. Muscle biopsy shows perifascicular atrophy. Diagnosis?
stations.vignette: A 50-year-old woman presents with 4 months of progressive symmetric proximal muscle weakness, particularly difficulty climbing stairs and combing her hair. Physical examination shows a violaceous rash over her eyelids (heliotrope) and erythematous papules over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints (Gottron papules). CK is elevated to 8,000. EMG shows myopathic units. Muscle biopsy shows perifascicular atrophy with perimysial inflammation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
keypoints: Symmetric proximal muscle weakness: Difficulty climbing stairs, getting up from chairs, combing hair, lifting arms overhead. Distal strength preserved early.

Skin findings:
  • Heliotrope rash: Violaceous (purple) discoloration of upper eyelids, often with edema.
Gottron papules: Erythematous, scaly papules over MCP, PIP, and DIP joints. Dorsal hand surface.
Shawl sign: Erythema over upper back/shoulders.
Mechanic's hands: Cracked, scaly fingertips/sides — associated with anti-Jo-1.

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: Rheumatoid arthritis
stations.subtitle: Why a 45-year-old woman with symmetric MCP joint swelling and morning stiffness lasting 2 hours has rheumatoid arthritis — and what the autoantibody RF actually targets.

flow.text: Symmetric MCP/PIP/wrist + morning stiffness >1h + boggy synovitis = RA.
flow.subtext: Pattern: small joint symmetric inflammatory arthritis.
flow.insight: Symmetric small joint + AM stiffness >1h = RA

flow.text: Morning stiffness >1h is inflammatory (vs OA <30 min).
flow.subtext: Boggy synovitis = synovial proliferation.
flow.insight: Morning stiffness >1h is inflammatory (vs OA <30 min)

flow.text: Rheumatoid factor (RF) is IgM autoantibody against the Fc portion of IgG.
flow.subtext: RF is your immune system making antibody against your own antibody. Specifically, IgM RF binds the Fc tail (constant region) of IgG — not the variable antigen-binding region.

flow.text: RF + IgG form immune complexes that deposit in synovium.
flow.subtext: Same Type III biology as SLE/HSP — circulating ag-ab complexes deposit in tissue.
flow.insight: RF-IgG complexes in joint = Type III in synovium

flow.text: In RA the deposition is in joint synovium.
flow.subtext: Activates complement, recruits neutrophils, drives inflammation.
flow.insight: In RA the deposition is in joint synovium

flow.text: Anti-CCP is the more SPECIFIC RA antibody — also positive in this patient.
flow.subtext: Anti-CCP targets citrullinated peptides. ~95% specific for RA.
flow.insight: Anti-CCP = more specific · predicts erosive disease

flow.text: Predicts more aggressive, erosive disease.
flow.subtext: Anti-CCP positive at diagnosis = treat aggressively.

flow.text: Joint destruction comes from pannus — invasive granulation tissue.
flow.subtext: Pannus contains activated synovial fibroblasts, macrophages, T cells.
flow.insight: Pannus = invasive synovial tissue erodes joint

flow.text: Releases TNF, IL-1, IL-6, MMPs.
Erodes cartilage.
flow.subtext: RANKL activates osteoclasts → bone erosion.

flow.text: Answer: C · Fc portion of IgG.
flow.subtext: Treatment: methotrexate first-line, escalate to anti-TNF or other biologics if needed. Anti-TNF has a notorious side effect — TB reactivation — covered in next station.
flow.insight: Answer: C · RF binds Fc of IgG

stations.bridge: Patient with symmetric MCP/wrist swelling, morning stiffness, RF positive. What does RF actually bind, and what's the second autoantibody you'd send?
stations.vignette: A 45-year-old woman presents with 6 months of symmetric joint pain and swelling involving the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints of both hands as well as both wrists. She reports morning stiffness lasting 2 hours that improves with activity. Examination shows boggy synovitis and reduced grip strength. Laboratory studies show rheumatoid factor positive at high titer and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody positive. Hand x-rays show juxta-articular osteopenia. Which of the following best describes the antigen target of rheumatoid factor (RF)?

choices.text: The Fc portion of IgG antibodies
The variable region of IgG antibodies
Type IV collagen in synovial basement membrane

keypoints: An autoantibody (typically IgM) that binds the Fc portion of IgG.
The patient is making antibody against their OWN antibodies.
RF + IgG = immune complexes → deposit in synovium → activate complement → recruit neutrophils → joint inflammation.
Sensitive (~70-80% of RA) but NOT specific. Also positive in Sjögren syndrome, SLE, hepatitis C, chronic infection, healthy elderly.

stations.label: Station 06
stations.title: Anti-TNF & TB
stations.subtitle: Why a patient with RA on infliximab develops cavitary pulmonary TB six months into treatment — and what cytokine the drug blocks that holds latent TB in check.
