flow.text: Latent TB sits inside macrophages contained by a Th1-driven granuloma.
flow.subtext: Initial TB infection generates a granuloma (Ch 11 Station 09).
flow.insight: Latent TB = granuloma containing live mycobacteria

flow.text: Macrophages, T cells walled off mycobacteria.
flow.subtext: Latent TB = stable equilibrium between immune containment and persistent organism.

flow.text: TNF-α is essential for granuloma maintenance. Without TNF, the structure falls apart.
flow.subtext: TNF drives macrophage fusion into giant cells, recruits new immune cells, sustains macrophage activation. TNF-deficient mice can't form proper granulomas at all.

flow.text: Infliximab = anti-TNF antibody → blocks TNF systemically.
flow.subtext: Infliximab is a chimeric IgG1 monoclonal that binds soluble and membrane TNF-α. Blocks all TNF activity, including in any granulomas the patient happens to have.
flow.insight: Anti-TNF blocks TNF including at granulomas

flow.text: Without TNF, granuloma dissolves → mycobacteria escape and multiply.
flow.subtext: Macrophages can no longer maintain the activated state.

flow.text: The structural integrity of the granuloma fails.
flow.insight: The structural integrity of the granuloma fails

flow.text: Live mycobacteria (which were never killed, just contained) escape and replicate.
flow.subtext: Active TB emerges.

flow.text: Reactivation typically within 6 months, often extrapulmonary or disseminated.
flow.subtext: Faster onset than spontaneous reactivation. More frequently extrapulmonary (cervical lymph nodes, bone, miliary, meningitis) compared to typical reactivation pulmonary TB.

flow.text: Prevention: screen for latent TB before starting anti-TNF (IGRA or PPD + CXR).
flow.subtext: Mandatory standard of care.

flow.text: If latent TB: treat with INH x 9 months (or rifampin x 4) before starting biologic.
flow.subtext: Same screening principle for HBV.
flow.insight: If latent TB: treat with INH x 9 months (or rifampin x 4)...

flow.text: Answer: B · Anti-TNF disrupts granuloma maintenance, allowing latent TB to reactivate.
flow.subtext: Same mechanism explains why anti-TNF helps refractory sarcoid (Ch 11 S9) — pathologic granulomas dissolve.

flow.text: The cytokine that maintains protective TB granulomas also maintains pathologic sarcoid granulomas.
flow.insight: The cytokine that maintains protective TB granulomas also...

stations.bridge: RA patient starts infliximab and develops cavitary TB six months later. Why does anti-TNF cause TB reactivation, and what should have been done before starting?
stations.vignette: A 52-year-old woman with poorly controlled rheumatoid arthritis starts infliximab (an anti-TNF-α monoclonal antibody) after methotrexate failure. Six months into therapy, she develops fever, night sweats, weight loss, and a productive cough. Chest CT shows a cavitary right upper lobe lesion. Sputum AFB stain is positive and confirmed as Mycobacterium tuberculosis. She likely had latent TB infection before starting infliximab. Which of the following best explains why anti-TNF therapy precipitated reactivation TB?

choices.text: Anti-TNF depletes CD4 T cells globally, like HIV
Anti-TNF disrupts granuloma maintenance, allowing reactivation

keypoints: Latent TB infection: M. tuberculosis sits inside macrophages within an intact granuloma in the lung.
The granuloma — formed by Th1 IFN-γ + macrophage activation (Ch 11 Station 09) — physically contains the organism.
TNF-α drives macrophage fusion into multinucleated giant cells, sustains macrophage activation, and maintains the granuloma's structural integrity.
TNF-α also recruits new T cells and macrophages to replace dying ones at the granuloma rim.
Block TNF-α → granuloma dissolves → mycobacteria escape into the lung → active disease.

stations.label: Station 07
stations.title: Giant cell arteritis
stations.subtitle: Why a 72-year-old woman's new headache, jaw claudication, and ESR of 110 demand prednisone before the biopsy result returns — and how PMR fits into the same disease spectrum.

flow.text: Classic GCA picture: elderly + new HA + jaw claudication + temporal artery tender + ↑↑ESR.
flow.subtext: All the classic features.

flow.text: The pre-test probability of GCA in this presentation is very high.
flow.subtext: The clinical decision to treat doesn't require biopsy confirmation.
flow.insight: The pre-test probability of GCA in this presentation is...

flow.text: Vision loss in GCA is sudden, IRREVERSIBLE, and preventable.
flow.subtext: Posterior ciliary artery vasculitis → anterior ischemic optic neuropathy → painless monocular vision loss.
flow.insight: Irreversible vision loss = the catastrophe to prevent

flow.text: Once it happens, no recovery.
flow.subtext: Other eye can follow within days.

flow.text: Start high-dose prednisone (60 mg/day) IMMEDIATELY on clinical suspicion.
flow.subtext: Don't wait for biopsy.

flow.text: Don't wait for any other test.
flow.insight: Don't wait for any other test

flow.text: The empiric treatment decision is based on the clinical-laboratory pattern.
flow.subtext: IV methylprednisolone if visual symptoms already present.
flow.insight: The empiric treatment decision is based on the...

flow.text: Biopsy can be done within 1-2 weeks of starting steroids without losing diagnostic yield.
flow.subtext: Steroids don't immediately erase the inflammatory infiltrate or giant cells in the temporal artery.

flow.text: Biopsy yield is preserved for at least 1-2 weeks.
flow.subtext: Treatment-then-biopsy is standard.
flow.insight: Biopsy yield is preserved for at least 1-2 weeks

flow.text: Biopsy shows granulomatous vasculitis with multinucleated giant cells.
flow.subtext: CD4 T cells + macrophages + giant cells in the vessel wall.
flow.insight: Granulomatous + giant cells in vessel wall

flow.text: Disruption of internal elastic lamina.
Intimal hyperplasia.
flow.subtext: Patchy involvement → take long biopsy specimen (≥1-2 cm) to maximize yield.

flow.text: Answer: A · Start prednisone IMMEDIATELY.
flow.subtext: PMR overlap is common (~50% of GCA patients have PMR symptoms).

flow.text: PMR alone is treated with low-dose prednisone (15-20 mg).
flow.subtext: Watch for vision symptoms in PMR patients → urgent GCA workup.
flow.insight: PMR alone is treated with low-dose prednisone (15-20 mg)

stations.bridge: Elderly woman with new headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness. ESR 110. Should you wait for biopsy results before treating?
stations.vignette: A 72-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of new-onset right temporal headache, scalp tenderness over the temporal area, and pain in her jaw when chewing. She also reports stiffness in her shoulders and hips that has worsened over the past month. Examination shows a tender, nodular right temporal artery with diminished pulsation. ESR is 110 mm/hr (elevated) and CRP is markedly elevated. Temporal artery biopsy is scheduled for tomorrow. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate next step?

choices.text: Begin high-dose prednisone immediately, before biopsy results return
Begin low-dose ibuprofen and wait for biopsy result
Schedule MRI of the brain before initiating any treatment
Wait for biopsy result before initiating any treatment to avoid masking pathology

keypoints: Age: >50 (almost always >65). Female > male 2:1.
New-onset headache: Usually unilateral temporal. Scalp tenderness.
Jaw claudication: Pain in jaw with chewing — highly suggestive (specificity especially high).
Tender, nodular, diminished-pulse temporal artery on exam.
Visual symptoms: Amaurosis fugax, diplopia, blurred vision, sudden visual loss. EMERGENCY — these can progress to permanent blindness within hours.
Constitutional: Fevers, weight loss, malaise.
PMR overlap (~50%): Bilateral shoulder/hip girdle pain and stiffness without true weakness.
Markedly elevated ESR (often >50, frequently >100) and CRP.

stations.label: Station 08
stations.title: GPA vasculitis
stations.subtitle: Why a 48-year-old with chronic sinusitis, cavitary lung nodules, and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis has GPA — and what c-ANCA actually targets.

flow.text: ENT + lung + kidney triad = GPA pattern.
flow.subtext: Chronic sinusitis + saddle nose + cavitary lung lesions + RPGN. The classic GPA triad is recognizable on history alone before any antibody testing.

flow.text: c-ANCA = cytoplasmic IF pattern · target = PR3 (proteinase 3).
flow.subtext: ANCA testing by indirect immunofluorescence shows cytoplasmic staining.

flow.text: ELISA confirms PR3 specificity.
flow.subtext: PR3 is a serine protease in neutrophil azurophilic granules.

flow.text: Renal biopsy: pauci-immune crescentic GN = ANCA-associated vasculitis pattern.
flow.subtext: Crescents (cellular proliferation in Bowman's space) on light microscopy.

flow.text: NO immune deposits on IF — "pauci-immune." Distinguishes from anti-GBM (linear IgG) and immune complex (granular).
Answer: C · Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) — c-ANCA, sinopulmonary + renal.
flow.subtext: Treatment: induction with cyclophosphamide OR rituximab + steroids.

flow.text: Plasma exchange for severe disease.
Maintenance with rituximab/MTX/AZA.
flow.subtext: PCP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX.

stations.bridge: 48yo with chronic sinusitis, hemoptysis, cavitary lung lesions, and RPGN. c-ANCA positive. What does c-ANCA target, and what's the diagnosis?
stations.vignette: A 48-year-old man with persistent sinusitis, recurrent epistaxis, and a saddle-nose deformity develops hemoptysis. Chest CT shows multiple cavitary pulmonary nodules. Urinalysis shows hematuria with red blood cell casts; serum creatinine has risen from 1.0 to 3.2 mg/dL over 3 weeks. Renal biopsy shows a pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis. ANCA testing is positive in a cytoplasmic (c-ANCA) pattern, with antibody specificity confirmed against proteinase 3 (PR3). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
keypoints: Upper airway / ENT: Chronic sinusitis, nasal congestion, epistaxis, oral ulcers, saddle-nose deformity (cartilage destruction), otitis, hearing loss, subglottic stenosis.
Lung: Cavitary pulmonary nodules (often multiple), hemoptysis, alveolar hemorrhage, infiltrates.
Kidney: Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis (RPGN) with crescents on biopsy, pauci-immune (no significant immune complex deposition).

stations.label: Station 09
stations.title: Receptor antibodies
stations.subtitle: Why myasthenia, LEMS, and Graves are all caused by autoantibodies against specific receptors — and how the SAME mechanism (Type II) produces three completely different syndromes.

flow.text: Patient 1: fatigable ptosis + diplopia + dysphagia + edrophonium-responsive = MG.
flow.subtext: Worsens throughout the day = fatigability. Edrophonium (short-acting AChE inhibitor) transiently improves symptoms by raising synaptic ACh.

flow.text: MG = anti-AChR antibodies block postsynaptic NMJ receptor.
flow.subtext: AChR is reduced/destroyed by autoantibody.

flow.text: Less ACh signaling per nerve impulse.
flow.subtext: Repeated stimulation depletes available signaling → fatigability worsens through the day.
flow.insight: Less ACh signaling per nerve impulse

flow.text: Patient 2: SCLC + proximal weakness IMPROVING with exercise = LEMS.
flow.subtext: Classic LEMS.

flow.text: Cancer + 'improving with use' is the giveaway.
flow.insight: Cancer + 'improving with use' is the giveaway

flow.text: Reflexes augment after brief exercise (post-tetanic facilitation).
flow.subtext: Repetitive stimulation shows incrementing response on EMG.

flow.text: LEMS = anti-VGCC antibodies block presynaptic calcium → less ACh release.
flow.subtext: Antibodies bind voltage-gated calcium channels on presynaptic motor neuron terminals.
flow.insight: Anti-VGCC · presynaptic Ca²⁺ block · less ACh

flow.text: Less Ca²⁺ entry → fewer ACh vesicles released per impulse.
flow.subtext: Sustained stimulation eventually compensates → 'improvement with exercise.'
flow.insight: Less Ca²⁺ entry → fewer ACh vesicles released per impulse

flow.text: Patient 3: heat intolerance + weight loss + lid lag + diffuse goiter = Graves.
flow.subtext: Classic hyperthyroidism with diffuse non-tender goiter.

flow.text: Lid lag, exophthalmos may be present.
flow.subtext: Suppressed TSH + elevated free T4.

flow.text: Graves = anti-TSH-R antibodies STIMULATE thyroid (not block).
flow.subtext: Antibodies mimic TSH at the receptor.
flow.insight: Anti-TSH-R · STIMULATING (Type V if you want)

flow.text: Continuous thyroid hormone production.
Not negative-feedback-regulated.
flow.subtext: Diffuse goiter from continuous follicular stimulation.

flow.text: Same Type II principle, three different receptors, three different syndromes.
flow.subtext: MG: blocks/destroys AChR.
flow.insight: Same mechanism · different receptor → different disease

flow.text: LEMS: blocks VGCC.
Graves: stimulates TSH-R.
flow.subtext: The receptor identity determines the disease.

flow.text: Answer: A · (1) anti-AChR · (2) anti-VGCC · (3) anti-TSH-R.
flow.subtext: Most specific autoantibodies in autoimmune disease.

flow.text: Clinical pattern + autoantibody = diagnosis.
flow.subtext: Receptor target determines syndrome.

flow.text: Chapter 12 closes. 9 stations · 17+ source questions · autoimmune catalog complete.
flow.subtext: ANA framework, SLE, scleroderma, DM, RA, anti-TNF/TB, GCA, GPA, receptor antibodies. Chapter 13 next: transplant immunology.

stations.bridge: Three patients: ptosis worsening throughout the day · proximal weakness improving with exercise · heat intolerance + tremor + lid lag. Match each to its autoantibody.
stations.vignette:
Three patients present with three different neurologic/endocrine syndromes:

(1) 35-year-old woman with bilateral ptosis worsening throughout the day, fatigable diplopia, and difficulty swallowing. Symptoms improve transiently after edrophonium administration.
(2) 65-year-old man with small cell lung cancer who has proximal lower extremity weakness that IMPROVES with brief exercise. Reduced deep tendon reflexes that augment after exercise.
(3) 28-year-old woman with heat intolerance, palpitations, weight loss, fine tremor, lid lag, and a diffusely enlarged non-tender thyroid gland.

Which of the following correctly matches each patient to the autoantibody target?
keypoints: Target: Postsynaptic acetylcholine receptor (AChR) at the neuromuscular junction.
Mechanism: Anti-AChR antibodies block, internalize, and degrade the AChR. Reduced AChR density at NMJ.
Clinical: Fatigable weakness, especially ocular (ptosis, diplopia), bulbar (dysphagia, dysarthria), and limb. WORSENS with use, improves with rest.
Pattern: Diurnal — worse at end of day. Symptoms improve with rest.
Tests: Anti-AChR antibody (~85% positive). Anti-MuSK in seronegative cases (~5-10%). Edrophonium (Tensilon) test or ice pack test for ptosis.
Thymoma: 10-15% of MG patients have thymoma. CT chest indicated.
Treatment: AChE inhibitors (pyridostigmine), corticosteroids, steroid-sparing agents, IVIG/plasmapheresis for myasthenic crisis, thymectomy.

chapters.title: Transplant Immunology
chapters.subtitle: Hyperacute, acute, chronic — the three rejection timelines and the immunology behind each one.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: HLA matching
stations.subtitle: Why a brother and sister with identical HLA still aren't perfect kidney donors for each other — and how the mixed lymphocyte reaction tests histocompatibility before transplant.

flow.text: HLA-identical siblings = same MHC class I + II haplotypes (25% chance of full match between siblings).
flow.subtext: Each parent contributes one HLA haplotype. 25% of sibling pairs inherit the SAME two haplotypes from each parent → HLA-identical.
flow.insight: HLA-identical = best major MHC match possible

flow.text: MLR shows minimal recipient T-cell proliferation against donor cells.
flow.insight: MLR shows minimal recipient T-cell proliferation against...

flow.text: But "HLA-identical" doesn't mean genetically identical. Genomes differ at thousands of non-MHC loci.
flow.subtext: Two siblings share half their non-MHC genome on average.

flow.text: Many genes outside the MHC are also polymorphic.
flow.subtext: Some encode proteins whose peptides differ between siblings.
flow.insight: Many genes outside the MHC are also polymorphic

flow.text: Polymorphic non-MHC peptides presented on shared MHC = MINOR histocompatibility antigens.
flow.subtext: Donor cells present (on shared HLA-I or HLA-II) peptides from polymorphic donor proteins that the recipient lacks.
flow.insight: Minor histocompatibility = donor peptide on shared MHC

flow.text: Recipient T cells see these as foreign because they were never tolerized to them in the thymus.
flow.insight: Recipient T cells see these as foreign because they were...

flow.text: Specific case here: male recipient + female donor → HY antigens on donor cells are FOREIGN to him.
flow.subtext: Y-chromosome-encoded proteins (HY antigens) generate peptides that male individuals tolerize against.
flow.insight: Cross-sex donation = HY antigen mismatch (in either direction)

flow.text: Male recipient of female donor — does he see HY peptides?
flow.subtext: Subtle direction.

flow.text: Actually it's the opposite: female donor cells DON'T present HY.
flow.subtext: No Y chromosome = no HY peptides on donor MHC.

flow.text: So HY rejection happens in female-recipient + male-donor pairs, not the reverse.
More precisely: female donor cells lack Y-encoded peptides; male recipient T cells trained against self HY won't see them.
flow.insight: Female donor → male recipient: no HY presented

flow.text: Conversely, female recipient of male donor: female T cells encounter HY peptides on donor cells and react to them as foreign.
flow.insight: Male donor → female recipient: HY = foreign

flow.text: female recipient never tolerized to HY).
flow.insight: female recipient never tolerized to HY)

flow.text: The vignette has male recipient + female donor — also leads to minor antigen mismatch but via different polymorphic loci.
flow.subtext: Either direction: HLA-identical siblings still differ at minor loci.
flow.insight: The vignette has male recipient + female donor — also leads...

flow.text: Result: slow T-cell-mediated chronic rejection over months/years.
flow.subtext: Minor histocompatibility-driven rejection is typically slower and lower-grade than full HLA mismatch rejection.
flow.insight: Minor antigen rejection = slow chronic T-cell infiltration

flow.text: Biopsy shows T-cell infiltration (cellular rejection pattern).
flow.subtext: Treatment: increase immunosuppression.

flow.text: Answer: C · Minor histocompatibility antigens (HY) — even HLA-identical sibs differ.
flow.subtext: MHC matching reduces but doesn't eliminate rejection risk.
flow.insight: Answer: C · minor antigens (HY) drive rejection

flow.text: Even HLA-identical sibling kidneys eventually fail without immunosuppression.
flow.subtext: Maintenance immunosuppression is needed for life.
flow.insight: Even HLA-identical sibling kidneys eventually fail without...

stations.bridge: HLA-identical siblings, sister donates kidney to brother. Why does he still mount a low-level rejection response despite "perfect" MHC matching?
stations.vignette: A 28-year-old man with end-stage renal disease receives a kidney transplant from his HLA-identical sister. Pre-transplant mixed lymphocyte reaction (MLR) showed minimal proliferation. Despite this match and standard immunosuppression, he develops slow progressive rejection over months, with biopsy showing T-cell infiltration. Which of the following best explains this rejection pattern in an HLA-matched transplant?

choices.text: Pre-formed anti-HLA antibodies missed on cross-match
T-cell response against minor histocompatibility antigens (HY)
De novo recipient anti-class I antibody production
Latent CMV reactivation in donor kidney

keypoints: Class I (HLA-A, HLA-B, HLA-C): Expressed on all nucleated cells. Recognized by recipient CD8 T cells.
Class II (HLA-DR, HLA-DQ, HLA-DP): Expressed on professional APCs. Recognized by recipient CD4 T cells.
Highly polymorphic: Hundreds of alleles per locus. Random mismatch between unrelated individuals is the rule, not the exception.
Inheritance: Each parent contributes one HLA haplotype (linked block of A, B, C, DR, DQ, DP alleles). Siblings have a 25% chance of being HLA-identical (sharing both haplotypes).

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: Rejection types
stations.subtitle: Why a transplanted kidney can fail in three completely different time windows — minutes, weeks, or years — and which immune mechanism dominates each.

flow.text: Three rejection types differ by timing + mechanism + biopsy.
flow.subtext: Time-of-onset narrows the differential immediately. Each rejection type has a characteristic immune mechanism and biopsy pattern.

flow.text: Case 1: 30 min + fibrinoid necrosis + neutrophils = HYPERACUTE.
flow.subtext: Only pre-formed antibodies act fast enough to cause rejection within minutes.

flow.text: Anti-donor antibodies (anti-ABO or anti-HLA) bind endothelium → complement → neutrophils → vascular thrombosis.
Hyperacute is preventable via pre-transplant ABO + HLA cross-match.
flow.subtext: Should never happen in modern practice.

flow.text: When it does, it's a clerical/lab error.
flow.subtext: Once it occurs, no treatment — must remove the graft.

flow.text: Case 2: 2 weeks + mononuclear infiltrate + tubulitis = ACUTE CELLULAR.
flow.subtext: Recipient CD8 T cells infiltrate the graft.
flow.insight: Days-weeks + T cells in tubules = acute cellular

flow.text: Tubulitis = T cells crossing tubular basement membrane into tubular epithelium.
flow.insight: Tubulitis = T cells crossing tubular basement membrane into...

flow.text: CD4 helpers also present.
flow.subtext: Most common acute rejection pattern.

flow.text: Acute cellular rejection: treat with pulse steroids + anti-T-cell agents if steroid-resistant.
flow.subtext: Methylprednisolone pulse first.

flow.text: ATG, alemtuzumab, or OKT3 (historically) for steroid-resistant cases.
flow.subtext: Often reversible if caught and treated promptly.

flow.text: Acute HUMORAL rejection (separate subtype): de novo donor-specific antibodies + C4d positive.
flow.subtext: Same time frame as acute cellular but antibody-mediated.
flow.insight: Acute humoral = DSA + C4d+ on capillaries

flow.text: C4d staining on peritubular capillaries is positive (complement activation marker).
flow.subtext: Treatment: plasmapheresis + IVIG + rituximab.
flow.insight: C4d staining on peritubular capillaries is positive...

flow.text: Case 3: 2 years + interstitial fibrosis + obliterative arteriolopathy = CHRONIC.
flow.subtext: Chronic allograft nephropathy.

flow.text: Mixed mechanism — chronic alloimmunity, drug toxicity, vascular injury.
Concentric intimal thickening narrows vessels.
flow.subtext: Limited treatment.

flow.text: Chronic rejection is the long-term limit on transplant survival (~50% of kidneys fail by 10-15 years).
flow.subtext: No effective treatment — optimize immunosuppression, manage CV risk, plan for re-transplant. Major area of ongoing research.
flow.insight: Chronic rejection caps long-term graft survival

flow.text: Answer: A · Hyperacute → acute cellular → chronic — minutes / weeks / years.
flow.subtext: The time + biopsy pattern uniquely maps each case. B/C/D/E all scramble the assignments.

stations.bridge: Three transplant patients reject in three different timeframes — minutes, weeks, years. Match each to its dominant mechanism.
stations.vignette:
A transplant surgeon evaluates three kidney transplant patients in clinic:

(1) Within 30 minutes of vascular anastomosis, the kidney becomes mottled and cyanotic; biopsy shows fibrinoid necrosis of small vessels with neutrophilic infiltrate.
(2) Two weeks post-transplant, rising creatinine; biopsy shows interstitial mononuclear infiltrate with tubulitis (CD8+ T cells in tubular epithelium).
(3) Two years post-transplant, gradual rise in creatinine; biopsy shows interstitial fibrosis, tubular atrophy, and obliterative arteriolopathy.

Which of the following correctly matches each patient to the dominant rejection mechanism?
keypoints: Mechanism: Pre-formed recipient antibodies against donor antigens (anti-ABO, anti-HLA from prior transplant/transfusion/pregnancy).
Histology: Fibrinoid necrosis of small vessels, neutrophilic infiltrate, microthrombi, complement deposition.
Outcome: Graft typically lost. No effective treatment — must remove the organ.
Prevention: Pre-transplant ABO match + cross-match to detect anti-donor HLA antibodies. Should never happen in modern practice; when it does, it's usually a clerical/lab error.

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: GVHD
stations.subtitle: Why a leukemia patient three weeks post-bone-marrow-transplant develops a maculopapular rash, watery diarrhea, and rising bilirubin — and which immune cells are causing it.

flow.text: Allogeneic BMT graft contains mature donor T cells along with the stem cells.
flow.subtext: Bone marrow harvest includes hematopoietic stem cells AND mature lymphocytes.
flow.insight: BMT graft brings donor T cells along with HSCs

flow.text: Those donor T cells are immunocompetent.
flow.subtext: After engraftment, they're in the recipient.
flow.insight: Those donor T cells are immunocompetent

flow.text: Conditioning regimen immunosuppresses the host — recipient cannot reject the graft.
flow.subtext: Total body irradiation + chemotherapy ablates host bone marrow and immune system.
flow.insight: Conditioning prevents graft rejection but enables GVHD

flow.text: This is necessary to make space for the graft and prevent rejection.
flow.subtext: But it also leaves the host defenseless against the incoming donor T cells.
flow.insight: This is necessary to make space for the graft and prevent...

flow.text: Donor T cells encounter host antigens (minor histocompat or MHC mismatch) → see them as foreign.
flow.subtext: Even HLA-matched siblings differ at minor histocompatibility loci.
flow.insight: Donor T cells never saw host minor antigens during selection

flow.text: Donor T cells were trained against donor self in donor's thymus, never tolerized to host minor antigens.
flow.subtext: They recognize host cells as foreign.
flow.insight: Donor T cells were trained against donor self in donor's...

flow.text: Donor T cells expand, traffic, and attack three target organs: skin, gut, liver.
flow.subtext: These three organ systems are the dominant acute GVHD targets.
flow.insight: Skin + gut + liver = acute GVHD triad

flow.text: Skin: maculopapular rash often on palms/soles.
flow.insight: Skin: maculopapular rash often on palms/soles

flow.text: Gut: voluminous watery diarrhea.
flow.subtext: Liver: cholestatic pattern (bile duct epithelium attacked).

flow.text: Timing: 2-6 weeks post-transplant = acute GVHD. After 100 days = chronic GVHD (different picture).
flow.subtext: Acute GVHD develops as donor T cells engraft and encounter host antigens.
flow.insight: Acute <100 days · chronic >100 days · different syndromes

flow.text: Chronic GVHD develops later with scleroderma-like skin, sicca syndrome, bronchiolitis obliterans — more like a chronic autoimmune syndrome.
flow.insight: Chronic GVHD develops later with scleroderma-like skin

flow.text: Answer: B · Donor T cells attacking host tissues = GVHD (skin/gut/liver triad).
flow.subtext: Treatment: high-dose steroids first-line.
flow.insight: Answer: B · GVHD = donor T → host

flow.text: Steroid-refractory: ruxolitinib, ATG, ECP.
flow.subtext: Prevention: HLA matching, calcineurin inhibitor + MTX, post-transplant cyclophosphamide.

stations.bridge: BMT recipient at 3 weeks develops rash + diarrhea + cholestasis. What's happening, and which cells are responsible?
stations.vignette: A 42-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia receives an allogeneic bone marrow transplant from his HLA-matched brother after total body irradiation conditioning. Three weeks post-transplant, he develops a diffuse maculopapular rash on his palms, soles, and trunk, watery diarrhea (>2 L/day), and rising serum bilirubin with cholestatic liver enzyme pattern. Which of the following best explains his clinical presentation?

choices.text: CMV reactivation affecting skin, gut, liver
Donor T cells attacking host tissues (graft-vs-host)
Recipient T cells rejecting the donor marrow

keypoints: Graft-versus-host disease (GVHD) is the unique complication of allogeneic bone marrow (or hematopoietic stem cell) transplantation. The transplanted bone marrow contains mature donor T cells. After engraftment, those donor T cells encounter recipient tissues — and recognize them as foreign. The result is an immune attack BY the graft AGAINST the host.
Three conditions for GVHD (Billingham's criteria):
BMT meets all three: marrow contains T cells, recipient is immunosuppressed by conditioning, even matched siblings have minor histocompatibility differences.

The graft-versus-leukemia (GVL) effect: the same donor T cells that cause GVHD also attack residual leukemia cells in the recipient — the GVL effect. This is therapeutically beneficial: allogeneic BMT cures more leukemia than syngeneic (identical twin) or autologous BMT precisely because of GVL. Removing all donor T cells eliminates GVHD but also eliminates GVL → higher leukemia relapse. The clinical balance is keeping enough donor T-cell activity for GVL while suppressing enough to prevent severe GVHD.

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: OKT3
stations.subtitle: Why an old anti-T-cell drug works by binding the CD3 epsilon chain — and what that tells us about how the TCR signals.

flow.text: TCR alone cannot signal — it has very short cytoplasmic tails.
flow.subtext: The αβ TCR heterodimer recognizes peptide-MHC but lacks signaling machinery. It depends on associated CD3 chains for downstream signaling.

flow.text: CD3 complex (γε, δε, ζζ pairs) contains the ITAMs that transmit TCR signals.
flow.subtext: ITAMs (immunoreceptor tyrosine-based activation motifs) on CD3 cytoplasmic tails are phosphorylated by Lck → recruit ZAP-70 → start the downstream signaling cascade.

flow.text: Without CD3, no T-cell activation.
OKT3 = mouse monoclonal antibody against CD3 epsilon chain.
flow.subtext: Murine IgG2a monoclonal.

flow.text: Approved 1986 — the first therapeutic monoclonal antibody.
flow.subtext: Binds the CD3 ε subunit on every T cell.
flow.insight: Approved 1986 — the first therapeutic monoclonal antibody

flow.text: First dose: massive crosslinking of CD3 → cytokine release syndrome.
flow.subtext: Anti-CD3 binding mimics TCR engagement, causing transient massive activation of all T cells before depletion occurs.
flow.insight: First dose = cytokine release before depletion

flow.text: Cytokine storm: fever, hypotension, pulmonary edema, capillary leak.
Then: CD3 internalized + T cells depleted via Fc-mediated clearance.
flow.subtext: TCR-CD3 complexes are pulled off the cell surface (modulation).

flow.text: Antibody-coated T cells are cleared by spleen Fc receptors and complement.
flow.subtext: Circulating T-cell count drops dramatically.
flow.insight: Antibody-coated T cells are cleared by spleen Fc receptors...

flow.text: Pre-medicate with steroids + antihistamine + antipyretic to blunt CRS.
flow.subtext: Standard pre-medication before each OKT3 dose. Severe cases need ICU monitoring with vasopressor and pulmonary support.

flow.text: Answer: C · Anti-CD3-ε · disables TCR signaling + depletes T cells.
flow.subtext: Modern alternatives (ATG, alemtuzumab, basiliximab) have largely replaced OKT3, but the CD3-ε mechanism remains a foundational teaching example.

stations.bridge: Steroid-refractory acute cellular rejection. Patient is given OKT3. What molecule does OKT3 actually bind, and why does that disable T-cell function?
stations.vignette: A 35-year-old kidney transplant recipient develops biopsy-proven acute cellular rejection 4 weeks post-transplant. Despite three days of high-dose methylprednisolone pulses, his creatinine continues to rise and biopsy still shows tubulitis. The transplant team initiates muromonab-CD3 (OKT3), a mouse IgG2a monoclonal antibody. The patient develops fever, hypotension, and pulmonary edema after the first dose. Which of the following best describes the molecular target and mechanism of OKT3?

choices.text: Binds CD20 on B cells, depleting B-cell precursors
Binds CD25 (IL-2Rα) on activated T cells, blocking IL-2 signaling
Binds CD3 epsilon on T cells, disabling TCR signaling and depleting circulating T cells
Binds CD52 on lymphocytes, causing pan-lymphocyte depletion
Binds the variable region of the T-cell receptor, blocking peptide-MHC recognition

keypoints: The TCR (αβ heterodimer) recognizes peptide-MHC but has very short cytoplasmic tails — it cannot signal on its own.
CD3 is a cluster of accessory chains (γε, δε, and ζζ pairs) that associate with the TCR.
CD3 chains contain ITAMs (immunoreceptor tyrosine-based activation motifs) in their cytoplasmic tails.
When the TCR engages peptide-MHC, conformational changes are TRANSMITTED through CD3 → ITAMs are phosphorylated by Lck → ZAP-70 recruitment → downstream signaling cascade.
Block CD3 = block TCR signaling regardless of antigen specificity.

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: Calcineurin inhibitors
stations.subtitle: Why cyclosporine and tacrolimus both block IL-2 production despite binding different intracellular partners — and how that explains their shared toxicity profile.

flow.text: TCR engagement → Ca²⁺ influx → calmodulin → calcineurin activation.
flow.subtext: PLCγ1 cleaves PIP2 → IP3 + DAG.
flow.insight: Ca²⁺ → calcineurin in normal T-cell activation

flow.text: IP3 releases ER calcium.
flow.subtext: Ca²⁺/calmodulin binds and activates calcineurin, a serine/threonine phosphatase.

flow.text: Calcineurin dephosphorylates NFAT → NFAT enters nucleus → IL-2 transcription.
flow.subtext: NFAT (nuclear factor of activated T cells) is held in cytoplasm by phosphates.

flow.text: Dephosphorylation reveals nuclear localization signals → translocation.
flow.subtext: With AP-1 partner from DAG/PKC pathway, drives IL-2 transcription.

flow.text: Cyclosporine binds cyclophilin. Tacrolimus binds FKBP-12. Different partners.
flow.subtext: Each drug has its own intracellular receptor protein.

flow.text: Cyclosporine + cyclophilin = one complex.
Tacrolimus + FKBP-12 = different complex.
flow.subtext: Different chemical structures, different binding pockets.

flow.text: Both drug-protein complexes converge on calcineurin → inhibit it.
flow.subtext: The drug-protein complex (not the drug alone) is what binds and inhibits calcineurin.

flow.text: Different binding partners produce structurally different complexes that both happen to inhibit calcineurin.
flow.insight: Different binding partners produce structurally different...

flow.text: Calcineurin inhibition → NFAT stays phosphorylated → no nuclear entry → no IL-2 transcription.
flow.subtext: Without IL-2 production, T cells can't proliferate or sustain effector function. Allograft rejection suppressed.
flow.insight: Final result: no IL-2, no T-cell proliferation

flow.text: Shared mechanism = shared toxicity: nephrotoxicity, HTN, hyperglycemia, neurotoxicity.
flow.subtext: Both drugs inhibit calcineurin systemically — not just in T cells.
flow.insight: Calcineurin inhibition everywhere = predictable systemic toxicity

flow.text: Vasoconstriction (afferent arteriole, peripheral vessels), beta-cell toxicity, neuronal effects.
flow.subtext: Distinguishing CNI nephrotoxicity from chronic rejection is a major clinical challenge.

flow.text: Distinguishing features: cyclosporine = gingival hyperplasia + hypertrichosis; tacrolimus = more diabetogenic + neurotoxic.
flow.subtext: Tacrolimus is now first-line in most centers because of better immunosuppression and absence of cosmetic toxicities, despite being more diabetogenic.
flow.insight: CsA cosmetic side effects · Tac more potent + diabetogenic

flow.text: Answer: A · Both block calcineurin via different immunophilin partners.
flow.subtext: Stations 06 and 07 deepen the IL-2 biology and calcineurin signaling.

stations.bridge: Cyclosporine and tacrolimus are different drugs that bind different cytoplasmic proteins. Why do they share the same final target and the same toxicity profile?
stations.vignette: A 50-year-old liver transplant recipient is maintained on tacrolimus (FK506) and prednisone. He develops new hypertension, hyperglycemia, and a 30% rise in creatinine. Drug levels are within therapeutic range. Cyclosporine, an alternative calcineurin inhibitor, would produce a similar toxicity profile despite binding to a different intracellular protein. Which of the following best describes how cyclosporine and tacrolimus both inhibit T-cell IL-2 production?

choices.text: Calcineurin block via cyclophilin / FKBP-12 partners
Direct IL-2 receptor antagonism on activated T cells
mTOR inhibition downstream of IL-2 receptor
Purine synthesis inhibition in proliferating T cells

keypoints: Cyclosporine and tacrolimus (FK506) are the two foundational calcineurin inhibitors of solid organ transplantation. They have different chemical structures and bind different intracellular partners — yet they converge on the SAME enzyme (calcineurin) and produce the SAME endpoint (no IL-2 transcription) and the SAME toxicity profile. This is one of the cleanest examples in clinical pharmacology of structurally diverse drugs converging on a shared mechanism.
The TCR-to-IL-2 signaling pathway:

FKBP twist — the sirolimus distinction: sirolimus (rapamycin) ALSO binds FKBP-12. But the sirolimus-FKBP complex does NOT inhibit calcineurin. Instead it inhibits mTOR (mechanistic target of rapamycin). Same intracellular partner, different ultimate target, different mechanism (blocks T-cell proliferation in response to IL-2 rather than IL-2 production itself). Sirolimus has different toxicity (less nephrotoxic, but causes hyperlipidemia, impaired wound healing, mouth ulcers, pneumonitis). Often combined with reduced-dose calcineurin inhibitor for kidney-sparing regimens.

stations.label: Station 06
stations.title: IL-2 therapy
stations.subtitle: Why a cytokine we work hard to SUPPRESS in transplant patients is the same one we INFUSE to treat metastatic melanoma — and what that tells us about IL-2's central role in T-cell biology.

flow.text: IL-2 is the master T-cell growth factor — same cytokine we suppress in transplant.
flow.subtext: Discovered as the activity that lets T cells grow continuously in culture.

flow.text: Made by activated CD4 T cells.
flow.subtext: Acts on T cells (autocrine + paracrine) and NK cells.
flow.insight: Made by activated CD4 T cells

flow.text: Pharmacologic IL-2 (aldesleukin) expands cytotoxic CD8 T cells and NK cells.
flow.subtext: Binds IL-2 receptor → JAK/STAT signaling → proliferation and effector differentiation.

flow.text: Tumor-reactive clones expand.
flow.subtext: NK cells become highly cytotoxic LAK cells.

flow.text: Expanded effector cells kill tumor — tumor regression in ~15-20% of metastatic melanoma/RCC.
flow.subtext: Some achieve durable complete remissions. Was the standard of care for these cancers before checkpoint inhibitors.

flow.text: Same expanded lymphocytes + IL-2 effects on endothelium → capillary leak syndrome.
flow.subtext: Endothelial cells express IL-2 receptors.

flow.text: Activated lymphocytes damage endothelium directly.
flow.subtext: Secondary cytokine release (TNF, IFN-γ) drives systemic endothelial activation.

flow.text: Capillary leak: hypotension, oliguria, pulmonary edema, pleural effusions.
flow.subtext: Fluid moves from intravascular to interstitial space throughout the body.

flow.text: Patients require ICU monitoring during treatment.
flow.subtext: Major reason for limited clinical use.
flow.insight: Patients require ICU monitoring during treatment

flow.text: Therapy and toxicity share mechanism — both come from systemic IL-2 signaling.
flow.subtext: You can't separate the antitumor benefit from the endothelial toxicity at high doses.

flow.text: Engineered IL-2 variants (e.g., bempegaldesleukin) try to selectively activate effector cells while sparing endothelium.
Answer: B · CD8 + NK expansion → tumor + endothelial activation → capillary leak.
flow.subtext: Block IL-2 for transplant; boost it for cancer. Same molecule, opposite clinical aims, both teach what IL-2 does in T-cell biology.
flow.insight: Answer: B · IL-2 mechanism = effector expansion + endothelial activation

stations.bridge: Patient with metastatic melanoma receives high-dose IL-2 and develops hypotension and pulmonary edema. What's the mechanism of both the antitumor effect and the toxicity?
stations.vignette: A 55-year-old man with metastatic melanoma is treated with high-dose interleukin-2 (aldesleukin) infusions. Several hours into treatment he develops hypotension, oliguria, and pulmonary edema requiring ICU admission. Imaging confirms diffuse pulmonary infiltrates and pleural effusions consistent with capillary leak syndrome. Tumor regression is later observed in 15% of patients receiving this regimen. Which of the following best describes how high-dose IL-2 produces both the antitumor effect and this toxicity profile?

choices.text: IL-2 directly lyses tumor cells through complement activation; capillary leak from complement byproducts
IL-2 expands cytotoxic CD8 T cells and NK cells (driving tumor lysis), while also activating systemic endothelium and causing diffuse capillary leak syndrome
IL-2 stimulates B-cell antibody production against tumor antigens; capillary leak from immune complexes
IL-2 inhibits regulatory T cells, releasing pre-existing antitumor immunity; capillary leak from cytokine release
IL-2 directly induces tumor apoptosis through caspase activation; capillary leak unrelated to mechanism

keypoints: Tumors evade immunity in part by failing to provide adequate T-cell stimulation. Anti-tumor T cells exist but are anergic, exhausted, or insufficiently expanded.
Pharmacologic IL-2 binds the IL-2 receptor on T cells (high-affinity IL-2Rα/β/γ on activated T cells, intermediate-affinity β/γ on resting T cells and NK cells).
IL-2 → JAK1/JAK3 → STAT5 → proliferation and effector differentiation.
Result: massive expansion of cytotoxic CD8 T cells and NK cells — including any tumor-reactive clones present.
"Lymphokine-activated killer (LAK) cells" — strongly cytotoxic NK-like cells generated by IL-2.
Tumor regression observed in ~15-20% of metastatic melanoma and RCC patients. Some achieve durable complete remissions.

stations.label: Station 07
stations.title: NFAT pathway
stations.subtitle: Why blocking calcineurin specifically prevents NFAT from getting into the nucleus — and why that one signaling step is enough to disable T-cell activation entirely.

flow.text: Calcineurin is a serine/threonine phosphatase activated by Ca²⁺/calmodulin.
flow.subtext: It removes phosphate groups from substrate proteins. In T cells, its critical substrate for IL-2 transcription is NFAT.

flow.text: Resting NFAT is phosphorylated and stuck in the cytoplasm.
flow.subtext: Phosphate groups mask NFAT's nuclear localization signal.

flow.text: Phosphorylated NFAT can't enter the nucleus.
flow.subtext: T cell stays in resting state.
flow.insight: Phosphorylated NFAT can't enter the nucleus

flow.text: On TCR signaling, calcineurin dephosphorylates NFAT → reveals its NLS.
flow.subtext: Active calcineurin removes phosphates.

flow.text: NFAT's nuclear localization signal becomes accessible to importin proteins.
flow.subtext: NFAT translocates to the nucleus.
flow.insight: NFAT's nuclear localization signal becomes accessible to...

flow.text: Nuclear NFAT partners with AP-1 at the IL-2 promoter → drives IL-2 transcription.
flow.subtext: AP-1 comes from the parallel DAG/PKC/MAPK arm of TCR signaling.

flow.text: NFAT alone is insufficient.
flow.subtext: NFAT + AP-1 = full IL-2 promoter activation.

flow.text: Cyclosporine inhibits calcineurin → NFAT stays phosphorylated → stays in cytoplasm → no IL-2.
flow.subtext: ZAP-70 phosphorylation is normal.

flow.text: Calcium release is normal.
flow.subtext: AP-1 is generated normally.

flow.text: Only NFAT translocation fails.
flow.subtext: That single step is enough to abolish IL-2 transcription.

flow.text: Answer: B · NFAT fails to dephosphorylate and translocate to nucleus.
flow.subtext: Calcineurin's job in T cells is NFAT dephosphorylation. Block calcineurin = block NFAT translocation = no IL-2 transcription.

stations.bridge: Cyclosporine inhibits calcineurin. What is the IMMEDIATE downstream consequence in the T cell — what doesn't happen that should happen?
stations.vignette: A 38-year-old kidney transplant recipient is maintained on cyclosporine. In a research assay using her T cells, anti-CD3 stimulation in the presence of cyclosporine fails to produce IL-2 mRNA, despite normal calcium influx and normal early TCR signaling. Cyclosporine forms a complex with cyclophilin and inhibits calcineurin. Which of the following best describes the IMMEDIATE downstream consequence of calcineurin inhibition that explains the loss of IL-2 transcription?

choices.text: Failure of NFAT to be dephosphorylated and translocate to the nucleus
Failure of IκB to be degraded, preventing NF-κB nuclear translocation
Failure to release intracellular calcium from the endoplasmic reticulum
Failure to activate PI3K/Akt downstream of CD28 costimulation

keypoints: Calcineurin has many possible substrates throughout the body, but in T cells its critical immunologic substrate is NFAT (Nuclear Factor of Activated T cells). The entire reason calcineurin inhibitors work as immunosuppressants is the NFAT dephosphorylation step. Knowing exactly which step gets blocked sharpens the mechanism.
The signaling sequence in detail:

Why NFAT activation by dephosphorylation is unusual: most transcription factors are activated by PHOSPHORYLATION (e.g., STAT5 needs to be phosphorylated to dimerize and translocate). NFAT is the opposite: it's PHOSPHORYLATED in the resting state to keep it inactive in the cytoplasm, and DEPHOSPHORYLATION reveals its nuclear localization signal. This unusual "activation by dephosphorylation" is exactly what makes calcineurin (a phosphatase, not a kinase) the activation enzyme.

stations.label: Station 08
stations.title: Azathioprine
stations.subtitle: Why azathioprine has to be METABOLIZED to 6-mercaptopurine to work — and why patients with low TPMT activity develop catastrophic bone marrow toxicity at standard doses.

flow.text: Azathioprine is a prodrug — does nothing on its own.
flow.subtext: Must be cleaved in vivo to release the active drug 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP). Glutathione and other thiols perform the cleavage.

flow.text: Active 6-MP has three competing fates: activation (HGPRT) or inactivation (TPMT or xanthine oxidase).
flow.subtext: HGPRT salvage pathway converts 6-MP to 6-thioguanine nucleotides (active).

flow.text: TPMT methylates 6-MP to inactive form.
XO oxidizes 6-MP to inactive uric acid.
flow.subtext: The balance determines effective drug exposure.
flow.insight: XO oxidizes 6-MP to inactive uric acid

flow.text: 6-Thioguanine nucleotides are the cytotoxic metabolites that drive immunosuppression.
flow.subtext: Incorporated into DNA → strand breaks, apoptosis.

flow.text: Also inhibit de novo purine synthesis enzymes.
flow.subtext: Proliferating cells (especially lymphocytes and marrow precursors) are most affected.
flow.insight: Also inhibit de novo purine synthesis enzymes

flow.text: Lymphocytes are especially dependent on de novo purine synthesis during clonal expansion → preferentially affected.
flow.subtext: T and B cells expanding rapidly during an immune response use de novo purine synthesis heavily.

flow.text: Anti-purine drugs hit them hard.
flow.subtext: Anti-rejection benefit comes from blocking T-cell proliferation.

flow.text: TPMT genotype determines toxicity. ~0.3% population is homozygous low-activity.
flow.subtext: Trimodal distribution: ~89% normal, ~11% intermediate, ~0.3% deficient.

flow.text: TPMT-deficient patients can't inactivate 6-MP → accumulates → 6-TGN reaches toxic levels → severe pancytopenia at standard doses.
Test TPMT before starting (genotype or activity assay). Deficient patients = avoid or massively reduce dose.
flow.subtext: Standard practice in transplant and oncology. Heterozygotes need reduced doses; homozygous-deficient patients should generally use alternative drugs.

flow.text: Classic interaction: allopurinol + azathioprine. Allopurinol inhibits XO → 6-MP accumulates.
flow.subtext: Both used in oncology and rheumatology.

flow.text: Combining them blocks one of the inactivation pathways.
flow.insight: Combining them blocks one of the inactivation pathways

flow.text: Severe marrow toxicity.
flow.subtext: Either avoid the combination or reduce azathioprine ~75%.

flow.text: Answer: B · AZA → 6-MP → thioguanine; TPMT inactivates 6-MP.
flow.subtext: Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) has largely replaced azathioprine in modern transplant maintenance — more selective for lymphocytes, less marrow toxicity.

stations.bridge: Azathioprine prescribed for transplant. Why must it be activated, and why does TPMT genotype matter so much for safety?
stations.vignette: A 44-year-old kidney transplant recipient is started on azathioprine as part of her maintenance immunosuppression. Two weeks later she develops profound pancytopenia. Investigation reveals she has homozygous low-activity TPMT (thiopurine methyltransferase) variant, leading to dangerously high levels of active metabolites. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which azathioprine produces immunosuppression and how TPMT influences toxicity?

choices.text: Azathioprine binds IL-2R; TPMT controls receptor expression
Azathioprine inhibits calcineurin; TPMT modulates activity
Azathioprine binds TCR; TPMT regulates TCR signaling
Azathioprine inhibits DHFR; TPMT controls folate metabolism

keypoints: Azathioprine is one of the oldest immunosuppressants in transplant medicine, predating cyclosporine. It's still used in transplant maintenance and in autoimmune diseases (RA, IBD, autoimmune hepatitis). Its pharmacology is the textbook example of a prodrug whose toxicity is shaped by pharmacogenetics — specifically TPMT genotype.
Azathioprine → 6-mercaptopurine activation:

Other azathioprine toxicities: dose-dependent myelosuppression (especially in TPMT-deficient patients), hepatotoxicity, pancreatitis, increased risk of skin cancer (especially squamous cell carcinoma), and lymphoma (especially EBV-driven post-transplant lymphoproliferative disease, PTLD).

stations.label: Station 09
stations.title: Corticosteroids
stations.subtitle: Why prednisone is in nearly every immunosuppressive regimen — and how the entire transplant pharmacology toolbox fits together at different points in the T-cell activation pathway.

flow.text: Glucocorticoids are lipid-soluble — diffuse across cell membranes.
flow.subtext: Cortisol, prednisone, methylprednisolone, dexamethasone all share this. No need for surface receptors — they enter cells freely.

flow.text: Bind cytoplasmic glucocorticoid receptor (GR) → translocate to nucleus.
flow.subtext: The GR-steroid complex moves to the nucleus where it acts as a transcription factor. This is the single mechanism behind both efficacy and toxicity.

flow.text: In nucleus: inhibit AP-1 and NF-κB + upregulate IκB.
flow.subtext: Two-pronged anti-inflammatory action.
flow.insight: AP-1 and NF-κB blocked + IκB increased

flow.text: Direct binding/inhibition of pro-inflammatory transcription factors.
flow.subtext: Increased IκB keeps NF-κB sequestered in cytoplasm.

flow.text: Net result: broad cytokine suppression (IL-1, IL-2, IL-6, TNF) + reduced T-cell activation.
flow.subtext: Pro-inflammatory cytokine genes silenced.

flow.text: Antigen presentation reduced.
Neutrophil and eosinophil function suppressed.
flow.subtext: Lymphocyte apoptosis at high doses.

flow.text: Triple therapy combines three different points in T-cell activation.
flow.subtext: Tacrolimus blocks NFAT/IL-2 (signaling).

flow.text: MMF blocks purine synthesis (proliferation).
Prednisone blocks cytokine transcription (broad suppression).
flow.subtext: Three layers, synergistic.

flow.text: Each drug at a tolerable dose. Avoids any single-drug toxicity ceiling.
flow.subtext: Calcineurin inhibitors are nephrotoxic at high doses.

flow.text: MMF causes diarrhea.
Steroids cause endocrine/bone/infection toxicity.
flow.subtext: Combining at low doses gets immunosuppressive synergy without driving any single toxicity to extreme.

flow.text: Steroid toxicities: diabetes, Cushingoid, osteoporosis, infection, mood, cataracts.
flow.subtext: Long list because of the breadth of glucocorticoid effects. Patients on chronic steroids need bone-density monitoring, diabetes screening, infection prophylaxis (e.g., PJP).
flow.insight: Many systemic toxicities from broad action

flow.text: Answer: B · Glucocorticoid receptor → broad anti-inflammatory action.
flow.subtext: Standard maintenance: calcineurin inhibitor + antimetabolite + corticosteroid.

flow.text: Each targets a different T-cell pathway node.
flow.subtext: Standard approach across kidney, liver, heart, lung transplants.
flow.insight: Each targets a different T-cell pathway node

flow.text: Chapter 13 closes. 9 stations · 17+ source questions · transplant immunology complete.
flow.subtext: MHC matching, three rejection types, GVHD, OKT3, calcineurin inhibitors, IL-2, NFAT, azathioprine, prednisone. Chapter 14 next: HIV.

stations.bridge: Why are corticosteroids in nearly every immunosuppressive regimen, and how do the major drug classes complement each other in a typical maintenance regimen?
stations.vignette: A 60-year-old kidney transplant recipient is maintained on a triple-drug regimen: tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil, and prednisone. The transplant pharmacist explains that each drug targets a different point in the T-cell activation pathway, providing combined immunosuppression while limiting any single drug's toxicity. Over years of treatment, the patient develops new-onset diabetes, osteoporosis, and Cushingoid features. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of corticosteroid immunosuppression and why this triple-drug strategy is used?

choices.text: Bind TCR directly; triple therapy = 3 TCR-blockers
Bind glucocorticoid receptor → broad anti-inflammatory effects
Inhibit calcineurin; triple therapy = 3 calcineurin inhibitors

keypoints: Corticosteroids are the most broadly used class of immunosuppressants and the oldest. They appear in essentially every transplant regimen, in autoimmune disease management, and in many oncologic settings. Their mechanism is broad transcriptional suppression of pro-inflammatory genes — and that breadth is both the source of their efficacy and the source of their toxicity.
How glucocorticoids work at the molecular level:

chapters.title: HIV
chapters.subtitle: A virus that disassembles the immune system — receptor binding, CD4 collapse, opportunistic infections.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: HIV life cycle
stations.subtitle: How HIV gets into a CD4 T cell, copies its RNA into DNA, integrates into the host genome — and why that DNA polymerase makes so many mistakes.

flow.text: HIV is a retrovirus — RNA genome that must be converted to DNA to replicate.
flow.subtext: Most viruses keep RNA throughout their life cycle. Retroviruses are unique.
flow.insight: Retrovirus = RNA → DNA → host genome integration

flow.text: Retroviruses use reverse transcriptase to make DNA from RNA.
flow.subtext: The DNA copy is then integrated into the host genome.
flow.insight: RT then integrase = the defining sequence

flow.text: Once integrated, the provirus is permanent.
flow.subtext: It replicates with the host every time the cell divides.

flow.text: Entry step 1: gp120 first binds CD4 on the target T cell.
flow.subtext: CD4 binding is the initial attachment.

flow.text: Entry step 2: CD4 binding triggers a conformational change in gp120.
flow.subtext: This exposes the previously hidden co-receptor binding site.

flow.text: Entry step 3: gp120 binds a co-receptor — CCR5 or CXCR4.
flow.subtext: CCR5 is used by transmitted strains; CXCR4 emerges later in disease.

flow.text: Entry step 4: gp41 fuses viral and cellular membranes.
flow.subtext: Capsid is delivered into the cytoplasm.

flow.text: Reverse transcriptase has three enzymatic activities.
flow.subtext: All three reside in a single enzyme.

flow.text: Activity 1: RNA-dependent DNA polymerase makes the first DNA strand.
flow.subtext: Uses host tRNA-Lys as the primer.

flow.text: Activity 2: RNase H degrades the RNA template after copying.
flow.subtext: Frees the new DNA strand for second-strand synthesis.
flow.insight: RNase H clears the RNA template

flow.text: Activity 3: DNA-dependent DNA polymerase makes the second DNA strand.
flow.subtext: Result: double-stranded DNA in the cytoplasm.

flow.text: RT lacks 3'→5' exonuclease (proofreading) activity.
flow.subtext: Most cellular and many viral DNA polymerases have a proofreading subunit.

flow.text: Misincorporated bases are NOT removed.
flow.subtext: Errors made during synthesis are kept in the new DNA.

flow.text: HIV genome ≈ 10⁴ nucleotides.
flow.subtext: Small RNA genome.

flow.text: RT misincorporation rate ≈ 10⁻⁴ per nucleotide.
flow.subtext: High error rate compared to host polymerases.

flow.text: 10⁴ nt × 10⁻⁴/nt = ~1 mutation per replication cycle.
flow.subtext: Roughly one new mutation each time the virus copies itself.

flow.text: Multiplied by 10⁹–10¹⁰ virions per day = enormous viral diversity within months.
flow.subtext: The patient harbors a swarm of related but non-identical variants.

flow.text: Diversity creates problem 1: antiretroviral resistance.
flow.subtext: Resistance variants pre-exist any drug exposure — drugs simply select them.

flow.text: Diversity creates problem 2: vaccine failure.
flow.subtext: Neutralizing antibody epitopes mutate constantly, escaping any fixed vaccine.

flow.text: Diversity creates problem 3: cytotoxic T-cell escape.
flow.subtext: CTL epitopes vary across the viral swarm.

flow.text: After RT: integrase inserts the proviral DNA into the host genome.
flow.subtext: The provirus becomes part of the cell's chromosome.

flow.text: The provirus is replicated by the host every time the cell divides.
flow.subtext: Indistinguishable from any host gene.

flow.text: Latent reservoirs of integrated provirus exist in resting memory T cells.
flow.subtext: These cells live for decades and harbor silent provirus.

flow.text: ART suppresses replication but does NOT touch silent integrated provirus.
flow.subtext: This is why HIV cannot be cured by current therapy.

flow.text: After budding: viral protease cleaves the Gag and Gag-Pol polyproteins.
flow.subtext: Polyproteins are precursors that contain multiple linked proteins.

flow.text: Cleavage releases individual structural and enzymatic proteins.
flow.subtext: These reorganize into the mature conical capsid.

flow.text: Without cleavage, virions remain immature and non-infectious.
flow.subtext: Maturation is the final required step for infectivity.

flow.text: Answer: C · RT lacks 3'→5' exonuclease proofreading.
flow.subtext: Each step of the life cycle is a drug target. Stations 06-09 walk through the major drug classes.
flow.insight: Answer: C · No proofreading = high error rate

stations.bridge: HIV genome is RNA. Host cells store DNA. How does an RNA virus permanently colonize a DNA-based host cell — and why does its replication machinery generate so many mutations?
stations.vignette: A 28-year-old man newly diagnosed with HIV asks how the virus actually replicates inside his cells. The clinician explains that HIV uses a unique enzyme that copies its RNA genome into DNA. This enzyme has an error rate roughly 10⁴-fold higher than typical human DNA polymerases — generating massive sequence diversity that fuels both immune escape and antiretroviral resistance. Which of the following best explains why this enzyme has such a high error rate?

choices.text: It uses RNA primers exclusively, which lack accuracy
It is a DNA-dependent DNA polymerase that operates outside the nucleus
It is reverse transcriptase, which lacks 3'→5' exonuclease (proofreading) activity
It deliberately introduces mutations to evade the immune system through a programmed mechanism
It uses error-prone host DNA polymerase β as its catalytic subunit

keypoints: HIV is a retrovirus — its genome is single-stranded RNA, but it must convert that RNA into DNA and integrate the DNA into the host genome to replicate. Understanding the life cycle is the framework for understanding everything that follows: vaccine failure, immune escape, antiretroviral mechanisms, and resistance.
The HIV life cycle in seven steps:

Why HIV vaccines have been so difficult: sequence diversity from RT error-proneness means the gp120 envelope (the main neutralizing antibody target) is one of the most variable proteins known. Glycan shielding further hides conserved regions. Designing a vaccine that elicits broadly neutralizing antibodies remains one of the great unsolved problems in immunology. Station 02 zooms in on this issue.

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: Antigen variation
stations.subtitle: Why neutralizing antibodies against HIV gp120 stop working within weeks of infection — and why this is the central reason 40 years of HIV vaccine research has failed.

flow.text: gp120 has five hypervariable loops (V1-V5) under intense immune selection.
flow.subtext: These surface-exposed loops are immunodominant — most antibodies target them. They tolerate massive sequence variation without losing CD4-binding function.

flow.text: Conserved regions (like CD4 binding site) are masked by host-derived glycans.
flow.subtext: ~50% of gp120 mass is N-linked glycans.

flow.text: Glycans are recognized as self → poor immunogenicity.
flow.subtext: They cover conserved protein surfaces, making them inaccessible to antibodies.
flow.insight: Glycans are recognized as self → poor immunogenicity

flow.text: Patient's antibodies select against the dominant variant → resistant variants emerge.
flow.subtext: Within weeks, the viral swarm in the patient shifts toward variants the antibodies don't neutralize.

flow.text: New antibody response starts against the new variant.
flow.subtext: Always one step behind.
flow.insight: New antibody response starts against the new variant

flow.text: Same problem applies between patients: vaccine envelope ≠ partner's envelope.
flow.subtext: If you make antibodies against vaccine strain gp120, they may not neutralize a transmitted virus from another patient with extensive V-loop differences.

flow.text: Vaccine-induced strain-specific immunity doesn't translate to broad protection.
Broadly neutralizing antibodies (bnAbs) target conserved sites — only ~10-20% of patients develop them, and only after years.
flow.subtext: bnAbs target CD4 binding site, V3 glycan, MPER, or quaternary trimer epitopes.
flow.insight: bnAbs rare and slow to develop

flow.text: Heavily somatically hypermutated.
Take years to develop.
flow.subtext: Inspiring current vaccine design.

flow.text: LTR variation adds another layer of viral diversity beyond envelope.
flow.subtext: Long terminal repeats contain promoter/enhancer elements.

flow.text: Variant LTRs have different transcriptional activities.
flow.subtext: More fitness variation for selection to act on.
flow.insight: Variant LTRs have different transcriptional activities

flow.text: Answer: B · Hypervariable loops shield conserved epitopes.
flow.subtext: This is the central reason 40 years of HIV vaccine research has failed.

flow.text: RV144 (31% efficacy, 2009) is the only positive vaccine trial ever; subsequent trials have all failed.
flow.subtext: Current focus: bnAb-based strategies.

stations.bridge: Patient develops neutralizing antibodies against gp120. Six weeks later, the antibodies don't work anymore. Why?
stations.vignette: A 32-year-old woman is enrolled in an HIV vaccine trial. Six months after vaccination, she develops anti-gp120 neutralizing antibodies that effectively block infection by the vaccine strain virus in vitro. However, when researchers test her antibodies against autologous virus from a recently infected partner, neutralization is poor. Sequencing of gp120 from the partner's virus reveals extensive amino acid differences from the vaccine strain in five hypervariable regions of the envelope. Which of the following best explains why anti-gp120 antibodies fail to provide broad protection against HIV?

choices.text: gp120 is fully conserved between strains
gp120 has hypervariable loops shielding conserved epitopes
gp120 is shed before antibodies can engage

keypoints: Five hypervariable loops (V1-V5): These loops are surface-exposed and immunodominant — most antibody responses target them. They tolerate enormous sequence variation without losing function.

Glycan shield: ~50% of gp120 mass is host-derived N-linked glycans. These glycans cover conserved regions of the protein, making them inaccessible to most antibodies. Glycans are recognized as "self" by the immune system → poor immunogenicity.
Conformational masking: The CD4-binding site is conserved (because it must bind CD4) but only fully exposed transiently after CD4 binding. Pre-binding gp120 conceals this region.
Trimeric envelope spike: gp120 functions as a trimer with gp41. Many epitopes are protein-protein interfaces that are inaccessible to antibodies.
Quaternary epitopes: Some functional epitopes only exist on the trimer, not on monomeric gp120 used in vaccines — so vaccine-induced antibodies often don't recognize the actual target.

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: Acute HIV
stations.subtitle: Why a 26-year-old with fever, sore throat, lymphadenopathy, and a rash gets a negative monospot — and what test should be sent next.

flow.text: Pattern: fever + pharyngitis + lymphadenopathy + maculopapular rash 2-4 weeks post-exposure = ARS.
flow.subtext: Acute retroviral syndrome.

flow.text: Occurs in 50-90% of newly infected patients.
flow.subtext: Often described as 'the worst flu of my life.' Commonly missed because it mimics other viral syndromes.
flow.insight: Occurs in 50-90% of newly infected patients

flow.text: Atypical lymphocytes + negative monospot tells you it's NOT EBV mono — high suspicion for ARS.
flow.subtext: EBV mono has positive heterophile antibody (monospot). Negative monospot in mono-like syndrome → expand differential to HIV ARS, CMV, acute toxo, primary syphilis.
flow.insight: Negative monospot + mono-like syndrome = expand DDx

flow.text: HIV antibody may still be negative at 3 weeks (window period before seroconversion).
flow.subtext: Antibody seroconversion occurs at 3-12 weeks post-infection. At 3 weeks, antibody-only tests can be negative even though the patient is highly infected and infectious.

flow.text: HIV RNA viral load is the earliest positive test — turns positive ~10-14 days post-exposure.
flow.subtext: During ARS, viral loads are typically >10⁶ copies/mL — extremely high.

flow.text: Highly infectious phase.
flow.subtext: RNA testing identifies infection well before antibody seroconversion.

flow.text: 4th-generation Ag/Ab test detects p24 antigen + antibody — turns positive earlier than antibody-only.
flow.subtext: Detects both HIV antibodies AND p24 viral capsid antigen.

flow.text: Catches infection 2-4 weeks earlier than 3rd-gen antibody-only tests.
flow.subtext: Modern standard of care first-line test.

flow.text: Standard ARS workup: 4th-gen Ag/Ab test + HIV RNA viral load together.
flow.subtext: RNA + Ag/Ab combination catches almost all ARS cases. Confirm with HIV-1/2 differentiation assay or Western blot once antibodies develop.

flow.text: Answer: C · HIV RNA + 4th-gen Ag/Ab — captures the seronegative window.
flow.subtext: Treatment as prevention (TasP): early diagnosis + immediate ART + sustained undetectable viral load = no sexual transmission (U=U). Public health and individual benefit.

stations.bridge: 26yo with mono-like illness + maculopapular rash 3 weeks after high-risk exposure. Monospot negative. What's next?
stations.vignette: A 26-year-old man presents with fever (39°C), severe pharyngitis, cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy, malaise, headache, and a diffuse maculopapular rash on his trunk for 7 days. He reports a high-risk sexual encounter approximately 3 weeks ago. Heterophile antibody (monospot) test is negative. CBC shows mild lymphocytosis with atypical lymphocytes. Which of the following is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

choices.text: HIV RNA viral load + 4th-gen Ag/Ab combination test

keypoints: Constitutional: Fever, fatigue, malaise, headache, myalgia, arthralgia. (Often described as "the worst flu of my life.")
Pharyngitis: Often without exudate (vs streptococcal pharyngitis with exudate).
Lymphadenopathy: Cervical, axillary, occipital — often bilateral.
Mucocutaneous: Maculopapular rash on trunk and proximal extremities (similar appearance to many viral rashes). Oral and genital ulcers in some patients.
GI: Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, weight loss.
Neurologic: Headache, aseptic meningitis, occasionally encephalitis.
Hematologic: Lymphocytosis with atypical lymphocytes (looks like mononucleosis), occasionally leukopenia.

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: OIs by CD4
stations.subtitle: Why CD4 count predicts which infections an HIV patient develops — and how the same threshold logic guides prophylaxis decisions.

flow.text: CD4 count is the single best predictor of HIV opportunistic infections.
flow.subtext: Each major OI has a characteristic CD4 threshold below which risk rises sharply. Memorize the thresholds and prophylaxis follows directly.

flow.text: CD4 <200 = PCP threshold. Pattern: bilateral interstitial + hypoxia worse with exertion + high LDH.
flow.subtext: Pneumocystis jirovecii (a fungus).

flow.text: Subacute progressive dyspnea over weeks.
Bilateral diffuse perihilar/interstitial infiltrates.
flow.subtext: Markedly elevated LDH correlates with severity.

flow.text: Diagnose with sputum induction or BAL + silver stain (GMS) or PCR.
flow.subtext: Cannot be cultured in vitro.
flow.insight: GMS stain or PCR confirms PCP

flow.text: Stain reveals the cysts.
PCR is highly sensitive.
flow.subtext: Treatment: TMP-SMX 21 days + corticosteroids if PaO₂ <70 mmHg.

flow.text: TMP-SMX prophylaxis at CD4 <200. Continue until CD4 >200 for >3 months on ART.
flow.subtext: Single-strength daily or double-strength 3x/week.

flow.text: Same TMP-SMX also prevents toxoplasmosis.
flow.subtext: Continue until immune recovery on ART.

flow.text: CD4 <100 thresholds: toxo encephalitis + cryptococcal meningitis.
flow.subtext: Toxo = multiple ring-enhancing brain lesions, responds to empiric pyrimethamine-sulfadiazine in ~2 weeks.

flow.text: Cryptococcus = subacute meningitis, ↑ICP, India ink + crypto antigen positive in CSF.
CD4 <50 thresholds: MAC + CMV + EBV-driven CNS lymphoma.
flow.subtext: MAC: disseminated mycobacterial infection, fevers, weight loss.

flow.text: CMV: retinitis (vision loss, pizza-pie appearance), colitis.
flow.subtext: CNS lymphoma: large periventricular brain lesion, CSF EBV PCR positive.

flow.text: Add azithromycin weekly at CD4 <50 for MAC prophylaxis.
flow.subtext: Azithromycin or clarithromycin.

flow.text: Continue until immune recovery.
flow.subtext: CMV prophylaxis not routinely given; surveillance for CMV viremia in some settings.

flow.text: Answer: B · PCP at CD4 <200 — prophylax with TMP-SMX.
flow.subtext: IRIS warning: starting ART in patients with low CD4 + active OI can paradoxically worsen the OI symptoms via reconstituting immune response. Continue both ART and OI treatment.

stations.bridge: HIV patient with CD4 count of 60 develops dyspnea + bilateral infiltrates. What's the most likely infection, and what other prophylaxis should be considered?
stations.vignette: A 38-year-old man with HIV (not on ART, last CD4 count 60 cells/μL six months ago) presents with 2 weeks of progressive dyspnea on exertion, dry cough, and low-grade fever. Chest X-ray shows bilateral diffuse interstitial infiltrates. Oxygen saturation is 88% on room air, dropping to 82% with ambulation. Serum LDH is markedly elevated. Sputum induction is performed. Which of the following infections is most likely, and at what CD4 thresholds should additional prophylaxis be considered?

choices.text: S. pneumoniae pneumonia; no prophylaxis required
PCP; start TMP-SMX prophylaxis at CD4 <200

keypoints:
CD4 <500:
  • Bacterial pneumonia (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae) — risk increased even at higher CD4.
Pulmonary tuberculosis (often atypical presentation if CD4 lower).
Mucocutaneous candidiasis (oral thrush, vaginal candidiasis).
Herpes zoster.
Kaposi sarcoma (HHV-8) can occur at any CD4 but is more common as CD4 drops.

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: HIV anergy
stations.subtitle: Why an HIV patient with active pulmonary TB has a 0 mm PPD reaction — and how immune dysfunction in HIV creates false-negative DTH testing across the board.

flow.text: PPD reads out CD4 Th1-mediated DTH (Ch 11 Station 08).
flow.subtext: Memory CD4 Th1 cells specific for mycobacterial antigens recognize PPD on local APC MHC II → release IFN-γ → activate macrophages → induration at 48-72 hours.

flow.text: HIV preferentially depletes and dysfunctions CD4 cells, including Th1 memory.
flow.subtext: CD4 count of 80 means most Th1 effector and memory cells are gone or exhausted.
flow.insight: HIV destroys the cells PPD needs

flow.text: Cytokine output (IFN-γ) is reduced.
flow.subtext: Macrophage activation through IFN-γ is impaired.

flow.text: Without functional CD4 Th1 memory, no DTH reaction = false-negative PPD.
flow.subtext: The patient may have prior TB exposure (in this case, active disease), but the recall response cannot mount. This is anergy — systemic loss of DTH responses.

flow.text: CDC uses ≥5 mm cutoff in HIV (vs ≥10/15 mm in immunocompetent) to maximize sensitivity.
flow.subtext: Lower threshold accepts more false positives but catches more true positives.

flow.text: Standard practice.
flow.subtext: Even so, severe CD4 depletion can produce PPD that's completely negative.

flow.text: IGRA also affected by CD4 depletion but somewhat more sensitive than PPD in HIV.
flow.subtext: IGRA tests in-vitro IFN-γ release by patient T cells exposed to TB-specific antigens.
flow.insight: IGRA somewhat better but not perfect

flow.text: Less affected by BCG vaccination.
flow.subtext: More sensitive than PPD in HIV but still loses sensitivity at very low CD4.

flow.text: If TB is clinically suspected → go directly to AFB smear, culture, NAAT. Don't rely on PPD/IGRA.
flow.subtext: Sputum AFB stain (this patient: positive).
flow.insight: Microbiology > immune testing in advanced HIV

flow.text: NAAT (PCR) confirms M. tuberculosis (this patient: positive).
flow.subtext: Direct microbiologic diagnosis bypasses the immune-test problem.

flow.text: Answer: B · CD4 depletion → anergy — DTH response fails.
flow.subtext: HIV/TB co-infection is dangerous: lifetime TB reactivation risk in immunocompetent latent TB is ~10%; in HIV+ it's 10% per YEAR. Latent TB treatment in HIV+ patients is critical.
flow.insight: Answer: B · anergy from CD4 depletion

stations.bridge: HIV patient with active cavitary TB has a 0 mm PPD reaction. Why does the test fail, and what's the standard PPD cutoff for HIV-positive patients?
stations.vignette: A 38-year-old man with HIV (CD4 count 80 cells/μL, not on ART) presents with 2 months of cough, hemoptysis, weight loss, and night sweats. Chest X-ray shows a right upper lobe cavitary lesion. Sputum AFB stain is positive and confirmed as Mycobacterium tuberculosis by PCR. Pre-treatment tuberculin skin test (PPD) shows 0 mm of induration at 48 hours. Which of the following best explains why his PPD is negative despite active pulmonary TB?

choices.text: Different mycobacterial species not cross-reactive with PPD
CD4 depletion → anergy → loss of delayed-type hypersensitivity
PPD requires intact B cells; lab error must explain it
Active TB always converts PPD to negative
CD8 cells suppress the PPD response

keypoints: HIV preferentially depletes CD4 T cells, including Th1 effector and memory cells.
Surviving CD4 cells become functionally exhausted (high PD-1, reduced cytokine production).
Th1-driven cytokine outputs (IFN-γ, TNF-α) decline.
Macrophage activation by Th1 IFN-γ is impaired.
The DTH skin reaction — which requires memory CD4 Th1 + IFN-γ + recruited macrophages — cannot mount.
PPD induration fails to develop even when prior mycobacterial exposure has occurred.

stations.label: Station 06
stations.title: NRTIs
stations.subtitle: Why zidovudine looks like thymidine to reverse transcriptase — but lacks the 3'-hydroxyl group needed to extend the DNA chain — and what that means for resistance.

flow.text: AZT is a thymidine analog. Other NRTIs are analogs of other nucleosides.
flow.subtext: AZT looks like thymidine to the polymerase.

flow.text: Lamivudine = cytidine analog.
flow.subtext: Tenofovir = adenine analog.

flow.text: Abacavir = guanine analog.
flow.subtext: Emtricitabine = cytidine analog (similar to lamivudine).

flow.text: All NRTIs lack a functional 3'-hydroxyl group — replaced by azide or other modification.
flow.subtext: AZT has azido group at the 3' position instead of OH.

flow.text: Tenofovir has acyclic structure.
flow.subtext: The missing 3'-OH is the key feature that makes them chain terminators.

flow.text: Cellular kinases phosphorylate NRTIs to triphosphate. Triphosphate is the active form.
flow.subtext: NRTIs are prodrugs.

flow.text: They need to be phosphorylated three times intracellularly.
flow.insight: They need to be phosphorylated three times intracellularly

flow.text: Tenofovir starts as nucleotide (already monophosphate) → easier activation.
flow.subtext: This step is rate-limiting for some drugs.

flow.text: RT incorporates the NRTI-TP into viral DNA, mistaking it for natural nucleotide.
flow.subtext: RT lacks proofreading (Station 01) — it doesn't discriminate well between natural and analog nucleotides. AZT-TP gets added to the 3' end of growing DNA strand.

flow.text: No 3'-OH → no phosphodiester bond → chain termination. Viral DNA synthesis aborts.
flow.subtext: Next nucleotide can't be added.

flow.text: Chain stops.
flow.subtext: Without complete viral DNA, no integration, no provirus, no replication.

flow.text: Marrow toxicity: NRTIs at high concentrations also affect host DNA polymerases (especially mtDNA Pol γ).
flow.subtext: Selectivity for RT is imperfect.
flow.insight: Off-target host pol effects → marrow toxicity

flow.text: High-replication cells (erythroid and myeloid precursors, mitochondria) are most affected.
flow.subtext: AZT classically causes anemia (often macrocytic) and neutropenia.

flow.text: Resistance: thymidine analog mutations (TAMs) in RT — alter binding pocket.
flow.subtext: M41L, D67N, K70R, L210W, T215Y/F, K219Q/E — accumulate sequentially.

flow.text: Some mutations let RT excise incorporated NRTIs.
flow.insight: Some mutations let RT excise incorporated NRTIs

flow.text: M184V (3TC resistance) is most common single mutation.
flow.subtext: Cross-resistance within the class is common.
flow.insight: M184V (3TC resistance) is most common single mutation

flow.text: Modern NRTI backbone: tenofovir/emtricitabine (TDF/FTC or TAF/FTC). AZT now rarely used.
