flow.subtext: TAF (tenofovir alafenamide) preferred over TDF — better renal and bone safety.

flow.text: Combined with FTC (emtricitabine, similar to lamivudine).
flow.subtext: Most modern HAART regimens use this NRTI backbone + integrase inhibitor.

flow.text: Answer: B · Thymidine analog → chain termination; marrow toxicity from mitochondrial damage.
flow.subtext: Tenofovir/emtricitabine NRTI backbone is the foundation of essentially every modern HIV treatment regimen. Same backbone is used for PrEP.

stations.bridge: Zidovudine (AZT) was the first antiretroviral. How does it actually stop HIV — and why does the patient need bone marrow toxicity monitoring?
stations.vignette: A 34-year-old patient with HIV is started on a regimen including zidovudine (AZT). Routine monitoring at 6 weeks shows a hemoglobin of 9.2 g/dL (down from 13.5) and absolute neutrophil count of 1100. The clinical pharmacist explains both the antiviral mechanism and the marrow toxicity. Which of the following best describes how zidovudine inhibits HIV replication, and why bone marrow suppression occurs?

choices.text: Allosteric RT inhibitor; marrow toxicity unrelated
Thymidine analog → AZT-TP causes chain termination + marrow toxicity
Protease inhibitor; marrow toxicity from cytokines
Envelope binder; marrow toxicity from immune complexes

keypoints: Nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs) were the first antiretroviral drug class — zidovudine (AZT) was approved in 1987. They remain the backbone of essentially every modern HAART regimen. Understanding the chain-termination mechanism is the foundation for understanding both efficacy and resistance.
NRTI mechanism in detail:

Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) and PEP: Tenofovir + emtricitabine (Truvada or Descovy) is the standard oral PrEP regimen for HIV prevention. Daily dosing reduces HIV acquisition by ~99% in adherent users. Newer: cabotegravir injectable PrEP every 2 months. PEP regimens combine NRTI backbone (TDF/FTC) with a third agent (typically dolutegravir or raltegravir) for 28 days.

stations.label: Station 07
stations.title: Protease inhibitors
stations.subtitle: Why protease inhibitors don't prevent virion assembly OR release — they just make sure the virions that bud off are non-infectious.

flow.text: HIV makes Gag and Gag-Pol polyproteins — long precursor proteins that need to be cleaved.
flow.subtext: Gag encodes structural proteins (MA, CA, NC, p6) as one polyprotein.
flow.insight: Polyproteins must be cleaved to mature

flow.text: Gag-Pol additionally encodes RT, integrase, and protease itself.
flow.subtext: All produced as long precursors needing cleavage.

flow.text: Polyproteins assemble at membrane → virion buds out, carrying polyproteins + protease.
flow.subtext: Budding happens normally with or without active protease. Virion structure depends on what happens AFTER budding.

flow.text: After budding, HIV protease cleaves Gag and Gag-Pol → individual mature proteins.
flow.subtext: HIV protease is an aspartyl protease (two Asp residues at active site).

flow.text: Cleaves at specific sites in the polyprotein.
flow.subtext: Result: discrete mature proteins (MA, CA, NC, p6, RT, IN, PR).
flow.insight: Cleaves at specific sites in the polyprotein

flow.text: Cleaved proteins reassemble into mature conical capsid → infectious virion.
flow.subtext: Mature capsid has the characteristic conical shape on EM.

flow.text: Without cleavage, capsid stays as immature doughnut shape.
flow.subtext: Mature shape required for infectivity.

flow.text: PIs bind protease active site → block cleavage → virions stay immature → non-infectious.
flow.subtext: Peptidomimetic drugs designed to fit the protease active site.
flow.insight: PI = active site inhibitor → no maturation

flow.text: Block polyprotein processing.
flow.subtext: Immature virions can bud but cannot establish productive infection in next cell.

flow.text: Ritonavir at low dose = CYP3A4 inhibitor, used to boost levels of other PIs.
flow.subtext: Ritonavir at therapeutic dose is itself a PI.

flow.text: At sub-therapeutic dose (100 mg), it potently inhibits CYP3A4, the cytochrome P450 enzyme that metabolizes most other PIs.
flow.subtext: Boosts plasma levels and prolongs half-life of co-administered PI.

flow.text: PI toxicity: insulin resistance + hyperlipidemia + lipodystrophy + drug interactions.
flow.subtext: Metabolic syndrome features.

flow.text: Newer PIs (darunavir) and modern regimens have reduced this.
flow.subtext: Atazanavir specifically: unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia (UGT1A1 inhibition).
flow.insight: Newer PIs (darunavir) and modern regimens have reduced this

flow.text: Indinavir: kidney stones.
flow.subtext: Many CYP3A4-mediated drug interactions.

flow.text: Resistance: multiple mutations needed for high-level PI resistance (high genetic barrier).
flow.subtext: Codons 30, 32, 46, 47, 48, 50, 54, 76, 82, 84, 88, 90 — multiple mutations typically required, especially for darunavir.

flow.text: Higher barrier than NRTIs, much higher than NNRTIs.
flow.subtext: Useful for salvage regimens.

flow.text: Modern context: INSTIs (dolutegravir, bictegravir) have replaced PIs as first-line. PIs reserved for resistance + pregnancy.
flow.subtext: Modern preferred regimens: bictegravir/TAF/FTC, dolutegravir/3TC/abacavir.
flow.insight: INSTIs > PIs as modern first-line anchor

flow.text: INSTIs have similar potency, fewer drug interactions, simpler dosing.
flow.subtext: PIs still valuable for salvage and pregnancy.

flow.text: Answer: B · PIs block Gag-Pol cleavage; ritonavir boosts via CYP3A4 inhibition.
flow.subtext: Same drug at high dose = anti-HIV PI.
flow.insight: Answer: B · protease block · ritonavir = booster

flow.text: At low dose = pharmacokinetic enhancer.
flow.subtext: Cobicistat is the newer alternative boost without anti-HIV activity.

stations.bridge: Patient on a PI-based regimen has electron microscopy of newly budded virions showing immature, uncleaved capsids. Why are these virions non-infectious?
stations.vignette: A 42-year-old patient with HIV is on a regimen including ritonavir-boosted darunavir (a protease inhibitor). Electron microscopy of newly budded virions from his cells shows immature particles with uncleaved Gag and Gag-Pol polyproteins still in a doughnut-shaped configuration rather than the mature conical capsid. Despite normal viral budding, these virions are non-infectious. Which of the following best describes how protease inhibitors work, and why ritonavir is added?

choices.text: PIs block reverse transcription; ritonavir is a stronger RT inhibitor
PIs block protease cleavage of Gag-Pol; ritonavir boosts via CYP3A4 inhibition
PIs block viral assembly; ritonavir prevents drug efflux
PIs block CD4 binding; ritonavir is an immune modulator
PIs integrate into viral DNA; ritonavir prolongs renal half-life

keypoints: HIV protease is one of the most successful drug targets in antiviral therapy. The enzyme is essential for the final maturation step of newly assembled virions: it cleaves the Gag and Gag-Pol polyproteins into individual structural proteins (matrix, capsid, nucleocapsid) and enzymatic proteins (RT, integrase, protease itself). Without this cleavage, virions bud off as immature, non-infectious particles.
HIV protease and Gag-Pol cleavage:

stations.label: Station 08
stations.title: Fusion inhibitors
stations.subtitle: Why enfuvirtide is a 36-amino-acid peptide given by SUBCUTANEOUS injection — and how it physically prevents HIV's entry into the cell.

flow.text: HIV entry: gp120 binds CD4 → CCR5/CXCR4 → triggers gp41 conformational change → membrane fusion.
flow.subtext: From Station 01. After CD4 + co-receptor binding, gp41 inserts its fusion peptide into target membrane and undergoes the conformational change that drives membrane fusion.
flow.insight: Entry sequence: CD4 → co-receptor → gp41 fusion

flow.text: gp41 has two heptad repeat regions: HR1 (N-terminal) and HR2 (C-terminal).
flow.subtext: These two regions interact during the fusion process.

flow.text: HR2 folds back on HR1, forming a structure called the six-helix bundle that physically pulls viral and cellular membranes together.
Six-helix bundle formation physically drives membrane fusion.
flow.subtext: Each gp41 monomer contributes HR1 and HR2 segments that interlock as a coiled-coil.

flow.text: Three gp41 molecules together form the six-helix bundle.
flow.subtext: The conformational change releases energy that pulls the membranes into contact.
flow.insight: Three gp41 molecules together form the six-helix bundle

flow.text: Enfuvirtide is a 36-aa peptide whose sequence mimics HR2.
flow.subtext: Designed to look like the HR2 region.

flow.text: When present, it binds HR1 instead of allowing native HR2 to bind.
flow.subtext: Acts as competitive decoy.

flow.text: Enfuvirtide binds HR1 → native HR2 can't zip up → no six-helix bundle → no fusion.
flow.subtext: Because enfuvirtide occupies the HR1 binding site, the native HR2 cannot complete the bundle.

flow.text: The mechanical fusion process can't proceed.
flow.subtext: Virus stays outside the cell.
flow.insight: The mechanical fusion process can't proceed

flow.text: Subcutaneous injection required because oral peptides would be digested.
flow.subtext: 36 amino acids is too large for oral absorption and would be cleaved by gastric/intestinal proteases.

flow.text: SC injection twice daily.
flow.subtext: Injection site reactions are common — limits adoption.

flow.text: Other entry inhibitors: maraviroc (CCR5 antagonist), ibalizumab (anti-CD4 mAb), fostemsavir (gp120 attachment), lenacapavir (capsid).
flow.subtext: Maraviroc = oral CCR5 blocker, requires tropism testing.

flow.text: Ibalizumab = IV anti-CD4 every 2 weeks.
Fostemsavir = oral gp120 inhibitor.
flow.subtext: Lenacapavir = subQ every 6 months capsid inhibitor (newest).

flow.text: Resistance: HR1 mutations (enfuvirtide) or tropism switch CCR5→CXCR4 (maraviroc).
flow.subtext: Enfuvirtide resistance is mutations in HR1 that reduce enfuvirtide binding without breaking fusion.

flow.text: Maraviroc resistance is selection of CXCR4-tropic variants that don't depend on CCR5.
flow.insight: Maraviroc resistance is selection of CXCR4-tropic variants...

flow.text: Answer: B · Enfuvirtide binds gp41 HR1, blocking the fusion bundle.
flow.subtext: Mostly used for salvage regimens in patients with multi-drug resistance. Newer agents (lenacapavir 2x/year SC) are reshaping the entry inhibitor landscape.

stations.bridge: Treatment-experienced patient with multi-drug-resistant HIV is started on enfuvirtide (T-20). It must be given by subcutaneous injection. What's the mechanism?
stations.vignette: A 50-year-old patient with HIV who has failed multiple antiretroviral regimens due to extensive resistance is started on enfuvirtide (T-20) as part of a salvage regimen. The drug is a 36-amino-acid peptide administered by subcutaneous injection twice daily. He develops painful injection site reactions. The infectious diseases consultant explains that enfuvirtide works at a step before the virus enters the cell. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of enfuvirtide?

choices.text: Enfuvirtide binds CD4, preventing gp120 attachment
Enfuvirtide binds gp41 HR1, preventing six-helix bundle fusion
Enfuvirtide inhibits RT by binding viral RNA directly
Enfuvirtide neutralizes free virions in plasma

keypoints: Enfuvirtide (T-20, brand name Fuzeon) was the first FDA-approved entry inhibitor for HIV (2003). It's a fascinating drug: a 36-amino-acid peptide that physically interferes with the membrane fusion step. Its peptide nature dictates the unusual subcutaneous administration (oral peptides would be digested) and the relatively limited adoption due to injection site reactions and inconvenient dosing.
HIV membrane fusion mechanism (the target):

stations.label: Station 09
stations.title: ART resistance
stations.subtitle: Why a patient with undetectable viral load on HAART should NEVER be told to stop therapy — and how the modern regimen ties together everything we've built.

flow.text: NEVER stop suppressive ART. Latent reservoir means undetectable ≠ eradicated.
flow.subtext: Plasma viral load reflects ACTIVE replication, which ART suppresses.

flow.text: But integrated proviral DNA in resting memory CD4 cells is invisible to ART — it just sits there, transcriptionally silent.
flow.insight: But integrated proviral DNA in resting memory CD4 cells is...

flow.text: Stopping ART → latent provirus reactivates → viral rebound within 2-4 weeks.
flow.subtext: The reservoir is the barrier to cure.

flow.text: Reactivation can come from any of the long-lived memory T cells carrying integrated provirus.
flow.subtext: Patient returns to viremic state within weeks.
flow.insight: Reactivation can come from any of the long-lived memory T...

flow.text: Combination therapy needed because monotherapy selects resistance within weeks.
flow.subtext: RT error-proneness (Station 01) means resistant variants pre-exist any drug exposure.

flow.text: Single-drug pressure rapidly selects them out.
flow.subtext: The question becomes which resistant variant emerges first, not whether resistance happens.
flow.insight: Single-drug pressure rapidly selects them out

flow.text: Three drugs at 2+ classes → simultaneous mutations needed at multiple targets → insurmountable barrier.
flow.subtext: For the virus to escape three drugs at three different sites, it would need three simultaneous mutations — exponentially less likely than single mutations.

flow.text: Suppression remains durable as long as adherence is maintained.
flow.insight: Suppression remains durable as long as adherence is...

flow.text: Modern first-line: 2 NRTIs + INSTI. Examples: BIC/TAF/FTC; DTG/3TC/ABC.
flow.subtext: INSTI as anchor drug (high potency, high genetic barrier).

flow.text: NRTI backbone (TAF/FTC most common).
Single tablet daily for adherence.
flow.subtext: Replaces older PI- and NNRTI-based regimens.

flow.text: U=U principle: Undetectable viral load → no sexual transmission of HIV.
flow.subtext: Established by HPTN 052, PARTNER 1/2, Opposites Attract.

flow.text: Treatment as prevention is a major public health tool.
flow.subtext: Identification + immediate ART + adherence reduces transmission to essentially zero through sexual routes.
flow.insight: Treatment as prevention is a major public health tool

flow.text: Major toxicities to monitor: renal (TDF), bone, hypersensitivity (abacavir/HLA-B*5701), weight gain (DTG).
flow.subtext: TDF replaced by TAF in most regimens (better renal/bone profile).

flow.text: HLA-B*5701 screening required before abacavir use.
flow.subtext: INSTIs cause weight gain and may have CNS effects.
flow.insight: HLA-B*5701 screening required before abacavir use

flow.text: Drug interactions: watch CYP3A4 (PIs, ritonavir, cobicistat); polyvalent cation interactions (INSTIs).
flow.subtext: Ritonavir/cobicistat boosting causes many drug interactions (statins, BZDs, ergots, sildenafil, anticonvulsants).

flow.text: INSTIs chelate with calcium, magnesium, iron, aluminum — separate from antacids/supplements.
Answer: D · Latent reservoir + combination therapy at multiple targets prevents resistance.
flow.subtext: Cure remains the unsolved problem in HIV.
flow.insight: Answer: D · Reservoir + combo at multiple targets

flow.text: Strategies under investigation: shock-and-kill, gene editing (CCR5 disruption), bnAbs, therapeutic vaccines.
flow.subtext: None yet broadly successful.

flow.text: Chapter 14 closes. 9 stations · 30+ source questions · HIV catalog complete.
flow.subtext: Life cycle, vaccine challenges, ARS, OIs, anergy, NRTIs, PIs, entry inhibitors, integrated regimens. Chapter 15 next: broader infection immunology.

stations.bridge: HIV patient on tenofovir/emtricitabine/dolutegravir for 3 years has undetectable viral load. He asks if he can stop the medications. What's the answer, and why is combination therapy needed?
stations.vignette: A 41-year-old man with HIV has been on a single-tablet regimen of tenofovir alafenamide / emtricitabine / bictegravir for 3 years. His most recent labs show HIV RNA <20 copies/mL (undetectable) and CD4 count of 720. He asks his physician whether he can stop antiretroviral therapy now that his virus is "gone." Which of the following is the most appropriate response and best explains why combination therapy is required?

choices.text: CD4 recovery to >500 indicates immune reconstitution → safe to discontinue
Sustained suppression eliminates infectiousness (U=U) → therapy can taper
Bictegravir's genetic barrier permits INSTI monotherapy after suppression
Integrated provirus persists in resting CD4; combo blocks pre-existing resistance
ART eradicates replicating virus; lifelong only because of reinfection

keypoints: HIV's high mutation rate (Station 01) means resistance variants pre-exist any drug exposure.
Monotherapy → resistant variant outgrows wild-type within weeks.
Two-drug therapy → resistance still develops, just slower.
Three-drug therapy with drugs from at least 2 classes → simultaneous mutations needed at multiple targets → essentially insurmountable barrier under suppressive treatment.
Modern first-line regimens use ≥3 active drugs targeting at least 2 different viral processes.

chapters.title: Infection Immunology
chapters.subtitle: How specific pathogens get past, hide from, or hijack the system you've just learned.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: Granulomas
stations.subtitle: Why sarcoidosis, tuberculosis, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, and toxoplasmosis all generate granulomas — and how histology distinguishes them.

flow.text: Granuloma = universal Th1/macrophage response to persistent antigen.
flow.subtext: Activated macrophages (epithelioid + giant cells) at center, CD4 Th1 cells at rim.

flow.text: IFN-γ + TNF maintain the structure.
flow.subtext: Same architecture across many diseases.

flow.text: Caseation (central necrosis) = persistent intracellular pathogen damaging cells.
flow.subtext: TB is the classic caseating granuloma.

flow.text: Fungal infections (histo, cocci) also caseate.
flow.subtext: The necrosis reflects accumulated cell death from intracellular pathogen burden.

flow.text: Non-caseating = sarcoid, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, Crohn, foreign body — no persistent pathogen requiring destruction.
flow.subtext: Same granuloma architecture, no central necrosis. Cause may be unknown (sarcoid), known antigen (HP), self (Crohn), or inert material (foreign body).

flow.text: Bilateral hilar adenopathy + erythema nodosum + non-caseating + negative stains = Löfgren syndrome / sarcoidosis.
flow.subtext: Classic Löfgren presentation.

flow.text: Strong association with female sex and Northern European or African-American ancestry.
flow.subtext: Often resolves spontaneously over months.
flow.insight: Strong association with female sex and Northern European or...

flow.text: Answer: B · Non-caseating granulomas of sarcoidosis (Löfgren variant).
flow.subtext: Treatment: most resolve spontaneously.

flow.text: Symptomatic patients get steroids first-line.
flow.subtext: Refractory: methotrexate, anti-TNF (infliximab — note this is the same drug that REACTIVATES TB granulomas; covered in Ch 12 Station 06).

stations.bridge: Patient with non-caseating granulomas in mediastinal lymph nodes + skin nodules. Differential includes sarcoid, TB, fungi, hypersensitivity pneumonitis. What additional features distinguish them?
stations.vignette: A 38-year-old woman presents with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, dyspnea on exertion, and tender erythematous nodules on her shins (erythema nodosum). Mediastinal lymph node biopsy shows multiple non-caseating granulomas with epithelioid macrophages, multinucleated giant cells, and surrounding CD4 T lymphocytes. AFB and fungal stains are negative. She has no occupational exposures. Which of the following best characterizes the granulomatous immune response and the most likely diagnosis?

choices.text: Caseating granulomas of pulmonary TB; treat with RIPE
Non-caseating granulomas of sarcoidosis; CD4 Th1-driven

keypoints: Center: Activated macrophages → epithelioid cells (elongated, eosinophilic) and multinucleated giant cells.
Rim: CD4 Th1 lymphocytes.
Driving cytokines: Th1 IFN-γ activates macrophages; TNF-α drives giant cell formation and maintains structure.

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: Herpesviruses
stations.subtitle: Why eight related viruses (HHV-1 through HHV-8) all establish lifelong latency — and how the syndromes they produce reflect the cell types they infect.

flow.text: Unilateral dermatomal vesicular rash + prior chickenpox = zoster (VZV reactivation).
flow.subtext: Pattern recognition.

flow.text: Dermatomal distribution that doesn't cross midline + history of varicella = shingles.
flow.subtext: Tzanck smear shows multinucleated giant cells (also positive in HSV).
flow.insight: Dermatomal distribution that doesn't cross midline +...

flow.text: VZV is latent in dorsal root ganglia after primary chickenpox.
flow.subtext: Primary chickenpox infection seeds the dorsal root ganglia.

flow.text: Virus persists in episomal form.
flow.subtext: Reactivation occurs years to decades later, often triggered by aging, stress, or immunosuppression.

flow.text: All 8 herpesviruses establish latency, each in characteristic cells.
flow.subtext: HSV-1 trigeminal ganglion, HSV-2 sacral DRG, VZV DRG, EBV B cells, CMV monocytes, HHV-6/7 T cells, HHV-8 endothelium/B cells. Reactivation pattern follows tissue tropism.

flow.text: Acyclovir/valacyclovir mechanism: activated by viral TK → triphosphate → DNA polymerase chain terminator.
flow.subtext: Prodrug enters cells.

flow.text: Step 1: viral thymidine kinase phosphorylates acyclovir.
flow.subtext: This is the selectivity step — only HSV/VZV-infected cells phosphorylate.

flow.text: Step 2: host kinases complete the phosphorylation to triphosphate.
flow.subtext: Active form is acyclovir-TP.

flow.text: Step 3: viral DNA polymerase incorporates acyclovir-TP into viral DNA.
flow.subtext: Acts as a chain terminator — replication stops.

flow.text: Selectivity: viral TK has much higher affinity for the drug than host TK.
flow.subtext: Host cells barely activate the drug.

flow.text: Viral TK in infected cells phosphorylates it efficiently.
flow.insight: Viral TK in infected cells phosphorylates it efficiently

flow.text: Result: high concentration of active drug in infected cells, low concentration elsewhere.
flow.subtext: Excellent therapeutic index.
flow.insight: Result: high concentration of active drug in infected cells

flow.text: Acyclovir spectrum: HSV-1, HSV-2, VZV. Limited activity against EBV/CMV.
flow.subtext: CMV doesn't have classic TK; uses UL97 kinase that activates ganciclovir but not acyclovir well. So CMV infections need ganciclovir/valganciclovir, foscarnet, or cidofovir.
flow.insight: Acyclovir for HSV/VZV; ganciclovir for CMV

flow.text: Influenza contrast: RNA virus, no latency, antigenic shift via reassortment in pigs.
flow.subtext: Influenza is the opposite paradigm to herpesviruses.

flow.text: Antigenic drift = point mutation, yearly seasonal evolution.
Antigenic shift = reassortment of segmented genome, pandemic potential.
Treat with neuraminidase inhibitors (oseltamivir).
Answer: B · VZV in dorsal root ganglia · valacyclovir activated by viral TK.
flow.subtext: Postherpetic neuralgia is the major complication, especially in elderly. Shingrix vaccine (recombinant subunit) recommended ≥age 50 for prevention.

stations.bridge: Patient with prior chickenpox develops painful unilateral dermatomal vesicles. What's reactivating, where was it latent, and what drug?
stations.vignette: A 65-year-old man develops a painful, vesicular rash in a unilateral T5 dermatomal distribution that does not cross the midline. He had chickenpox at age 6. The vesicles are on an erythematous base. Tzanck smear shows multinucleated giant cells. He is treated with valacyclovir. Which of the following correctly describes the virus, its site of latency, and the antiviral mechanism?

choices.text: VZV, dorsal root ganglia; valacyclovir requires viral TK to activate
EBV, B cells, valacyclovir for nasopharyngeal carcinoma
HHV-8, endothelial cells, acyclovir for Kaposi sarcoma

keypoints: HHV-1 (HSV-1): Trigeminal ganglion. Reactivation = orolabial herpes (cold sores), HSV-1 keratitis, HSV encephalitis (temporal lobe).
HHV-2 (HSV-2): Sacral dorsal root ganglia. Reactivation = genital herpes; can cause neonatal HSV via vertical transmission during delivery.
HHV-3 (VZV, varicella-zoster): Dorsal root ganglia (multiple levels) after primary chickenpox. Reactivation = zoster (shingles) in unilateral dermatomal distribution.
HHV-4 (EBV): Memory B cells. Primary = infectious mononucleosis. Reactivation/persistence in immunosuppressed = PTLD, CNS lymphoma, Burkitt, NPC, gastric carcinoma.

HHV-5 (CMV): Monocytes, dendritic cells, endothelium. Primary mostly asymptomatic in immunocompetent. Disease in immunocompromised = retinitis, esophagitis, colitis, pneumonitis, encephalitis, congenital infection.
HHV-6 and HHV-7: T cells. HHV-6 causes roseola (sixth disease) in children. Often subclinical.
HHV-8 (KSHV): Endothelial cells, B cells. Causes Kaposi sarcoma (especially in HIV/AIDS), primary effusion lymphoma, multicentric Castleman disease.

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: TORCH infections
stations.subtitle: Why a newborn with petechiae, sensorineural hearing loss, and periventricular calcifications gets a focused infection workup — and what each TORCH agent contributes.

flow.text: TORCH framework: Toxo · Other · Rubella · CMV · HSV.
flow.subtext: Mnemonic for congenital infections crossing placenta or transmitted intrapartum. "Other" includes syphilis, parvo, VZV, listeria, HIV, Zika.

flow.text: PERIVENTRICULAR calcifications = CMV signature.
flow.subtext: CMV calcifications cluster around the ventricles.

flow.text: Toxo calcifications are typically more diffuse throughout the brain.
flow.subtext: Distinct neuroimaging pattern.
flow.insight: Toxo calcifications are typically more diffuse throughout...

flow.text: Sensorineural hearing loss + microcephaly + blueberry muffin = classic CMV.
flow.subtext: CMV is the most common cause of congenital sensorineural hearing loss.

flow.text: Microcephaly reflects severe CNS infection.
flow.subtext: Blueberry muffin = extramedullary hematopoiesis (also seen in rubella).

flow.text: CMV is the most common congenital viral infection (~1% of live births).
flow.subtext: Most adults are seropositive.
flow.insight: CMV = most common congenital viral infection

flow.text: Primary maternal CMV in pregnancy carries highest risk.
flow.insight: Primary maternal CMV in pregnancy carries highest risk

flow.text: Reactivation also possible.
flow.subtext: Most congenital CMV is asymptomatic at birth.

flow.text: Diagnose by CMV PCR from infant urine or saliva in first 3 weeks of life.
flow.subtext: After 3 weeks, can't distinguish congenital from postnatally acquired infection.

flow.text: Universal newborn hearing screening with reflex CMV testing is key for early identification of asymptomatic cases.
flow.insight: Universal newborn hearing screening with reflex CMV testing...

flow.text: Treatment: oral valganciclovir for symptomatic congenital CMV. Improves hearing outcomes.
flow.subtext: 6 months of valganciclovir reduces hearing loss progression in symptomatic infants. Asymptomatic cases generally not treated, but monitored for hearing.

flow.text: Answer: C · Congenital CMV.
flow.subtext: Universal newborn hearing screening + reflex CMV testing in positive screens = best current strategy for early identification. CMV vaccine in development.

stations.bridge: Newborn with maculopapular rash, periventricular calcifications, sensorineural hearing loss. What's the differential, and which finding distinguishes CMV from rubella?
stations.vignette: A 36-week-gestation newborn presents with petechial rash described as "blueberry muffin," sensorineural hearing loss on screening, microcephaly, and periventricular calcifications on neuroimaging. Mother had a febrile illness with rash early in pregnancy but did not seek care. Maternal IgG titer to a specific TORCH agent is markedly elevated. Which of the following best describes the clinical pattern, the most likely agent, and how it is distinguished from other TORCH infections?

choices.text: Toxoplasmosis: ring-enhancing brain lesions and chorioretinitis from maternal raw meat or cat exposure; calcifications are diffuse, not periventricular
Rubella: classic triad of cataracts, sensorineural deafness, and patent ductus arteriosus from first-trimester maternal infection; preventable by MMR vaccination
Cytomegalovirus: most common congenital viral infection; PERIVENTRICULAR calcifications, sensorineural hearing loss, microcephaly; diagnosed by infant urine/saliva CMV PCR in first 3 weeks of life
Herpes simplex virus: vesicular skin lesions and encephalitis from intrapartum exposure
Syphilis: snuffles, saddle nose, hepatosplenomegaly, Hutchinson teeth

keypoints:
T · Toxoplasma gondii:
  • Maternal exposure: undercooked meat, cat litter (oocysts in feces).
Classic triad: chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, intracranial calcifications (typically diffuse).
Most affected: severe disease in primary maternal infection during first trimester.
Treatment: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + leucovorin postnatally.

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: Parasites
stations.subtitle: Why Plasmodium vivax requires PRIMAQUINE in addition to chloroquine to prevent relapse — and how each Plasmodium species + treatment differs.

flow.text: P. vivax + P. ovale form HYPNOZOITES — dormant liver-stage parasites.
flow.subtext: Sporozoites enter hepatocytes; some replicate (causing blood-stage disease), others enter dormancy. Hypnozoites can persist for months to years before reactivating.

flow.text: Chloroquine kills blood-stage parasites only. Doesn't touch hypnozoites.
flow.subtext: Symptoms resolve with chloroquine because blood stage is cleared.

flow.text: But hypnozoites in liver are unaffected.
flow.subtext: Patient is symptom-free until hypnozoites reactivate.
flow.insight: But hypnozoites in liver are unaffected

flow.text: PRIMAQUINE eradicates hypnozoites — required for radical cure of vivax/ovale.
flow.subtext: Only primaquine (and newer tafenoquine) effective against liver hypnozoites. Without it, treatment is incomplete and relapse inevitable.

flow.text: Always check G6PD before primaquine. Severe oxidative hemolysis if deficient.
flow.subtext: G6PD-deficient patients lack the NADPH/glutathione system to neutralize oxidative stress.

flow.text: Primaquine causes hemolysis.
flow.subtext: Test first — never give primaquine empirically without G6PD result.

flow.text: Plasmodium species map: falciparum severe (no hypnozoites); vivax/ovale hypnozoites; malariae mild persistent.
flow.subtext: Falciparum = deadly, cerebral malaria, pregnancy risk, treat with artemisinin combination.

flow.text: Vivax/ovale = hypnozoites, need primaquine.
flow.subtext: Malariae = quartan, low-level persistent.

flow.text: Other parasites: Leishmania (sandfly/macrophage), Schistosoma (praziquantel), Toxoplasma (cat/meat).
flow.subtext: Each has characteristic transmission, immunology, and treatment.

flow.text: Praziquantel disrupts schistosome tegument by altering Ca²⁺ permeability.
flow.subtext: Liposomal ampho B for visceral leishmaniasis.
flow.insight: Praziquantel disrupts schistosome tegument by altering Ca²⁺...

flow.text: Helminths drive Th2 response: IL-4, IL-5, IL-13 → eosinophilia + IgE.
flow.subtext: Same Th2/IgE/eosinophil axis as allergy.

flow.text: Eosinophils damage worm tegument via MBP/ECP.
flow.subtext: May explain the hygiene hypothesis (allergy increasing as helminth burden decreases).
flow.insight: Eosinophils damage worm tegument via MBP/ECP

flow.text: Answer: C · P. vivax hypnozoites · primaquine after G6PD.
flow.subtext: Modern alternative: tafenoquine (single-dose, also requires G6PD testing). For severe falciparum: IV artesunate is first-line.

stations.bridge: Patient with P. vivax malaria treated with chloroquine relapses 6 months later. Why, and what should have been added to the original regimen?
stations.vignette: A 30-year-old man returns from a 6-month assignment in Papua New Guinea with cyclic fevers every 48 hours, headache, and splenomegaly. Thick blood smear reveals Plasmodium vivax. He is treated with chloroquine and his symptoms resolve. Six months later, he has recurrence of identical symptoms; smear again shows P. vivax. He has not traveled since. Which of the following best explains the relapse and how it should have been prevented?

choices.text: Chloroquine resistance; treat with mefloquine for relapse
Reinfection from local mosquitoes; no original error
P. vivax hypnozoites; need primaquine after G6PD testing
Subdosed chloroquine; redose at higher dose

keypoints: P. falciparum: The DEADLY species. No hypnozoites (no relapse). High parasitemia possible. Causes cerebral malaria, severe anemia, AKI, ARDS. Pregnancy malaria a major cause of maternal/fetal mortality. Africa, parts of Asia/SE Asia, Oceania. Treat with artemisinin combination therapy (ACT). Resistance to chloroquine is widespread.

P. vivax: HYPNOZOITES in liver → relapse. Less severe acute disease but persistent. Most common malaria globally outside sub-Saharan Africa. Treat with chloroquine for blood stage + PRIMAQUINE for liver stage.
P. ovale: HYPNOZOITES like vivax. Also needs primaquine. Mostly West Africa.
P. malariae: Mild but persistent low-level parasitemia. NO hypnozoites, but can cause recrudescence from blood stage if not fully treated. Quartan fever (every 72 hours).
P. knowlesi: Zoonotic, primarily Borneo. Can be severe.

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: Antifungals
stations.subtitle: Why amphotericin B works against virtually any fungus but causes nephrotoxicity — and how the four major antifungal classes target different fungal cell wall and membrane components.

flow.text: Four antifungal classes: azoles, echinocandins, amphotericin B, flucytosine.
flow.subtext: Each targets a different fungal-specific structure.

flow.text: Azoles → ergosterol synthesis.
flow.subtext: Echinocandins → cell wall β-glucan.

flow.text: Amphotericin → ergosterol membrane.
flow.subtext: Flucytosine → DNA/RNA via cytosine deaminase.

flow.text: Echinocandins (caspofungin, micafungin, anidulafungin) inhibit β-1,3-glucan synthase.
flow.subtext: Block synthesis of β-1,3-glucan, a major fungal cell wall component.

flow.text: Fungicidal against most Candida.
flow.subtext: IV only — large peptides, no oral absorption.

flow.text: Why echinocandin for unstable Candida fungemia: fungicidal, well-tolerated, minimal renal toxicity.
flow.subtext: Critically ill patient needs fungicidal (not just fungistatic).
flow.insight: Echinocandin = first-line for unstable invasive Candida

flow.text: AKI rules out amphotericin B.
flow.subtext: Echinocandins active against many fluconazole-resistant species (glabrata, krusei).

flow.text: Azoles inhibit lanosterol 14α-demethylase → ergosterol synthesis blocked.
flow.subtext: Fluconazole = good for Candida albicans + Crypto; NO Aspergillus.

flow.text: Voriconazole = Aspergillus first-line.
Posaconazole/isavuconazole = broadest.
flow.subtext: Toxicity: hepatotoxicity, QT, drug interactions via CYP.

flow.text: Amphotericin B binds ergosterol → pores in fungal membrane → fungicidal. Broadest spectrum.
flow.subtext: Best activity against most fungi.

flow.text: Used for cryptococcal meningitis (with flucytosine), severe histo, mucormycosis.
Major toxicity: NEPHROTOXICITY.
flow.subtext: Liposomal formulations safer.

flow.text: Flucytosine = antimetabolite (5-FC → 5-FU). Always combined with amphotericin to prevent resistance.
flow.subtext: Selective because fungi have cytosine deaminase, mammalian cells don't.

flow.text: Resistance develops rapidly with monotherapy.
flow.subtext: Combined with amphotericin for cryptococcal meningitis induction.

flow.text: Pathogen-drug map: Candida → echinocandin; Aspergillus → voriconazole; Crypto → ampho+5-FC; Mucor → ampho + surgery.
flow.subtext: Each major fungal pathogen has a preferred regimen. Knowing the map predicts the answer for clinical scenarios.

flow.text: Answer: B · β-1,3-glucan synthase block · fungicidal · nephro-sparing.
flow.subtext: Modern IDSA guidelines: echinocandins first-line for invasive candidiasis in critically ill or hemodynamically unstable patients.

stations.bridge: ICU patient on broad-spectrum antibiotics develops Candida fungemia from a central line. What's the first-line agent and why?
stations.vignette: A 65-year-old man in the ICU on broad-spectrum antibiotics for sepsis develops new fevers and Candida albicans growth from blood cultures drawn through his central venous catheter. He is hemodynamically unstable and has acute kidney injury. The infectious diseases consultant initiates micafungin (an echinocandin) rather than amphotericin B or fluconazole. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of echinocandins and why this class is preferred for hemodynamically unstable Candida fungemia with renal impairment?

choices.text: Inhibit ergosterol synthesis at lanosterol; nephro-sparing
Inhibit β-1,3-glucan synthase; fungicidal vs Candida; nephro-sparing
Inhibit fungal protein synthesis; CNS penetrant

keypoints:
Azoles (fluconazole, itraconazole, voriconazole, posaconazole, isavuconazole):
  • Inhibit fungal lanosterol 14α-demethylase (a CYP enzyme) → block ergosterol synthesis at the lanosterol step → defective membrane.
Fungistatic for most yeasts; some are fungicidal against Aspergillus (voriconazole, posaconazole).
Activity varies: fluconazole = good for Candida albicans + Cryptococcus, NO Aspergillus activity. Voriconazole = good for Aspergillus + Candida + endemic fungi. Posaconazole/isavuconazole = broadest.

Toxicity: hepatotoxicity, QT prolongation. Voriconazole specifically: visual disturbances, photosensitivity. Drug interactions via CYP inhibition (especially itraconazole + omeprazole increases gastric pH and reduces itraconazole absorption — Q12 source).

stations.label: Station 06
stations.title: Bacterial toxins
stations.subtitle: Why diphtheria's lethal toxin is encoded by a BACTERIOPHAGE — not the bacterium itself — and how phage and plasmid transfer reshape bacterial virulence.

flow.text: Diphtheria toxin is encoded by corynephage β — a bacteriophage that infects C. diphtheriae.
flow.subtext: The tox gene sits in the phage genome. When phage integrates into bacterial chromosome as prophage, the bacterium acquires the toxin gene.

flow.text: Lysogenic conversion: phage-infected strains make toxin; phage-free strains don't.
flow.subtext: Same bacterial species, different virulence depending on phage status.

flow.text: Phage-free C. diphtheriae are non-toxigenic and don't cause severe disease.
Diphtheria toxin is an AB toxin: A subunit ADP-ribosylates EF-2 → no protein synthesis → cell death.
flow.subtext: B subunit binds heparin-binding EGF-like growth factor on host cells.

flow.text: A subunit catalyzes ADP-ribosylation of elongation factor 2, halting host protein synthesis.
flow.insight: A subunit catalyzes ADP-ribosylation of elongation factor 2

flow.text: Cell dies.
flow.subtext: Same mechanism as Pseudomonas exotoxin A.

flow.text: Vaccine = TOXOID (formaldehyde-inactivated toxin), not whole bacterium.
flow.subtext: Disease is caused by toxin, not by bacterial colonization itself.

flow.text: Anti-toxin antibodies neutralize toxin and prevent disease.
flow.subtext: Vaccinated individuals can still be carriers but don't get sick.
flow.insight: Anti-toxin antibodies neutralize toxin and prevent disease

flow.text: Other phage-encoded toxins: shiga (EHEC), Botulinum, Cholera, Diphtheria, Erythrogenic (strep).
flow.subtext: Mnemonic: shigA, Botulinum, Cholera, Diphtheria, Erythrogenic. Many critical bacterial toxins come from phage rather than chromosome.

flow.text: Plasmid-encoded: most antibiotic resistance, ESBL, vanA/vanB, some toxins.
flow.subtext: Plasmids transfer horizontally between bacteria via conjugation.

flow.text: Major mechanism of resistance spread.
flow.subtext: Plasmids can be lost when selection pressure removed (e.g., vanR plasmid loss without vancomycin pressure — Q77).

flow.text: Map of major bacterial pathogens by syndrome (selected).
flow.subtext: Each has signature features.

flow.text: Meningococcus serogroup B = vaccine challenge (no polysaccharide vaccine).
Lyme disease has staged presentation.
Legionella is intracellular (atypical pneumonia).
Lyme: doxycycline first-line (amoxicillin in pregnancy/young children).
Legionella: macrolide or fluoroquinolone (intracellular).
flow.subtext: Plague: streptomycin or doxycycline.

flow.text: Answer: B · Phage-encoded · lysogenic conversion — toxin gene rides on the phage.
flow.subtext: Vaccine principle: target the toxin (toxoid), not the bacterium.

flow.text: Same logic as tetanus toxoid.
flow.subtext: DTaP combines diphtheria + tetanus + acellular pertussis toxoids/antigens.

stations.bridge: Diphtheria toxin is the lethal virulence factor of Corynebacterium diphtheriae. Where is it encoded, and why does that matter for vaccine biology?
stations.vignette: A 4-year-old unvaccinated child develops a thick gray pseudomembrane in the posterior pharynx, fever, and difficulty breathing. Diphtheria is suspected. The pseudomembrane and severe systemic toxicity (myocarditis, neuropathy) are caused by diphtheria toxin produced by Corynebacterium diphtheriae. Genetic analysis shows the diphtheria toxin gene is NOT located on the bacterial chromosome. Which of the following best describes the genetic basis of diphtheria toxin and its relevance to disease?

choices.text: Phage-encoded (corynephage β); only lysogenic strains are toxigenic
Chromosomal; expressed only under low iron
Host-derived; produced in response to bacterial colonization

keypoints: The diphtheria toxin gene (tox) is encoded by corynephage β — a temperate bacteriophage that infects C. diphtheriae.
Lysogenic conversion: When the phage integrates into the bacterial chromosome as a prophage, the bacterium acquires the toxin gene and becomes capable of toxin production.
C. diphtheriae strains lacking the phage are non-toxigenic and don't cause severe disease.
Diphtheria toxin (an AB toxin): B subunit binds heparin-binding EGF-like growth factor on host cells; A subunit catalyzes ADP-ribosylation of EF-2, halting protein synthesis → cell death.
Vaccine = diphtheria TOXOID (formaldehyde-inactivated toxin). Antibodies neutralize toxin; bacteria can still colonize but disease is prevented.

stations.label: Station 07
stations.title: Antibiotic resistance
stations.subtitle: Why ESBL Klebsiella is sensitive to carbapenems but resistant to most penicillins and cephalosporins — and how horizontal gene transfer drives the resistance epidemic.

flow.text: ESBL = Extended-Spectrum β-Lactamases — plasmid-encoded.
flow.subtext: Evolved from older narrow-spectrum β-lactamases (TEM, SHV) by point mutations expanding substrate range.

flow.text: Most common in Klebsiella, E. coli.
flow.subtext: Carried on conjugative plasmids → spread between species.

flow.text: ESBLs hydrolyze penicillins + 1st-3rd gen cephalosporins + aztreonam. NOT carbapenems.
flow.subtext: Spectrum of resistance: ampicillin, piperacillin, ceftriaxone, ceftazidime, aztreonam. Carbapenems escape because of different β-lactam stereochemistry.

flow.text: Carbapenems (meropenem, imipenem, ertapenem) = first-line for serious ESBL infections.
flow.subtext: Carbapenems retain activity against ESBLs.

flow.text: Standard first-line for ESBL bacteremia, severe infections.
flow.subtext: Newer alternative: ceftazidime-avibactam (avibactam inhibits ESBL).

flow.text: Resistance escalation: ESBL → carbapenemase (KPC/NDM/OXA-48) = CRE.
flow.subtext: Carbapenemases are distinct enzyme class that DO hydrolyze carbapenems.

flow.text: CRE (carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales) is the next escalation.
flow.subtext: Treatment requires newer agents: ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, cefiderocol.

flow.text: Other resistance: MRSA (mecA → PBP2a), VRE (vanA → D-Ala-D-Lac), RpoB mutations (rifampin).
flow.subtext: MRSA = altered PBP.

flow.text: VRE = vancomycin target replacement.
Rifampin resistance = RNA polymerase β-subunit mutations (Q34).
flow.subtext: Each represents a different molecular strategy.
flow.insight: Rifampin resistance = RNA polymerase β-subunit mutations...

flow.text: Plasmid loss without selection: vanR can be lost when vancomycin pressure removed (Q77).
flow.subtext: Resistance carries fitness cost.

flow.text: Without antibiotic pressure, plasmid-bearing strains may be outcompeted by plasmid-free counterparts.
flow.subtext: Antibiotic stewardship can reduce resistance prevalence.

flow.text: Hand hygiene = single most effective intervention to prevent resistant organism transmission.
flow.subtext: Alcohol hand rub for most organisms; soap and water for spores (C. difficile).

flow.text: Compliance is the biggest modifiable factor in healthcare-associated infection.
flow.insight: Compliance is the biggest modifiable factor in...

flow.text: Influenza analog: reassortment occurs in pig hosts.
flow.subtext: Pigs are the mixing vessel for influenza viruses.

flow.text: Pigs uniquely express both α-2,3 (avian) and α-2,6 (human) sialic acid receptors.
flow.subtext: They can be co-infected with avian + human strains.

flow.text: Co-infection allows reassortment producing novel HA/NA combinations.
flow.subtext: Antigenic shift = pandemic potential.

flow.text: 2009 H1N1 pandemic originated from swine reassortment.
flow.subtext: Triple reassortant of avian, swine, and human influenza segments.

flow.text: Answer: B · ESBL = plasmid-borne β-lactamase · carbapenem first-line.
flow.subtext: ESBL is the dominant gram-negative resistance pattern in many ICUs.

flow.text: Knowing the mechanism predicts which drugs work (carbapenems, ceftazidime-avibactam) and which don't (ceftriaxone, piperacillin-tazobactam variable).
flow.insight: Knowing the mechanism predicts which drugs work (carbapenems

stations.bridge: ICU patient with Klebsiella bacteremia. Susceptibility shows resistance to ceftriaxone but sensitivity to meropenem. What's the mechanism, and what's the appropriate treatment?
stations.vignette: A 60-year-old ICU patient develops Klebsiella pneumoniae bacteremia from a urinary tract source. Susceptibility testing shows resistance to ampicillin, piperacillin-tazobactam, ceftriaxone, ceftazidime, and aztreonam — but susceptibility to meropenem and to ceftazidime-avibactam. The microbiology lab calls to confirm the isolate is an extended-spectrum β-lactamase (ESBL) producer. Which of the following best explains the resistance mechanism and the recommended treatment?

choices.text: Chromosomal mutation in PBPs; treat with vancomycin
Plasmid-borne β-lactamases hydrolyzing most cephalosporins; treat with carbapenem
Efflux of all β-lactams; treat with high-dose vancomycin
Outer membrane porin loss; treat with topical mupirocin
Activate β-lactam prodrugs to inactive form; treat with cipro only

keypoints: Narrow-spectrum penicillinases (e.g., S. aureus blaZ): Hydrolyze penicillin G/V, ampicillin. Sensitive to penicillinase-stable penicillins (oxacillin, nafcillin) and to β-lactamase inhibitors.

Extended-spectrum β-lactamases (ESBLs, e.g., CTX-M, SHV, TEM variants): Hydrolyze penicillins, 1st-, 2nd-, and 3rd-generation cephalosporins, and aztreonam. NOT active against carbapenems. Often inhibited by clavulanate, tazobactam, avibactam (variable). Plasmid-borne. Most common in Klebsiella, E. coli.

AmpC β-lactamases (chromosomal in some Enterobacterales — SPACE: Serratia, Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter, Citrobacter, Enterobacter): Hydrolyze penicillins, cephalosporins (including 3rd-gen) but variable cefepime. Inducible — treatment with 3rd-gen cephalosporin can SELECT for AmpC overexpression. Carbapenems and cefepime usually still work.

Carbapenemases (KPC, NDM, OXA-48, VIM, IMP): Hydrolyze carbapenems. Major global threat. Treatment: ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, imipenem-relebactam, cefiderocol, occasionally polymyxins.

stations.label: Station 08
stations.title: Antibiotic mechanisms
stations.subtitle: Why ciprofloxacin is one of the few oral antibiotics with bioavailability rivaling IV — and how knowing each antibiotic's class, target, and key feature collapses the pharmacology.

flow.text: Antibiotics group by mechanism (target): cell wall, 30S, 50S, DNA, folate, RNA, membrane.
flow.subtext: Each class targets a specific bacterial structure or process.

flow.text: Cell wall = β-lactams + vancomycin. 30S ribosome = aminoglycosides + tetracyclines. 50S = macrolides + clindamycin + linezolid.
flow.subtext: DNA = fluoroquinolones.

flow.text: Folate = TMP-SMX.
flow.subtext: RNA pol = rifampin. Membrane = daptomycin + polymyxin.

flow.text: Fluoroquinolones inhibit DNA gyrase (topoisomerase II) + topoisomerase IV → DNA fragmentation → cell death.
flow.subtext: DNA gyrase manages supercoiling.
flow.insight: FQ = DNA gyrase + topoisomerase IV inhibition

flow.text: Topoisomerase IV separates daughter chromosomes after replication.
flow.insight: Topoisomerase IV separates daughter chromosomes after...

flow.text: Inhibiting both halts replication and triggers DNA damage.
flow.subtext: Bactericidal, concentration-dependent killing.
flow.insight: Inhibiting both halts replication and triggers DNA damage

flow.text: Ciprofloxacin has ~70-95% oral bioavailability — exceptional for an antibiotic.
flow.subtext: Most antibiotics have much lower oral bioavailability (vanc ~0%, aminoglycosides ~0%, many β-lactams 30-60%).

flow.text: Cipro and levofloxacin enable oral therapy for serious infections that traditionally required IV.
flow.insight: Cipro and levofloxacin enable oral therapy for serious...

flow.text: Cation chelation: cipro absorption blocked by antacids, calcium, iron, magnesium.
flow.subtext: Polyvalent cations (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Al³⁺, Fe²⁺) chelate fluoroquinolones in the gut → reduce absorption. Separate antacids and supplements by 2-6 hours from cipro dose.

flow.text: FQ toxicities: tendon rupture, QT prolongation, C. diff, peripheral neuropathy, dysglycemia. Avoid in children/pregnancy.
flow.subtext: Tendon rupture especially with concurrent steroids.

flow.text: Cartilage damage in immature animals → contraindicated in children and pregnancy.
flow.subtext: Black box warnings (FDA): tendon, peripheral neuropathy, CNS effects.
flow.insight: Cartilage damage in immature animals → contraindicated in...

flow.text: Other key class facts: doxy = 30S A-site block; syphilis = penicillin all stages; Lyme in pregnancy = NOT doxy.
flow.subtext: Tetracyclines block aminoacyl-tRNA A-site binding (Q24).

flow.text: Treponema pallidum remains universally PCN-susceptible (Q42).
flow.subtext: Doxy contraindicated in pregnancy and children <8 — use amoxicillin or cefuroxime for Lyme (Q51).

flow.text: Aminoglycoside + β-lactam = synergy: cell wall damage improves aminoglycoside uptake.
flow.subtext: Aminoglycosides need active uptake. β-lactam-induced cell wall damage allows better penetration → synergistic killing.

flow.text: Standard for enterococcal endocarditis (ampicillin + gentamicin).
flow.subtext: Q57 source.

flow.text: Macrolides = 50S binding + intracellular pathogens (Mycoplasma, Chlamydia, Legionella).
flow.subtext: Macrolides accumulate in macrophages and other cells.

flow.text: Active against intracellular pathogens that β-lactams can't reach.
flow.insight: Active against intracellular pathogens that β-lactams can't...

flow.text: Atypical pneumonia coverage.
flow.subtext: QT prolongation, GI motility (erythro = motilin agonist), CYP3A4 issues.

flow.text: Answer: B · DNA gyrase + topo IV · PO ≈ IV bioavailability.
flow.subtext: Excellent oral bioavailability is the practical reason FQs revolutionized outpatient management of pyelonephritis, prostatitis, and IV-to-PO step-down therapy in many infections.
flow.insight: Answer: B · FQ · DNA gyrase · PO ≈ IV

stations.bridge: Outpatient with uncomplicated UTI from a fluoroquinolone-susceptible E. coli. Why is ciprofloxacin a particularly attractive oral choice for many infections that traditionally needed IV therapy?
stations.vignette: A 45-year-old woman with a complicated pyelonephritis caused by a fluoroquinolone-susceptible E. coli is being considered for outpatient oral therapy after initial IV antibiotics. The infectious diseases consultant notes that ciprofloxacin has nearly equivalent oral and IV exposures and is a reasonable transition. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of fluoroquinolones AND the unusual pharmacokinetic feature that makes ciprofloxacin (and other fluoroquinolones) attractive for oral therapy of serious infections?

choices.text: Inhibit cell wall synthesis; PO bioavail ~30%
Inhibit DNA gyrase + topo IV; PO bioavail ~70-95%
Inhibit folate synthesis; PO requires high gastric pH
Bind ergosterol; PO requires fatty meals

keypoints: Penicillins (penicillin G/V, ampicillin, amoxicillin, oxacillin/nafcillin, piperacillin): β-lactam ring binds PBPs (transpeptidases) → blocks peptidoglycan cross-linking → cell lysis. Bactericidal. Time-dependent killing. Hypersensitivity (Type I IgE-mediated to Type IV delayed). Allergic cross-reactivity ~1-3% with cephalosporins. Penicillin = treatment of choice for syphilis at all stages (Q42 source) — only drug with sufficient duration in CSF for tertiary/neuro forms; pregnant penicillin-allergic patients must be desensitized.

Cephalosporins: Same β-lactam mechanism. Generations: 1st (cefazolin, surgical prophylaxis), 2nd (cefuroxime, includes some Lyme coverage), 3rd (ceftriaxone for meningitis, gonorrhea, ceftazidime for Pseudomonas), 4th (cefepime, Pseudomonas + improved gram-positive), 5th (ceftaroline, MRSA active).
Carbapenems (meropenem, imipenem, ertapenem, doripenem): Broadest β-lactam spectrum. ESBL coverage. Imipenem requires cilastatin (blocks renal dehydropeptidase degradation).
Monobactams (aztreonam): Gram-negative only (including Pseudomonas). Safe in penicillin allergy.

Glycopeptides (vancomycin): Binds D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of peptidoglycan precursor → blocks cross-linking. Gram-positive only (can't cross gram-negative outer membrane). MRSA, severe C. diff (oral). Toxicity: nephrotoxicity, red man syndrome (rate-related histamine release, NOT allergy), ototoxicity.

stations.label: Station 09
stations.title: Asplenia
stations.subtitle: Why a sickle cell patient with functional asplenia needs lifelong penicillin prophylaxis — and how splenic immunity ties together everything we've learned about infection.

flow.text: Sickle cell disease causes functional asplenia from repeated splenic infarction.
flow.subtext: Sickled RBCs sequester in splenic sinuses → infarction → fibrosis → eventual autosplenectomy by school age in HbSS. Howell-Jolly bodies on smear confirm functional asplenia.

flow.text: Spleen filters bloodborne encapsulated organisms via macrophage phagocytosis.
flow.subtext: Sinusoidal architecture forces blood through tight spaces where red pulp macrophages screen for opsonized organisms.

flow.text: Loss of this filter = encapsulated organisms persist in blood → sepsis.
flow.insight: Loss of this filter = encapsulated organisms persist in...

flow.text: Spleen also produces anti-polysaccharide antibodies via marginal zone B cells.
flow.subtext: Marginal zone B cells respond to T-independent polysaccharide antigens (capsules) → IgM and IgG2 production. Without spleen, this anti-capsular response is severely impaired.

flow.text: Encapsulated organisms (mnemonic): S. pneumo, H. flu type b, N. meningitidis, Klebsiella, group B strep, Salmonella.
flow.subtext: Polysaccharide capsules resist complement deposition and inhibit phagocytosis.

flow.text: Need anti-capsular antibody for opsonization.
flow.subtext: Asplenic patients can't generate or sustain this response.

flow.text: Risk: overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI) — pneumococcal sepsis killing within hours.
flow.subtext: Mortality 50-70% even with intensive care.
flow.insight: OPSI = high mortality even with treatment

flow.text: S. pneumo most common organism.
flow.subtext: Why prophylaxis and immediate fever response are non-negotiable.

flow.text: Daily penicillin V prophylaxis until at least age 5 in sickle children.
flow.subtext: Reduces pneumococcal sepsis incidence by >80% in sickle cell children.

flow.text: Some centers continue lifelong.
flow.subtext: Amoxicillin alternative.

flow.text: Comprehensive vaccination: PCV13/15/20 + PPSV23 + Hib + MenACWY + MenB + flu.
flow.subtext: Pneumococcal conjugate (PCV) for T-cell help.

flow.text: PPSV23 for additional serotype coverage in adults.
flow.subtext: Hib conjugate.
flow.insight: PPSV23 for additional serotype coverage in adults

flow.text: MenACWY conjugate + separate MenB (capsule resembles polysialic acid).
flow.subtext: Annual influenza.

flow.text: Fever in asplenic patient = immediate empiric IV antibiotics (typically ceftriaxone) before culture results.
flow.subtext: OPSI can kill within hours.
flow.insight: Fever protocol = immediate empiric IV abx

flow.text: Cannot wait for culture.
MedicAlert bracelet identifies asplenic status to first responders.
flow.subtext: Patients counseled to seek care immediately for any fever.
flow.insight: MedicAlert bracelet identifies asplenic status to first...

flow.text: Answer: B · Splenic filter + anti-polysaccharide IgM — both lost → encapsulated risk.
flow.subtext: The two pillars of splenic immunity.
flow.insight: Answer: B · spleen + IgM = encapsulated defense

flow.text: Sickle cell + functional asplenia integrates immunology, vaccine biology, and pharmacology.
flow.subtext: Paradigm clinical scenario.

flow.text: Encapsulated organism vulnerability = the immunology lesson.
flow.subtext: Polysaccharide capsules evade T-independent responses.

flow.text: Conjugate vaccines provide T-cell help = the vaccine biology lesson.
flow.subtext: Polysaccharide-protein conjugates enable IgG and memory.

flow.text: Penicillin prophylaxis until age 5 = the pharmacology lesson.
flow.subtext: Lifelong vaccination + meningococcal/pneumococcal/Hib coverage.

flow.text: Chapter 15 closes. 9 stations · 50+ source questions · infection immunology survey complete.
flow.subtext: Granulomas, herpesviruses, TORCH, parasites, fungi, bacterial virulence, resistance, antibiotics, splenic immunity. Chapter 16 next: supporting concepts.

stations.bridge: Sickle cell child who has had multiple splenic infarcts now has functional asplenia. Why daily penicillin until age 5, and what's the lifelong vaccination strategy?
stations.vignette: A 3-year-old girl with sickle cell disease (HbSS) is brought to clinic. Her spleen is no longer palpable, and Howell-Jolly bodies are present on peripheral smear, indicating functional asplenia from repeated splenic infarction. She has received age-appropriate vaccinations including pneumococcal conjugate vaccine. She is started on daily oral penicillin V. Which of the following best explains the rationale for penicillin prophylaxis and the lifelong infection management strategy in asplenic/hyposplenic patients?

choices.text: Penicillin reduces sickle inflammation; vaccines unnecessary
Splenic macrophage filter + anti-polysaccharide IgM lost → encapsulated organism risk
Penicillin treats subclinical strep carriage; only flu vaccine needed
Penicillin prevents PCP; immunosuppressives required for sickle
Penicillin treats Salmonella osteomyelitis; no vaccine changes

keypoints: Mechanical filtration: Sinusoidal architecture forces blood through narrow spaces where macrophages screen for damaged RBCs, encapsulated bacteria, and immune complexes.
Macrophage phagocytosis: Splenic red pulp macrophages remove opsonized organisms — particularly important for encapsulated bacteria that resist complement-mediated lysis.
Anti-polysaccharide antibody production: Marginal zone B cells respond to T-independent polysaccharide antigens of encapsulated bacterial capsules, producing IgM and IgG2.
Tuftsin production: Splenic peptide that enhances neutrophil phagocytosis.
Properdin production: Complement pathway component supporting alternative pathway.

chapters.title: Supporting Concepts
chapters.subtitle: Pharmacology, lab values, and adjacent topics that round out the immunology picture.

stations.label: Station 01
stations.title: Apoptosis
stations.subtitle: Why caspases are stored in cells as inactive zymogens — and how the intrinsic and extrinsic pathways converge to dismantle the cell from within.

flow.text: Caspases = cysteine-aspartate proteases stored as inactive procaspases.
flow.subtext: Cysteine in active site.

flow.text: Cleave substrates after specific aspartate residues.
flow.subtext: Stored as inactive zymogens — autoinhibitory pro-domain prevents activity until proteolytic cleavage activates them.
flow.insight: Cleave substrates after specific aspartate residues

flow.text: Two classes: initiator (8, 9) start cascade; executioner (3, 6, 7) dismantle the cell.
flow.subtext: Initiators are activated by upstream signaling complexes.

flow.text: Executioners cleave hundreds of substrates: nuclear lamins, ICAD (releasing CAD DNase), cytoskeleton, kinases.
flow.subtext: Cell falls apart in controlled way.
flow.insight: Executioners cleave hundreds of substrates: nuclear lamins

flow.text: EXTRINSIC pathway: Fas/FasL or TNFR → DISC → procaspase-8.
flow.subtext: Death receptor (Fas, TNFR1) engagement → trimerization → recruits FADD/TRADD adapters → procaspase-8 → DISC formation → proximity activation of caspase-8.

flow.text: INTRINSIC pathway: stress → Bax/Bak → cytochrome c → Apaf-1 → procaspase-9.
flow.subtext: Mitochondria-driven apoptosis cascade.

flow.text: Pro-apoptotic stimuli: DNA damage, growth factor withdrawal, ER stress.
flow.subtext: Cellular stresses converge on this pathway.

flow.text: BH3-only proteins activate Bax/Bak.
flow.subtext: BH3-only = sensors of cellular stress.

flow.text: Bax/Bak form mitochondrial outer membrane pores.
flow.subtext: Cytochrome c is released into the cytosol.

flow.text: Cytochrome c + Apaf-1 + procaspase-9 = apoptosome.
flow.subtext: Activates caspase-9, the initiator caspase of intrinsic.

flow.text: Both pathways converge on executioner caspases 3/6/7.
flow.subtext: Caspase-8 (extrinsic) and caspase-9 (intrinsic) both cleave and activate executioners.

flow.text: Same downstream dismantling regardless of upstream trigger.
flow.subtext: Caspase-8 also cleaves Bid → tBid → links to intrinsic pathway for amplification.
flow.insight: Same downstream dismantling regardless of upstream trigger

flow.text: Bcl-2 family regulates intrinsic pathway: anti-apoptotic (Bcl-2, Bcl-xL) vs pro-apoptotic (Bax, Bak) vs sensors (BH3-only).
flow.subtext: Balance determines whether mitochondria release cytochrome c.

flow.text: Bcl-2 overexpression in follicular lymphoma (t(14;18)) blocks apoptosis.
flow.subtext: Venetoclax (BCL-2 inhibitor) reactivates apoptosis in CLL/AML.

flow.text: Apoptotic features: cell shrinkage, chromatin condensation, membrane blebbing, apoptotic bodies, NO inflammation.
flow.subtext: Membrane stays intact (vs necrosis = membrane rupture).

flow.text: Phosphatidylserine flipped to outer leaflet → 'eat me' signal for phagocytes.
flow.subtext: DNA cleaved into 180 bp nucleosomal fragments (ladder on gel).
flow.insight: Phosphatidylserine flipped to outer leaflet → 'eat me'...

flow.text: Answer: B · Caspase cascade · extrinsic + intrinsic pathways.
flow.subtext: Defects in apoptosis: ALPS (Fas mutations → lymphoproliferation + autoimmunity), follicular lymphoma (Bcl-2 overexpression). Therapeutics: venetoclax for CLL/AML.

stations.bridge: Cytotoxic T cell engages an infected target via Fas/FasL. What enzyme family executes the resulting apoptosis, and how is it regulated to prevent accidental self-destruction?
stations.vignette: A cytotoxic T cell engages an HSV-infected hepatocyte through interaction of FasL on the T cell with Fas on the target. Within hours the hepatocyte shows nuclear chromatin condensation, cell shrinkage, membrane blebbing, and apoptotic body formation — without inflammation. The destruction is mediated by a family of proteases stored in cells as inactive zymogens that are activated by proteolytic cleavage. Which of the following best describes this enzyme family and the dual pathway architecture of apoptosis?

choices.text: Lysosomal hydrolases activated by membrane permeabilization (necrosis)
Caspase cascade · extrinsic (Fas/TNFR) and intrinsic (cyt c) pathways
Matrix metalloproteinases activated by Fas crosslinking

keypoints: Family of cysteine proteases that cleave substrates after specific aspartate residues.
Stored as inactive zymogens (procaspases) — autoinhibitory pro-domain prevents activity.
Activated by proteolytic cleavage: removal of pro-domain + cleavage between large and small subunits → active heterotetramer (two large + two small subunits).
Initiator caspases (8, 9, 10): activated by upstream signaling complexes; cleave executioner caspases.
Executioner caspases (3, 6, 7): cleave hundreds of cellular substrates — nuclear lamins, ICAD (releases CAD endonuclease that fragments DNA), cytoskeletal proteins, kinases.

stations.label: Station 02
stations.title: Second messengers
stations.subtitle: Why a single PIP2 molecule splits into two second messengers (IP3 and DAG) — and how that bifurcation drives both calcium release AND PKC activation in parallel.

flow.text: PLC cleaves PIP2 (phosphatidylinositol-4,5-bisphosphate) at the phosphodiester bond.
flow.subtext: PIP2 sits in the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane. PLC is recruited and activated by upstream signals (Gq-coupled GPCRs activate PLCβ; immune receptors and RTKs activate PLCγ).

flow.text: Two products: IP3 (water-soluble) and DAG (membrane-bound).
flow.subtext: IP3 = inositol-1,4,5-trisphosphate.

flow.text: DAG = diacylglycerol.
flow.subtext: The bifurcation creates two parallel signaling streams from one upstream event.

flow.text: IP3 → ER → IP3 receptor → Ca²⁺ release into cytoplasm.
flow.subtext: IP3 diffuses to ER, binds ligand-gated IP3R Ca²⁺ channels, opens them.

flow.text: ER-stored Ca²⁺ flows into cytoplasm down gradient.
flow.subtext: Cytoplasmic Ca²⁺ rises 10-100 fold.
flow.insight: ER-stored Ca²⁺ flows into cytoplasm down gradient

flow.text: ER depletion triggers SOCE (store-operated Ca²⁺ entry) via STIM/Orai in plasma membrane.
flow.subtext: STIM proteins on ER sense Ca²⁺ depletion → activate Orai channels in plasma membrane → sustained Ca²⁺ influx from outside cell. Maintains the Ca²⁺ signal.

flow.text: DAG stays in membrane → recruits and activates PKC (with Ca²⁺ for conventional isoforms).
flow.subtext: DAG binds C1 domains of PKC, recruiting it to membrane.

flow.text: Conventional PKC (α, βI, βII, γ) need both DAG and Ca²⁺.
flow.subtext: Novel PKC (δ, ε, η, θ) need DAG only.

flow.text: Activated PKC phosphorylates many substrates → NF-κB, AP-1, cytoskeletal regulation.
flow.subtext: PKC has broad substrate range.

flow.text: Drives parallel signaling to NF-κB and AP-1 transcription factors.
flow.subtext: Cooperates with NFAT (from Ca²⁺/calcineurin arm) at IL-2 promoter in T cells.
flow.insight: Drives parallel signaling to NF-κB and AP-1 transcription...

flow.text: Why this bifurcation matters: both NFAT (Ca²⁺ arm) and AP-1 (DAG arm) needed for IL-2 transcription.
flow.subtext: T-cell activation requires both signals.
flow.insight: Both arms needed for full activation

flow.text: Cyclosporine blocks NFAT arm → no IL-2 even though AP-1 fine.
flow.subtext: The same logic applies to mast cell activation, B-cell signaling, and many other immune contexts.
flow.insight: Cyclosporine blocks NFAT arm → no IL-2 even though AP-1 fine

flow.text: Pharmacology: lithium blunts the pathway (inositol depletion). Phorbol esters activate PKC directly.
flow.subtext: Lithium inhibits inositol monophosphatase → can't recycle inositol → blunts PLC signaling.
flow.insight: Lithium and phorbol esters target this pathway

flow.text: Hypothesized mechanism in bipolar treatment.
flow.subtext: Phorbol esters (TPA, PMA) are plant-derived DAG mimics; tumor promoters experimentally.

flow.text: Answer: B · PIP2 → IP3 + DAG · IP3 → Ca²⁺ · DAG → PKC.
flow.subtext: This is THE T-cell activation pathway upstream of calcineurin/NFAT.
flow.insight: Answer: B · PIP2 split · IP3 + DAG

flow.text: Same architecture in mast cells, B cells, neurons, smooth muscle.
flow.subtext: Universal second messenger system.

stations.bridge: T cell receives TCR signal → activates PLCγ1 → cleaves PIP2. What two products are generated, and what does each do?
stations.vignette: A research immunology lab is studying T-cell receptor signaling. After TCR engagement, phospholipase C-γ1 (PLCγ1) is recruited to the membrane and cleaves a specific membrane phospholipid into two second messengers. One is water-soluble and diffuses into the cytoplasm to release calcium from the endoplasmic reticulum; the other remains anchored in the membrane and activates a protein kinase. Which of the following correctly identifies the substrate, products, and downstream effects?

choices.text: PLC cleaves phosphatidylcholine into choline + DAG
PLC cleaves PIP2 into IP3 (Ca²⁺) + DAG (PKC)
PLC cleaves PIP2 into IP3 + PKC; IP3 activates Ca²⁺-ATPase
PLC cleaves PIP3 into IP4 + arachidonic acid; activates cAMP
PLC produces cAMP from ATP; cAMP activates PKA

keypoints: The PLC/PIP2 pathway is one of the most ubiquitous signaling systems in cell biology. It operates in T cells (TCR signaling — Ch 7 Station 05 calcineurin), B cells (BCR signaling), mast cells (FcεRI signaling — Ch 11), neurons, smooth muscle, platelets, and many other cell types. The pathway converts a single upstream stimulus into two parallel second messenger outputs.
The PLC reaction:

cAMP system as a parallel second messenger pathway: Many GPCRs signal through Gs (activating adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → PKA) or Gi (inhibiting adenylyl cyclase). cAMP/PKA is a separate second messenger system parallel to PLC/IP3/DAG. β-adrenergic agonists (Gs → cAMP) are a major pharmacologic class. cGMP is yet another parallel system (NO/sGC, ANP/pGC).

stations.label: Station 03
stations.title: Coagulation
stations.subtitle: Why coagulation factors are stored as zymogens that activate each other in a sequential proteolytic cascade — and how DIC turns the system against itself.

flow.text: Coagulation factors stored as inactive zymogens (numbered with Roman numerals).
flow.subtext: Same principle as caspase cascade: stored inactive, activated on demand by proteolytic cleavage. Provides speed + safety + cascade amplification.

flow.text: Two upstream pathways converge on the common pathway: extrinsic (TF + VII) and intrinsic (XII → XI → IX → VIII).
flow.subtext: Extrinsic = in vivo trigger via tissue factor exposure.
flow.insight: Two upstream pathways, one common pathway

flow.text: Intrinsic = contact activation in vitro.
flow.subtext: PT tests extrinsic + common; PTT tests intrinsic + common.

flow.text: Common pathway: Xa + V → thrombin (II) → fibrin (I) → cross-linked clot (XIII).
flow.subtext: Activated X cleaves prothrombin (II) to thrombin.

flow.text: Thrombin cleaves fibrinogen (I) to fibrin monomers, which polymerize.
flow.subtext: Factor XIII cross-links them into stable clot.
flow.insight: Thrombin cleaves fibrinogen (I) to fibrin monomers

flow.text: DIC = pathologic systemic activation of this cascade.
flow.subtext: Trigger: sepsis (gram-negative endotoxin), trauma, malignancy (APML, mucinous adenoCA), obstetric emergencies, burns.

flow.text: Releases tissue factor and cytokines that activate cascade everywhere.
flow.insight: Releases tissue factor and cytokines that activate cascade...

flow.text: Widespread thrombin generation → microthrombi → end-organ ischemia (fingers, kidneys).
flow.subtext: Small vessel thrombosis throughout body.

flow.text: Digital ischemia → purpura fulminans appearance.
AKI.
flow.subtext: Microangiopathic hemolysis (schistocytes from RBC shearing through fibrin strands).

flow.text: Simultaneous consumption of platelets, fibrinogen, and clotting factors → BLEEDING.
flow.subtext: Massive consumption depletes the same factors needed to control bleeding.

flow.text: Patient has both thrombosis (microcirculation) and bleeding (puncture sites, mucous membranes) — paradoxical.
flow.insight: Patient has both thrombosis (microcirculation) and bleeding...

flow.text: Plasmin activated in parallel → cleaves fibrin → D-dimer release (marker of active fibrinolysis).
flow.subtext: Fibrinolytic system tries to keep up with thrombin generation.

flow.text: Cleaves cross-linked fibrin → degradation products including D-dimer.
flow.subtext: Marker of active clotting + breakdown.

flow.text: Lab pattern: ↑PT, ↑PTT, ↓fibrinogen, ↓platelets, ↑D-dimer, schistocytes.
flow.subtext: Both coagulation pathways prolonged from factor consumption.

flow.text: Fibrinogen consumed.
flow.subtext: Platelets consumed.

flow.text: D-dimer elevated from fibrinolysis.
flow.subtext: Schistocytes from microangiopathic hemolysis.

flow.text: Treatment: treat underlying cause (e.g., sepsis); transfuse FFP/cryo/platelets as needed.
flow.subtext: DIC is a manifestation of underlying disease, not a primary disease.

flow.text: Treating the trigger is essential.
Supportive transfusion for active bleeding.
flow.subtext: Heparin in selected cases (controversial).

flow.text: Answer: C · DIC · treat underlying cause + replace consumed factors.
flow.subtext: Same architectural principle as caspase cascade — proteolytic zymogen activation with amplification.
flow.insight: Answer: C · DIC · root cause + factor replacement

flow.text: Pathologic systemic activation overwhelms the regulatory checks (antithrombin, protein C/S, TFPI).
flow.insight: Pathologic systemic activation overwhelms the regulatory...

stations.bridge: Septic patient with bleeding from venipuncture sites + microthrombi in fingers + low platelets + low fibrinogen + elevated D-dimer. What's the pathophysiology?
stations.vignette: A 62-year-old woman with severe gram-negative sepsis develops oozing from venipuncture sites and gingival bleeding. Labs show: platelets 30,000 (low), PT and PTT prolonged, fibrinogen 80 mg/dL (low), D-dimer markedly elevated, and schistocytes on smear. She also develops purplish discoloration of her fingertips. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiology and how it relates to the normal coagulation cascade?

choices.text: HIT type II; escalate to argatroban or fondaparinux
TTP from ADAMTS13 deficiency; plasma exchange
DIC from systemic cascade activation; treat underlying cause
Hemophilia A factor VIII deficiency; replace factor
Vitamin K deficiency; reverse with vitamin K + FFP

keypoints:
Extrinsic pathway (faster, in vivo trigger):
  • Tissue factor (TF, factor III) released from damaged tissue → binds factor VII → activates it (VIIa).
TF/VIIa complex activates factor X.
Tested by PT (prothrombin time).

stations.label: Station 04
stations.title: Heparin & warfarin
stations.subtitle: Why heparin works in seconds and warfarin takes days — and how that single PK difference dictates which is used for acute vs chronic anticoagulation.

flow.text: Heparin amplifies antithrombin → ~1000-fold more active → inhibits thrombin (IIa) + Xa.
flow.subtext: Binds AT, induces conformational change, accelerates AT inhibition of clotting factors.

flow.text: Works on existing active factors.
flow.subtext: Onset is immediate.

flow.text: UFH inhibits IIa + Xa equally; LMWH preferentially Xa; fondaparinux pure Xa.
flow.subtext: UFH chains long enough to bridge AT to thrombin.

flow.text: LMWH chains shorter — usually only catalyze Xa inhibition.
flow.subtext: Fondaparinux is pure pentasaccharide — pure Xa.
flow.insight: LMWH chains shorter — usually only catalyze Xa inhibition

flow.text: Heparin: parenteral, IMMEDIATE onset, monitor with PTT or anti-Xa, reverse with protamine.
flow.subtext: Parenteral because oral absorption is essentially zero (highly negatively charged molecule).

flow.text: Immediate effect because it works on existing factors.
flow.subtext: Reversal: protamine binds and neutralizes heparin.
flow.insight: Immediate effect because it works on existing factors

flow.text: Warfarin blocks VKORC1 → can't regenerate active vitamin K → no γ-carboxylation of new factors II, VII, IX, X.
flow.subtext: Vitamin K is required as cofactor for γ-glutamyl carboxylase.

flow.text: This enzyme adds carboxyl groups to glutamate residues on factors II, VII, IX, X (and proteins C and S).
flow.subtext: Without γ-carboxylation, factors can't bind Ca²⁺ → inactive.
flow.insight: This enzyme adds carboxyl groups to glutamate residues on...

flow.text: Warfarin only affects NEW factor synthesis. Existing active factors must be cleared.
flow.subtext: This is the key difference from heparin.
flow.insight: Warfarin = slow because waits for clearance

flow.text: Pre-existing γ-carboxylated factors are still functional.
flow.subtext: Patient remains anticoagulant-naive until those factors are catabolized.
flow.insight: Pre-existing γ-carboxylated factors are still functional

flow.text: Factor half-lives: VII = 6 hr (shortest), IX = 24 hr, X = 36 hr, II (prothrombin) = 60-72 hr (longest).
flow.subtext: Factor VII clears fastest, so INR rises early (PT primarily reflects factor VII).

flow.text: Factor II takes 3 days to deplete.
flow.subtext: True antithrombotic effect requires II depletion.
flow.insight: Factor II takes 3 days to deplete

flow.text: Bridge for 5 days because INR therapeutic ≠ truly anticoagulated. Need factor II to deplete.
flow.subtext: INR can be in range by day 2-3 from VII depletion alone, but factor II still 50-70% of baseline.

flow.text: Patient NOT yet protected.
flow.subtext: Heparin maintains coverage until factor II clears.

flow.text: Bonus: warfarin causes transient hypercoagulability early (protein C ~8 hr half-life, falls before factor II) → skin necrosis risk.
flow.subtext: Protein C is an anticoagulant protein with shorter half-life than factor II.
flow.insight: Protein C drops fast → transient hypercoagulability

flow.text: Falls first.
flow.subtext: Patient is briefly MORE coagulable.

flow.text: Heparin overlap prevents this.
flow.subtext: Worse in pre-existing protein C deficiency → warfarin-induced skin necrosis.

flow.text: Reversal: protamine for heparin, vitamin K + 4F-PCC for warfarin.
flow.subtext: Protamine binds heparin (full UFH reversal, partial LMWH).

flow.text: Vitamin K is slow (hours to days for new factor synthesis). 4F-PCC immediately replaces factors II, VII, IX, X for emergent reversal.
flow.insight: Vitamin K is slow (hours to days for new factor synthesis)

flow.text: DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran) have largely replaced warfarin for AF and DVT/PE. Warfarin still preferred for mechanical valves, APLS.
flow.subtext: DOACs = oral, no monitoring, fewer interactions.
flow.insight: DOACs replacing warfarin for many indications

flow.text: Reversal: andexanet for FXa inhibitors, idarucizumab for dabigatran.
flow.subtext: Warfarin retained for specific indications where DOACs don't have evidence (mechanical valves, antiphospholipid syndrome, severe renal failure).
