flow.text: Answer: B · Heparin (immediate) + warfarin (delayed) · bridge to prevent skin necrosis.
flow.subtext: Heparin and warfarin remain foundational despite DOACs.
flow.insight: Answer: B · bridge = onset gap + protein C

flow.text: Pregnancy: heparin (especially LMWH); warfarin teratogenic.
Mechanical valves: warfarin.
flow.subtext: APLS: warfarin.

stations.bridge: Patient with new DVT started on heparin and warfarin simultaneously. Why does the heparin continue for 5 days even though warfarin is therapeutic by INR within 2-3 days?
stations.vignette: A 55-year-old woman with newly diagnosed DVT is started on therapeutic enoxaparin (low molecular weight heparin) and oral warfarin simultaneously. Her INR reaches the therapeutic range (2.0-3.0) by day 3. The clinician continues enoxaparin for at least 5 days total, even after the INR is therapeutic. Which of the following best explains the mechanisms of these two drugs and why heparin must overlap with warfarin for at least 5 days?

choices.text: Both directly inhibit thrombin; overlap for safety
Heparin (immediate IIa/Xa via AT-III) + warfarin (delayed Vit-K factors); bridge avoids skin necrosis
Heparin oral, warfarin parenteral; overlap for absorption
Warfarin causes initial hypercoagulability via protein C depletion; bridge prevents this
Both work via the same pathway; redundancy

keypoints: Binds antithrombin (AT) → induces conformational change → AT becomes ~1000-fold more active.
Activated AT primarily inhibits thrombin (IIa) and factor Xa.
Unfractionated heparin (UFH): Inhibits both IIa and Xa equally (requires longer chains to bridge AT to thrombin). Monitored by PTT. Half-life ~1.5 hours. Reverse with protamine sulfate.

Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH, enoxaparin/dalteparin): Preferentially inhibits Xa (chains too short to bridge AT to thrombin in most cases). More predictable PK. Monitored by anti-Xa level if needed (often not monitored). Half-life ~4-6 hours. Partial reversal with protamine.
Fondaparinux: Pure synthetic pentasaccharide. Pure Xa inhibition. Subcutaneous, daily. No HIT risk.
Onset: IMMEDIATE (works on existing thrombin/Xa).
Major adverse effects: bleeding, HIT (heparin-induced thrombocytopenia — IgG to PF4-heparin complex → paradoxical thrombosis), osteoporosis with prolonged use.

stations.label: Station 05
stations.title: G-CSF
stations.subtitle: Why G-CSF saves chemotherapy patients from fatal infections — and how the same xanthine-oxidase pathway that allopurinol blocks can ALSO catastrophically interact with azathioprine.

flow.text: G-CSF binds G-CSF receptor on myeloid progenitors → proliferation + differentiation + release.
flow.subtext: Drives mature neutrophil production from bone marrow precursors.

flow.text: Within 3-5 days, ANC rises significantly.
flow.subtext: Reduces duration of chemotherapy-induced neutropenia.

flow.text: Filgrastim (G-CSF), pegfilgrastim (long-acting) = clinical agents.
flow.subtext: Recombinant human G-CSF.

flow.text: Pegylation extends half-life → single dose per chemotherapy cycle.
flow.subtext: Used for febrile neutropenia, stem cell mobilization, severe congenital neutropenia.
flow.insight: Pegylation extends half-life → single dose per chemotherapy...

flow.text: G-CSF toxicity: bone pain (marrow expansion), rare splenic rupture.
flow.subtext: Bone pain from rapid marrow expansion stretching periosteum.

flow.text: Treat with NSAIDs or loratadine.
flow.subtext: Splenic rupture rare but serious — counsel about LUQ pain.

flow.text: Other growth factors: EPO (erythroid), GM-CSF (broader), TPO mimetics (platelets).
flow.subtext: EPO: chronic renal anemia, chemo anemia.

flow.text: Black box for thrombosis/CV at high Hb targets.
flow.insight: Black box for thrombosis/CV at high Hb targets

flow.text: GM-CSF: broader, more SE.
flow.subtext: Romiplostim/eltrombopag: TPO receptor agonists for ITP.

flow.text: Now azathioprine: prodrug → 6-MP → has 3 competing metabolic fates.
flow.subtext: From Ch 13 S8.

flow.text: Glutathione cleavage of azathioprine releases 6-mercaptopurine.
flow.subtext: 6-MP has 3 competing metabolic fates.

flow.text: Fate 1: HGPRT salvage to active 6-thioguanine nucleotides (6-TGN).
flow.subtext: This is the desired pharmacologic effect.

flow.text: Fate 2: TPMT methylation to inactive metabolites.
flow.subtext: TPMT polymorphism affects toxicity risk.

flow.text: Fate 3: xanthine oxidase oxidation to inactive 6-thiouric acid.
flow.subtext: Allopurinol blocks XO and shunts metabolism toward 6-TGN.

flow.text: Allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase — used for gout, tumor lysis.
flow.subtext: Same XO that converts hypoxanthine → xanthine → uric acid.

flow.text: Allopurinol is a hypoxanthine analog.
Lowers serum urate.
flow.subtext: Standard gout treatment.

flow.text: Combine allopurinol + AZA → blocks one inactivation pathway → 6-MP accumulates → toxic 6-TGN levels → severe pancytopenia.
flow.subtext: With XO blocked, more 6-MP funnels through HGPRT salvage to active 6-TGN.

flow.text: Severe bone marrow toxicity.
flow.subtext: Can be life-threatening if undetected.

flow.text: Management: avoid combination if possible; if required, reduce AZA dose ~75% + monitor CBC closely.
flow.subtext: Standard practice.

flow.text: Some clinicians use the interaction therapeutically — combining low-dose AZA with allopurinol to maintain higher 6-TGN.
flow.subtext: Requires careful monitoring.
flow.insight: Some clinicians use the interaction therapeutically —...

flow.text: Answer: B · G-CSF expands myeloid lineage · allopurinol blocks AZA via XO inhibition.
flow.subtext: Two pharmacologic principles: cytokine substitution (growth factors) and metabolic interference (drug interaction).

flow.text: Both demonstrate how endogenous biology becomes clinical pharmacology.
flow.insight: Both demonstrate how endogenous biology becomes clinical...

stations.bridge: Chemotherapy patient with febrile neutropenia and ANC 200. What growth factor is given to drive recovery, and how does it work?
stations.vignette: A 58-year-old woman receiving chemotherapy for breast cancer is admitted with fever (38.8°C) and an absolute neutrophil count of 200 cells/μL (severe neutropenia). She is started on broad-spectrum antibiotics and granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (filgrastim, G-CSF). Within 5 days her ANC recovers to over 1500. Separately, the team notes she has gout managed with allopurinol and is also being considered for adjunct azathioprine — a combination that requires special caution. Which of the following best describes the mechanisms of G-CSF and the allopurinol-azathioprine interaction?

choices.text: G-CSF directly kills bacteria in marrow; allopurinol enhances AZA via XO
G-CSF expands myeloid lineage; allopurinol blocks AZA metabolism via XO inhibition
G-CSF inhibits neutrophil apoptosis; allopurinol decreases AZA activity
G-CSF is a CYP inhibitor; allopurinol increases CYP3A4 metabolism
G-CSF is a glucocorticoid analog; allopurinol blocks renal excretion

keypoints:
G-CSF (granulocyte colony-stimulating factor; filgrastim, pegfilgrastim):
  • Binds G-CSF receptor on myeloid progenitors and mature neutrophils.
Drives proliferation, differentiation, and release of neutrophils from marrow.
Reduces severity and duration of chemotherapy-induced neutropenia.
Used for febrile neutropenia, mobilization of stem cells before autologous transplant, severe congenital neutropenia.
Toxicity: bone pain (marrow expansion), splenic rupture (rare but serious).

stations.label: Station 06
stations.title: Jarisch-Herxheimer
stations.subtitle: Why a syphilis patient gets WORSE within hours of receiving penicillin — and how the same principle of 'massive antigen release' explains IRIS in HIV and serum sickness in foreign-protein therapy.

flow.text: Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction: paradoxical worsening hours after starting effective antibiotics for spirochetes.
flow.subtext: Classic with penicillin for syphilis.

flow.text: Also occurs with Lyme treatment, leptospirosis, relapsing fever, brucellosis.
flow.subtext: Onset 1-12 hours after first dose.

flow.text: Mechanism: massive spirochete lysis releases lipoproteins and cytokine-inducing molecules → TNF, IL-6 surge → systemic inflammation.
flow.subtext: Bacterial cell wall components and cytokine-inducing fragments released en masse activate TLRs and innate immunity. Self-limited inflammatory cascade.

flow.text: Clinical: fever, chills, headache, myalgia, worsening rash. NO urticaria, NO airway compromise.
flow.subtext: Distinguishes from anaphylaxis (which would have allergic features).

flow.text: Resolves in 24-48 hours.
flow.subtext: Pregnancy: can cause contractions/fetal distress — counsel + monitor.

flow.text: Management: SUPPORTIVE care, do NOT stop antibiotic. Antipyretics, fluids, rest.
flow.subtext: JH is a sign treatment is working.

flow.text: Stopping antibiotic allows spirochete to progress.
flow.subtext: Counsel patient pre-treatment so reaction isn't misinterpreted.
flow.insight: Stopping antibiotic allows spirochete to progress

flow.text: Related: IRIS = paradoxical worsening of OI symptoms after starting HIV ART.
flow.subtext: Recovering immune system mounts inflammatory response against pathogens previously tolerated due to CD4 depletion.

flow.text: Two patterns: paradoxical (known OI worsens) or unmasking (subclinical OI revealed).
flow.insight: Two patterns: paradoxical (known OI worsens) or unmasking...

flow.text: IRIS pathogens: TB, MAC, cryptococcus, CMV, JC virus, KS. Onset 4-8 weeks post-ART.
flow.subtext: Highest risk: CD4 <50 at ART start, rapid CD4 recovery. Manage by continuing both ART and OI treatment + adding corticosteroids if severe (especially TB-IRIS, cryptococcal IRIS).

flow.text: Related: serum sickness = Type III hypersensitivity 7-14 days after foreign protein.
flow.subtext: Classic delayed Type III reaction.

flow.text: Triggers: equine antivenom, chimeric monoclonal antibodies, sometimes drugs (cefaclor).
flow.subtext: Foreign proteins drive antibody response.

flow.text: Patient develops antibodies against the foreign protein.
flow.subtext: IgG and IgM form immune complexes.

flow.text: Immune complexes deposit in vessels, joints, glomeruli, and skin.
flow.subtext: Activate complement → vasculitis.

flow.text: Serum sickness clinical: fever + urticarial/maculopapular rash + arthralgia + LAD ± GN; ↓C3/C4.
flow.subtext: Complement consumed by immune complexes → low C3, C4.

flow.text: Self-limited as antigen clears (days to weeks).
flow.subtext: Discontinue offending agent + supportive care + steroids if severe.
flow.insight: Self-limited as antigen clears (days to weeks)

flow.text: Common logic: treatment changes immune landscape → inflammatory damage to host.
flow.subtext: JH: pathogen lysis → cytokine surge.
flow.insight: All three: effective treatment → host inflammation

flow.text: IRIS: immune reconstitution → recall response.
Serum sickness: persistent antigen → immune complex formation.
flow.subtext: All three commonly misidentified.
flow.insight: Serum sickness: persistent antigen → immune complex...

flow.text: Answer: B · Jarisch-Herxheimer · spirochete lysis releases inflammatory lipoproteins.
flow.subtext: Recognizing these three syndromes prevents inappropriate discontinuation of effective therapy and misattribution as drug allergy or treatment failure.
flow.insight: Answer: B · J-H · post-treatment cytokine surge

stations.bridge: Patient with secondary syphilis given IM benzathine penicillin develops fever, chills, headache, and worsening rash within 4 hours. What's happening, and is it treatment failure?
stations.vignette: A 35-year-old man is treated for early latent syphilis with a single dose of intramuscular benzathine penicillin G. Within 4 hours of the injection he develops fever (39.2°C), chills, headache, myalgia, and intensification of his existing maculopapular rash on the trunk. He has no signs of anaphylaxis (no urticaria, no hypotension, no airway compromise). The reaction resolves over 24 hours with supportive care. Which of the following best explains this reaction and how it relates to two analogous post-treatment immune phenomena?

choices.text: Penicillin allergy (Type I); switch to doxycycline
Jarisch-Herxheimer · spirochete lysis releases endotoxin-like lipoproteins
Penicillin-induced serum sickness; switch to ceftriaxone
Treponema penicillin resistance; treat with azithromycin
Stevens-Johnson syndrome; admit to burn unit

keypoints: Trigger: Initiation of antimicrobial therapy effective against spirochetes — most classically penicillin for syphilis, also occurs with treatment of Lyme disease, leptospirosis, relapsing fever (Borrelia recurrentis), and brucellosis.

Mechanism: Rapid bacterial lysis releases lipoprotein-rich cell wall components and cytokine-inducing molecules → systemic inflammatory cytokine release (TNF, IL-6, IL-8) → fever and inflammatory symptoms.
Clinical: Fever, chills, headache, myalgia, rigors, tachycardia, hypotension (sometimes), worsening of existing rash. Onset within 1-12 hours of treatment. Resolves in 24-48 hours.
Distinguishing from anaphylaxis: NO urticaria, NO airway compromise, NO bronchospasm. Anaphylaxis would also have hypotension but with allergic features.
Management: Supportive — antipyretics, fluids, bed rest. DO NOT discontinue effective antibiotic. Counsel patients about reaction before treatment.
Pregnancy concern: Can cause uterine contractions and fetal distress in pregnant syphilis patients — pre-treatment counseling and monitoring critical.

stations.label: Station 07
stations.title: Anaphylaxis treatment
stations.subtitle: Why epinephrine is the ONLY first-line drug for anaphylaxis — and how the rest of the allergy pharmacopeia (antihistamines, mast cell stabilizers, omalizumab, decongestants) targets different points in the type I hypersensitivity cascade.

flow.text: Anaphylaxis = systemic Type I hypersensitivity. Multiple organ systems hit at once: hypotension + bronchospasm + angioedema + urticaria.
flow.subtext: IgE on FcεRI on mast cells → massive degranulation → histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes, PAF released systemically. Treatment must address all manifestations.

flow.text: Epinephrine = ONLY first-line drug. Multi-receptor coverage matches anaphylaxis multi-system pathology.
flow.subtext: α1 + β1 + β2 + mast cell stabilization.
flow.insight: Epi = the only thing that does it all

flow.text: No other drug addresses hypotension, bronchospasm, and angioedema simultaneously.
flow.subtext: Delayed epi = strongest predictor of fatal outcome.

flow.text: Epinephrine has multiple receptor effects, each addressing one anaphylaxis element.
flow.subtext: Each receptor effect maps to one element of anaphylaxis.

flow.text: α1 vasoconstriction reverses vasodilation: ↑ BP, ↓ edema.
β2 bronchodilation reverses bronchospasm.
β1 inotropy maintains cardiac output.
β2 on mast cells limits further degranulation.
Route: IM into lateral thigh (vastus lateralis) for fastest absorption. EpiPen autoinjector for at-risk patients.
flow.subtext: IM thigh > IM deltoid > SC.

flow.text: IV reserved for CV collapse with monitoring (risk of arrhythmia).
flow.subtext: Adult dose: 0.3-0.5 mg IM, repeat q5-15 min as needed.
flow.insight: IV reserved for CV collapse with monitoring (risk of...

flow.text: 1st-gen H1 antihistamines (diphenhydramine) cross BBB → drowsiness + anticholinergic SE.
flow.subtext: Diphenhydramine, hydroxyzine, chlorpheniramine.

flow.text: Sedating.
Anticholinergic (dry mouth, urinary retention, confusion in elderly).
flow.subtext: Used for acute allergic reactions, motion sickness, sleep.

flow.text: 2nd-gen H1 antihistamines (loratadine, cetirizine, fexofenadine) don't cross BBB → non-drowsy.
flow.subtext: Allergic rhinitis, chronic urticaria, daily allergy management.

flow.text: Better tolerated.
flow.subtext: Both gens block H1 receptors → reduce itch, urticaria, vascular permeability — but neither treats hypotension/bronchospasm.

flow.text: Mast cell stabilizers (cromolyn) prevent degranulation PROPHYLACTICALLY. Inhaled or topical.
flow.subtext: Won't treat acute symptoms.

flow.text: Must be used regularly before exposure.
flow.insight: Must be used regularly before exposure

flow.text: Excellent safety profile (used in pediatrics and pregnancy).
flow.subtext: Largely replaced by ICS for asthma.
flow.insight: Excellent safety profile (used in pediatrics and pregnancy)

flow.text: Omalizumab = anti-IgE mAb. Severe asthma, chronic urticaria, nasal polyps.
flow.subtext: Humanized mAb binds free IgE → prevents binding to FcεRI on mast cells → reduces mast cell sensitization.

flow.text: SC every 2-4 weeks.
flow.subtext: Black box: paradoxical anaphylaxis from the drug itself (observe after first doses).

flow.text: α-agonist decongestants: pseudoephedrine (oral, α1+β), oxymetazoline (topical, α1+α2 — rebound!).
flow.subtext: Constrict nasal mucosal vessels → reduce congestion.

flow.text: Pseudoephedrine = systemic CV effects.
Oxymetazoline >3 days = rhinitis medicamentosa rebound congestion.
flow.subtext: Phenylephrine less effective orally (first-pass).

flow.text: MBL deficiency (small but USMLE-listed): most common complement deficiency, often subclinical, recurrent childhood infections.
flow.subtext: Mannose-binding lectin initiates lectin complement pathway.

flow.text: Low MBL alleles in 10-30% population; most asymptomatic.
flow.subtext: Severity depends on co-existing immune defects.

flow.text: Answer: B · Epi covers all 4 anaphylaxis elements simultaneously via α1, β1, β2.
flow.subtext: Biphasic anaphylaxis (5-20%) → observe 4-6 hours minimum. Discharge with EpiPen prescription, allergy referral, written action plan.

stations.bridge: Bee-sting patient with hives, throat tightness, hypotension. What's the FIRST drug, what does it do at α and β receptors, and why are antihistamines + steroids supportive only?
stations.vignette: A 28-year-old man develops generalized urticaria, throat tightness, wheezing, and hypotension (BP 80/50) within 5 minutes of a bee sting. He is given an emergency dose of intramuscular epinephrine into the lateral thigh, then transported to the ED where he receives IV diphenhydramine and methylprednisolone. Which of the following best describes why epinephrine is first-line for anaphylaxis and how the other agents in the allergy pharmacopeia work?

choices.text: Epi works only via β2 bronchodilation; antihistamines first-line
Epi acts at α1 (BP) + β1 (CO) + β2 (bronchodil + mast cell stabilization) simultaneously
Epi acts only on β1 cardiac receptors; antihistamines do most work
Epi acts on muscarinic receptors; others are GABA agonists
Epi inhibits IgE production; others treat secondary effects

keypoints: α1 vasoconstriction: Raises blood pressure (counters vasodilation), reduces mucosal edema (counters angioedema).
β2 bronchodilation: Reverses bronchospasm.
β1 cardiac inotropy/chronotropy: Improves cardiac output to maintain perfusion.
Mast cell β2 stabilization: Reduces further mast cell degranulation, limiting ongoing mediator release.
Route: IM into the LATERAL THIGH (vastus lateralis) for fastest absorption. SC absorption is slower and less reliable. IV reserved for cardiovascular collapse with monitoring.
Dose: Adult 0.3-0.5 mg IM; may repeat every 5-15 minutes. Pediatric: 0.01 mg/kg IM.
EpiPen: Pre-filled autoinjector for at-risk patients. EVERY patient with anaphylaxis history should carry two.

stations.label: Station 08
stations.title: Monoclonal antibodies
stations.subtitle: Why the mAb naming convention spells out target + species — and how the major clinical mAbs map to disease targets across immunology, oncology, and cardiology.

flow.text: mAb suffix encodes species (immunogenicity ladder): -omab mouse, -ximab chimeric, -zumab humanized, -umab fully human.
flow.subtext: More mouse content = more immunogenic = more anti-drug antibody risk. Modern mAbs are increasingly humanized or fully human precisely to reduce this.

flow.text: Stem before suffix encodes target system: -ci- CV, -tu- tumor, -li- immune, -k(in)- cytokine.
flow.subtext: Reading the full name tells you mechanism.

flow.text: Abciximab = ab + ci + ximab = chimeric anti-CV target (GP IIb/IIIa).
flow.insight: Abciximab = ab + ci + ximab = chimeric anti-CV target (GP...

flow.text: Bevacizumab = bev + a + ci + zumab... actually bevacizumab uses -tu- in some classifications.
flow.subtext: Examples below.

flow.text: Abciximab (CV, chimeric): GP IIb/IIIa antagonist → blocks platelet aggregation → ACS/PCI.
flow.subtext: Binds platelet GP IIb/IIIa receptor → prevents fibrinogen cross-linking.

flow.text: Used during percutaneous coronary intervention.
flow.subtext: Risk: bleeding, thrombocytopenia.

flow.text: Bevacizumab (tumor, humanized): anti-VEGF → blocks tumor angiogenesis. Colorectal, NSCLC, GBM, AMD.
flow.subtext: Starves tumor of new vasculature.

flow.text: Toxicity: HTN, proteinuria, bleeding, impaired wound healing, GI perforation.
flow.subtext: Avoid around surgery.

flow.text: Adalimumab (fully human) + Infliximab (chimeric): anti-TNF for RA, IBD, psoriasis, AS. Black box: TB reactivation.
flow.subtext: Both block TNF-α.
flow.insight: Anti-TNF = autoimmune treatment + TB reactivation risk

flow.text: Adalimumab less immunogenic (fully human).
Infliximab requires INH co-treatment if latent TB.
flow.subtext: Pre-screen PPD/IGRA + HBV + CXR.
flow.insight: Infliximab requires INH co-treatment if latent TB

flow.text: Rituximab (chimeric, anti-CD20): depletes B cells. NHL/CLL, RA, GPA, MS, ITP, AIHA.
flow.subtext: Originally lymphoma drug, now broad autoimmune use.

flow.text: Risk: HBV reactivation, PML (rare), prolonged B-cell depletion.
flow.subtext: Pre-screen HBV.

flow.text: Omalizumab (humanized, anti-IgE): severe persistent allergic asthma, chronic urticaria. (Already covered Station 07.)
flow.subtext: Binds free IgE → prevents binding to FcεRI on mast cells → blunts type I HS.

flow.text: SC every 2-4 weeks.
flow.subtext: Black box: paradoxical anaphylaxis (observe).

flow.text: Methotrexate: folate analog → inhibits DHFR. Low-dose anti-inflammatory for RA (first-line DMARD), psoriasis, IBD. Hi-dose oncology.
flow.subtext: Blocks dihydrofolate reductase → reduces tetrahydrofolate → impairs thymidylate synthesis → blocks DNA.

flow.text: Hepatotoxicity, marrow suppression, mucositis (rescue with leucovorin), pneumonitis, teratogenic.
Omeprazole + itraconazole: PPI raises gastric pH → reduces itraconazole absorption (azole needs acid).
flow.subtext: Itraconazole and ketoconazole require acidic pH for dissolution.

flow.text: PPIs/H2 blockers cause subtherapeutic levels.
flow.subtext: Alternatives: take with cola, switch to fluconazole/voriconazole (pH-independent), or use itraconazole solution.

flow.text: Vaccine adverse effects: local pain, fever, anaphylaxis (rare), GBS post-flu, intussusception post-rota, myocarditis post-mRNA-COVID.
flow.subtext: Most are mild and self-limited.
flow.insight: Vaccine AEs mostly mild; rare serious

flow.text: Serious AEs are rare. 15-30 min post-vaccine observation for anaphylaxis.
flow.subtext: Anti-vaccine misinformation (MMR-autism) comprehensively debunked.

flow.text: Answer: B · Suffix encodes species · stem encodes target.
flow.subtext: Reading the name tells you immunogenicity (species) and target. This is among the most useful naming systems in clinical pharmacology.
flow.insight: Answer: B · mAb naming · species + target

stations.bridge: Patient on infliximab develops antibodies against the drug, reducing efficacy. What does the 'ximab' suffix tell you about why this happens?
stations.vignette: A 45-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis on infliximab develops loss of response over 18 months, with formation of anti-drug antibodies. Her rheumatologist explains that the suffix '-ximab' indicates the drug is a chimeric (mouse-human hybrid) monoclonal antibody and is more immunogenic than fully humanized or human variants. She is switched to adalimumab. Which of the following best describes the monoclonal antibody nomenclature system and the relevance of multiple major clinical mAbs to disease targets?

choices.text: Suffixes are arbitrary; abciximab/infliximab/rituximab share the same target
Suffix encodes species (-omab/-ximab/-zumab/-umab) + stem encodes target
Suffix -mab simply means "antibody"; species/target unrelated to name
Only the stem matters; species suffix is clinically irrelevant
All mAbs are mouse-derived; differences in use unrelated to nomenclature

keypoints: -omab: Fully MOUSE (e.g., muromonab/OKT3 from Ch 13). Most immunogenic. Patients form HACA (human anti-chimeric/mouse antibodies) → loss of efficacy + anaphylaxis risk on re-exposure. Largely abandoned for chronic therapy.

-ximab: CHIMERIC (mouse variable region grafted onto human constant region; ~33% mouse). Less immunogenic than full mouse. Examples: rituximab, infliximab, abciximab. Still develop anti-drug antibodies in some patients.
-zumab: HUMANIZED (only the CDR loops mouse, rest human; ~5-10% mouse). Even less immunogenic. Examples: bevacizumab, omalizumab, trastuzumab, alemtuzumab.
-umab: Fully HUMAN (made via transgenic mice with human Ig genes or phage display). Lowest immunogenicity. Examples: adalimumab, ipilimumab, nivolumab, panitumumab, denosumab.

stations.label: Station 09
stations.title: Capstone
stations.subtitle: Why retroviral oncogenes prove that single-gene mutations can drive cancer — and how the entire 16-chapter immunology curriculum ties together as one integrated story of host defense.

flow.text: Retroviruses integrate into host genome. Occasionally capture host gene fragments during packaging errors.
flow.subtext: Standard retrovirus replication: integrate provirus → transcribe → package new RNA → bud.

flow.text: Sometimes a host gene fragment ends up in the viral RNA.
flow.subtext: Now the virus carries that gene.
flow.insight: Sometimes a host gene fragment ends up in the viral RNA

flow.text: Captured gene is often a proto-oncogene — normal cellular gene whose dysregulation drives cancer.
flow.subtext: c-src, c-myc, c-ras, c-erbB.

flow.text: Normal cellular versions (c-onc) tightly regulated.
flow.subtext: Captured viral versions (v-onc) typically truncated/mutated/under viral promoter → constitutively active.

flow.text: v-onc is constitutively active → expressed in newly infected cells → transformation → cancer.
flow.subtext: Lacks normal regulatory controls of c-onc.

flow.text: Continuous signaling drives uncontrolled proliferation.
Rous sarcoma virus = chicken sarcomas.
flow.subtext: Many other examples in animal cancer biology.

flow.text: Discovery (Bishop & Varmus, Nobel 1989): cellular proto-oncogenes EXIST and can be activated by mutation, translocation, amplification.
flow.subtext: Before this, cancer assumed to be external mutagens or unique viral genes.

flow.text: After: realized normal cells carry these genes; cancer is dysregulation of normal cellular biology.
flow.subtext: Foundation of modern cancer molecular biology.
flow.insight: After: realized normal cells carry these genes

flow.text: Retroviruses keep intersecting immunology: HIV (Ch 14), HTLV-1, endogenous retroviruses, gene therapy vectors.
flow.subtext: HIV destroys CD4 immunity.

flow.text: HTLV-1 causes adult T-cell leukemia. ~8% of human genome is endogenous retroviral remnants.
flow.subtext: Engineered retroviruses (lentiviruses) deliver therapeutic genes (ADA-SCID gene therapy, CAR-T engineering).

flow.text: Curriculum capstone — Module 1 (Ch 1-4): innate immunity foundation (phagocytes, inflammation, complement, granulomas).
flow.subtext: PRR/PAMP recognition → NF-κB → cytokines → leukocyte recruitment → phagocytosis → ROS killing.

flow.text: Defects: LAD, CGD, Chediak-Higashi.
flow.subtext: Granulomas as walled-off persistent antigen.

flow.text: Module 2 (Ch 5-9): adaptive immunity foundations (complement, MHC, T cells, B cells, vaccines).
flow.subtext: Antigen presentation on MHC → TCR recognition → T-cell activation → B-cell help → antibody production → vaccine memory. Class switching, somatic hypermutation, neutralization.

flow.text: Module 3 (Ch 10-13): immune dysregulation (PIDs, hypersensitivity, autoimmunity, transplant).
flow.subtext: When the system breaks: too little (PIDs), too much (hypersensitivity), wrong target (autoimmunity), or rejecting allografts (transplant).

flow.text: Drug toolkit (calcineurin inhibitors, anti-TNF, B-cell depletion).
Module 4 (Ch 14-16): pathogens + pharmacology (HIV, infection immunology, supporting concepts).
flow.subtext: Virus that destroys immunity (HIV).

flow.text: Survey of pathogens and treatments.
flow.subtext: Supporting molecular and pharmacologic concepts that integrate everything.

flow.text: Course-wide themes: innate vs adaptive · pattern recognition · tolerance vs response · memory · cytokine signaling · mobile genetic elements.
flow.subtext: Six unifying threads run through every chapter.

flow.text: Each clinical scenario maps to one or more of these themes.
flow.subtext: Pattern recognition collapses memorization to first-principles reasoning.
flow.insight: Each clinical scenario maps to one or more of these themes

flow.text: Answer: B · Retroviruses capture proto-oncogenes (v-onc) + activate via insertional mutagenesis.
flow.subtext: 16 chapters · ~140 stations · ~1200+ teaching steps.

flow.text: Every USMLE immunology topic covered.
Story-mode learning sequence built.
flow.subtext: Diagram rebuild remains as future visual revision pass.

stations.bridge: Discovery of v-src in Rous sarcoma virus revealed that retroviruses can carry CELLULAR oncogenes. What does this teach us about the relationship between viruses and cancer, and how does it close the immunology curriculum we've built?
stations.vignette: The Rous sarcoma virus, a retrovirus that causes sarcomas in chickens, was found in 1976 by Bishop and Varmus to carry a viral oncogene (v-src) that is a hijacked, mutated version of a normal cellular gene (c-src, a proto-oncogene). This discovery proved that cells contain proto-oncogenes — normal genes whose dysregulation drives cancer. It also illustrates how retroviruses, beyond their direct pathogenicity (e.g., HIV in Ch 14), can transform cells through oncogene capture. Which of the following best describes the retroviral oncogene mechanism and ties together the major themes of the immunology curriculum?

choices.text: Retroviral oncogenes are entirely viral inventions; no cellular counterparts
Retroviruses can capture cellular proto-oncogenes (v-onc); insertional mutagenesis activates c-onc
Retroviruses cause cancer by direct DNA damage; oncogenes unrelated
Retroviral oncogenes are produced by host immune systems
Cellular proto-oncogenes do not exist; cancer is purely environmental

keypoints: Bishop and Varmus's 1976 discovery of cellular proto-oncogenes (via the Rous sarcoma virus v-src/c-src story) opened modern cancer molecular biology. It also illustrates how retroviruses — including HIV (Ch 14) — repeatedly intersect with immunology. We use this final station as the curriculum capstone.
Retroviral oncogene biology:
